> 5. **OpenLR misuse.** OpenLR is a **referencing method**, not a map format. Marking it as a "navigation database standard" is a common conceptual error.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **GDF (ISO 14825):** file-based, map-exchange, ISO open standard. **5.1 (2017)** is the latest.
- ✅ **NDS:** runtime, tiled (R/P tiles), modular building blocks, supports incremental OTA updates.
- ✅ **NDS.Live:** cloud-native REST successor since 2020, replacing binary tile files.
- ✅ **TMC (RDS 57 kHz)** vs **TPEG (DAB+ / IP)** — different transport, different richness.
- ✅ **OpenLR:** map-agnostic location referencing; solves cross-vendor map interop.
- ✅ **ADASIS v3:** horizon provider built on NDS ADAS BB — used for predictive ACC, curve speed warning.
- ✅ Tile index formulas: $X = \lfloor (\lambda + 180)/360 \cdot 2^L \rfloor$, $Y = \lfloor (\phi + 90)/180 \cdot 2^L \rfloor$.
- ✅ WGS-84: $R \approx 6371 \text{ km}$; storage uses **micro-degrees** as **32-bit integers**.
- ✅ DATEX II → C-ITS / V2X domain; not used inside the head unit's offline map.
- ✅ Always pair **NDS** with **in-vehicle** and **GDF** with **map-shop** when answering KTU theory questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPS-Based Navigation in Autonomous Vehicles: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** GPS-Based Navigation in Autonomous Vehicles: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based radio-navigation system owned by the **United States Government** and operated by the **United States Space Force (USSF)**. It provides **geo-spatial positioning (3D position: latitude, longitude, altitude), velocity, and precise time (PVT)** information to a GPS receiver located anywhere on or near the Earth where there is an unobstructed line of sight to **four or more GPS satellites**.

In the context of **Autonomous Vehicles (AVs)**, GPS-Based Navigation is defined as the process of continuously determining the **real-time geographic coordinates, heading (yaw), and speed** of a self-driving vehicle using signals from a constellation of satellites, and feeding this information into the vehicle's **localization, path planning, and motion control** subsystems.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> - **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System):** The umbrella term encompassing all satellite navigation systems (GPS-USA, GLONASS-Russia, Galileo-EU, BeiDou-China, IRNSS/NAVIC-India, QZSS-Japan).
> - **PVT Solution:** Position, Velocity, and Time — the three primary outputs of a GPS receiver.
> - **Pseudorange:** The apparent distance from a satellite to the receiver, measured using the travel time of a signal **before clock errors are corrected**.
> - **Trilateration:** A geometric method of determining an unknown position by measuring distances to known reference points (satellites).
> - **Localization:** The AV's ability to pinpoint its exact position and orientation within a high-definition (HD) map.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you are **lost in a dark forest at night** and you have a walkie-talkie that can call exactly three friends living in three different lighthouses. Each friend can see the stars and shout their own *exact* coordinates down to you. If all three friends measure *exactly how long* it takes their voice to reach you, they can each calculate the **distance** between themselves and you (using the known speed of sound, $c$). With three distances and three known points, there is only **one location in the entire forest** that satisfies all three distances simultaneously — **that is your location**.

A GPS works the same way, except:
- The **lighthouses are satellites** orbiting at 20,200 km altitude.
- The **voices are radio signals** traveling at the **speed of light** ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s).
- The **walkie-talkie is the GPS receiver** in the car.
- The **fourth friend** is needed because the car's clock is cheap and not perfectly synchronized — the 4th satellite solves for this **time error**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is GPS alone insufficient for full AV autonomy?** Civilian GPS has an accuracy of only **±2.5 m to ±5 m CEP (Circular Error Probable)**. For lane-keeping autonomy (where a lane is ~3.5 m wide), this is not enough. Hence, GPS is **fused** with **Inertial Measurement Units (IMU)**, **LiDAR**, **Cameras**, and **HD Maps** — a concept called **Sensor Fusion**.

## 1.4 The Three Segments of GPS (The Heart of the System)

| Segment | Component | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Space Segment** | 24+ MEO satellites | Broadcast continuous L-band signals |
| **Control Segment** | Master + Monitor stations | Track satellites, correct orbits/clocks |
| **User Segment** | GPS receivers in vehicles | Compute PVT from signals |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How GPS Determines Position

The entire GPS positioning process can be broken down into **six sequential logic steps**:

- **Step 1 — Atomic Clock Synchronization:** Each satellite carries **four atomic clocks** (Cesium/Rubidium) that are pre-synchronized to a master atomic clock at the **United States Naval Observatory (USNO)**. This guarantees that *satellite time* $t_s$ is known with nanosecond-level accuracy.

- **Step 2 — Continuous Signal Broadcast:** Every satellite continuously transmits a **spread-spectrum L-band signal** carrying two key pieces of information:
  (a) The satellite's precise *orbital coordinates* (ephemeris) — its position in space at the time of transmission.
  (b) The *time of transmission* $t_s$, encoded in the navigation message.

- **Step 3 — Travel Time Measurement:** The receiver (in the car) records the **time of arrival** $t_u$ of the signal. The travel time is:
  $$\Delta t = t_u - t_s$$
  Since signal travels at $c$, the geometric range is:
  $$r = c \cdot \Delta t$$

- **Step 4 — Pseudorange Computation:** Because the vehicle's quartz clock is *not* perfectly synchronized with satellite time, a **clock bias** $b_u$ exists. The measured range is therefore a *pseudorange*:
  $$\rho = r + c \cdot b_u$$
  where $b_u$ is the receiver clock error in seconds.

- **Step 5 — Geometric Trilateration:** Using **four pseudoranges** from four satellites with known positions $(x_s, y_s, z_s)$, the receiver solves a system of four equations to find its own $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ and the clock bias $b_u$.

- **Step 6 — Velocity Computation (Doppler Shift):** The relative motion between satellite and vehicle causes a **Doppler frequency shift** in the carrier signal. By measuring this shift over time, the receiver computes its **3D velocity vector** in the ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) frame.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why 4 satellites" rule:** Three satellites are mathematically sufficient to solve for 3D position $(x, y, z)$ via 2D-style geometric intersection of spheres. However, the **4th unknown — clock bias $b_u$** — requires a 4th independent equation, hence **a minimum of 4 satellites** is mandatory for an absolute 3D PVT solution.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **all high-yield equations** required for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations on this topic. Master these before attempting numericals.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Geometric range = speed of light × travel time | m, m/s, s |
| 2 | $\rho = r + c \cdot b_u$ | Pseudorange equation (includes receiver clock bias) | m |
| 3 | $\rho = \sqrt{(x_s - x_u)^2 + (y_s - y_u)^2 + (z_s - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u$ | Pseudorange in 3D Cartesian (ECEF) frame | m |
| 4 | $N = \min(4)$ | Minimum satellites for 3D PVT solution | integer |
| 5 | $\text{CEP} = 0.5 \cdot \text{rms}$ | Circular Error Probable (50% of fixes fall within this radius) | m |
| 6 | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy} + q_{zz} + q_{tt}}$ | Geometric Dilution of Precision (overall accuracy multiplier) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{PDOP} = \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy} + q_{zz}}$ | Position DOP (3D position accuracy only) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{HDOP} = \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy}}$ | Horizontal DOP (latitude + longitude only) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\sigma_{\text{pos}} = \text{PDOP} \cdot \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ | Positioning accuracy = DOP × user-equivalent range error | m |
| 10 | $\text{UERE} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{clock}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{eph}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{iono}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{tropo}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{mp}}^2}$ | Total user-equivalent range error (RSS of all error sources) | m |
| 11 | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f_L}$ | Carrier wavelength (for L1 = 1575.42 MHz → λ ≈ 19.05 cm) | m, m/s, Hz |
| 12 | $v = \frac{\Delta f}{f_L} \cdot c$ | Velocity from Doppler shift $\Delta f$ of carrier | m/s |

> **Where:** $q_{ii}$ are diagonal elements of the position-covariance matrix $\mathbf{Q}_x = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}$, $\mathbf{H}$ is the geometry matrix of unit vectors to satellites, and $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ aggregates all error sources.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Pilot & Adaptive Cruise Control:** GPS provides **lane-level** location on highways so the AV can stay centered.
- **Fleet Management & Logistics:** GPS + cellular modem enables **real-time tracking** of commercial vehicles for ETA prediction.
- **ADAS Emergency Call (eCall):** Mandated in EU vehicles, transmits GPS coordinates automatically after airbag deployment.
- **Geofencing:** Logistics AVs auto-stop when leaving a predefined virtual boundary.
- **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything):** GPS provides a common **time reference** (microsecond-level) that all nearby vehicles share to coordinate maneuvers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Distance from a Single Satellite

**Starting Principle:** Radio waves travel at the constant speed of light in vacuum $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1 —** The satellite embeds the precise moment of signal transmission, $t_s$, into the navigation message.

$$t_s \text{ is broadcast by satellite}$$

**Step 2 —** The receiver logs the moment the signal arrives, $t_u$.

$$t_u \text{ is recorded by receiver}$$

**Step 3 —** The time of flight of the signal is the difference.

$$\Delta t = t_u - t_s$$

**Step 4 —** Distance equals speed × time, so the geometric range is:

$$r = c \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5 —** Substituting the expression for $\Delta t$:

$$r = c \cdot (t_u - t_s)$$

> **Insight:** If the receiver's clock were perfectly synchronized, one satellite would mathematically give the distance. But it is *not* — hence the pseudorange concept below.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Pseudorange Equation

**Starting Premise:** The receiver's quartz oscillator drifts by a small unknown amount $b_u$ (in seconds). This makes the receiver's measured time $t_u'$ differ from true GPS time $t_u$.

$$t_u' = t_u + b_u$$

**Step 1 —** The receiver computes an *apparent* time of flight.

$$\Delta t' = t_u' - t_s = (t_u + b_u) - t_s$$

**Step 2 —** The apparent range (pseudorange) is:

$$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t' = c \cdot (t_u - t_s) + c \cdot b_u$$

**Step 3 —** Recognizing that $c \cdot (t_u - t_s) = r$ (the true geometric range), we get:

$$\rho = r + c \cdot b_u$$

**Step 4 —** In 3D Cartesian coordinates, the geometric range from satellite $s$ to receiver $u$ is the Euclidean distance:

$$r = \sqrt{(x_s - x_u)^2 + (y_s - y_u)^2 + (z_s - z_u)^2}$$

**Step 5 —** Substituting back, the **complete pseudorange equation** becomes:

$$\rho = \sqrt{(x_s - x_u)^2 + (y_s - y_u)^2 + (z_s - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u$$

> **Interpretation:** The pseudorange is the "fake" range we measure; the true range is shorter by $c \cdot b_u$. We need a 4th equation to find $b_u$ since it is the 4th unknown.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: 3D Position Solution (Why 4 Satellites?)

**Given:** Pseudoranges $\rho_i$ from $N \geq 4$ satellites, with known satellite positions $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 1 —** Write the pseudorange equation for each satellite.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= \sqrt{(x_1 - x_u)^2 + (y_1 - y_u)^2 + (z_1 - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u \\
\rho_2 &= \sqrt{(x_2 - x_u)^2 + (y_2 - y_u)^2 + (z_2 - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u \\
\rho_3 &= \sqrt{(x_3 - x_u)^2 + (y_3 - y_u)^2 + (z_3 - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u \\
\rho_4 &= \sqrt{(x_4 - x_u)^2 + (y_4 - y_u)^2 + (z_4 - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 —** This is a **nonlinear system** of 4 equations in 4 unknowns: $x_u, y_u, z_u, b_u$. Solve iteratively using **linearization** (Newton-Raphson method).

**Step 3 —** Express each equation as $f_i(x_u, y_u, z_u, b_u) = \rho_i$. Taylor-expand around an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i &\approx f_i(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0) + \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_u}\Delta x + \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_u}\Delta y + \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial z_u}\Delta z + \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial b_u}\Delta b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 —** Compute the partial derivatives. Let $r_i$ be the geometric range from guess to satellite $i$:

$$r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_0)^2 + (y_i - y_0)^2 + (z_i - z_0)^2}$$

The partial derivatives are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial f_i}{\partial x_u} &= \frac{-(x_i - x_0)}{r_i} \\
\frac{\partial f_i}{\partial y_u} &= \frac{-(y_i - y_0)}{r_i} \\
\frac{\partial f_i}{\partial z_u} &= \frac{-(z_i - z_0)}{r_i} \\
\frac{\partial f_i}{\partial b_u} &= c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 —** Define the **geometry matrix** $\mathbf{H}$ whose $i$-th row is $\left[ -\frac{x_i - x_0}{r_i},\ -\frac{y_i - y_0}{r_i},\ -\frac{z_i - x_0}{r_i},\ c \right]$ and the **residual vector** $\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \rho_i - f_i(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$.

**Step 6 —** Solve the linear system using **least-squares**:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 7 —** Update the guess and iterate until convergence ($\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < 10^{-6}$ m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_0 &\leftarrow x_0 + \Delta x \\
y_0 &\leftarrow y_0 + \Delta y \\
z_0 &\leftarrow z_0 + \Delta z \\
b_0 &\leftarrow b_0 + \Delta b
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The final $(x_u, y_u, z_u, b_u)$ gives the **3D position** of the vehicle in ECEF coordinates and the **receiver clock error**. This is why **4 satellites** are mathematically required.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Velocity from Doppler Shift

**Step 1 —** The satellite transmits a carrier of known frequency $f_L$ (e.g., L1 = 1575.42 MHz). The receiver sees a Doppler-shifted frequency $f_R$:

$$f_R = f_L \left(1 - \frac{v_{rel}}{c}\right)$$

**Step 2 —** The Doppler shift is $\Delta f = f_L - f_R = f_L \cdot \frac{v_{rel}}{c}$.

**Step 3 —** Solve for the radial (line-of-sight) relative velocity:

$$v_{rel} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_L} \cdot c$$

**Step 4 —** Using 4 satellites, set up a linear system of $v_{rel}$ values along unit line-of-sight vectors, then solve least-squares for the **3D velocity vector** $\mathbf{v}_u = (v_x, v_y, v_z)$ in ECEF.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GPS System Architecture (Three-Segment Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete **functional architecture** of the GPS system, showing the flow of signals and data between the three operational segments and how the AV's onboard receiver integrates this into the autonomous driving stack.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPACE[SPACE SEGMENT 24 plus MEO Satellites at 20200 km]
        satA[SAT 1 Atomic Clock L1 L2 Broadcast]
        satB[SAT 2 Atomic Clock L1 L2 Broadcast]
        satC[SAT 3 Atomic Clock L1 L2 Broadcast]
        satD[SAT 4 Atomic Clock L1 L2 Broadcast]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL SEGMENT Ground Based]
        ms[Master Station USNO Time Reference]
        monA[Monitor Station 1 to 16 Global]
        monB[Monitor Station Global Network]
        uplink[Ground Antenna Uplink Station]
    end

    subgraph USER[USER SEGMENT VEHICLE GPS RECEIVER]
        ant[GPS Patch Antenna Roof Mounted]
        rf[RF Front End LNA Filter]
        acq[Signal Acquisition and Tracking]
        nav[PVT Solver Pseudorange Least Squares]
        pos[Position Velocity Time Output]
    end

    subgraph AV[AV LOCALIZATION AND CONTROL STACK]
        fuse[Sensor Fusion EKF UKF GPS plus IMU]
        plan[Path Planning Module]
        ctrlAV[Vehicle Motion Controller]
    end

    satA -->|RF Signal 1575 MHz| ant
    satB -->|RF Signal| ant
    satC -->|RF Signal| ant
    satD -->|RF Signal| ant

    monA -->|Tracking Data| ms
    monB -->|Tracking Data| ms
    ms -->|Ephemeris Correction| uplink
    uplink -->|S Band Uplink| satA
    uplink -->|S Band Uplink| satB
    uplink -->|S Band Uplink| satC
    uplink -->|S Band Uplink| satD

    ant --> rf
    rf --> acq
    acq --> nav
    nav --> pos
    pos --> fuse
    fuse --> plan
    plan --> ctrlAV
```

## 4.2 GPS Signal Acquisition and PVT Computation Flow

This diagram traces the **internal signal processing pipeline** of a typical automotive-grade GPS receiver module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a[Antenna LNA] --> b[RF Downconversion to Baseband]
    b --> c[Correlation with PRN Code]
    c --> d[Code Phase and Carrier Phase Extraction]
    d --> e[Sub millisecond Pseudorange Generation]
    e --> f[Ephemeris Decode from Navigation Message]
    f --> g[Linearization Newton Raphson]
    g --> h[ECEF Position x y z]
    h --> i[Conversion to LLA Latitude Longitude Altitude]
    h --> j[Velocity from Doppler]
    i --> k[PVT Output to AV CAN Bus]
    j --> k
```

## 4.3 Dilution of Precision (DOP) Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    gdop[GDOP Geometric Dilution] --> pdop[PDOP Position DOP]
    gdop --> tdop[TDOP Time DOP]
    pdop --> hdop[HDOP Horizontal DOP]
    pdop --> vdop[VDOP Vertical DOP]
    hdop --> lat[Latitude Error]
    hdop --> lon[Longitude Error]
    vdop --> alt[Altitude Error]
    tdop --> clk[Receiver Clock Error]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define GPS and list its three operational segments.
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based radio-navigation system that provides geo-spatial positioning, velocity, and time (PVT) information to a receiver anywhere on or near Earth with an unobstructed sky view. It is operated by the **United States Space Force (USSF)**.

The **three operational segments** of GPS are:

1. **Space Segment:** A constellation of **24+ satellites** in **6 orbital planes** at an altitude of **20,200 km**, with an orbital period of **~11 hours 58 minutes**. Each satellite carries atomic clocks and broadcasts L-band signals.
2. **Control Segment:** A global network of **1 Master Control Station (Schriever AFB, Colorado)**, **16 monitoring stations**, and **4 ground antenna uplink stations** that track satellites, correct orbital errors, and synchronize atomic clocks.
3. **User Segment:** The **GPS receiver** in the vehicle (or any device) that decodes the signals and computes its own PVT.

> **Validity for 3 marks:** Definition (1 mark) + 3 segments with 1-line description (2 marks).

---

### Question 2: Why are a minimum of four satellites required to determine a 3D position in GPS?
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

A 3D position fix requires solving for **four unknowns**: the three Cartesian coordinates $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ of the receiver **plus** the receiver's clock bias $b_u$, because the vehicle's quartz clock is not perfectly synchronized with GPS atomic time.

Each satellite provides **one independent equation** (the pseudorange equation):

$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u$$

Therefore, to solve the resulting nonlinear system of equations for 4 unknowns, **a minimum of 4 satellites** is mathematically required. If we had perfect clocks, 3 satellites would suffice; however, real-world receiver clocks have unknown drift, making the 4th satellite essential.

> **Validity for 3 marks:** 4 unknowns identification (1 mark) + pseudorange reasoning (1 mark) + minimum 4 conclusion (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In Module-wise End-Semester Evaluation (ESE), Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both alternatives are given below — only one is to be attempted.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — COMPREHENSIVE GPS IN AUTONOMOUS VEHICLES

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of GPS with a neat block diagram of the receiver. Discuss the role of pseudorange measurement in determining position.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working Principle of GPS (4 marks):**

The GPS works on the principle of **trilateration using one-way time-of-arrival (TOA) ranging**. The operational flow is:

- **Step 1:** All satellites in the constellation transmit **spread-spectrum L1 (1575.42 MHz)** and **L2 (1227.60 MHz)** signals, each modulated with a unique **Pseudo-Random Noise (PRN) code** and a **navigation message** containing the satellite's ephemeris (precise position) and clock correction parameters.

- **Step 2:** The vehicle-mounted GPS receiver **detects** these signals and, by correlating them with locally generated PRN code replicas, measures the **code phase** — from which the **time of arrival** is determined.

- **Step 3:** The difference between the time of transmission (extracted from the navigation message) and the time of arrival yields the **signal travel time** $\Delta t$. Multiplying by $c$ gives the **pseudorange** $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$.

- **Step 4:** Using pseudoranges from at least 4 satellites and the satellites' known positions, the receiver solves a system of equations to determine its own 3D position and clock bias.

**2. Pseudorange Equation (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_u)^2 + (y_i - y_u)^2 + (z_i - z_u)^2} + c \cdot b_u
$$

Here $b_u$ is the receiver clock bias. The 4th satellite is needed specifically to solve for this unknown bias.

**3. Role in Position Determination (1 mark):**

Pseudoranges act as **observed inputs** to the iterative Newton-Raphson least-squares solver, which converges to the receiver's ECEF coordinates $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$. These are subsequently converted to the geodetic **latitude, longitude, and altitude (LLA)** representation used by navigation maps.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the various error sources in GPS and explain the concept of Dilution of Precision (DOP). How does DOP affect autonomous vehicle navigation accuracy?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Major GPS Error Sources (3 marks):**

| Error Source | Typical Magnitude | Mitigation Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionosphere delay** | 1–5 m | Dual-frequency receivers, Klobuchar model |
| **Troposphere delay** | 0.5–2.5 m | Saastamoinen/Hopfield model |
| **Satellite clock error** | 1–2 m | Broadcast ephemeris corrections |
| **Ephemeris (orbital) error** | ~1 m | Updated almanac, WAAS corrections |
| **Multipath** | 0.5–1 m (urban canyons up to 10 m) | Choke-ring antennas, RAIM |
| **Receiver noise** | 0.1–1 m | Better RF front-end, longer integration |

The total error is aggregated as **UERE (User-Equivalent Range Error)** via root-sum-squares:

$$\text{UERE} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{clock}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{eph}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{iono}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{tropo}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{mp}}^2 + \sigma_{\text{noise}}^2}$$

**2. Concept of DOP (2 marks):**

Dilution of Precision is a **geometric multiplier** that captures how satellite-receiver geometry amplifies the UERE into positioning error:

$$\sigma_{\text{position}} = \text{DOP} \times \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$$

DOP is computed from the **covariance matrix** of the position solution:

$$\mathbf{Q}_x = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}$$

DOP variants:
- **GDOP** — geometry + time
- **PDOP** — 3D position only
- **HDOP** — horizontal (lat/lon) only — *most critical for AV lane-keeping*
- **VDOP** — vertical (altitude) only
- **TDOP** — time only

**3. Impact on AV Navigation (2 marks):**

A **low DOP (< 2)** implies satellites are well spread out across the sky, giving good geometry and low positioning error. A **high DOP (> 6)** means satellites are clustered, multiplying UERE and worsening accuracy. For AVs:
- **High HDOP in tunnels/urban canyons** causes the vehicle to lose confident GPS fixes, forcing reliance on **dead-reckoning** with IMU.
- **Low HDOP is mandatory** for highway autonomous driving to maintain **lane-level accuracy (sub-meter)**.
- Modern AVs use **tightly-coupled INS/GPS fusion** so that even during momentary GPS outages, position is predicted via IMU integration.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE TOPIC: GPS SIGNAL STRUCTURE & VELOCITY

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the GPS signal structure (carrier, PRN code, navigation message) and explain how the receiver acquires and tracks signals from multiple satellites simultaneously.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. GPS Signal Structure (3 marks):**

The GPS signal on the L1 carrier (1575.42 MHz) is constructed as:

$$s_{L1}(t) = A_c \cdot C(t) \cdot D(t) \cdot \sin(2\pi f_{L1} t) + A_p \cdot P(t) \cdot D(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_{L1} t)$$

Where:
- $C(t)$ = **C/A (Coarse/Acquisition) code** — a 1023-chip **Gold code** at 1.023 Mbps with a period of 1 ms; each satellite has a unique PRN sequence for **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)**.
- $P(t)$ = **P(Y) code** — a 10.23 Mbps precision code for military use; encrypted to Y-code for non-US users.
- $D(t)$ = **Navigation message** — a 50 bps data stream of 25 frames (each 1500 bits), containing satellite ephemeris, almanac, clock correction, and ionospheric model parameters.
- The L2 carrier (1227.60 MHz) carries only the P(Y) code and is used for **ionospheric error correction** by dual-frequency receivers.

**2. Signal Acquisition (2 marks):**

The receiver must search across a 2D **code-phase × Doppler-frequency** grid to find the peak correlation for each satellite's PRN. For each candidate (code phase $\tau$, Doppler $f_d$):
- The incoming signal is multiplied by a locally generated PRN code and carrier replica.
- The output is integrated over 1 ms (one C/A period).
- The integrator output is squared and compared against a threshold. A peak above threshold means **acquisition** of that satellite.

Modern receivers use FFT-based **parallel code-phase search** to reduce acquisition time from ~30 s to <1 s.

**3. Signal Tracking (2 marks):**

Once acquired, the receiver enters a **tracking loop** consisting of:
- **Code-tracking Delay-Locked Loop (DLL):** Maintains alignment of local PRN replica with incoming code.
- **Carrier-tracking Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** or **Frequency-Locked Loop (FLL):** Maintains phase/frequency lock on the carrier.
- Outputs of these loops feed back to refine the pseudorange and Doppler estimate continuously.

Multiple channels (typically 12–24) run in parallel, allowing simultaneous tracking of all visible satellites.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With the help of relevant equations, explain how the velocity of a GPS receiver is determined. A vehicle's GPS receiver measures a Doppler shift of +150 Hz on the L1 carrier. Calculate the radial velocity of the vehicle relative to the satellite. Given: $f_{L1}$ = 1575.42 MHz and $c$ = 3 × 10⁸ m/s.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Velocity Determination Principle (3 marks):**

The relative motion between a satellite and the receiver induces a **Doppler frequency shift** in the received carrier. If the receiver moves *towards* the satellite, the frequency increases (positive Doppler); if it moves *away*, the frequency decreases.

The Doppler-shifted received frequency is:

$$f_R = f_L \left(1 - \frac{v_{rel}}{c}\right)$$

The **Doppler shift** is:

$$\Delta f = f_L - f_R = f_L \cdot \frac{v_{rel}}{c}$$

Therefore, the **radial (line-of-sight) velocity** is:

$$v_{rel} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_L} \cdot c$$

To get the **full 3D velocity vector** $(v_x, v_y, v_z)$, the receiver uses Doppler measurements from at least 4 satellites and solves a linear least-squares system where the geometry matrix $\mathbf{H}$ is the same as for position, but the observation vector contains range-rate values $\dot{\rho}_i$:

$$\dot{\rho}_i = \frac{(x_i - x_u)(v_{x_i} - v_{x_u}) + (y_i - y_u)(v_{y_i} - v_{y_u}) + (z_i - z_u)(v_{z_i} - v_{z_u})}{r_i} + c \cdot \dot{b}_u$$

The receiver's satellite motion $(v_{x_i}, v_{y_i}, v_{z_i})$ is known from the ephemeris; hence the unknowns are the receiver's velocity components and clock drift rate $\dot{b}_u$.

**2. Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\Delta f = +150$ Hz
- $f_{L1} = 1575.42 \times 10^6$ Hz
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1 —** Substitute the radial velocity formula:

$$v_{rel} = \frac{\Delta f}{f_{L1}} \cdot c$$

**Step 2 —** Plug in the numerical values:

$$v_{rel} = \frac{150 \text{ Hz}}{1575.42 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \times 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 —** Simplify the fraction:

$$v_{rel} = \frac{150 \times 3 \times 10^8}{1575.42 \times 10^6} \text{ m/s} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{10}}{1.57542 \times 10^9} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 —** Final calculation:

$$v_{rel} = 28.566 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 —** Converting to km/h:

$$v_{rel} = 28.566 \times 3.6 = 102.84 \text{ km/h}$$

> **Conclusion:** The vehicle is moving *towards* the satellite (positive Doppler) at a radial velocity of **≈ 28.57 m/s (≈ 102.84 km/h)**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the receiver clock bias $b_u$:** Many students write the pseudorange equation as $r = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2}$ without the $+ c \cdot b_u$ term. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing pseudorange with geometric range:** The "pseudo" prefix is non-negotiable. Always state $b_u$ exists because the receiver uses a cheap clock.
> 3. **Mixing up L1 and L2 frequencies:** L1 = 1575.42 MHz (C/A + P(Y) code), L2 = 1227.60 MHz (P(Y) only). Marked as common error.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why 4 satellites" reasoning:** Just saying "4 is needed" is not enough — must explicitly state **4 unknowns $(x, y, z, b_u)$**.
> 5. **Doppler sign convention:** Positive $\Delta f$ means the vehicle is *approaching* the satellite, **not** moving away. This is the most common sign error in numericals.
> 6. **Unit errors in velocity numericals:** Final answer **must be in m/s** OR clearly state conversion to km/h. Partial marks lost for missing units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before Exam Day**

- [x] **GPS = Global Positioning System**, US-owned, currently operated by **USSF**.
- [x] **Three segments:** Space (24+ MEO satellites at 20,200 km), Control (1 master + 16 monitor + 4 uplink stations), User (the receiver).
- [x] **Two carrier frequencies:** L1 (1575.42 MHz) — civilian + military; L2 (1227.60 MHz) — military / dual-frequency iono correction.
- [x] **One-way ranging:** Only the **satellite transmits**; the receiver is a passive listener (unlike radar which is two-way).
- [x] **Pseudorange equation:** $\rho = r + c \cdot b_u$ — the clock bias $b_u$ is the reason a 4th satellite is mandatory.
- [x] **Minimum 4 satellites** for full 3D PVT (position + velocity + time) solution.
- [x] **Trilateration** (NOT triangulation) is the underlying geometry — uses distances, not angles.
- [x] **C/A code** is the civilian ranging code — 1023-chip Gold code, 1.023 Mbps, 1 ms period.
- [x] **P(Y) code** is the encrypted military precision code at 10.23 Mbps.
- [x] **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** is a *geometric* multiplier; $\sigma_{pos} = \text{DOP} \times \text{UERE}$.
- [x] **DOP Hierarchy:** GDOP ⊃ PDOP (HDOP + VDOP) and TDOP.
- [x] **Doppler shift** $\Delta f$ on the carrier gives radial velocity: $v_{rel} = (\Delta f / f_L) \cdot c$.
- [x] **Major error sources:** ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, satellite clock/ephemeris errors, multipath, receiver noise.
- [x] **Civilian GPS accuracy:** ~2.5–5 m CEP — **insufficient for full AV autonomy**, hence sensor fusion with IMU, LiDAR, cameras, and HD maps is essential.
- [x] **Velocity accuracy** of GPS is typically **~0.1 m/s RMS** — very useful for AV speed control.
- [x] **WAAS / SBAS** systems improve civilian accuracy to ~1–2 m over a region.
- [x] **Real-Time Kinematic (RTK-GPS)** with carrier-phase corrections can achieve **cm-level** accuracy — used in high-end AV mapping and precision agriculture.
- [x] **Newton-Raphson linearization** of pseudorange equations is the standard numerical method for iterative PVT solution.
- [x] **ECEF coordinates** from the solver are converted to **LLA (latitude, longitude, altitude)** for human-readable map display.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Architecture of Autonomous GPS Vehicle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Architecture of Autonomous GPS Vehicle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Autonomous GPS Vehicle** is a self-driving ground vehicle that uses the **Global Positioning System (GPS)** as its primary absolute localization reference, fused with onboard inertial, perception, and proprioceptive sensors, to perceive its environment, plan a trajectory, and execute actuation commands without continuous human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Tone):** *The basic architecture of an autonomous GPS vehicle is the layered functional framework — comprising **Sensing, Perception, Localization, Planning, Control, and Actuation** subsystems — that integrates GPS-based absolute position with relative sensing to deliver a self-navigating automotive platform.*

The architecture is built on three foundational engineering pillars:
1. **Where am I?** → Localization (GPS + IMU + map matching)
2. **What is around me?** → Perception (Radar / LiDAR / Camera)
3. **What should I do next?** → Decision & Control (Planner + Controller)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "New Driver in Kochi Traffic"

Imagine a foreigner driving through the chaotic streets of Kochi for the very first time:
- **The GPS** is the **Google Maps voice** telling him "You are currently on MG Road" → this is *absolute localization*.
- **The IMU + Wheel Speed Sensors** are his **inner ear and sense of speed** → they tell him "I just turned left and slowed down" even when GPS signal drops inside a tunnel → *Dead Reckoning*.
- **Cameras & Radar** are his **eyes and ears** → detecting the auto-rickshaw that just cut him off → *Perception*.
- **The Brain (ECU + Planner)** decides: "Brake gently, then change lane" → *Planning*.
- **His hands and feet** turn the wheel and press the pedal → *Actuation*.

The **autonomous vehicle architecture** is exactly this — a **closed-loop cybernetic system** where each layer passes refined data to the next, ultimately moving the car safely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Metric (SAE J3016):** Automation is graded from **Level 0 (No Automation)** to **Level 5 (Full Automation)**. Most GPS-based autonomous prototypes studied in KTU curriculum sit at **Level 3 (Conditional)** to **Level 4 (High)** automation.

## 1.3 Why a "Layered" Architecture?

A monolithic (single-block) design fails because:
- Sensor noise contaminates planning decisions.
- Real-time constraints (typically **< 100 ms** end-to-end latency) cannot be guaranteed.
- Fault isolation becomes impossible.

A **layered architecture** enables modularity, testability, and ISO 26262 functional safety compliance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Functional Layers of an Autonomous GPS Vehicle

### Layer 1 — Sensor / Sensing Layer (The "Raw Data" Layer)
- **GNSS / GPS Receiver** (L1 = 1575.42 MHz, L2 = 1227.60 MHz, L5 = 1176.45 MHz)
- **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — 6-DOF: 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope (sometimes magnetometer → 9-DOF).
- **Wheel Odometry** — encoders on each driven wheel.
- **Exteroceptive Sensors** — Camera, Radar (24/77 GHz), LiDAR (905 nm / 1550 nm), Ultrasonic.

### Layer 2 — Perception Layer (The "Understanding" Layer)
- **Sensor Fusion** using Kalman Filter (KF), Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), or Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF).
- **Object Detection & Classification** — YOLO, Faster-RCNN (deep learning).
- **Lane & Free-Space Detection.**
- **Traffic Sign Recognition (TSR).**

### Layer 3 — Localization Layer (The "Where am I?" Layer)
- **GNSS Positioning** (Standalone, DGPS, RTK-GPS).
- **Map Matching** — snapping GPS fix to digital HD map lane.
- **INS / GNSS Integration** — bridges GPS outages via dead reckoning.
- **LiDAR Localization / Visual SLAM** as backup.

### Layer 4 — Planning Layer (The "Brain" Layer)
- **Global Route Planner** (A*, Dijkstra, RRT* on road network).
- **Behavioral Planner** (Finite State Machine, POMDP).
- **Local Trajectory Planner** (polynomial, frenet-frame, lattice planner).

### Layer 5 — Control Layer (The "Reflex" Layer)
- **Lateral Control** — Stanley Controller, Pure Pursuit, MPC.
- **Longitudinal Control** — PID, Adaptive Cruise (ACC).
- **Vehicle Dynamics Model** — Bicycle Model.

### Layer 6 — Actuation Layer (The "Muscle" Layer)
- **Drive-by-Wire (DBW)** — electronic throttle, brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire.
- **CAN Bus / Automotive Ethernet** communication.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Values |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pseudorange $P = c \cdot (t_r - t_s)$ | Distance from satellite to receiver ($c$ = speed of light) | $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 2 | Trilateration (minimum satellites) | Solve for $(x, y, z, t)$ | **Min 4 SVs** for 3D fix |
| 3 | DGPS Error Correction | $\epsilon_{corrected} = \epsilon_{rover} - \epsilon_{base}$ | Accuracy $\sim$ **1–3 m** |
| 4 | RTK-GPS Carrier Phase | Uses $\lambda$ (wavelength) ambiguity resolution | Accuracy $\sim$ **±2 cm** |
| 5 | Dilution of Precision (DOP) | $GDOP = \sqrt{q_{xx}+q_{yy}+q_{zz}+q_{tt}}$ | Lower is better ($< 2$ excellent) |
| 6 | IMU Strapdown Integration | $\theta(t) = \int \omega(t) \, dt$ | Drift $\sim$ **degrees/hour** |
| 7 | Kalman Filter Prediction | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + Bu_{k-1}$ | State vector update |
| 8 | Kalman Filter Update | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman gain |
| 9 | Bicycle Model (Kinematic) | $\dot{x} = v \cos\psi$, $\dot{y} = v \sin\psi$, $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$ | $L$ = wheelbase |
| 10 | PID Controller | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ tunable |
| 11 | Stanley Lateral Error | $\delta(t) = \psi(t) + \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{k \cdot e_{fa}}{v(t)}\right)$ | $e_{fa}$ = front-axle error |
| 12 | Pure Pursuit Lookahead | $\delta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2L \sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$ | $l_d$ = lookahead distance |
| 13 | GPS Update Rate | Standard: **1–10 Hz**, High-end: **20–50 Hz** | Latency $\sim$ **50 ms** |
| 14 | End-to-End Latency Budget | Sense → Fuse → Plan → Control → Actuate | **< 100 ms** target |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The same layered architecture powers KTU-relevant technologies such as **ADAS in Tata / Mahindra vehicles**, **ISRO's GAGAN (SBAS)**, and **BIS-approved ARAI autonomous testbed** platforms in Pune.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — How GPS Trilateration Yields Vehicle Position

The GPS receiver measures **pseudorange** $\rho_i$ to the $i$-th satellite. For four satellites, we get four equations:

$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$$

Where:
- $(x, y, z)$ = unknown vehicle position in ECEF frame
- $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ = known satellite coordinates from ephemeris
- $\delta t_r$ = receiver clock bias (the fourth unknown)

**Step 1:** Subtract the equation for satellite 1 from those of satellites 2, 3, 4 to eliminate the nonlinear square root.

$$\rho_i - \rho_1 = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2} - \sqrt{(x - x_1)^2 + (y - y_1)^2 + (z - z_1)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Linearize using Taylor expansion around an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_{r,0})$.

$$\Delta \rho_i \approx \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z}\Delta z + c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_r)$$

**Step 3:** Build the linear system $\mathbf{H} \Delta \mathbf{x} = \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$ and solve iteratively via **Least Squares**:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 4:** Update estimate $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \Delta \mathbf{x}$ until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < \epsilon$.

> This iterative linearization is the **Extended Least Squares** method, mathematically the precursor to EKF used in INS/GPS fusion.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison for Autonomous Vehicles

| Sensor | Range | Accuracy | Update Rate | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GPS (L1)** | Global | 5–10 m | 1–10 Hz | Absolute, no drift | Multipath, urban canyon |
| **RTK-GPS** | Global | **±2 cm** | 10–20 Hz | Survey-grade | Needs base station |
| **IMU (MEMS)** | Local | Drift ~1°/s | 100–1000 Hz | High rate, no ext. dep. | Drift accumulates |
| **LiDAR (32/64 layer)** | 100–200 m | ±2 cm | 10–20 Hz | 3D point cloud | Cost, weather |
| **Radar (77 GHz)** | 0.2–250 m | ±0.1 m | 20 Hz | Works in fog/rain | Low resolution |
| **Camera (Mono/Stereo)** | 50–250 m | Pixel-level | 30–60 Hz | Semantic info | Lighting sensitive |
| **Ultrasonic** | 0.1–5 m | ±1 cm | 20 Hz | Parking use | Very short range |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SAE Automation Levels vs GPS Architecture Role

| SAE Level | Driver Role | GPS Role | Architecture Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Full control | Navigation only (inform) | None |
| 1 | Continuous | Cruise/spacing | ACC / LKA helper |
| 2 | Continuous | Map-aware | Tesla Autopilot basic |
| 3 | Fallback | HD-map + GPS precise | Waymo early |
| 4 | Optional | RTK-GPS + HD map | Geo-fenced robotaxi |
| 5 | None | Full reliance | Theoretical only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — End-to-End Architecture of an Autonomous GPS Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[GNSS Satellite Constellation] -->|RF L1/L2/L5 Signal| B[GPS Receiver Antenna on Vehicle]
    B --> C[GNSS Receiver Module]
    C -->|Raw Pseudorange + Ephemeris| D[Sensor Fusion EKF Core]

    E[IMU 6-DOF] -->|Accel + Gyro @ 200 Hz| D
    F[Wheel Speed Encoders] -->|Vehicle Speed| D
    G[LiDAR Sensor] -->|Point Cloud| H[Perception Stack]
    I[Front Camera] -->|Image Frames| H
    J[Radar 77GHz] -->|Tracks| H

    H -->|Object List + Free Space| D

    D -->|Fused Pose x,y,z,heading| K[Localization Module]
    K -->|Matched to HD Map Lane| L[Global Route Planner A Star]
    L -->|High Level Waypoints| M[Behavioral Planner FSM]
    M -->|Decision: Lane Keep / Change / Stop| N[Local Trajectory Planner Frenet]
    N -->|Smooth Trajectory| O[Control Layer MPC and PID]

    P[V2X Receiver] -->|Signal Phase Timing| D
    Q[Driver HMI Display] --> L
    Q --> M

    O -->|Throttle Command| R[Drive by Wire Throttle ECU]
    O -->|Brake Command| S[Brake by Wire ECU]
    O -->|Steer Command| T[Steer by Wire ECU]

    R --> U[Vehicle Dynamics]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U -->|IMU Feedback| E
    U -->|Wheel Feedback| F

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style U fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Closed-Loop Perception to Actuation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sense1[GPS Fix] --> Fuse[Sensor Fusion]
    Sense2[IMU] --> Fuse
    Sense3[Camera] --> Fuse
    Sense4[LiDAR] --> Fuse
    Fuse --> Local[Localization]
    Local --> Plan[Planner]
    Plan --> Ctrl[Controller]
    Ctrl --> Act[Actuator]
    Act --> Dyn[Vehicle Motion]
    Dyn --> Env[Environment Update]
    Env --> Sense1
    Env --> Sense3
    Dyn --> Sense2
```

## 4.3 Data Flow Timing Diagram (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GPS as GNSS Receiver
    participant IMU as IMU
    participant EKF as Fusion Core
    participant Plan as Planner
    participant Ctrl as Controller
    participant Veh as Vehicle Actuator

    loop Every 100 ms cycle
        GPS->>EKF: Pseudorange @ 10 Hz
        IMU->>EKF: Accel/Gyro @ 200 Hz
        EKF->>Plan: Fused Pose (latency 5 ms)
        Plan->>Ctrl: Trajectory (latency 20 ms)
        Ctrl->>Veh: Throttle/Brake/Steer (latency 10 ms)
        Veh-->>IMU: New motion feedback
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU viva:** Examiners frequently ask *"What is the role of the EKF in an autonomous GPS vehicle?"* — answer: *It optimally fuses the high-rate, short-term accurate IMU data with the low-rate, absolute, long-term stable GPS data, overcoming each sensor's individual weakness.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]
**List and briefly explain the four main functional blocks of an autonomous GPS vehicle architecture.**

**Model Answer (KTU-marker preferred format):**
1. **Sensing Layer** — GPS, IMU, LiDAR, Camera, Radar collect raw environmental and kinematic data.
2. **Perception & Localization Layer** — Processes raw data (sensor fusion via EKF) to estimate vehicle pose (x, y, heading) and detect objects/lanes.
3. **Planning Layer** — Generates a safe, drivable trajectory from current pose to goal, using global (A*) + local (Frenet) planners.
4. **Control & Actuation Layer** — Converts planned trajectory into throttle, brake, steering commands via drive-by-wire ECUs (PID/MPC controllers).

*(3 marks — 1 mark for listing blocks + 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]
**Differentiate between Standalone GPS and RTK-GPS in the context of autonomous vehicle localization.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Standalone GPS | RTK-GPS |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | **5–10 m** | **±2 cm** |
| Method | Pseudorange only | Carrier phase + base station correction |
| Cost | Low | High (needs base / NTRIP) |
| Use case | Navigation display | Lane-level autonomy |
| Latency | Low | Slightly higher (link dependent) |

*(3 marks — comparison table form preferred.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]
**With a neat block diagram, describe the basic architecture of an autonomous GPS vehicle. Explain the role of the GPS receiver and IMU in localization.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (3 marks):**
Draw the six-layer architecture: *Sensors → Perception → Localization → Planning → Control → Actuation*. Include the **closed-loop feedback** from vehicle dynamics back to IMU/encoders.

**Step 2 — GPS Receiver Role (2 marks):**
The GPS receiver provides **absolute, globally referenced position** (latitude, longitude, altitude) using trilateration from at least 4 satellites. It eliminates long-term drift, anchors the vehicle to a digital map, and supports geo-fencing (e.g., "operate only inside Infosys campus").

**Step 3 — IMU Role (2 marks):**
The IMU provides **high-rate (100–1000 Hz) relative motion** (acceleration + angular velocity). During GPS outages (tunnels, urban canyons), the IMU **dead-reckons** the position via strapdown integration: $\theta(t) = \int \omega(t) \, dt$. It also resolves GPS ambiguities at high dynamics (sharp turns).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]
**Explain the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) based sensor fusion used in autonomous vehicles. State the prediction and update equations.**

**Model Solution:**

The EKF is the **central fusion engine** of the autonomous GPS architecture. It non-linearly fuses GPS (slow, absolute) and IMU (fast, drifting) into an optimal pose estimate.

**Prediction Step (Time Update):**
$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = f(\hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1}, u_{k-1})$$
$$P_{k\mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1\mid k-1} F_k^T + Q_k$$

Where $F_k$ is the Jacobian of $f(\cdot)$ (vehicle dynamics model) and $Q_k$ is process noise covariance.

**Update Step (Measurement Update):**
$$K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}$$
$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1}))$$
$$P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\mid k-1}$$

**Engineering Interpretation (for 7-mark completion):**
- $H_k$ is the Jacobian of measurement model $h(\cdot)$ linking state to GPS pseudorange.
- $R_k$ models GPS noise (~3 m), $Q_k$ models IMU drift (~0.01°/s).
- The filter **balances trust** — when GPS is good (low $R_k$), the prediction is corrected heavily; when IMU dominates (GPS blocked), the filter coasts on prediction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

#### (a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]
**Discuss the various types of sensors used in an autonomous GPS vehicle. Compare LiDAR, Radar, and Camera-based perception.**

**Model Solution:**

**Sensor Taxonomy (3 marks):**
- **Positioning:** GPS, IMU, wheel odometry
- **Exteroceptive:** LiDAR, Radar, Camera, Ultrasonic
- **Communication:** V2X, DSRC, C-V2X

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Property | LiDAR | Radar (77 GHz) | Camera |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output | 3D point cloud | Range + velocity | RGB image |
| Range | 100–200 m | 0.2–250 m | 50–250 m |
| Resolution | High | Low | High (semantic) |
| Weather robustness | Poor (fog) | Excellent | Poor (night) |
| Cost | High | Medium | Low |
| Use | Mapping, localization | ACC, AEB, blind spot | Lane, signs, lights |

A robust autonomous vehicle **fuses all three** — sensor redundancy is mandated by **ISO 26262 ASIL-D** for safety-critical functions.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]
**Derive the GPS pseudorange equation and explain how a minimum of four satellites is required to obtain a 3D position fix.**

**Model Solution:**

The pseudorange measured by a GPS receiver to satellite $i$ is:

$$P_i = \rho_i + c \cdot (\delta t_r - \delta t_s^i) + I_i + T_i + \epsilon_i$$

Where:
- $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 + (z - z_i)^2}$ is the **true geometric range**.
- $c \cdot \delta t_r$ = receiver clock error.
- $c \cdot \delta t_s^i$ = satellite clock error (corrected via ephemeris).
- $I_i$ = ionospheric delay, $T_i$ = tropospheric delay, $\epsilon_i$ = measurement noise.

**Why 4 Satellites?**
The unknowns are $(x, y, z, \delta t_r)$ → **four unknowns** → minimum **four independent equations** are required, which are provided by four satellites. With 3 satellites, the receiver clock bias $\delta t_r$ cannot be solved (it manifests as a 3D bias of up to 100 m), so a 3D fix is impossible — only a 2D fix with assumed altitude is possible.

**Engineering Insight:** Modern receivers track **8–12 satellites simultaneously** to compute **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** and discard poor-geometry sets, improving reliability to < 2 m CEP under open sky.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Pseudorange with True Range:** Always include clock bias term. Losing 2 marks here is the #1 KTU paper-complaint.
> 2. **Writing "3 satellites for 3D fix":** This is a classic blunder — the 4th unknown (clock bias) is **mandatory**.
> 3. **Skipping the IMU role in fusion:** Examiners expect the *integration* justification, not just listing sensors.
> 4. **Missing closed-loop feedback arrows** in the block diagram → 1–2 marks deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting units** ($c$ in m/s, frequencies in Hz, accuracy in cm/m) — KTU strictly penalizes unit-less numerical answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Six-layer architecture**: Sensing → Perception → Localization → Planning → Control → Actuation.
- ✅ **GPS minimum satellites**: **4 for 3D fix** (solve x, y, z, clock bias).
- ✅ **Pseudorange equation**: $P = \rho + c \cdot \delta t_r + \text{atmospheric errors}$.
- ✅ **L1 = 1575.42 MHz**, **L2 = 1227.60 MHz**, **L5 = 1176.45 MHz**.
- ✅ **Standalone GPS accuracy**: **5–10 m**; **DGPS**: **1–3 m**; **RTK**: **±2 cm**.
- ✅ **EKF is the workhorse** of INS/GPS fusion — predict + update cycle.
- ✅ **End-to-end latency target**: **< 100 ms** for safe autonomy.
- ✅ **IMU role**: bridges GPS outages via dead reckoning; complements GPS rate.
- ✅ **Sensor redundancy** (LiDAR + Radar + Camera) is mandated by **ISO 26262**.
- ✅ **SAE Level 3–4** is the realistic target for current GPS-based autonomous vehicles.
- ✅ **Bicycle Model** is the standard kinematic abstraction for control design.
- ✅ **V2X + GPS** represents the next-gen cooperative autonomy layer (5G NR-V2X).
- ✅ **KTU viva favorite**: *"What happens if GPS is jammed?"* — Answer: *The INS dead-reckons for a few seconds; perception-based localization (LiDAR SLAM) takes over; the system enters a degraded "safe-stop" mode if uncertainty exceeds threshold.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance and Direction Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distance and Direction Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Navigation and Control**, *Distance and Direction Calculation* refers to the mathematical procedures used by a vehicle's positioning module to determine the spatial separation (geodesic distance) and the angular orientation (bearing/azimuth) between two geographic points on the Earth's surface, typically expressed using the **WGS-84 (World Geodetic System 1984)** reference ellipsoid. The vehicle's position is given by a pair of geodetic coordinates: **latitude (φ)** measured from the equator, and **longitude (λ)** measured from the **Prime Meridian (Greenwich, 0°)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 OEMUT723 syllabus, Module 1 introduces the geometric and trigonometric foundations a navigation ECU uses to plan a route, estimate time-to-arrival (ETA), and issue steering commands.

The two principal quantities computed are:

1. **Geodesic Distance (d):** The shortest path along the curved surface of the Earth between point A(φ₁, λ₁) and point B(φ₂, λ₂), measured in **meters (m)** or **kilometers (km)**.
2. **Bearing / Direction (θ):** The clockwise angle from **True North** to the line joining A to B, measured in **degrees (°)** from **0° to 360°**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are sitting in a car at *Kochi (9.93°N, 76.26°E)* and want to drive to *Bengaluru (12.97°N, 77.59°E)*. Before you even start the engine, your **GPS-enabled head-unit** does two things invisibly:

- **"How far?"** → It draws an imaginary string tightly across the curved globe between the two cities and measures its length. This is the *distance*.
- **"Which way?"** → It places a giant protractor at your current location, with the 0° mark pointing to the North Pole, and reads off the angle toward Bengaluru. This is the *bearing*.

If the string were a piece of elastic, it would naturally settle along the *Great Circle* — the shortest path a long-haul flight would take. If the string were stretched so that it always crossed every meridian (longitude line) at the *same* angle, it would form a **Rhumb Line** — the path a ship steering a constant compass course would follow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Every navigation problem in a modern car is reduced to two simple questions: *How many meters?* and *How many degrees clockwise from North?* All the formulas below are just systematic ways to answer those two questions on a curved planet.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants

| Symbol | Constant | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| R | Mean Earth Radius (WGS-84) | **6371.0088** | km |
| a | WGS-84 Semi-major axis | **6378.137** | km |
| b | WGS-84 Semi-minor axis | **6356.7523** | km |
| f | WGS-84 Flattening | 1/298.257223563 | — |
| e² | First Eccentricity Squared | 0.00669437999014 | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Geometric Models of "Distance"

On a flat map, distance is trivial (Pythagoras). On a sphere, two distinct paths matter in navigation:

1. **Great Circle Path (Orthodrome):**
   - The intersection of the Earth's surface with a plane passing through the Earth's center.
   - It is the **shortest** path between two points.
   - The bearing *changes* continuously along the path (except along the equator or meridians).
   - Used by **aerial and long-distance marine navigation**.

2. **Rhumb Line (Loxodrome):**
   - A path that crosses every meridian at the **same constant bearing**.
   - It is generally **longer** than the great circle, except when both points lie on the equator or on the same meridian.
   - Used by **classic ship navigation** and useful for plotting simple waypoint legs on a road map.

## 2.2 Why a Special Formula? (The "How" and "Why")

A standard 3D Cartesian distance formula cannot be used directly because:
- Geographic coordinates are **angular**, not linear.
- The Earth is an **oblate spheroid**, not a perfect sphere.
- One degree of longitude shrinks in length as we move toward the poles (cos φ factor).

The **Haversine formula** solves the spherical case elegantly by exploiting the half-angle identity `hav(θ) = sin²(θ/2)`, which keeps numerical error small even for tiny distances — critical when a vehicle is computing centimeter-level lane positioning.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All angles must be in **radians** when used inside `sin`, `cos`, `tan`, `atan2`. Multiply degrees by **π/180** to convert.

### 2.3.1 Haversine Formula (Spherical Earth Distance)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\varphi}{2}\right) + \cos\varphi_1 \cdot \cos\varphi_2 \cdot \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
c &= 2 \cdot \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{a},\;\sqrt{1-a}\right) \\[4pt]
d &= R \cdot c
\end{aligned}
$$

where
- $\Delta\varphi = \varphi_2 - \varphi_1$ (latitude difference, rad)
- $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_2 - \lambda_1$ (longitude difference, rad)
- $R$ = **6371.0088 km**
- $d$ = great-circle distance in **km**

### 2.3.2 Initial Bearing (Forward Azimuth) — Spherical Earth

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \sin(\Delta\lambda) \cdot \cos(\varphi_2) \\[4pt]
x &= \cos(\varphi_1)\cdot\sin(\varphi_2) - \sin(\varphi_1)\cdot\cos(\varphi_2)\cdot\cos(\Delta\lambda) \\[4pt]
\theta &= \operatorname{atan2}(y,\;x) \quad \text{(in radians)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Normalize to compass bearing:
$$
\theta_{\text{compass}} = \big(\theta_{\text{deg}} + 360\big) \bmod 360
$$

### 2.3.3 Rhumb Line Distance and Bearing

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\psi &= \ln\!\left(\frac{\tan\!\big(\tfrac{\pi}{4} + \tfrac{\varphi_2}{2}\big)}{\tan\!\big(\tfrac{\pi}{4} + \tfrac{\varphi_1}{2}\big)}\right) \\[4pt]
q &= \frac{\Delta\psi}{\Delta\lambda} \quad \text{(if } \Delta\lambda \neq 0\text{)} \\[4pt]
\text{If } \vert\Delta\lambda\vert > \pi &: \text{ adjust sign of } \Delta\lambda \text{ via wrap correction} \\[4pt]
d_{\text{rhumb}} &= R \cdot \sqrt{(\varphi_2 - \varphi_1)^2 + q^2 \cdot (\Delta\lambda)^2} \\[4pt]
\theta_{\text{rhumb}} &= \operatorname{atan2}(\Delta\lambda,\;\Delta\psi)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.4 Mid-Point (for plotting route center)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_m &= \operatorname{asin}\!\Big(\sin\varphi_1 \cdot \cos\!\tfrac{d}{2R} + \cos\varphi_1 \cdot \sin\!\tfrac{d}{2R} \cdot \cos\theta\Big) \\[4pt]
\lambda_m &= \lambda_1 + \operatorname{atan2}\!\Big(\sin\theta \cdot \sin\!\tfrac{d}{2R} \cdot \cos\varphi_1,\; \cos\!\tfrac{d}{2R} - \sin\varphi_1 \cdot \sin\varphi_m\Big)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.5 Quick Reference Summary Table

| Quantity | Formula Type | Use Case | Inputs Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| Great-circle distance | Haversine | ETA, fuel estimate | $\varphi_1,\lambda_1,\varphi_2,\lambda_2$ |
| Initial bearing | $\operatorname{atan2}$ | First steering heading | $\varphi_1,\lambda_1,\varphi_2,\lambda_2$ |
| Final bearing | Symmetric of initial | Arrival heading | $\varphi_1,\lambda_1,\varphi_2,\lambda_2$ |
| Rhumb-line distance | $\sqrt{(\Delta\varphi)^2+q^2(\Delta\lambda)^2}$ | Constant-heading leg | $\varphi_1,\lambda_1,\varphi_2,\lambda_2$ |
| Mid-point | $\operatorname{asin}$+$\operatorname{atan2}$ | Map labelling | $\varphi_1,\lambda_1,d,\theta$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Modern automotive GNSS receivers (u-blox, STMicro Teseo) typically implement Haversine in firmware. Rhumb-line math is found in older marine ECDIS modules still referenced in OEMUT723 textbooks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of the Haversine Formula

### Step 1 — Start with the Spherical Law of Cosines

For a sphere of radius $R$ and two points $A$ and $B$ with central angle $\Delta\sigma$ between them, the chord length $d$ satisfies:

$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \sin\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 + \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)
$$

This is numerically **unstable** for very small distances because $\cos(d/R) \approx 1$ and floating-point precision is lost.

### Step 2 — Introduce the Haversine Function

Define
$$
\operatorname{hav}(\theta) = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{2}
$$

Using the trigonometric identity for the cosine of a difference, and after algebraic expansion (see worked expansion below), the central angle satisfies:

$$
\operatorname{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \operatorname{hav}(\Delta\varphi) + \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cdot \operatorname{hav}(\Delta\lambda)
$$

### Step 3 — Expand Both Sides

The right-hand side becomes:

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\varphi}{2}\right) + \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)
$$

The left-hand side, by the haversine definition, becomes:

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\frac{d}{2R}\right) = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{c}{2}\right)
$$

where $c = d/R$ is the angular distance in radians.

### Step 4 — Solve for the Angular Distance

Let $a = \sin^2(\Delta\varphi/2) + \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \sin^2(\Delta\lambda/2)$. Then:

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\frac{c}{2}\right) = a \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \sin\!\left(\frac{c}{2}\right) = \sqrt{a}
$$

$$
\frac{c}{2} = \arcsin(\sqrt{a}) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; c = 2\arcsin(\sqrt{a})
$$

Using the **equivalent and numerically safer** form with `atan2`:

$$
c = 2 \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{a},\; \sqrt{1-a}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Recover the Linear Distance

$$
d = R \cdot c = 2R \cdot \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{a},\; \sqrt{1-a}\right)
$$

This is the final Haversine distance formula.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Initial Bearing

The bearing is the angle of the great-circle path measured clockwise from True North. At the starting point, the direction vector tangent to the great circle and pointing toward the destination has:

- a **north-component** $= \cos\varphi_2 \sin(\Delta\lambda)$
- an **east-component** $= \cos\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 - \sin\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)$

The compass bearing is the angle of this vector in the local east-north-up (ENU) frame:

$$
\theta = \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(\sin(\Delta\lambda)\cos\varphi_2,\; \cos\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 - \sin\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)\right)
$$

The `atan2(y, x)` function (note: y first, then x) returns an angle in **[-π, π]**, so we convert to a positive compass bearing by:

$$
\theta_{\text{compass}} = \left(\theta \cdot \frac{180}{\pi} + 360\right) \bmod 360
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A vehicle starts at **Kochi** ($\varphi_1 = 9.9312°$N, $\lambda_1 = 76.2673°$E) and travels to **Coimbatore** ($\varphi_2 = 11.0168°$N, $\lambda_2 = 76.9558°$E). Compute (a) the great-circle distance and (b) the initial bearing.

### Step A — Convert Degrees to Radians

$$
\varphi_1 = 9.9312° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.17334 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\varphi_2 = 11.0168° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.19228 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = 76.2673° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.33107 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = 76.9558° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.34308 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step B — Compute Differences

$$
\Delta\varphi = 0.19228 - 0.17334 = 0.01894 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta\lambda = 1.34308 - 1.33107 = 0.01201 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step C — Compute the Haversine `a` Term

$$
\sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\varphi}{2}\right) = \sin(0.00947) = 0.009470
$$

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\varphi}{2}\right) = 0.00008968
$$

$$
\cos\varphi_1 = \cos(0.17334) = 0.98500
$$

$$
\cos\varphi_2 = \cos(0.19228) = 0.98157
$$

$$
\sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) = \sin(0.006005) = 0.006005
$$

$$
\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right) = 0.00003606
$$

$$
a = 0.00008968 + (0.98500)(0.98157)(0.00003606) = 0.00008968 + 0.00003488 = 0.00012456
$$

### Step D — Compute the Angular Distance `c`

$$
\sqrt{a} = \sqrt{0.00012456} = 0.011160
$$

$$
\sqrt{1-a} = \sqrt{0.99987544} = 0.99993772
$$

$$
c = 2 \cdot \operatorname{atan2}(0.011160,\; 0.99993772) = 2 \times 0.011160 = 0.022320 \text{ rad}
$$

### Step E — Compute the Linear Distance

$$
d = R \cdot c = 6371.0088 \times 0.022320 = 142.18 \text{ km}
$$

The great-circle distance from **Kochi to Coimbatore** is **≈ 142.2 km**.

### Step F — Compute the Initial Bearing

$$
y = \sin(\Delta\lambda) \cdot \cos\varphi_2 = \sin(0.01201)(0.98157) = (0.012010)(0.98157) = 0.011789
$$

$$
x = \cos\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 - \sin\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)
$$

Compute each piece:

- $\cos\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 = (0.98500)(0.19092) = 0.18806$
- $\sin\varphi_1 = 0.17260$
- $\cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda) = (0.98157)(0.99993) = 0.98150$
- Product: $(0.17260)(0.98150) = 0.16941$

Therefore:

$$
x = 0.18806 - 0.16941 = 0.01865
$$

### Step G — Bearing Angle and Compass Normalization

$$
\theta = \operatorname{atan2}(0.011789,\; 0.01865) = 0.5629 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\theta_{\text{deg}} = 0.5629 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 32.25°
$$

Since the angle is already positive, no wrap-around is needed:

$$
\boxed{\theta_{\text{compass}} \approx 32.25° \text{ (roughly North-Northeast)}}
$$

### Step H — Verification by Real-World Sanity Check

A road distance of ≈ 150 km is the actual Kochi-Coimbatore highway length, and the direction **NNE** matches the road's general orientation. The math is consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: On-Vehicle Distance & Direction Module

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how raw GNSS coordinates flow through a typical automotive navigation ECU and emerge as distance and bearing values for the HMI and ADAS layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GNSS Antenna on Roof] --> B[GNSS Receiver Chip]
    B --> C[WGS-84 Lat and Long Stream]
    C --> D[Dead Reckoning Fusion with IMU]
    D --> E[Filtered Vehicle Position phi1 lam1]
    F[Destination Waypoint DB] --> G[Target phi2 lam2]
    E --> H[Distance and Direction Calculator]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Haversine Engine]
    H --> J[atan2 Bearing Engine]
    I --> K[Great Circle Distance d]
    J --> L[Initial Bearing theta]
    K --> M[Route Planner]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Steering Command]
    M --> O[HMI Display Unit]
    M --> P[ETA and Fuel Estimator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the antenna on the left. Data flows rightward: GNSS → IMU fusion → (current pos, target pos) → distance/bearing engine → consumer modules. The **Haversine Engine** and **atan2 Bearing Engine** are the two computational cores corresponding to the formulas in Section 2.

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Decision Logic for Choosing Path Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Two waypoints given] --> Q{Long distance over 500 km}
    Q -- Yes --> GC[Use Great Circle Haversine]
    Q -- No --> C{Constant heading required}
    C -- Yes --> RL[Use Rhumb Line Calculation]
    C -- No --> GC2[Use Great Circle Haversine]
    GC --> E[Output d and theta]
    RL --> E
    GC2 --> E
    E --> END[Send to Display]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the terms *Geodesic Distance* and *Initial Bearing* as used in automotive navigation. State the two key inputs required by the Haversine formula.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Geodesic Distance:** The shortest path between two points along the curved surface of the Earth (WGS-84 ellipsoid), measured in meters or kilometers.
- **Initial Bearing:** The clockwise angle, measured in degrees from True North (0°–360°), from the start point to the destination along the great-circle path.
- **Haversine Inputs:** The latitude and longitude of the start point ($\varphi_1, \lambda_1$) and the latitude and longitude of the destination point ($\varphi_2, \lambda_2$), plus the mean Earth radius $R = 6371.0088$ km.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per definition + inputs)*

---

### Question 2  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a Great Circle path and a Rhumb Line. State one practical application of each in vehicle/vehicle-adjacent navigation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Great Circle | Rhumb Line |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Shortest path on sphere | Constant-bearing path |
| Bearing along path | Changes continuously | Remains constant |
| Used by | Aircraft and GNSS ETA | Marine ECDIS, road-segment waypoint legs |

- **Great Circle application:** Estimating the *shortest possible* distance and fuel between two cities for an aircraft or autonomous long-haul truck.
- **Rhumb Line application:** Plotting a constant-heading leg between two adjacent road waypoints in a turn-by-turn navigation system.

*(3 marks: 2 for differentiation + 1 for applications)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**A vehicle is at point A with coordinates (12.5°N, 75.0°E) and must navigate to point B at (15.0°N, 78.0°E).**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the great-circle distance using the Haversine formula. Apply the formula to compute the distance between A and B. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compute the initial bearing from A to B. Explain why an `atan2` function is preferred over a simple `arctan` for this calculation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Haversine Derivation and Numerical Evaluation**

Recall the spherical law of cosines:
$$
\cos\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \sin\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 + \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)
$$

Apply the haversine substitution:
$$
\operatorname{hav}\!\left(\frac{d}{R}\right) = \operatorname{hav}(\Delta\varphi) + \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cdot \operatorname{hav}(\Delta\lambda)
$$

Expand:
$$
a = \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\varphi}{2}\right) + \cos\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\lambda}{2}\right)
$$

Therefore:
$$
d = 2R \cdot \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(\sqrt{a},\;\sqrt{1-a}\right)
$$

**Numerical evaluation (converted to radians):**

- $\varphi_1 = 0.21817$ rad, $\varphi_2 = 0.26180$ rad
- $\lambda_1 = 1.30899$ rad, $\lambda_2 = 1.36136$ rad
- $\Delta\varphi = 0.04363$ rad, $\Delta\lambda = 0.05236$ rad

$$
a = \sin^2(0.02182) + \cos(0.21817)\cos(0.26180)\sin^2(0.02618)
$$

$$
a = (0.02182)^2 + (0.97630)(0.96593)(0.02618)^2 = 0.000476 + 0.000661 = 0.001137
$$

$$
c = 2 \operatorname{atan2}(\sqrt{0.001137},\; \sqrt{0.998863}) = 2 \times 0.03372 = 0.06744 \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\boxed{d = 6371.0088 \times 0.06744 = 429.65 \text{ km}}
$$

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 3 for numerical computation, 1 for final answer with unit)*

---

**Part (b) — Initial Bearing and atan2 Justification**

Using the bearing formula:
$$
y = \sin(\Delta\lambda)\cos\varphi_2 = \sin(0.05236)(0.96593) = 0.05232 \times 0.96593 = 0.05054
$$

$$
x = \cos\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 - \sin\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda)
$$

- $\cos\varphi_1 \sin\varphi_2 = (0.97630)(0.25882) = 0.25269$
- $\sin\varphi_1 \cos\varphi_2 \cos(\Delta\lambda) = (0.21644)(0.96593)(0.99863) = 0.20874$

$$
x = 0.25269 - 0.20874 = 0.04395
$$

$$
\theta = \operatorname{atan2}(0.05054,\; 0.04395) = 0.85476 \text{ rad} = 48.97°
$$

Normalize: $\theta_{\text{compass}} = (48.97 + 360) \bmod 360 = 48.97°$ (already positive).

$$
\boxed{\theta_{\text{compass}} \approx 48.97° \text{ (Northeast direction)}}
$$

**Why `atan2` and not `arctan`?**

The two-argument function `atan2(y, x)` correctly preserves the **quadrant** of the resulting angle using the sign of both `y` and `x`. A single-argument `arctan(y/x)` returns values only in $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$, which is **wrong for bearings greater than 90° or less than 0°** (e.g., southwest directions). In automotive navigation, a vehicle must steer in all 360°, so `atan2` is mandatory.

*(7 marks: 4 for numerical bearing, 3 for the `atan2` explanation)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the aid of neat expressions, explain the *Rhumb Line* method of distance computation between two waypoints. A ship sails from (10°N, 70°E) to (15°N, 75°E). Compute the rhumb-line distance. (7 marks)
**(b)** Compare the great-circle and rhumb-line paths qualitatively. Under what practical condition does the rhumb-line distance equal the great-circle distance? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Rhumb Line Derivation and Numerical Evaluation**

A rhumb line crosses every meridian at the same angle $\theta_{\text{rhumb}}$. The Mercator projection is conformal and straightens a rhumb line, giving:

$$
\Delta\psi = \ln\!\left(\frac{\tan(\pi/4 + \varphi_2/2)}{\tan(\pi/4 + \varphi_1/2)}\right)
$$

$$
q = \frac{\Delta\psi}{\Delta\lambda} \quad \text{(constant for a given leg)}
$$

Distance:
$$
d_{\text{rhumb}} = R \cdot \sqrt{(\varphi_2 - \varphi_1)^2 + q^2 (\Delta\lambda)^2}
$$

**Numerical values (radians):**
- $\varphi_1 = 0.17453$, $\varphi_2 = 0.26180$
- $\lambda_1 = 1.22173$, $\lambda_2 = 1.30899$
- $\Delta\varphi = 0.08727$, $\Delta\lambda = 0.08727$ (both happen to be 5° here)

$$
\tan(\pi/4 + 0.08727/2) = \tan(0.82930) = 1.11061
$$

$$
\tan(\pi/4 + 0.13090/2) = \tan(0.91630) = 1.30323
$$

$$
\Delta\psi = \ln(1.30323 / 1.11061) = \ln(1.17345) = 0.15999
$$

$$
q = 0.15999 / 0.08727 = 1.83344
$$

$$
d = 6371.0088 \cdot \sqrt{(0.08727)^2 + (1.83344)^2(0.08727)^2}
$$

$$
d = 6371.0088 \cdot 0.08727 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 3.3615} = 6371.0088 \cdot 0.08727 \cdot 2.0879
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{\text{rhumb}} = 1160.5 \text{ km}}
$$

For reference, the Haversine (great-circle) distance for the same points is ≈ 1157.0 km. The rhumb line is only ≈ 0.3 % longer.

*(7 marks: 3 for derivation, 3 for numerical, 1 final)*

---

**Part (b) — Qualitative Comparison and Equality Condition**

| Property | Great Circle | Rhumb Line |
|---|---|---|
| Length | Shortest possible | Longer (except special cases) |
| Bearing variation | Continuous change | Constant |
| Difficulty to steer | Requires heading updates | Easy — hold heading |
| Best for | Long distances, aircraft | Marine, simple waypoint legs |

**Equality Condition:** A rhumb line coincides with a great circle **only when both points lie on the same meridian** (same longitude) **or on the equator**. In all other cases, the rhumb line is strictly longer than the great circle.

*(7 marks: 4 for comparison table, 3 for the equality condition with justification)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the radian conversion.** Trigonometric functions in C/MATLAB/Python take radians. Plugging in degrees gives silently wrong answers and full mark deduction. (–2 to –3 marks)
> 2. **Using `atan` instead of `atan2`.** The bearing may be off by 180°, especially for south-westerly routes. Always justify the use of `atan2(y, x)`. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up the argument order of `atan2`.** It is `atan2(y, x)` — north-component first, east-component second. Reversing it gives a bearing 90° off. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not normalizing the bearing to 0°–360°.** A raw `atan2` result of -47.3° must be reported as 312.7°. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to state the Earth radius $R = 6371.0088$ km.** Examiners award a mark for explicit constants. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the wrap correction for $\Delta\lambda$ in rhumb-line math** when the points straddle the antimeridian (±180°). (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two outputs, one problem:** Every navigation question asks for *distance* and *direction* — keep them separate in your answer.
- **Haversine = great-circle distance** on a sphere; use it as the default unless the question explicitly asks for a rhumb line.
- **Initial bearing formula** uses `atan2(sin(Δλ)·cos φ₂, cos φ₁·sin φ₂ − sin φ₁·cos φ₂·cos(Δλ))` — memorize the numerator and denominator order.
- **Radians are mandatory** inside every trig call; degrees are mandatory for the *final* bearing output.
- **Normalize bearing** with `(deg + 360) mod 360`.
- **Rhumb line = constant heading**; great circle = shortest path.
- **Special equality:** Rhumb line equals great circle **only** on the equator or on a common meridian.
- **Mean Earth radius** for KTU problems: **R = 6371.0088 km** (WGS-84 spherical mean).
- **WGS-84 eccentricity squared $e^2$** = 0.00669437999014 — required if the question upgrades to Vincenty's formula on the ellipsoid.
- **`atan2` always beats `atan`** in navigation because it preserves the quadrant — non-negotiable for 360° steering.
- **Sanity-check the result:** A Kochi-Bengaluru bearing must be in the NE quadrant (0°–90°). A negative or > 180° answer signals an error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) Based Positioning in Autonomous Vehicles: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) Based Positioning in Autonomous Vehicles: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
