**Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) Based Positioning** in autonomous vehicles refers to the use of satellite constellations orbiting the Earth to provide continuous, all-weather, three-dimensional Position, Velocity, and Time (PVT) information to a vehicle's onboard navigation computer. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *OEMUT723 – Automotive Navigation and Control*, GNSS is identified as the **primary absolute reference sensor** in the autonomous vehicle (AV) localization stack, supplying a globally referenced coordinate frame (typically WGS-84 for GPS) against which all other relative sensors (Inertial Measurement Units, LiDAR, Radar, Cameras) are corrected, fused, or referenced.

Mathematically, a GNSS receiver determines its position $\mathbf{x}_{rx} = [x, y, z]^T$ in the Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed (ECEF) frame by measuring the **propagation delay** of electromagnetic signals transmitted by at least **four (4)** satellites in line-of-sight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** GNSS is treated as the foundational localization layer. The student must clearly distinguish between *absolute positioning* (GNSS — global reference) and *relative positioning* (dead-reckoning, INS — local reference). This distinction is a high-frequency examiner trigger.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in an open field on a foggy night and cannot see any landmarks. Suddenly, four lighthouses at known distant locations each flash a light at a precisely known instant. You measure **how long the light took to reach your eyes** — knowing the speed of light is constant, you multiply (time × speed) to obtain the *distance* to each lighthouse. Once you know your distance to four known points, geometry uniquely fixes your position in 3D space and even corrects your faulty clock.

**Satellites** are the lighthouses. Your **GNSS receiver** is your eye. The **speed of light** is the constant conversion factor. This process is called **trilateration** (not triangulation — a common student mistake, since no angles are measured, only distances).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (**exact**: 299,792,458 m/s, since the metre is defined from it).
- **Standard GNSS carrier frequencies:**
    - **GPS L1:** 1575.42 MHz
    - **GPS L2:** 1227.60 MHz
    - **GPS L5:** 1176.45 MHz
- **Minimum satellites for 3D fix:** **4** (3 for geometry + 1 to resolve receiver clock bias).
- **Standard accuracy metric:** **CEP (Circular Error Probable)** — the radius of a circle centered on the true position that contains **50%** of the position fixes.
- **Higher-tier metric:** **DRMS (Distance Root Mean Square)** and **2DRMS = 2 × DRMS** (~95–98% confidence).
- **Nominal horizontal accuracy of standalone civilian GPS:** **2.5 m to 5 m** (95% confidence, with SA off).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — WGS-84 (World Geodetic System 1984):** The standard Earth-centered, Earth-fixed reference frame used by GPS. Autonomous vehicle maps, HD maps, and lane-level localization all ultimately resolve into WGS-84 latitude, longitude, altitude coordinates via GNSS.

### 1.4 The Four Global GNSS Constellations (GNSS ≠ GPS)

| System | Owner | Status (as of 2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **GPS** | United States | Fully operational, 31+ satellites |
| **GLONASS** | Russia | Fully operational, 24 satellites |
| **Galileo** | European Union | Fully operational, 30 satellites |
| **BeiDou (BDS-3)** | China | Fully operational, 35+ satellites |

**IRNSS / NavIC** (India) and **QZSS** (Japan) are regional augmentation systems. Modern automotive GNSS receivers are **multi-constellation, multi-frequency (MCMF)** — capable of tracking 100+ satellites simultaneously, which is critical for AV availability in urban canyons.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown: The Three Segments of GNSS

GNSS operation is conventionally decomposed into three logical segments. The KTU examiner frequently frames 3-mark or 7-mark questions around these segments.

#### 2.1.1 Space Segment
- Constellation of **Medium Earth Orbit (MEO)** satellites (altitude ~20,200 km for GPS).
- Each satellite broadcasts:
    1. **Navigation message** (ephemeris — satellite's own orbital position).
    2. **Almanac data** (coarse orbital info of all satellites).
    3. **Pseudo-random noise (PRN) code** for ranging.
    4. **Carrier signal** (L1, L2, L5 for GPS).

#### 2.1.2 Control Segment (Ground Segment)
- **Master Control Station (MCS)** + monitoring stations spread globally.
- Functions:
    - Track satellite orbits.
    - Predict ephemeris (the *broadcast ephemeris*).
    - Synchronize satellite atomic clocks.
    - Upload navigation message corrections.

#### 2.1.3 User Segment
- The **GNSS receiver** in the autonomous vehicle.
- Computes pseudoranges and resolves the **navigation solution** (PVT).

### 2.2 Operational Logic: How the Receiver Solves for Position

The receiver performs the following **sequential logic steps**:

1. **Signal Acquisition:** The receiver scans all PRN codes to detect visible satellites above the horizon (mask angle typically 5°–10°).
2. **Code Tracking:** A Delay-Locked Loop (DLL) aligns a locally generated replica of the PRN code with the incoming code, measuring the **time shift** $\Delta t$.
3. **Pseudorange Computation:** The measured shift is converted to a distance using the speed of light: $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$. Because the receiver clock has an unknown bias $\delta t_{rx}$, this distance is a *pseudo*-range (it contains the clock error).
4. **Navigation Message Decoding:** The receiver demodulates the 50 bps navigation message to extract the satellite's position $(x_s, y_s, z_s)$ at the instant of transmission (the *broadcast ephemeris*).
5. **Position Fix:** With $\geq 4$ satellites, the receiver solves a system of nonlinear equations (next section) for $[x, y, z, \delta t_{rx}]$.
6. **Velocity Estimation:** Using the **Doppler shift** on the carrier, the receiver estimates 3D velocity without extra satellites.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Memorize the pseudorange equation and the error-source table. They appear in nearly every KTU OEMUT723 exam cycle.

| # | Concept | Equation / Definition | Key Constant / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | True geometric range | $r = \sqrt{(x_s-x_r)^2 + (y_s-y_r)^2 + (z_s-z_r)^2}$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Pseudorange (with clock bias) | $\rho = r + c(\delta t_{rx} - \delta t_{sv}) + I + T + \varepsilon$ | metres (m) |
| 3 | Linearization of pseudorange | $\Delta \rho \approx \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z}\Delta z - c\Delta t$ | m |
| 4 | Line-of-sight unit vector component | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \frac{x_r - x_s}{r}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Position Dilution of Precision (PDOP) | $PDOP = \sqrt{Q_{11}+Q_{22}+Q_{33}}$ where $Q = (H^T H)^{-1}$ | unitless (ideal ≤ 2) |
| 6 | Horizontal accuracy | $HPE \approx HDOP \cdot \sigma_{URE}$ | metres |
| 7 | Ionospheric delay (1st order, L1) | $I_1 = \frac{40.3 \cdot TEC}{f_1^2}$ | metres |
| 8 | Tropospheric delay (simplified) | $T \approx 2.5\ \text{m}$ at zenith (× 1/sin(elevation)) | metres |
| 9 | Carrier-phase pseudorange | $\rho = (\phi + N)\lambda$ | metres |
| 10 | Velocity from Doppler | $v = -\lambda \cdot f_D$ | m/s |

**Notation Key:**
- $\delta t_{rx}$: receiver clock bias (unknown to be solved)
- $\delta t_{sv}$: satellite clock bias (modelled from navigation message)
- $I$: ionospheric path delay
- $T$: tropospheric path delay
- $\varepsilon$: residual measurement noise + multipath
- $TEC$: Total Electron Content (electrons/m²)
- $N$: integer ambiguity (cycles); $\lambda$: carrier wavelength; $\phi$: measured phase
- $H$: geometry matrix (Jacobian of pseudorange w.r.t. position)

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Autonomous Vehicles

| AV Function | GNSS Role |
|---|---|
| **Lane-level localization** | Sub-metre accuracy via RTK-GNSS + IMU fusion |
| **HD map referencing** | GNSS absolute pose aligns the car within pre-mapped HD map |
| **V2X and Platooning** | Common time reference (GNSS-derived UTC) for inter-vehicle coordination |
| **Dead-reckoning reset** | Periodically corrects INS drift error |
| **Geofencing & ODD definition** | Operational Design Domain checks use GNSS-derived position |
| **Sensor timestamp alignment** | GNSS-PPS (pulse-per-second) synchronizes LiDAR, camera, radar |

### 2.5 GNSS Error Budget (Critical KTU Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Standard GNSS Error Sources** (KBTU/SAE standard model):

| Error Source | Typical Magnitude | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Ionospheric delay** | 1–10 m | Dual-frequency correction, Klobuchar model |
| **Tropospheric delay** | 2–25 m | Saastamoinen/Marini model |
| **Ephemeris/orbit error** | ~1 m | SBAS, PPP corrections |
| **Satellite clock error** | ~1 m | Broadcast clock corrections |
| **Multipath** | 0.5–5+ m | Choke-ring antennas, software filters, **critical for AV in urban canyons** |
| **Receiver noise** | 0.1–1 m | Carrier-phase smoothing |
| **User Equivalent Range Error (UERE)** | **~2–3 m** (1σ) | Combined RSS |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pseudorange Equation (Signal Propagation Model)

The full physical model of GNSS ranging is built by tracing each error term from signal emission at the satellite to reception at the vehicle antenna.

**Step 1 — True geometric range.**
For a satellite $S_i$ at known ECEF position $(x_{si}, y_{si}, z_{si})$ and a receiver $R$ at unknown position $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$, the true Euclidean distance is:

$$r_i = \sqrt{(x_{si}-x_r)^2 + (y_{si}-y_r)^2 + (z_{si}-z_r)^2}$$

**Step 2 — True time of flight.**
Since the signal travels at speed $c$, the true propagation time is:

$$\tau_i = \frac{r_i}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Measured (imperfect) time of flight.**
The receiver clock has bias $\delta t_r$ relative to true GNSS system time; the satellite clock has bias $\delta t_{si}$. The receiver *measures*:

$$\tau_{i,measured} = \tau_i + \delta t_r - \delta t_{si}$$

(The satellite transmits earlier than it thinks by $\delta t_{si}$; the receiver timestamps the arrival later than it really is by $\delta t_r$.)

**Step 4 — Atmospheric path delays.**
The signal also slows in the ionosphere ($I_i$) and troposphere ($T_i$):

$$\tau_{i,measured} = \frac{r_i}{c} + \delta t_r - \delta t_{si} + \frac{I_i}{c} + \frac{T_i}{c}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply through by $c$ to obtain the pseudorange:**

$$\rho_i = c \cdot \tau_{i,measured} = r_i + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_{si}) + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_i$$

This is the canonical KTU **pseudorange observation equation** you must reproduce in exams.

### 3.2 Linearization and the Linearized Position Fix

The pseudorange equation is **nonlinear** because $r_i$ contains the unknown receiver coordinates inside a square root. The KTU-expected solution method is **first-order Taylor linearization around an initial estimate** $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$.

**Step 6 — Initial estimate.**
The receiver uses its last known position (or an almanac-based coarse guess) as $(x_0, y_0, z_0, t_0)$.

**Step 7 — Compute predicted pseudorange.**
Using the broadcast ephemeris, the satellite position $(x_{si}, y_{si}, z_{si})$ at transmission time is known. The predicted range is:

$$\rho_{i,0} = \sqrt{(x_{si}-x_0)^2 + (y_{si}-y_0)^2 + (z_{si}-z_0)^2} + c\,t_0$$

**Step 8 — Compute measurement residual.**
This is the difference between what the receiver actually measured ($\rho_i$) and what it predicted ($\rho_{i,0}$):

$$\Delta\rho_i = \rho_i - \rho_{i,0}$$

**Step 9 — First-order Taylor expansion.**
For small corrections $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, \Delta t)$, and noting that $\partial r_i/\partial x = -(x_{si}-x_r)/r_i$:

$$\Delta\rho_i = \frac{(x_0-x_{si})}{r_{i,0}}\Delta x + \frac{(y_0-y_{si})}{r_{i,0}}\Delta y + \frac{(z_0-z_{si})}{r_{i,0}}\Delta z - c\Delta t$$

Define the unit line-of-sight vector components:

$$a_{xi} = \frac{x_0-x_{si}}{r_{i,0}}, \quad a_{yi} = \frac{y_0-y_{si}}{r_{i,0}}, \quad a_{zi} = \frac{z_0-z_{si}}{r_{i,0}}$$

So:

$$\Delta\rho_i = a_{xi}\Delta x + a_{yi}\Delta y + a_{zi}\Delta z - c\Delta t$$

**Step 10 — Stack equations from all $n$ visible satellites into matrix form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta\rho_1 \\ \Delta\rho_2 \\ \Delta\rho_3 \\ \Delta\rho_4 \\ \vdots \\ \Delta\rho_n
\end{bmatrix}
&=
\begin{bmatrix}
a_{x1} & a_{y1} & a_{z1} & -c \\
a_{x2} & a_{y2} & a_{z2} & -c \\
a_{x3} & a_{y3} & a_{z3} & -c \\
a_{x4} & a_{y4} & a_{z4} & -c \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\
a_{xn} & a_{yn} & a_{zn} & -c
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ \Delta t
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

In compact notation: $\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho} = H\,\Delta\mathbf{x}$

**Step 11 — Solve by weighted least squares.**
For $n \geq 4$, the optimal correction vector is:

$$\Delta\mathbf{x} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \Delta\boldsymbol{\rho}$$

**Step 12 — Iterate until convergence.**
The receiver updates $\mathbf{x}_{0} \leftarrow \mathbf{x}_0 + \Delta\mathbf{x}$ and repeats until $\vert \Delta\mathbf{x} \vert < \epsilon$ (typically 1 m). The final state is the navigation solution.

### 3.3 Dilution of Precision (DOP) — Why Satellite Geometry Matters

**Step 13 — Define the covariance of the position estimate.**
If measurement noise covariance is $R = \sigma_{URE}^2 I$, the state covariance is:

$$P = (H^T H)^{-1}\sigma_{URE}^2$$

**Step 14 — Define DOP components.**
Let the diagonal entries of $Q = (H^T H)^{-1}$ be $Q_{11}, Q_{22}, Q_{33}, Q_{44}$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
HDOP &= \sqrt{Q_{11}+Q_{22}} \\
VDOP &= \sqrt{Q_{33}} \\
TDOP &= \sqrt{Q_{44}} \\
PDOP &= \sqrt{HDOP^2 + VDOP^2} \\
GDOP &= \sqrt{PDOP^2 + TDOP^2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 15 — Final accuracy prediction.**
Position accuracy $\sigma_{pos} \approx PDOP \cdot \sigma_{URE}$. **Bad geometry (satellites clustered in the sky) inflates DOP, dramatically worsening accuracy** — a key point for AV positioning in tunnels or urban canyons where geometry collapses.

### 3.4 Numerical Sanity-Check Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Verification (use this in class tests):**
>
> Suppose $n = 4$, $\sigma_{URE} = 2$ m, and $PDOP = 1.5$. Then:
>
> $$\sigma_{pos} \approx 1.5 \times 2 = 3\ \text{m (1σ)}$$
>
> At 95% confidence ($\approx 2\sigma$):
>
> $$\sigma_{pos,95\%} \approx 6\ \text{m}$$
>
> This is **insufficient** for lane-level AV autonomy. Hence the need for **RTK / PPP corrections** to drop $\sigma_{URE}$ to a few cm.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: GNSS vs. INS in an AV Stack

| Property | GNSS | INS (Inertial) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference frame | **Absolute (global, WGS-84)** | **Relative (local, body)** |
| Error behaviour | **Bounded** (~few m) | **Unbounded drift** (~km/h) |
| Update rate | 1–20 Hz | 100–1000 Hz |
| Signal loss vulnerability | **High** (tunnels, urban canyons) | **None** (self-contained) |
| Provides velocity | Yes (Doppler) | Yes (integration of accel) |
| Provides attitude | No | Yes |
| Cost | Low | High (tactical-grade IMU costly) |
| AV role | **Global reference** | **Bridge during GNSS outage** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Module 1, Section 1:** A real AV uses **GNSS + INS + perception fusion** (loosely or tightly coupled Kalman filter). Neither sensor alone is sufficient for SAE Level 3+ autonomy.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — GNSS Functional Architecture in an Autonomous Vehicle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below maps the **end-to-end signal and data flow** from space segment to the AV's planning stack. It conforms to the Mermaid safeguards: all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed; labels are plain uppercase text with no markdown formatting inside quotes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[SATELLITE SEGMENT - GPS GLONASS GALILEO BEIDOU] --> B1[BROADCAST L1 L2 L5 CARRIER PLUS PRN CODE PLUS NAV MESSAGE]
    B1 --> C1[VEHICLE ANTENNA - CHOKE RING OR PATCH]
    C1 --> C2[RF FRONT END - LNA PLUS FILTER]
    C2 --> C3[BASEBAND PROCESSOR - DLL PLUS PLL PLUS ACQUISITION]
    C3 --> D1[PSEUDORANGE PLUS DOPPLER PLUS PHASE]
    D1 --> D2[NAVIGATION SOLVER - LSQ OR KALMAN]
    D2 --> E1[PVT OUTPUT - LAT LON ALT VEL TIME]
    E1 --> F1[SENSOR FUSION EKF - WITH INS PLUS LIDAR PLUS CAMERA]
    F1 --> F2[LOCALIZATION - HD MAP MATCHING]
    F2 --> G1[PLANNING AND CONTROL - LANE KEEP PLUS BRAKING PLUS STEER]
    H1[CONTROL SEGMENT - MASTER STATION PLUS MONITOR STATIONS] --> A1
    I1[CORRECTION SERVICE - SBAS RTK OR PPP] --> C3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pseudorange Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[SAT CLOCK TIME T_SV] --> N2[TRANSMIT CARRIER PLUS CODE]
    N2 --> N3[ATMOSPHERE - IONO PLUS TROPO DELAY]
    N3 --> N4[RECEIVER ANTENNA]
    N4 --> N5[RX CLOCK TIME T_R]
    N5 --> N6[COMPUTE TAU = T_R MINUS T_SV]
    N6 --> N7[PSEUDORANGE RHO = C TIMES TAU]
    N7 --> N8[CORRECT FOR IONO TROPO AND CLOCK BIAS]
    N8 --> N9[TRUE GEOMETRIC RANGE R]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — GNSS / INS / Perception Fusion Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[GNSS RECEIVER - PVT AT 10 HZ] --> Q1[LOOSELY COUPLED EKF]
    P2[IMU - ACCEL PLUS GYRO AT 200 HZ] --> Q1
    P3[LIDAR - POINT CLOUDS AT 10 HZ] --> Q2[PERCEPTION MODULE]
    P4[CAMERA - LANE MARKERS AT 30 HZ] --> Q2
    Q2 --> Q1
    Q1 --> R1[STATE VECTOR - POS PLUS VEL PLUS ATT PLUS BIASES]
    R1 --> R2[AV DECISION AND CONTROL LAYER]
    P5[HD MAP] --> R1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — GNSS Error Source Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0[GNSS TOTAL ERROR] --> E1[IONOSPHERIC DELAY - 1 TO 10 M]
    E0 --> E2[TROPOSPHERIC DELAY - 2 TO 25 M]
    E0 --> E3[EPHEMERIS ERROR - 1 M]
    E0 --> E4[CLOCK ERROR - 1 M]
    E0 --> E5[MULTIPATH - 0.5 TO 5 PLUS M]
    E0 --> E6[RECEIVER NOISE - 0.1 TO 1 M]
    E1 --> E1A[DUAL FREQ CORRECTION]
    E2 --> E2A[SAASTAMOINEN MODEL]
    E3 --> E3A[SBAS OR PPP]
    E4 --> E4A[BROADCAST CLOCK MODEL]
    E5 --> E5A[ANTENNA DESIGN PLUS FILTERING]
    E6 --> E6A[CARRIER PHASE SMOOTHING]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define the term **pseudorange** in GNSS positioning. Why is it called "pseudo" and not the true range?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Pseudorange is the apparent distance from a GNSS satellite to the receiver, computed by multiplying the measured signal travel time by the speed of light $c$. The pseudorange equation is:
>
> $$\rho = r + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_{sv}) + I + T + \varepsilon$$
>
> It is called *pseudo* because it is corrupted by (i) the receiver clock bias $\delta t_r$, (ii) the satellite clock bias $\delta t_{sv}$, (iii) ionospheric delay $I$, (iv) tropospheric delay $T$, and (v) measurement noise including multipath $\varepsilon$. The true geometric range $r$ can be obtained only after these errors are corrected or solved out — which is why at least **4 satellites** are needed: 3 for the 3D position $(x,y,z)$ and 1 for the receiver clock bias.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State **any four** major error sources affecting GNSS positioning and briefly mention one mitigation strategy for each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Ionospheric delay** (1–10 m): mitigated by dual-frequency receivers using the L1–L2 ionospheric-free combination.
> 2. **Tropospheric delay** (2–25 m): mitigated by the Saastamoinen or Hopfield model using surface meteorological data.
> 3. **Multipath** (0.5–5+ m): mitigated by choke-ring antennas or by receiver-internal narrow correlator DLLs.
> 4. **Satellite clock/orbit (ephemeris) error** (~1 m): mitigated by SBAS corrections (e.g., GAGAN in India, WAAS in the US).
> *(Full credit for any 4 correctly paired error–mitigation pairs.)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) Module questions for a 14-mark sub-part typically allocate **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students answer **either (a) OR (b)**.

---

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **three segments of GNSS**. Explain the role of the **control segment** in maintaining system accuracy. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Derive the **linearized pseudorange observation equation** for a GNSS receiver tracking $n$ satellites. Show clearly why a minimum of **four satellites** is required to obtain a 3D position fix and resolve the receiver clock bias. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Block diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[SPACE SEGMENT - MEO SATELLITES] --> US[USER SEGMENT - VEHICLE RECEIVER]
    CS[CONTROL SEGMENT - MASTER PLUS MONITOR STATIONS] --> SP
    CS -- UPLINK NAV DATA --> SP
    SP -- RF SIGNAL --> US
```

2. **Space Segment (1 mark):** Constellation of MEO satellites at ~20,200 km altitude, each carrying atomic clocks and broadcasting navigation messages on L-band carriers.

3. **Control Segment (2 marks):** Globally distributed monitor stations track satellites, compute their orbits and clock offsets, and predict *broadcast ephemeris*. The Master Control Station uploads these corrections, ensuring the navigation message remains accurate. Without the control segment, satellite orbit predictions would drift beyond usable accuracy within hours.

4. **User Segment (1 mark):** The GNSS receiver in the vehicle — captures signals, measures pseudoranges, and computes PVT.

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

The pseudorange equation for satellite $i$ is:

$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_{si}-x_r)^2 + (y_{si}-y_r)^2 + (z_{si}-z_r)^2} + c\delta t_r + \varepsilon_i$$

This is nonlinear in $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$. Linearizing about an initial estimate $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ using a first-order Taylor expansion:

$$\Delta\rho_i = \frac{x_0-x_{si}}{r_{i,0}}\Delta x + \frac{y_0-y_{si}}{r_{i,0}}\Delta y + \frac{z_0-z_{si}}{r_{i,0}}\Delta z - c\Delta t$$

Stacking for $n$ satellites gives $\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho} = H\,\Delta\mathbf{x}$. The unknowns are **four**: $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, \Delta t$. Therefore at least **$n = 4$** satellites are required to obtain a unique solution. Three satellites would yield 3 equations and 4 unknowns (under-determined). The fourth satellite is *not* used for geometry — its role is exclusively to **resolve the receiver clock bias** $\Delta t$, which is unknown because consumer-grade receivers use cheap quartz clocks.

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to KTU 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Dilution of Precision (DOP)** in GNSS. Describe how **PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP, and GDOP** are computed from the geometry matrix $H$. Why is low DOP critical for autonomous vehicle positioning? *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Explain with a diagram how **GNSS is integrated with INS** in an autonomous vehicle localization stack. Why is neither sensor alone sufficient for SAE Level 3+ autonomy? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]:**

DOP quantifies how satellite geometry amplifies ranging errors into position errors. From the geometry matrix $H$, define:

$$Q = (H^T H)^{-1}$$

Let the diagonal entries of $Q$ be $q_{xx}, q_{yy}, q_{zz}, q_{tt}$. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
PDOP &= \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy} + q_{zz}} \\
HDOP &= \sqrt{q_{xx} + q_{yy}} \\
VDOP &= \sqrt{q_{zz}} \\
TDOP &= \sqrt{q_{tt}} \\
GDOP &= \sqrt{PDOP^2 + TDOP^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Final horizontal position accuracy is approximately $HDOP \cdot \sigma_{URE}$. Low DOP occurs when satellites are **well-distributed across the sky** (ideally one near each cardinal direction + zenith). High DOP occurs when satellites are clustered, e.g., during a tunnel exit where only satellites along the tunnel bore are visible. For lane-keeping AV applications, **PDOP ≤ 2** is generally required, otherwise positioning errors exceed the lane width (~3.5 m).

**Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[GNSS RECEIVER] --> F[EKF SENSOR FUSION]
    I[IMU] --> F
    F --> O[LOCALIZATION OUTPUT]
    H[HD MAP] --> F
    O --> P[PLANNING AND CONTROL]
```

**Why neither alone is sufficient:**
- **GNSS alone** fails in tunnels, urban canyons, parking garages, and under heavy foliage — the AV would "lose itself".
- **INS alone** drifts unboundedly (a typical MEMS IMU drifts ~km/h; even tactical-grade drifts ~1 km/h). Within minutes the AV would lose lane-level accuracy.
- **Fused (GNSS + INS via EKF)**: GNSS provides **bounded absolute reference** at low rate (1–20 Hz), while INS provides **smooth high-rate bridging** (100–1000 Hz). The EKF optimally combines them — INS short-term precision with GNSS long-term stability — yielding continuous, robust localization essential for SAE Level 3+.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Writing "triangulation" instead of "trilateration"** — instant 1-mark deduction in pseudorange/positioning questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the receiver clock bias** in the pseudorange equation — you *must* include the $c\,\delta t_r$ term. Examiners specifically test whether you know why 4 satellites (not 3) are needed.
> 3. **Confusing PDOP with accuracy** — DOP is a *multiplicative geometry factor*, not an error itself. Final accuracy = DOP × UERE.
> 4. **Writing "GNSS" and "GPS" interchangeably** — GPS is *one* GNSS constellation. Galileo/GLONASS/BeiDou are also GNSS.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing PDOP = 1.5 without stating "unitless") — KTU strict-marking scheme deducts for missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **GNSS = Global Navigation Satellite System** (umbrella term); **GPS is one constellation** under GNSS (along with GLONASS, Galileo, BeiDou).
- [x] GNSS provides **Position, Velocity, Time (PVT)** — it is the *absolute* reference in any AV stack.
- [x] **Pseudorange** = $r + c(\delta t_r - \delta t_{sv}) + I + T + \varepsilon$ — corrupted true range.
- [x] **Minimum 4 satellites** are required: 3 for $(x,y,z)$ and 1 for the receiver clock bias.
- [x] The **three segments** are Space, Control (Ground), and User.
- [x] **Trilateration** (distances from known points) — *not* triangulation (angles).
- [x] **DOP** quantifies geometry — $PDOP = \sqrt{\text{diag sum of }(H^TH)^{-1}}$ for position.
- [x] **Final accuracy** $\approx DOP \times \sigma_{URE}$.
- [x] **5 main error sources:** ionosphere, troposphere, ephemeris, satellite clock, multipath (plus receiver noise).
- [x] **Multipath** is the dominant error in **urban canyons** — critical for AV scenarios.
- [x] **Dual-frequency** (L1+L5) receivers largely cancel ionospheric delay (1st-order).
- [x] **GNSS alone is not enough** for AV — must be fused with INS via EKF (loosely or tightly coupled).
- [x] **Reference frame:** WGS-84 ECEF (latitude, longitude, altitude).
- [x] **Speed of light:** $c = 299{,}792{,}458$ m/s (exact by SI definition).
- [x] **Carrier frequencies:** GPS L1 = 1575.42 MHz, L2 = 1227.60 MHz, L5 = 1176.45 MHz.
- [x] **Velocity estimation:** via Doppler shift on the carrier ($v = -\lambda f_D$).
- [x] **Autonomous vehicle need:** Sub-metre to decimetre accuracy → drives demand for **RTK / PPP** corrections over standard GNSS.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Features and Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Key Features and Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Navigation System (ANS):** An integrated electro-mechatronic on-board system that determines the real-time geographical position of a vehicle using signals from the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), inertial/dead-reckoning sensors, and a geo-referenced digital map database, and provides route guidance, map visualization, and driver information through a Human–Machine Interface (HMI).

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OEMUT723**, *Key Features* refers to the functional capabilities that a modern automotive navigation system must deliver to the driver, while *Components* refers to the hardware and software building blocks that together realize these capabilities.

### 1.2 Intuition — A Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Vehicle as a Smartphone with a Compass on Wheels"**

Imagine you are walking inside a huge unfamiliar shopping mall with your smartphone. The phone knows its location (because of GPS + Wi-Fi), knows where the stores are (because of an in-built map), and tells you "turn left after 20 m" (voice guidance). An **automotive navigation system** does *exactly the same thing* for a car, but with extra layers:

| Smartphone Navigation | Automotive Navigation |
|---|---|
| Uses only GPS | Uses GPS **plus** gyroscope, accelerometer, wheel speed sensor, steering angle sensor |
| Loses signal inside tunnels | Continues to estimate position using **dead reckoning** |
| Map on a small screen | Map + HUD + voice + instrument cluster integration |
| Driver is responsible for lane | **Lane-level** guidance, ADAS co-operation |

So in one line: *an automotive navigation system = smartphone navigation + car-grade sensors + safety-grade reliability + ADAS/control integration.*

### 1.3 Key Features of a Modern Automotive Navigation System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus (OEMUT723 Module 1) lists the following as the **canonical key features**:

1. **Real-time Vehicle Positioning** — continuous absolute and relative position estimation (typically at **1 Hz to 10 Hz** update rate).
2. **Map Display & Visualization** — 2-D / 3-D / bird's-eye map rendering with North-up or Heading-up orientation.
3. **Route Calculation / Planning** — optimum route search using algorithms like **Dijkstra's** or **A\*** over the road network graph.
4. **Turn-by-Turn Route Guidance** — visual arrows + voice prompts at the right moment and distance.
5. **Dead Reckoning (DR) Capability** — sensor-based position estimation when GNSS signals are unavailable (tunnels, urban canyons).
6. **Map Matching** — snapping the estimated position onto the correct road segment of the digital map.
7. **Points of Interest (POI) Database** — fuel stations, restaurants, parking, hospitals, EV chargers.
8. **Traffic Information / TMC / RDS-TMC** — real-time congestion-aware re-routing.
9. **Voice Guidance (TTS)** — hands-free spoken instructions for driver safety.
10. **Integration with ADAS / Vehicle Dynamics Control** — sharing position data with adaptive cruise control, lane-keep assist, e-horizon.
11. **Geo-fencing and Location-based Services (LBS).**
12. **Multi-modal and Hybrid Positioning** — GNSS + DR + cellular + Wi-Fi fingerprinting.

### 1.4 Why These Features Matter

- **Safety:** Driver distraction is reduced because the driver does not need to read a paper map.
- **Efficiency:** Optimal routing reduces fuel/energy consumption by **5–15 %** on average.
- **Comfort:** Voice and HUD keep the driver's eyes on the road.
- **Regulatory:** EU eCall (since **2018**) and similar mandates require automatic location transmission after a crash.
- **ADAS Enabler:** Modern ACC, lane-keeping, and predictive efficiency systems depend on *e-Horizon* data from the navigation ECU.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — "Why" and "How"

The automotive navigation system is best understood as a **closed-loop information pipeline**. Each block does a specific job:

**Step 1 — Raw Signal Acquisition**
*Why:* We need an *absolute* reference of "where am I on Earth."
*How:* The GNSS antenna receives microwave signals (**L1 = 1575.42 MHz**, **L2 = 1227.60 MHz**) from at least 4 satellites. From the travel time $\tau$, the receiver computes pseudoranges.

**Step 2 — Dead-Reckoning (DR) Sensor Reading**
*Why:* GNSS alone fails in tunnels, urban canyons, and parking lots.
*How:* A **gyroscope** measures yaw rate $\psi$, a **wheel speed sensor** measures vehicle speed $v$, and a **steering angle sensor** measures steering-wheel angle $\delta$. Together they propagate the last known position.

**Step 3 — Sensor Fusion**
*Why:* Each sensor has its own bias, noise, and drift.
*How:* A **Kalman Filter** (or its variants — EKF, UKF, Particle Filter) fuses GNSS pseudoranges with DR data and produces an optimal state estimate: position $(x, y)$, velocity $(v_x, v_y)$, heading $\psi$, and sensor biases.

**Step 4 — Map Matching**
*Why:* Even a fused estimate is still a noisy point on the map; we must decide which road segment the vehicle is on.
*How:* Geometric (nearest-segment), topological (connectivity), and probabilistic (Hidden Markov Model) methods are used.

**Step 5 — Route Planning**
*Why:* The driver wants to reach a destination.
*How:* The road network is represented as a **weighted directed graph** $G(V,E,W)$. Edge weights $w$ can be distance, time, fuel, or a multi-criteria combination. **Dijkstra's algorithm** or **A\*** returns the minimum-cost path.

**Step 6 — Route Guidance**
*Why:* The driver must be told what to do at each intersection.
*How:* The system monitors the *distance to next maneuver* $d_{maneuver}$; when $d_{maneuver} < d_{trigger}$, a voice prompt and visual arrow are issued.

**Step 7 — HMI Output**
*Why:* The driver must perceive the information without distraction.
*How:* Display unit (LCD/OLED), instrument cluster, Head-Up Display (HUD), and loudspeaker.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield formula reference** for solving OEMUT723 Module 1 numerical problems. All equations are mathematically verifiable from standard GNSS and dead-reckoning theory.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pseudorange from one satellite | $\rho_i = c \cdot (t_r - t_s)$ | $c$ = speed of light $\approx 3\times10^8$ m/s; $t_r$ receive time; $t_s$ send time | m |
| 2 | True range with clock bias | $r_i = \rho_i - c \cdot b$ | $b$ = receiver clock bias | m, s |
| 3 | Haversine distance (great-circle) | $d = 2R \arcsin\!\bigl(\sqrt{\sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\phi}{2} + \cos\phi_1\cos\phi_2\sin^2\!\tfrac{\Delta\lambda}{2}}\bigr)$ | $R \approx 6371$ km (Earth radius); $\phi$ latitude; $\lambda$ longitude | m |
| 4 | Initial bearing (forward azimuth) | $\theta = \operatorname{atan2}\!\bigl(\sin\Delta\lambda \cos\phi_2,\;\cos\phi_1\sin\phi_2 - \sin\phi_1\cos\phi_2\cos\Delta\lambda\bigr)$ | $\theta$ measured clockwise from North | rad / deg |
| 5 | Dead-reckoning position update | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\psi_k$ | $v_k$ speed; $\psi_k$ heading; $\Delta t$ sample time | m |
| 6 | Dead-reckoning position update | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin\psi_k$ | as above | m |
| 7 | Heading from yaw rate | $\psi_{k+1} = \psi_k + \dot{\psi}_k \Delta t$ | $\dot{\psi}_k$ yaw rate from gyroscope | rad |
| 8 | Curvature from steering angle (bicycle model) | $\kappa = \dfrac{\tan\delta}{L}$ | $\delta$ steering angle; $L$ wheelbase | 1/m |
| 9 | Yaw rate from bicycle model | $\dot{\psi} = \dfrac{v \tan\delta}{L}$ | $v$ vehicle speed | rad/s |
| 10 | Kalman Filter state prediction | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B_k u_k$ | $F$ state-transition; $B$ control-input matrix | state-units |
| 11 | Kalman covariance prediction | $P_{k\mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1\mid k-1} F_k^\top + Q_k$ | $Q$ process-noise covariance | state-units² |
| 12 | Kalman gain | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^\top (H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^\top + R_k)^{-1}$ | $H$ measurement; $R$ measurement-noise covariance | – |
| 13 | Kalman state update | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k(z_k - H_k\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$ | $z_k$ measurement vector | state-units |
| 14 | DOP (dilution of precision) | $\text{DOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^\top H)^{-1}}$ | smaller is better (GDOP $<2$ good, $>5$ poor) | – |
| 15 | Route-cost edge weight (time) | $w_e = \dfrac{d_e}{v_{e,\text{limit}}} \cdot \alpha_{\text{traffic}}$ | $d_e$ edge length; $v_{e,\text{limit}}$ speed limit; $\alpha$ traffic factor | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical reminder for valuation:** In KTU board exams, when writing the **Haversine** formula, write the *full* form with $\sin^2$ and $\cos^2$ — examiners frequently deduct marks if students write the simplified linear (flat-earth) version when the question clearly asks for the great-circle distance on a sphere.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not just academic. They drive:

- **GNSS chip firmware** in smartphones and car head units (Qualcomm, U-Blox, STMicro Teseo).
- **Dead-reckoning modules** for tunnels (used by **BMW**, **Mercedes-Benz**, and **Tesla** map data pipelines).
- **HD-map-based autonomous driving** stacks from **Waymo**, **Mobileye**, and **Bosch**.
- **eCall** emergency-call location accuracy specifications in the EU (Galileo SAR, mandated since **March 2018**).
- **Fleet management and ride-hailing routing** at Ola/Uber scale, where A* with time-dependent edge weights replaces the textbook static-graph Dijkstra.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pseudorange and the 4-Satellite Position Fix

A GNSS receiver does not measure *distance* directly; it measures *time of flight* of a coded signal.

**Step 1:** The satellite $i$ transmits a navigation message at satellite-clock time $t_s^{(i)}$; the receiver picks it up at receiver-clock time $t_r$. The interval is

$$
\Delta t_i = t_r - t_s^{(i)}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiplying by the speed of light $c$ converts this time interval into an *apparent* distance, called the **pseudorange**:

$$
\rho_i = c \, \Delta t_i = c\,(t_r - t_s^{(i)})
$$

**Step 3:** Because the receiver's clock is not synchronized with the GNSS time, there is an unknown bias $b$ (in seconds). The *true* geometric range is therefore

$$
r_i = \rho_i - c\,b
$$

**Step 4:** Geometrically, if the receiver is at unknown position $(x,y,z)$ and satellite $i$ is at known position $(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)}, z^{(i)})$, then

$$
r_i = \sqrt{(x-x^{(i)})^2 + (y-y^{(i)})^2 + (z-z^{(i)})^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Equating the two expressions of $r_i$,

$$
\sqrt{(x-x^{(i)})^2 + (y-y^{(i)})^2 + (z-z^{(i)})^2} + c\,b = \rho_i
$$

**Step 6:** Because we have **four unknowns** $(x, y, z, b)$ and each satellite provides one such equation, we need at least **four satellites** simultaneously visible. The non-linear system is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{(x-x^{(1)})^2 + (y-y^{(1)})^2 + (z-z^{(1)})^2} + c\,b &= \rho_1 \\
\sqrt{(x-x^{(2)})^2 + (y-y^{(2)})^2 + (z-z^{(2)})^2} + c\,b &= \rho_2 \\
\sqrt{(x-x^{(3)})^2 + (y-y^{(3)})^2 + (z-z^{(3)})^2} + c\,b &= \rho_3 \\
\sqrt{(x-x^{(4)})^2 + (y-y^{(4)})^2 + (z-z^{(4)})^2} + c\,b &= \rho_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** This is linearized about an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$ and iterated (Gauss–Newton / Levenberg–Marquardt) until convergence. The closed-form dilution-of-precision metric is then

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\bigl((H^\top H)^{-1}\bigr)}
$$

where $H$ is the Jacobian of the four equations with respect to $(x,y,z,b)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner tip:** If you mention "linearization," KTU evaluators expect the **Jacobian matrix $H$** to appear, because that directly motivates the GDOP formula and the basis of least-squares GNSS positioning.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dead-Reckoning Update in the Vehicle Frame

We want to propagate the position when GNSS is unavailable. Let the vehicle's pose at time $k$ be $(x_k, y_k, \psi_k)$ in a local East–North tangent frame.

**Step 1:** During a small interval $\Delta t$, the vehicle moves with speed $v_k$ and heading $\psi_k$.

**Step 2:** Decompose motion into East and North components:

$$
\Delta x = v_k \Delta t \cos\psi_k \qquad \Delta y = v_k \Delta t \sin\psi_k
$$

**Step 3:** Add to the previous pose:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{k+1} &= x_k + v_k \Delta t \cos\psi_k \\
y_{k+1} &= y_k + v_k \Delta t \sin\psi_k \\
\psi_{k+1} &= \psi_k + \dot{\psi}_k \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the bicycle-model yaw rate $\dot{\psi}_k = \dfrac{v_k \tan\delta_k}{L}$,

$$
\psi_{k+1} = \psi_k + \frac{v_k \tan\delta_k}{L}\,\Delta t
$$

**Step 5:** In matrix form,

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x_{k+1} \\ y_{k+1} \\ \psi_{k+1} \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ y_k \\ \psi_k \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix}
\cos\psi_k & 0 \\
\sin\psi_k & 0 \\
\dfrac{\tan\delta_k}{L} & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} v_k \Delta t \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the classical DR model whose **Jacobian $F_k = \partial f / \partial x$** is the state-transition matrix used by the Kalman Filter in the navigation ECU.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kalman Filter Predict–Update Cycle (One Iteration)

**Predict:**

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} = F_k \hat{x}_{k-1 \mid k-1} + B_k u_k
$$

$$
P_{k \mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1 \mid k-1} F_k^\top + Q_k
$$

**Innovation (measurement residual):**

$$
\tilde{y}_k = z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1}
$$

**Innovation covariance:**

$$
S_k = H_k P_{k \mid k-1} H_k^\top + R_k
$$

**Kalman gain:**

$$
K_k = P_{k \mid k-1} H_k^\top S_k^{-1}
$$

**Update state and covariance:**

$$
\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k \tilde{y}_k
$$

$$
P_{k \mid k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k \mid k-1}
$$

These six equations are the **heart** of every sensor-fused automotive navigation estimator — including the unit installed in the head unit of every modern car produced after **2015**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping KTU Syllabus Terms to Equations

| KTU syllabus term (verbatim) | Mathematical anchor | Where it appears in the derivation |
|---|---|---|
| Position determination | Pseudorange equations | Derivation 1, Step 6 |
| Satellite geometry | $H$ matrix, GDOP | Derivation 1, Step 7 |
| Dead reckoning | $x_{k+1}, y_{k+1}, \psi_{k+1}$ update | Derivation 2, Step 3 |
| Vehicle dynamics coupling | Bicycle model yaw rate | Derivation 2, Step 4 |
| Sensor fusion | Kalman Filter cycle | Derivation 3, all six steps |
| Optimality criterion | Minimization of trace($P$) via $K$ | Derivation 3, gain $K$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of an Automotive Navigation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    satA[SATELLITE 1] --> gnssA[GNSS ANTENNA]
    satB[SATELLITE 2] --> gnssA
    satC[SATELLITE 3] --> gnssA
    satD[SATELLITE 4] --> gnssA
    gnssA --> gnssR[GNSS RECEIVER MODULE]
    gnssR -->|PSEUDORANGES| fusion[NAVIGATION ECU SENSOR FUSION KALMAN FILTER]

    gyroR[YAW RATE GYRO] --> drSENS[DR SENSOR UNIT]
    accR[ACCELEROMETER 3 AXIS] --> drSENS
    wsR[WHEEL SPEED SENSOR] --> drSENS
    saR[STEERING ANGLE SENSOR] --> drSENS

    fusion -->|STATE X Y PSI BIAS| match[MAP MATCHING MODULE]
```
