## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define the terms pseudorange, carrier phase, and Doppler shift in the context of GPS-based vehicle dynamics estimation. Mention the typical accuracy each provides.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Pseudorange (1 mark):** Pseudorange is the apparent distance between the GPS satellite and the receiver obtained by multiplying the measured code-phase signal travel time by the speed of light. It contains the receiver clock bias and atmospheric errors. Accuracy is typically **2–5 m** for civilian L1 C/A code.

- **Carrier phase (1 mark):** Carrier phase is the beat-phase measurement between the received carrier wave and a receiver-generated replica. It is highly precise, providing **mm-level** accuracy, but suffers from an integer-cycle ambiguity.

- **Doppler shift (1 mark):** Doppler shift is the frequency change observed on the carrier due to relative motion between satellite and receiver. It enables direct velocity measurement with typical accuracy of **~0.05 m/s** without ambiguity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*Explain the concept of Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP). State the minimum number of satellites needed for a 3D position fix and explain why.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** GDOP is a dimensionless scalar quantifying the geometric quality of satellite configuration as seen by the receiver. Lower GDOP implies better accuracy, and it is computed as $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\operatorname{tr}[(\mathbf{G}^\top \mathbf{G})^{-1}]}$.

- **Minimum satellites (1 mark):** Four (4) satellites are needed for a 3D position fix because there are **four unknowns** to solve: the three ECEF coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and the receiver clock bias $\delta t_r$.

- **Reason (1 mark):** Each pseudorange equation is non-linear; after linearization, the geometry matrix $\mathbf{G}$ is $n \times 4$. To obtain a unique solution (rank = 4), the minimum number of equations equals the number of unknowns, hence $n \geq 4$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget the 4th column (clock term) $c$** in the geometry matrix — resulting in a rank-deficient system and zero credit on derivations.
> - Many confuse **pseudorange accuracy** with **position accuracy** — they are not the same; the latter is amplified by DOP.
> - Always **state units** in numerical problems — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> - In Doppler-velocity problems, students often omit the **satellite velocity term** $\mathbf{u}_i^\top \mathbf{v}_{s_i}$; this is a frequent cause of wrong answers.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a GPS-based vehicle dynamics estimation system. Discuss the role of the Kalman Filter in fusing GPS measurements with inertial sensors. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The system consists of the following functional blocks:
1. **GNSS Antenna & RF Front-End**: Receives L1/L2/L5 signals, low-noise amplifies, filters, and down-converts.
2. **Acquisition & Tracking**: Detects visible satellites, locks onto code and carrier, extracts pseudorange, carrier phase, and Doppler.
3. **Navigation Decoder**: Demodulates the navigation message (50 bps) containing ephemeris, almanac, and clock correction parameters.
4. **PVT Solver**: Uses least-squares or Kalman filter to compute position, velocity, and time.
5. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**: Provides high-rate acceleration and angular rate measurements.
6. **State Estimator (EKF/UKF)**: Fuses GPS (low-rate, absolute) with IMU (high-rate, relative) to produce a smooth vehicle dynamic state.
7. **Vehicle Dynamics Output**: Yaw-rate, sideslip, acceleration, heading, speed.

**(ii) Kalman Filter Role (4 marks):**

The Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) solves the fusion problem by:
- Using a **prediction step** based on the IMU-driven vehicle motion model (constant turn / constant acceleration) to extrapolate state.
- Using the **GPS update step** at 1–10 Hz to correct drift and bound error growth.
- Providing **optimal blending** in the sense of minimum mean-square error (MMSE), with the Kalman gain $\mathbf{K}_k$ adaptively weighting observations vs. prediction.
- Estimating **unmeasured states** (e.g., sideslip angle) that are not directly observable from GPS.
- Allowing **sensor-fault detection** through innovation monitoring $\nu_k = \mathbf{z}_k - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}_{k|k-1}$.

**(b)** *A GPS receiver mounted on a vehicle tracks four satellites. The pseudoranges measured are $\rho_1 = 22{,}500{,}000$ m, $\rho_2 = 24{,}800{,}000$ m, $\rho_3 = 26{,}100{,}000$ m, $\rho_4 = 21{,}300{,}000$ m. The satellite ECEF positions (in m) are:*
- *Sat 1: (12,000,000, 15,000,000, 8,000,000)*
- *Sat 2: (-5,000,000, 18,000,000, 20,000,000)*
- *Sat 3: (20,000,000, -10,000,000, 6,000,000)*
- *Sat 4: (-18,000,000, 5,000,000, 22,000,000)*

*The receiver initial estimate is $(x_0, y_0, z_0) = (1{,}500{,}000, 4{,}500{,}000, 4{,}000{,}000)$ m with initial clock bias $\delta t_{r,0} = 0$ s. Compute the position correction $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ using one Gauss–Newton iteration. Use $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the geometric range $r_i^{(0)}$ for each satellite (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1^{(0)} &= \sqrt{(1.5 - 12)^2 + (4.5 - 15)^2 + (4 - 8)^2} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{110.25 + 110.25 + 16} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{236.5} \times 10^6 \approx 15.378 \times 10^6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_2^{(0)} &= \sqrt{(1.5+5)^2 + (4.5-18)^2 + (4-20)^2} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{42.25 + 182.25 + 256} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{480.5} \times 10^6 \approx 21.92 \times 10^6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_3^{(0)} &= \sqrt{(1.5-20)^2 + (4.5+10)^2 + (4-6)^2} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{342.25 + 210.25 + 4} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{556.5} \times 10^6 \approx 23.59 \times 10^6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_4^{(0)} &= \sqrt{(1.5+18)^2 + (4.5-5)^2 + (4-22)^2} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{380.25 + 0.25 + 324} \times 10^6 \\
&= \sqrt{704.5} \times 10^6 \approx 26.54 \times 10^6 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the residuals $\Delta \rho_i = \rho_i - r_i^{(0)}$ (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta \rho_1 \approx 22.5 - 15.378 = 7.122 \text{ (×10⁶ m)}, \quad
\Delta \rho_2 \approx 24.8 - 21.92 = 2.88 \text{ (×10⁶ m)}
$$
$$
\Delta \rho_3 \approx 26.1 - 23.59 = 2.51 \text{ (×10⁶ m)}, \quad
\Delta \rho_4 \approx 21.3 - 26.54 = -5.24 \text{ (×10⁶ m)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute direction cosines $a_{x_i}, a_{y_i}, a_{z_i}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
a_{x_1} = \frac{1.5 - 12}{15.378} = -0.683, \quad a_{y_1} = \frac{4.5 - 15}{15.378} = -0.683, \quad a_{z_1} = \frac{4 - 8}{15.378} = -0.260
$$

$$
a_{x_2} = \frac{1.5+5}{21.92} = 0.297, \quad a_{y_2} = \frac{4.5-18}{21.92} = -0.616, \quad a_{z_2} = \frac{4-20}{21.92} = -0.730
$$

$$
a_{x_3} = \frac{1.5-20}{23.59} = -0.784, \quad a_{y_3} = \frac{4.5+10}{23.59} = 0.615, \quad a_{z_3} = \frac{4-6}{23.59} = -0.085
$$

$$
a_{x_4} = \frac{1.5+18}{26.54} = 0.735, \quad a_{y_4} = \frac{4.5-5}{26.54} = -0.019, \quad a_{z_4} = \frac{4-22}{26.54} = -0.679
$$

**Step 4 — Form $\mathbf{G}$ and $\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$, and solve (2 marks):**

$$
\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix}
-0.683 & -0.683 & -0.260 & 1 \\
0.297 & -0.616 & -0.730 & 1 \\
-0.784 & 0.615 & -0.085 & 1 \\
0.735 & -0.019 & -0.679 & 1
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \begin{bmatrix} 7.122 \\ 2.88 \\ 2.51 \\ -5.24 \end{bmatrix} \times 10^6
$$

Solving $(\mathbf{G}^\top \mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^\top \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$ gives:

$$
\Delta x \approx 2.93 \times 10^6 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta y \approx 2.31 \times 10^6 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta z \approx 1.86 \times 10^6 \text{ m}, \quad c \Delta \delta t_r \approx -0.42 \times 10^6 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Update estimate (0.5 marks):**

$$
x_1 = x_0 + \Delta x = 4.43 \times 10^6 \text{ m}, \quad y_1 = 6.81 \times 10^6 \text{ m}, \quad z_1 = 5.86 \times 10^6 \text{ m}
$$

The correction is large because the initial guess was far from the true position, illustrating the importance of iteration in real receivers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** *Derive the GPS velocity vector using Doppler-shift measurements from four or more satellites. Show all matrix steps. Why is Doppler-based velocity superior to differentiating position over time? (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from the geometric range-rate:

$$
\dot{r}_i = (\mathbf{v}_r - \mathbf{v}_{s_i}) \cdot \mathbf{u}_i
$$

The Doppler shift on carrier $L$ is $f_{d_i} = -\dfrac{\dot{r}_i}{c} f_L$, which gives:

$$
-\frac{c}{f_L} f_{d_i} = \mathbf{u}_i^\top \mathbf{v}_r - \mathbf{u}_i^\top \mathbf{v}_{s_i}
$$

Regrouping with clock-drift $\dot{b}$:

$$
\mathbf{u}_i^\top \mathbf{v}_r + \dot{b} = -\frac{c}{f_L} f_{d_i} + \mathbf{u}_i^\top \mathbf{v}_{s_i}
$$

Stacking for $n$ satellites:

$$
\mathbf{G}\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_r \\ \dot{b}\end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{d}, \quad
\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} u_{x_1} & u_{y_1} & u_{z_1} & 1 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots \\ u_{x_n} & u_{y_n} & u_{z_n} & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{d}_i = -\frac{c}{f_L} f_{d_i} + \mathbf{u}_i^\top \mathbf{v}_{s_i}
$$

Solving via weighted least-squares:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}_r \\ \dot{b}\end{bmatrix} = (\mathbf{G}^\top \mathbf{W}\mathbf{G})^{-1} \mathbf{G}^\top \mathbf{W} \mathbf{d}
$$

Finally, convert to ENU using $\mathbf{R}_{ECEF \rightarrow ENU}(\phi, \lambda)$ to obtain heading and speed:

$$
\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(v_E, v_N), \quad v_h = \sqrt{v_E^2 + v_N^2}
$$

**Why Doppler is superior (2 marks):**

1. **No differencing noise amplification:** Differentiating position multiplies noise by $1/\Delta t$ (or $1/(2\Delta t)$ for central difference), producing very noisy velocity. Doppler measures velocity *directly*.

2. **Independent error statistics:** Doppler errors are dominated by receiver thermal noise (typically ~0.05 m/s 1σ), whereas position-difference errors include atmospheric and multipath residuals.

3. **Higher effective update rate:** Doppler can be integrated over short intervals (e.g., 0.1 s) to provide sub-second velocity updates without latency.

4. **No cumulative drift:** Differentiation of position drifts with each step; Doppler is a snapshot estimator with no memory.

**(b)** *A GPS receiver outputs horizontal velocity $(v_E, v_N) = (12.5, -8.7)$ m/s. Determine the (i) ground speed, (ii) heading angle (measured clockwise from North), (iii) longitudinal and lateral velocity components in the vehicle body frame if the vehicle's yaw is $\psi = 35^\circ$. Also compute (iv) the vehicle sideslip angle $\beta$. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Ground speed (1.5 marks):**

$$
v_h = \sqrt{v_E^2 + v_N^2} = \sqrt{12.5^2 + (-8.7)^2} = \sqrt{156.25 + 75.69} = \sqrt{231.94} \approx 15.23 \text{ m/s}
$$

Converting to km/h: $15.23 \times 3.6 \approx 54.83$ km/h.

**(ii) Heading angle (1.5 marks):**

$$
\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(v_E, v_N) = \operatorname{atan2}(12.5, -8.7)
$$

Since $v_E > 0$ and $v_N < 0$, the vehicle is in the **second quadrant** of the ENU plane (i.e., southeast direction relative to North). The angle measured from North (clockwise) is:

$$
\psi = 180^\circ - \arctan\!\left(\frac{12.5}{8.7}\right) = 180^\circ - 55.18^\circ \approx 124.82^\circ
$$

**(iii) Body-frame velocity components (2.5 marks):**

Using the heading rotation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_x^{\text{body}} &= v_E \sin\psi + v_N \cos\psi \\
v_y^{\text{body}} &= v_E \cos\psi - v_N \sin\psi
\end{aligned}
$$

With $\psi = 35^\circ$ (vehicle yaw), $\sin 35^\circ \approx 0.574$, $\cos 35^\circ \approx 0.819$:

$$
v_x^{\text{body}} = 12.5 \times 0.574 + (-8.7) \times 0.819 = 7.175 - 7.125 = 0.05 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_y^{\text{body}} = 12.5 \times 0.819 - (-8.7) \times 0.574 = 10.24 + 4.99 = 15.23 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Note:** A common student error is to substitute the heading $\psi = 124.82^\circ$ from part (ii) instead of the vehicle yaw (35°). The heading in (ii) is the direction of motion, while $\psi$ in (iii) is the vehicle body orientation.

**(iv) Sideslip angle (1.5 marks):**

$$
\beta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{v_y^{\text{body}}}{v_x^{\text{body}}}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{15.23}{0.05}\right) \approx \arctan(304.6) \approx 89.81^\circ
$$

The very large sideslip angle implies the vehicle is essentially sliding sideways relative to its body frame — a typical scenario in **loss-of-control** or **drift maneuvers**. This result confirms the strong consistency check: the velocity vector is nearly perpendicular to the vehicle's longitudinal axis.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Flash-Card — Vehicle Dynamics Estimation Using GPS**

- **PVT = Position + Velocity + Time** — the GPS triad for vehicle state inference.
- **Pseudorange equation:** $\rho_i = r_i + c\delta t_r + \varepsilon_i$ — non-linear, requires 4 satellites for 3D fix.
- **Carrier phase** is more precise than pseudorange but contains **integer ambiguity** $N$.
- **Doppler-based velocity** uses 4 satellites with linear equations — directly yields $\mathbf{v}_r$ and clock drift $\dot{b}$.
- **Minimum satellites required:** 4 for position/velocity (with clock), 3 if external clock is used, 5+ for **RAIM** integrity.
- **GDOP** < 4 ⇒ excellent; GDOP > 8 ⇒ poor geometry. Always check GDOP before trusting a fix.
- **Heading** comes from $\psi = \operatorname{atan2}(v_E, v_N)$, **not** from a magnetic compass when GPS velocity is available.
- **Acceleration** is best estimated via a **Kalman filter**; raw differentiation amplifies noise by $\sigma_v / (\sqrt{2} \Delta t)$.
- **Sideslip angle** $\beta = \arctan(v_y^{\text{body}} / v_x^{\text{body}})$ — critical for ESC and vehicle stability control.
- **Coordinate frames:** ECEF → ENU → Body — always apply the correct rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}(\phi, \lambda, \psi)$.
- **Error sources ranked (typical magnitudes):** Ionospheric ≈ 2–10 m, Tropospheric ≈ 2–5 m, Multipath ≈ 0.5–50 m, Clock ≈ 1–2 m, Receiver noise ≈ 0.1–1 m.
- **Dual-frequency (L1 + L2/L5) receivers** can cancel ionospheric error to first order.
- **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)** with carrier-phase achieves cm-level accuracy; **SBAS (WAAS, EGNOS, GAGAN)** gives sub-meter accuracy.
- **Tightly-coupled INS/GPS** integration outperforms loosely-coupled when fewer than 4 satellites are visible (e.g., urban canyon).
- **GPS update rate** is typically 1–10 Hz; IMU runs at 100–1000 Hz for high-dynamics applications.
- **Sigma values to memorize:** Pseudorange noise σ ≈ 0.5 m (carrier-aided); Velocity σ ≈ 0.05 m/s (Doppler); Acceleration σ ≈ 0.5–1.5 m/s² (raw).
- **KTU Examiner Loves:** DOP derivations, geometry-matrix construction, Kalman filter equations, body-frame transformations, and numerical worked examples.
- **Standard constant:** Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — never assume any other value unless explicitly given.
- **Clock bias cost:** A 1 microsecond clock error causes ~300 m position error — the reason GPS uses atomic clocks and 4th-unknown estimation.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — Module 3 | Vehicle Dynamics Estimation Using GPS.**
> Refer to standard texts such as *Misra & Enge — "Global Positioning System: Signals, Measurements, and Performance"* and *Grewal, Andrews & Bartone — "Global Navigation Satellite Systems, Inertial Navigation, and Integration"* for further depth on Kalman filtering and tight/loose INS/GPS coupling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Side Slip: Effects, Calculation, and Tyre Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Side Slip: Effects, Calculation, and Tyre Parameters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Side Slip (Lateral Slip):** Side slip is the relative lateral motion that occurs between a pneumatic tyre's contact patch and the road surface when the wheel plane is oriented at an angle to the instantaneous direction of travel of the vehicle's centre of gravity. It is quantified by the **slip angle (α)**, defined as the angle between the wheel's heading direction (steered angle line) and the actual velocity vector of the tyre's contact centre. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, side slip is one of the most fundamental tyre kinematics studied under vehicle dynamics. It directly governs the generation of **lateral (cornering) force**, which is the only mechanism by which a vehicle can change direction. The side slip phenomenon arises because the pneumatic tyre is an *elastic, viscoelastic, and frictionally coupled* medium — it deforms laterally before generating the full reaction force needed to keep the wheel aligned with the road. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rowing a Canoe" Intuition Imagine you are rowing a canoe, but instead of pointing the oar straight into the water, you angle it slightly. The oar (your tyre) is pointed one way, but the canoe (the vehicle mass) is gliding in a slightly different direction because the water (road) resists the oar's angled push. The small angle between the oar's axis and the actual path of the canoe is exactly the **slip angle**. - If you angle the oar a *little*, water resists gently and the canoe turns smoothly (small slip angle → linear region). - If you angle the oar *too much*, the oar slips out of the water and you lose steering authority completely (large slip angle → saturated friction region). This analogy helps visualize why a tyre must "slip" laterally to generate a cornering force. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics for Side Slip in KTU 2024 Scheme:** > - **Slip Angle (α):** Measured in **radians (rad)** for computation, **degrees (°)** for interpretation. Typical operational range: **−6° to +6°** for linear cornering. > - **Cornering Stiffness (C_α):** Measured in **N/rad** (Newtons per radian). Typical passenger car tyre: **30,000 N/rad to 80,000 N/rad** per tyre. > - **Lateral Force (F_y):** Measured in **Newtons (N)**. > - **Relaxation Length (σ):** Measured in **metres (m)**. Typical: **0.1 m to 0.5 m**. > - **Pneumatic Trail (t_p):** Measured in **metres (m)**. Typical: **0.02 m to 0.05 m**. ### 1.4 Why Side Slip Matters in Automotive Engineering Side slip is the *foundational link* between steering input and directional change. Without slip, no cornering force is generated, and the vehicle cannot turn. The magnitude of slip angle determines: 1. **Steering responsiveness** of the vehicle. 2. **Understeer / Oversteer gradient (K)** — a critical handling metric. 3. **Stability margins** during transient manoeuvres (lane change, double lane change). 4. **Tyre wear patterns** (camber wear, shoulder wear). 5. **ABS / ESP / ESC control algorithm inputs** in modern Electronic Stability Programs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Side slip analysis bridges **kinematics** (geometry of motion) and **tyre mechanics** (force generation). It is the most frequently tested topic in Module 3 of OEMUT723. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Origin of Side Slip — The Mechanics of Tyre Deformation A pneumatic tyre is constructed of **ply, belt, sidewall, and tread rubber compounds** bonded together. When a vertical load $F_z$ is applied, the contact patch flattens and the carcass deflects. Upon the application of a lateral force $F_y$ at the wheel centre: 1. The contact patch is **sheared sideways** relative to the wheel plane. 2. The leading edge of the contact patch is **laterally displaced** more than the trailing edge. 3. Because the road cannot shear (it is rigid), the rubber must deform until the elastic shear stress equals the available friction $\mu F_z$. 4. The wheel, however, is constrained by the axle to move in a direction that is *not aligned* with the wheel plane — this angular deviation is the **slip angle α**. In the **linear region** (small α, dry asphalt, normal loads), the lateral force generated is proportional to α: $$F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$$ where $C_\alpha$ is the **cornering stiffness** of the tyre. ### 2.2 The Slip Angle Triangle — Vehicle and Wheel Velocities For a wheel moving with longitudinal velocity $V_x$ and lateral velocity $V_y$ at its contact centre, the slip angle is geometrically defined as: $$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right) \approx \frac{V_y}{V_x} \quad \text{(for small } \alpha\text{)}$$ For a complete vehicle during a steady-state cornering manoeuvre on a flat road, the **vehicle sideslip angle (β)** at the CG is: $$\beta = \arctan\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right)$$ The slip angle of each individual axle is then: $$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{L_f \cdot \dot\psi}{V_x}, \quad \alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{L_r \cdot \dot\psi}{V_x}$$ where: - $\delta$ = steering angle at the road wheel - $L_f$, $L_r$ = distance of CG from front and rear axles - $\dot\psi$ = yaw rate (rad/s) - $V_x$ = longitudinal velocity ### 2.3 Cornering Force Beyond the Linear Region In reality, the relationship $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ holds only for small slip angles. As α increases, the tyre enters the **non-linear (frictional saturation) region**. The Magic Formula of Pacejka is the industry-standard empirical model: $$F_y = D \cdot \sin\left(C \cdot \arctan\left(B \cdot \alpha - E \cdot \left(B \cdot \alpha - \arctan(B \cdot \alpha)\right)\right)\right)$$ where $B$ = stiffness factor, $C$ = shape factor, $D$ = peak factor, $E$ = curvature factor. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Use of Side Slip Data | |-------------|-----------------------| | **Vehicle Dynamics Simulation (CarSim, ADAMS, MATLAB/Simulink)** | Slip angles feed the tyre sub-models to compute cornering forces in real-time simulation. | | **Electronic Stability Control (ESC / ESP)** | Compares actual vs. target yaw rate using slip-angle-derived lateral forces; applies differential braking when deviation exceeds threshold. | | **Steering System Design** | Determines steering ratio, rack travel, and Ackermann geometry to minimize inner/outer wheel slip-angle mismatch. | | **Tyre Development (Michelin, Continental, Pirelli)** | Cornering stiffness and peak slip angle are key KPIs. | | **Autonomous Driving (Path Tracking)** | Model Predictive Control (MPC) requires side-slip estimates for lateral stability assurance. | | **Motorsport Setup (F1, WRC)** | Suspension geometry is tuned to optimize slip angle distribution across the four wheels. | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table consolidates every formula needed for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\alpha = \arctan(V_y / V_x)$ | Slip angle from velocity components | rad | | 2 | $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$ | Linear cornering force | N | | 3 | $C_\alpha = \partial F_y / \partial \alpha$ | Cornering stiffness (slope at origin) | N/rad | | 4 | $\beta = \arctan(V_y / V_x)$ | Vehicle sideslip angle at CG | rad | | 5 | $\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - L_f \dot\psi / V_x$ | Front axle slip angle | rad | | 6 | $\alpha_r = -\beta + L_r \dot\psi / V_x$ | Rear axle slip angle | rad | | 7 | $M_z = -F_y \cdot t_p$ | Aligning torque from pneumatic trail | N·m | | 8 | $\sigma = C_\alpha / K_t$ | Relaxation length (with $K_t$ = tread stiffness) | m | | 9 | $F_y = D \sin(C \arctan(B \alpha - E(B\alpha - \arctan B\alpha)))$ | Magic Formula (Pacejka) lateral force | N | | 10 | $K_{us} = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$ | Understeer gradient | rad/g | | 11 | $\tan \delta = \frac{L}{R} + K_{us} \cdot \frac{V_x^2}{g L}$ | Understeer / oversteer criterion | — | | 12 | $\alpha_{peak} = \arctan(\frac{1}{B}) \cdot \text{shape factor}$ | Slip angle at peak lateral force | rad | | 13 | $F_{y,\max} = \mu \cdot F_z$ | Maximum lateral friction force | N | | 14 | $C_\alpha = \pi (F_z^2 / 2) \cdot p^{-1} \cdot \tan \phi$ | Simplified theoretical (Fiala) formula | N/rad | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** Whenever a numerical question is asked, examiners will award full marks only if the **units are explicitly stated** and the **formula is cited before substitution**. Always show the formula first, then substitute, then evaluate. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Cornering Stiffness from First Principles (Fiala Brush Model) The **Fiala Brush Model** is the simplest analytical tyre model that yields a closed-form expression for cornering stiffness. The model assumes: - The contact patch is rectangular of length $2a$ and width $2b$. - The tread elements act as **independent linear springs** with lateral stiffness per unit area $k_y$. - The friction limit is governed by Coulomb's law. **Step 1 — Define the un-deflected tread element position.** Let $x$ be the longitudinal coordinate of a tread element measured from the contact-patch centre, with $-a \le x \le a$. The lateral stiffness contribution of an element $dx$ is $dK = k_y \cdot 2b \cdot dx$. **Step 2 — Define the deflection field under a slip angle α.** For a small slip angle α, the lateral deflection of a tread element at position $x$ relative to the wheel plane is: $$y(x) = \alpha \cdot (a - x) \cdot \sigma_c(x)$$ where $\sigma_c(x)$ is a weighting function describing how the slip builds up along the contact patch. In the steady-state, the deflection is linearly distributed: $$y(x) = \alpha \cdot (a - x)$$ **Step 3 — Identify the adhesion and sliding regions.** The lateral force from the spring is $f(x) = k_y \cdot y(x)$. The element is in **adhesion** as long as $\vert f(x) \vert \le \mu p(x)$, where $p(x)$ is the contact pressure distribution. For a uniform pressure distribution $p_0 = F_z / (4ab)$: $$f(x) = k_y \cdot \alpha (a - x)$$ **Step 4 — Determine the critical boundary $x_c$ where sliding begins.** Setting $f(x_c) = \mu p_0$: $$k_y \alpha (a - x_c) = \mu p_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_c = a - \frac{\mu p_0}{k_y \alpha}$$ **Step 5 — Integrate the lateral force over the contact patch.** $$\begin{aligned} F_y &= \int_{-a}^{a} f(x) \, dx \\ &= 2b k_y \alpha \int_{x_c}^{a} (a - x) \, dx + 2b k_y \alpha \int_{-a}^{x_c} \min\left[(a - x), \frac{\mu p_0}{k_y \alpha}\right] dx \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Solve the integral in the linear (small α) limit.** When $\alpha$ is sufficiently small, the entire contact patch remains in adhesion ($x_c = -a$). Then: $$\begin{aligned} F_y &= 2b k_y \alpha \int_{-a}^{a} (a - x) \, dx \\ &= 2b k_y \alpha \left[ ax - \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{-a}^{a} \\ &= 2b k_y \alpha \left[ a(a) - \frac{a^2}{2} - a(-a) + \frac{a^2}{2} \right] \\ &= 2b k_y \alpha \left[ a^2 - \frac{a^2}{2} + a^2 - \frac{a^2}{2} \right] \\ &= 2b k_y \alpha \cdot 2a^2 \\ &= 4 a^2 b k_y \alpha \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Identify the cornering stiffness.** $$\boxed{C_\alpha = 4 a^2 b k_y}$$ The **theoretical cornering stiffness is proportional to $a^2$** (contact-patch half-length squared), the contact width $b$, and the tread lateral stiffness per unit area $k_y$. This is why **larger diameter tyres** (larger $a$) and **wider tyres** (larger $b$) have higher cornering stiffness. ### 3.2 Derivation: Vehicle Sideslip Angle from Bicycle Model The **bicycle (single-track) model** is a 2-DOF vehicle model that captures lateral and yaw dynamics. Consider a vehicle of mass $m$, yaw moment of inertia $I_z$, with CG located at distances $L_f$ (front) and $L_r$ (rear) from the respective axles. The track width is neglected (front and rear wheels treated as single centred wheels). **Step 1 — Write the lateral force equilibrium at the CG.** $$m V_x (\dot\beta + \dot\psi) = F_{y f} \cos\delta + F_{y r}$$ For small steering angles ($\cos\delta \approx 1$): $$m V_x (\dot\beta + \dot\psi) = F_{y f} + F_{y r}$$ **Step 2 — Write the yaw moment equilibrium about the CG.** $$I_z \ddot\psi = L_f F_{y f} - L_r F_{y r}$$ **Step 3 — Express lateral forces using cornering stiffness and slip angles.** $$F_{y f} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f, \quad F_{y r} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the slip-angle expressions from Section 2.2.** $$\begin{aligned} F_{y f} &= C_{\alpha f}\left(\delta - \beta - \frac{L_f \dot\psi}{V_x}\right) \\ F_{y r} &= C_{\alpha r}\left(-\beta + \frac{L_r \dot\psi}{V_x}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Combine into steady-state cornering (constant $\dot\psi$, $\dot\beta = 0$).** In steady-state, $\dot\beta = 0$, so $\dot\psi$ is constant and the centripetal acceleration is $a_y = V_x \dot\psi$. Solving the two simultaneous equations yields the **Ackermann-like steady-state relation**: $$\frac{\delta}{V_x \dot\psi} = \frac{L}{V_x^2} + \frac{m(L_r C_{\alpha r} - L_f C_{\alpha f})}{2 L C_{\alpha f} C_{\alpha r}} \cdot a_y$$ or equivalently, the **steering sensitivity gradient**: $$\frac{\delta}{a_y} = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$$ where $W_f$ and $W_r$ are the front and rear axle weights. This is the **understeer gradient $K_{us}$**: - $K_{us} > 0$ → **Understeer** (safe, predictable). - $K_{us} < 0$ → **Oversteer** (unstable, requires driver skill). - $K_{us} = 0$ → **Neutral steer**. ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A passenger car travelling at $V_x = 20$ m/s has a front lateral velocity $V_y = 1.2$ m/s. The steering angle at the road wheel is $\delta = 3°$. The CG is located such that $L_f = 1.2$ m, $L_r = 1.4$ m. The cornering stiffnesses are $C_{\alpha f} = 60{,}000$ N/rad and $C_{\alpha r} = 55{,}000$ N/rad. Yaw rate $\dot\psi = 0.15$ rad/s. Calculate: (a) Vehicle sideslip angle, (b) Front and rear slip angles, (c) Lateral forces, (d) Understeer gradient. Take $m = 1500$ kg. **Step 1 — Vehicle sideslip angle β.** $$\beta = \arctan\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{1.2}{20}\right) = \arctan(0.06) = 0.0599 \text{ rad} = 3.43°$$ **Step 2 — Front slip angle α_f.** Convert $\delta$ to radians: $\delta = 3° \times \pi/180 = 0.0524$ rad. $$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{L_f \dot\psi}{V_x} = 0.0524 - 0.0599 - \frac{1.2 \times 0.15}{20}$$ $$\alpha_f = 0.0524 - 0.0599 - 0.009 = -0.0165 \text{ rad} = -0.945°$$ **Step 3 — Rear slip angle α_r.** $$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{L_r \dot\psi}{V_x} = -0.0599 + \frac{1.4 \times 0.15}{20}$$ $$\alpha_r = -0.0599 + 0.0105 = -0.0494 \text{ rad} = -2.83°$$ **Step 4 — Lateral cornering forces.** $$F_{y f} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f = 60{,}000 \times (-0.0165) = -990 \text{ N}$$ $$F_{y r} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r = 55{,}000 \times (-0.0494) = -2717 \text{ N}$$ The negative signs indicate forces directed toward the centre of the turn (consistent with sign convention). **Step 5 — Understeer gradient $K_{us}$.** Weight distribution: $W_f = m g L_r / L = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.4 / 2.6 = 7922$ N, $W_r = m g L_f / L = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.2 / 2.6 = 6791$ N. $$K_{us} = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} = \frac{7922}{60{,}000} - \frac{6791}{55{,}000}$$ $$K_{us} = 0.1320 - 0.1235 = +0.0085 \text{ rad/g}$$ Since $K_{us} > 0$, the vehicle is **understeering** — an inherently stable configuration. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Side Slip Effects | Effect Category | Description | Quantitative Metric | Consequence | |-----------------|-------------|---------------------|-------------| | **Steering Feel** | Driver-perceived heaviness/lightness of steering | $\partial F_y / \partial \delta$ gradient | Too stiff → numb steering; too soft → twitchy | | **Vehicle Stability** | Tendency to maintain or diverge from path | $K_{us}$ sign and magnitude | Understeer = stable; Oversteer = unstable at high speed | | **Tyre Wear** | Uneven wear across tread | Slip-angle distribution across axle | Inner-wheel wear in oversteer, shoulder wear in extreme slip | | **Comfort vs Handling Trade-off** | Suspension compliance affects transient slip angle build-up | Relaxation length σ | Long σ = smoother build-up, but slower response | | **Braking / Accelerating in Turn** | Combined slip behaviour | Friction ellipse utilization | Side-slip + longitudinal slip uses same friction budget | | **Aerodynamic Loading** | Downforce increases vertical load | $F_y$ capacity scales with $F_z$ | Race cars exploit this for high-speed cornering | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Slip Angle Geometry — Velocity Triangle The following diagram illustrates the velocity triangle and the slip angle α formed at a tyre's contact patch during cornering. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vehicle CG Velocity Vector V] --> B[Velocity Components] B --> C[Longitudinal Velocity Vx] B --> D[Lateral Velocity Vy] C --> E[Wheel Heading Direction] D --> F[Actual Direction of Travel] E --> G[Slip Angle alpha] F --> G G --> H[Cornering Force Fy direction] H --> I[Perpendicular to Wheel Plane] A --> J[Steering Input delta] J --> K[Wheel Plane Orientation] K --> G ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Side Slip → Cornering Force → Vehicle Dynamics ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Driver Steering Input delta] --> B[Steering Geometry
and Ratio] B --> C[Road Wheel Angle] C --> D[Slip Angle Generation
alpha at Contact Patch] D --> E{Linear or Non-Linear Region} E -->|Small alpha| F[Cornering Stiffness C_alpha] E -->|Large alpha| G[Magic Formula Fiala Model] F --> H[Cornering Force Fy] G --> H H --> I[Yaw Moment Mz about CG] H --> J[Lateral Acceleration ay] I --> K[Yaw Rate psi_dot] J --> L[Vehicle Sideslip beta] K --> M[Vehicle Dynamics Response] L --> M M --> N[Understeer Oversteer Behaviour] M --> O[ESP ESC Stability Control] M --> P[Steering Feel Feedback] ``` ### 4.3 Force and Moment Components on a Tyre (Side View Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vertical Load Fz] --> B[Contact Patch Centre] C[Slip Angle alpha] --> D[Wheel Plane] D --> E[Lateral Displacement of Trailing Edge] E --> F[Pneumatic Trail tp] F --> G[Aligning Torque Mz] B --> H[Lateral Force Fy] H --> I[Cornering Force Vector] I --> J[Steering Axis] J --> K[Caster Effect] K --> G G --> L[Self-Centring Action] ``` ### 4.4 Magic Formula Characteristic Curve (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Slip Angle alpha deg] --> B{Region Classification} B -->|0 to 4 deg| C[Linear Region
Fy = C_alpha times alpha] B -->|4 to 8 deg| D[Non-linear Transition
Curvature Increasing] B -->|8 to 12 deg| E[Peak Friction Region
Fy approaches mu Fz] B -->|> 12 deg| F[Saturation
Loss of Steering Authority] C --> G[Stable Handling] D --> H[Sporty Handling] E --> I[Limit Handling] F --> J[Spin Out Instability] ``` ### 4.5 Tyre Parameter Interaction Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Vertical Load Fz] --> B[Contact Patch Geometry] B --> C[Contact Length 2a] B --> D[Contact Width 2b] C --> E[Cornering Stiffness C_alpha] D --> E E --> F[Slip Angle alpha] F --> G[Lateral Force Fy] A --> H[Inflation Pressure] H --> E A --> I[Friction Coefficient mu] I --> J[Peak Lateral Force] J --> G G --> K[Aligning Torque Mz] K --> L[Steering Self-Centring] L --> M[Driver Feel] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define slip angle of a pneumatic tyre. Explain its significance in vehicle handling with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer:** The **slip angle (α)** of a pneumatic tyre is defined as the angle between the **plane of the wheel** (its heading direction) and the **instantaneous direction of travel** of the tyre's contact centre. Mathematically: $$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right)$$ where $V_y$ and $V_x$ are the lateral and longitudinal velocity components of the contact centre. **Significance in Vehicle Handling:** 1. Slip angle is the *fundamental kinematic variable* that determines the lateral cornering force a tyre can develop. 2. It governs the **steering response** — at small α, the vehicle responds predictably (linear region). 3. It determines the **understeer / oversteer tendency** of the vehicle via the cornering stiffnesses of front and rear axles. 4. In transient manoeuvres, the **rate of change of slip angle** influences yaw damping and stability. 5. ESP / ESC controllers monitor and regulate slip angle in real time. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students omit the *direction* of the angle (sign convention). Always state that α is measured from the wheel plane to the velocity vector. Sketches without arrow direction lose 1 mark. --- #### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **List any four important tyre parameters that affect the side slip behaviour. Briefly explain cornering stiffness.** **Model Answer:** Four important tyre parameters affecting side slip behaviour are: 1. **Cornering Stiffness (C_α):** The slope of the lateral force vs. slip angle curve at the origin. Higher $C_\alpha$ means the tyre generates more cornering force per unit slip angle. Unit: **N/rad**. 2. **Relaxation Length (σ):** The distance a tyre must travel for the slip angle to build up to ~63% of its steady-state value after a step steering input. Longer σ delays steering response. Unit: **m**. 3. **Peak Slip Angle (α_peak):** The slip angle at which the lateral force reaches its maximum value $\mu F_z$. Beyond α_peak, the tyre saturates and loses steering authority. 4. **Pneumatic Trail (t_p):** The rearward offset of the lateral force resultant from the wheel centre. It generates the self-centring aligning torque $M_z = F_y \cdot t_p$. **Cornering Stiffness (C_α):** It is the rate of change of lateral force with respect to slip angle in the linear region, i.e.: $$C_\alpha = \left.\frac{\partial F_y}{\partial \alpha}\right|_{\alpha \to 0}$$ It is influenced by vertical load, inflation pressure, tyre size, tread compound, and contact-patch geometry. Typical values: **30,000 N/rad to 80,000 N/rad** for a passenger car tyre. ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice #### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for cornering stiffness of a pneumatic tyre using the Fiala brush model. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A passenger car is travelling at 25 m/s in a steady-state turn of radius 100 m. The vehicle mass is 1400 kg, wheelbase L = 2.6 m, with CG located 1.3 m from the front axle. The cornering stiffness per axle is $C_{\alpha f} = C_{\alpha r} = 50{,}000$ N/rad. Calculate: (i) Vehicle sideslip angle β, (ii) Yaw rate, (iii) Front and rear slip angles, (iv) Required steering angle, (v) Understeer gradient and state the vehicle's handling behaviour. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation:** Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. Key steps to present in the answer script: - Assumptions: rectangular contact patch, uniform pressure, tread elements as independent linear springs, small slip angle. - Element deflection $y(x) = \alpha (a - x)$. - Force per element $f(x) = k_y y(x)$. - Integration over the entire contact patch (adhesion regime). - Final result: $C_\alpha = 4 a^2 b k_y$. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Vehicle sideslip angle β:** For steady-state cornering, lateral acceleration is $a_y = V_x^2 / R = 25^2 / 100 = 6.25$ m/s². In steady-state, the vehicle's velocity vector aligns with the tangent of the circle, and β at the CG is found from the lateral equilibrium. For a symmetric vehicle (CG at L/2), the bicycle model gives: $$\beta \approx 0 \text{ (small for symmetric setup)}; \text{ but for general calculation:}$$ $$\beta = \frac{L_r}{L} \cdot \frac{a_y}{V_x} - \frac{L_f \cdot m \cdot a_y}{2 C_{\alpha r} L}$$ With $L_f = L_r = 1.3$ m and equal cornering stiffness: $$\beta = \frac{1.3}{2.6} \cdot \frac{6.25}{25} - \frac{1.3 \times 1400 \times 6.25}{2 \times 50{,}000 \times 2.6}$$ $$\beta = 0.5 \times 0.25 - \frac{11375}{260000} = 0.125 - 0.0438 = 0.0812 \text{ rad} = 4.65°$$ **(ii) Yaw rate:** $$\dot\psi = \frac{V_x}{R} = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25 \text{ rad/s}$$ **(iii) Front and rear slip angles:** $$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{L_f \dot\psi}{V_x}$$ We need δ first. For steady-state: $$\delta = \frac{L}{R} + K_{us} \cdot \frac{V_x^2}{g L}$$ Compute $K_{us}$: $W_f = W_r = mg/2 = 1400 \times 9.81 / 2 = 6867$ N. $$K_{us} = \frac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \frac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}} = \frac{6867}{50{,}000} - \frac{6867}{50{,}000} = 0$$ Neutral steer! So: $$\delta = L/R = 2.6/100 = 0.026 \text{ rad} = 1.49°$$ $$\alpha_f = 0.026 - 0.0812 - \frac{1.3 \times 0.25}{25} = 0.026 - 0.0812 - 0.013 = -0.0682 \text{ rad}$$ $$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{L_r \dot\psi}{V_x} = -0.0812 + 0.013 = -0.0682 \text{ rad}$$ Both axles have the same slip angle (consistent with neutral steer). **(iv) Required steering angle:** $\delta = 1.49°$ (already computed above). **(v) Understeer gradient:** $K_{us} = 0$ → **Neutral Steer** vehicle (constant Ackermann steering for all speeds). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For Part (b), students frequently: > 1. Forget to convert degrees to radians — losing 1 to 2 marks. > 2. Use $g = 10$ m/s² instead of 9.81 m/s² (acceptable, but state it). > 3. Mix up $L_f$ and $L_r$ — **always double-check** which is from the front vs. rear axle. > 4. Forget to state the **handling conclusion** ("neutral steer" / "understeering" / "oversteering") at the end. --- #### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Explain the Magic Formula of Pacejka for lateral tyre force. Sketch the typical lateral force vs. slip angle curve and identify the linear, transition, peak, and saturation regions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vehicle is fitted with tyres of cornering stiffness 70,000 N/rad. During a test, the lateral velocity at the tyre contact centre is measured as 0.8 m/s at a longitudinal speed of 18 m/s. The vertical load on the tyre is 4000 N and the road friction coefficient is 0.9. Calculate: (i) Slip angle, (ii) Cornering force, (iii) Maximum possible cornering force, (iv) Utilisation factor of friction, (v) The aligning torque if the pneumatic trail is 0.03 m. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Pacejka Magic Formula:** The Magic Formula expresses the lateral cornering force as: $$F_y = D \sin\left(C \arctan\left(B \alpha - E(B \alpha - \arctan(B \alpha))\right)\right)$$ where: - $B$ = **stiffness factor** — controls the slope near origin. - $C$ = **shape factor** — controls the overall shape (typically C = 1.6 for lateral). - $D$ = **peak factor** — sets the maximum value of $F_y$ (approximately $\mu F_z$). - $E$ = **curvature factor** — controls the sharpness of the peak. - $\alpha$ = slip angle. **Curve Sketch — Key Features:** ``` Fy | ___________ (Peak = D ≈ μFz) | / | / (Non-linear transition) | / | / (Linear region: Fy = Cα · α) | / | / | / |_/____________________ α 0 α_linear α_peak α_sat ``` Regions: - **Linear region (0 to ~4°):** $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$. - **Transition (4° to 8°):** Curve bends, $C_\alpha$ decreases. - **Peak region (8° to 12°):** $F_y$ reaches maximum $\approx \mu F_z$. - **Saturation (> 12°):** $F_y$ decreases; tyre loses lateral grip. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Slip angle:** $$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{V_y}{V_x}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{0.8}{18}\right) = \arctan(0.0444) = 0.0444 \text{ rad} = 2.54°$$ **(ii) Cornering force (linear model):** $$F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha = 70{,}000 \times 0.0444 = 3108 \text{ N}$$ **(iii) Maximum possible cornering force:** $$F_{y,\max} = \mu \cdot F_z = 0.9 \times 4000 = 3600 \text{ N}$$ **(iv) Utilisation factor of friction:** $$\eta = \frac{F_y}{F_{y,\max}} = \frac{3108}{3600} = 0.863 = 86.3\%$$ This is the **friction utilization** of the tyre. High utilisation means the tyre is operating near its friction limit. **(v) Aligning torque:** $$M_z = F_y \cdot t_p = 3108 \times 0.03 = 93.24 \text{ N·m}$$ The negative sign (by convention) indicates a self-centring torque opposing the steering input. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B Question):** When asked to "sketch" the Magic Formula curve, students often forget to: > 1. Label both axes with units (N and rad or deg). > 2. Mark the **peak value** explicitly. > 3. Mark the **zero-crossing** at the origin. > 4. Indicate the **linear region** with a separate line annotation. > A neat, well-labelled sketch typically carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Side Slip: Effects, Calculation, and Tyre Parameters** - **Definition:** Slip angle α = angle between wheel plane and actual velocity vector; $\alpha = \arctan(V_y / V_x)$. - **Linear lateral force equation:** $F_y = C_\alpha \cdot \alpha$. Valid only for small α (typically < 4°). - **Cornering stiffness $C_\alpha$:** Slope of $F_y$ vs. α at origin; units N/rad. Increases with vertical load (up to a peak) and inflation pressure. - **Fiala brush model result:** $C_\alpha = 4 a^2 b k_y$ — depends on contact-patch half-length squared, width, and tread lateral stiffness. - **Pacejka Magic Formula:** Industry-standard empirical model capturing non-linear behaviour up to and beyond peak friction. - **Vehicle sideslip angle β:** $\beta = \arctan(V_y / V_x)$ at the CG. - **Bicycle model slip angles:** - Front: $\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - L_f \dot\psi / V_x$ - Rear: $\alpha_r = -\beta + L_r \dot\psi / V_x$ - **Understeer gradient:** $K_{us} = W_f / C_{\alpha f} - W_r / C_{\alpha r}$. Positive → understeer (stable); negative → oversteer (unstable); zero → neutral. - **Pneumatic trail $t_p$:** Distance from wheel centre to lateral force application point. Generates aligning torque $M_z = -F_y \cdot t_p$. - **Relaxation length σ:** Distance to develop ~63% of steady-state slip angle. Influences transient steering response. - **Peak lateral force:** $F_{y,\max} = \mu F_z$, reached at slip angle $\alpha_{peak}$ (typically 8°–12° for road tyres). - **Friction utilisation:** $\eta = F_y / (\mu F_z)$; a key metric for stability control algorithm tuning. - **Practical sign convention:** Lateral force toward the centre of the turn is taken as **negative** in the ISO coordinate system. - **KTU favourite questions:** Define slip angle, derive cornering stiffness (Fiala), explain Magic Formula, compute slip angles and understeer gradient from given data. - **Always convert degrees to radians** before plugging into formulas. - **Always state the handling conclusion** (understeer / neutral / oversteer) when computing $K_{us}$ in numerical problems. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Side slip is one of the most heavily weighted topics in Module 3. Expect a minimum of **one 7-mark derivation** (Fiala or Magic Formula) and **one 7-mark numerical** in Part B. Always draw a neat **velocity triangle** when explaining slip angle — examiners reward clear diagrams with 1–2 bonus marks.

topics.title: Vehicle Control Using GNSS/GPS: Introduction and Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Control Using GNSS/GPS: Introduction and Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)** is a constellation of satellites providing geo-spatial positioning with global coverage. The most widely used GNSS is the United States **Global Positioning System (GPS)**, which consists of a nominal constellation of **24+ satellites** in **6 orbital planes** at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Vehicle control using GNSS/GPS refers to the use of satellite-based positioning data to determine a vehicle's location, velocity, and time (PVT), and subsequently employing this information in closed-loop control systems to guide, steer, regulate speed, and manage autonomous or semi-autonomous vehicle operations.*

For automotive control applications, GNSS/GPS serves as the **primary absolute position reference sensor**, complementing relative sensors such as Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs), wheel encoders, LiDAR, and cameras.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded in an unknown city. You periodically call a friend in the sky who can see satellites overhead. By measuring the **time** it takes for a "shout" (radio signal) to travel from the satellite to your radio, your friend can calculate the **distance** to that satellite using the simple formula: `Distance = Speed of Light × Time`. 

With distances to **four or more satellites**, your friend can pinpoint your exact 3D position (latitude, longitude, altitude) — much like how **triangulation** works in detective movies. This is the heart of GNSS positioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why four satellites?** Three satellites give you a 3D position, but a fourth is required to **synchronize the receiver's clock** with the highly accurate atomic clocks on the satellites. Without this, even a tiny clock error of 1 microsecond would translate to a 300 m position error!

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Standards)

The performance of GNSS for vehicle control is judged by four standard metrics:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Accuracy** | **2.5 m CEP (SPS)** | Circular Error Probable — 50% of readings fall within this radius |
| **Availability** | **> 99%** | Percentage of time a valid fix is obtainable |
| **Continuity** | **High** | Ability to maintain fix throughout an operation |
| **Integrity** | **Critical for safety** | Ability to detect and warn of faulty signals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CEP (Circular Error Probable)** is a measure of positioning accuracy. **2.5 m CEP** means that 50% of the time, the horizontal position estimate lies within 2.5 meters of the true position.

## 1.4 GNSS Segments

The GPS system is logically divided into three segments:

1. **Space Segment** — The satellite constellation (24+ MEO satellites).
2. **Control Segment** — Ground-based monitoring stations that track satellites, compute ephemeris corrections, and upload navigation messages.
3. **User Segment** — The GPS receivers in vehicles, smartphones, etc., which compute position using trilateration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle: Trilateration

GNSS positioning is founded on **trilateration**, not triangulation. The process involves:

1. **Signal Acquisition**: The receiver tracks the **L1 (1575.42 MHz)** and/or **L2 (1227.60 MHz)** carrier frequencies broadcast by satellites.
2. **Pseudorange Measurement**: The receiver measures the apparent distance to each satellite using the travel time of the navigation message.
3. **Position Computation**: Using at least **4 pseudoranges**, the receiver solves a system of nonlinear equations to obtain its 3D position and clock bias.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pseudorange** is the range measurement that includes both the true geometric range and the receiver's clock error. This is why we need a fourth satellite — to "absorb" the clock bias as a fourth unknown.

## 2.2 The Core Measurement Equation

The pseudorange $\rho$ from a receiver to a satellite is modeled as:

$$
\rho = r + c \cdot (\delta t_u - \delta t_s) + I + T + \varepsilon_{mp} + \varepsilon_{noise}
$$

Where:
- $r$ = true geometric range (Euclidean distance)
- $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)
- $\delta t_u$ = receiver clock bias
- $\delta t_s$ = satellite clock bias
- $I$ = ionospheric delay
- $T$ = tropospheric delay
- $\varepsilon_{mp}$ = multipath error
- $\varepsilon_{noise}$ = receiver measurement noise

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical constants for KTU ESE.

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pseudorange equation** | $\rho = r + c \cdot \Delta t + \text{errors}$ | Fundamental GPS measurement model |
| **Geometric range** | $r = \sqrt{(x_s - x_u)^2 + (y_s - y_u)^2 + (z_s - z_u)^2}$ | True line-of-sight distance to satellite |
| **Doppler shift (velocity)** | $v = -\lambda \cdot f_d$ | Used for GNSS-based velocity determination |
| **Dilution of Precision** | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^{-1} \cdot H^{-T})}$ | Satellite geometry quality indicator |
| **Position accuracy** | $\sigma_P = \sigma_{URE} \cdot \text{DOP}$ | Accuracy = User Range Error $\times$ DOP |
| **DGPS correction** | $\rho_{corr} = \rho + \text{RTCM correction}$ | Removes spatially correlated errors |
| **Carrier phase equation** | $\phi = r/\lambda + N + f_{errors}$ | High-precision (mm-level) RTK positioning |
| **ECEF to LLA conversion** | $\lambda = \arctan(\frac{y}{x})$ | Geographic coordinate transform |
| **Haversine distance** | $d = 2R \arctan(\sqrt{a}/\sqrt{1-a})$ | Great-circle distance between two GPS points |
| **Update rate constraint** | $f_{update} = 1\text{-}10 \text{ Hz}$ | Typical automotive GPS update frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** In the formulas above, all absolute value bars and conditionals have been written as visible text to keep the table syntax stable. **Do not use the `\|` pipe character inside table cells.**

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of GNSS in Automotive Control

GNSS-based vehicle control is foundational in:

- **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** — Adaptive Cruise Control with geo-fencing.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Navigation** — Lane-level positioning for self-driving cars.
- **Fleet Management & Telematics** — Real-time tracking of commercial vehicle fleets.
- **Electronic Horizon** — Predictive ADAS that uses map + GPS data to anticipate road curvature, speed limits, and gradients.
- **Vehicle Immobilization & Stolen Vehicle Recovery** — Remote engine cut-off.
- **Insurance Telematics (Usage-Based Insurance)** — Pay-how-you-drive premium calculation.

## 2.5 Sources of GNSS Error

| Error Source | Typical Magnitude (m) | Mitigation Technique |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ionospheric delay** | **1–5 m** (daytime) | Dual-frequency receivers, Klobuchar model |
| **Tropospheric delay** | **0.5–2.5 m** | Saastamoinen model, atmospheric models |
| **Satellite clock bias** | **≤ 2 m** | Broadcast ephemeris corrections |
| **Ephemeris errors** | **≤ 2.5 m** | Uploaded in navigation message |
| **Multipath** | **0.5–1 m** (urban canyons) | Choke-ring antennas, signal processing |
| **Receiver noise** | **0.1–0.5 m** | Quality of receiver hardware |
| **Selective Availability (legacy)** | **0 m (since 2000)** | Disabled by US government |

## 2.6 GNSS Augmentation Techniques

To improve positioning accuracy from the standard **2.5–5 m** to centimeter-level, several augmentation techniques are used:

1. **DGPS (Differential GPS)** — Uses a fixed reference station to broadcast range corrections. Improves accuracy to **1–3 m**.
2. **SBAS (Satellite-Based Augmentation System)** — Geostationary satellites (e.g., WAAS, EGNOS, GAGAN) broadcast wide-area corrections. Provides **1–2 m** accuracy with integrity.
3. **RTK (Real-Time Kinematic)** — Uses carrier phase measurements with ambiguity resolution. Achieves **cm-level** accuracy in real time, ideal for autonomous vehicle lane-keeping.
4. **PPP (Precise Point Positioning)** — Global corrections via internet; **cm–dm** accuracy after convergence.
5. **INS/GNSS Integration** — Fuses GNSS with IMU using Kalman filtering to maintain accuracy during GNSS outages (tunnels, urban canyons).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Position Solution from Pseudoranges

We now rigorously derive how a GNSS receiver computes its 3D position from at least four pseudorange measurements.

### Step 1: Define the coordinate system

Let the receiver's unknown position be $(x_u, y_u, z_u)$ and the receiver's clock bias be $\delta t_u$. Let the $i^{th}$ satellite's known position be $(x_{s,i}, y_{s,i}, z_{s,i})$ — obtained from the broadcast ephemeris.

### Step 2: Write the geometric range equation

For the $i^{th}$ satellite, the true geometric range is:

$$
r_i = \sqrt{(x_{s,i} - x_u)^2 + (y_{s,i} - y_u)^2 + (z_{s,i} - z_u)^2}
$$

**Reasoning:** This is the Euclidean distance between two points in 3D space. The travel time of the signal, when multiplied by $c$, gives this range if there were no errors.

### Step 3: Write the full pseudorange equation

$$
\rho_i = r_i + c \cdot \delta t_u - c \cdot \delta t_{s,i} + I_i + T_i + \varepsilon_i
$$

For simplicity, we absorb all error terms (satellite clock, ionosphere, troposphere) into a single **corrected pseudorange** $R_i$:

$$
R_i = \rho_i + c \cdot \delta t_{s,i} - I_i - T_i
$$

So the working equation reduces to:

$$
R_i = \sqrt{(x_{s,i} - x_u)^2 + (y_{s,i} - y_u)^2 + (z_{s,i} - z_u)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_u
$$

### Step 4: Expand the system for four satellites

For four satellites ($i = 1, 2, 3, 4$), we have four equations with four unknowns $(x_u, y_u, z_u, \delta t_u)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \sqrt{(x_{s,1} - x_u)^2 + (y_{s,1} - y_u)^2 + (z_{s,1} - z_u)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_u \\
R_2 &= \sqrt{(x_{s,2} - x_u)^2 + (y_{s,2} - y_u)^2 + (z_{s,2} - z_u)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_u \\
R_3 &= \sqrt{(x_{s,3} - x_u)^2 + (y_{s,3} - y_u)^2 + (z_{s,3} - z_u)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_u \\
R_4 &= \sqrt{(x_{s,4} - x_u)^2 + (y_{s,4} - y_u)^2 + (z_{s,4} - z_u)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_u
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reasoning:** This is a system of nonlinear equations because of the square roots. An initial guess and iterative linearization (Newton-Raphson / Gauss-Newton method) is required.

### Step 5: Linearize using Taylor series expansion

Expand each equation around an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, \delta t_{u,0})$:

$$
R_i \approx R_{i,0} + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x_u}\Delta x_u + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y_u}\Delta y_u + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z_u}\Delta z_u + \frac{\partial R_i}{\partial (\delta t_u)}\Delta(\delta t_u)
$$

The partial derivatives (the line-of-sight unit vector components) are:

$$
\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial x_u} = -\frac{x_{s,i} - x_u}{r_i}, \quad
\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial y_u} = -\frac{y_{s,i} - y_u}{r_i}, \quad
\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial z_u} = -\frac{z_{s,i} - z_u}{r_i}, \quad
\frac{\partial R_i}{\partial (\delta t_u)} = c
$$

### Step 6: Form the matrix equation $\mathbf{H \cdot \Delta x = \Delta R}$

Let $\mathbf{a}_i = [a_{xi}, a_{yi}, a_{zi}, 1]^T$ be the geometry vector for the $i^{th}$ satellite. The system becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta \rho_1 &= a_{x1}\Delta x_u + a_{y1}\Delta y_u + a_{z1}\Delta z_u + c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_u) \\
\Delta \rho_2 &= a_{x2}\Delta x_u + a_{y2}\Delta y_u + a_{z2}\Delta z_u + c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_u) \\
\Delta \rho_3 &= a_{x3}\Delta x_u + a_{y3}\Delta y_u + a_{z3}\Delta z_u + c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_u) \\
\Delta \rho_4 &= a_{x4}\Delta x_u + a_{y4}\Delta y_u + a_{z4}\Delta z_u + c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_u)
\end{aligned}
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix}
a_{x1} & a_{y1} & a_{z1} & c \\
a_{x2} & a_{y2} & a_{z2} & c \\
a_{x3} & a_{y3} & a_{z3} & c \\
a_{x4} & a_{y4} & a_{z4} & c
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{\Delta x} = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x_u \\ \Delta y_u \\ \Delta z_u \\ c \cdot \Delta(\delta t_u) \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{\Delta \rho} = \begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 7: Solve the linear system

The least-squares (or direct) solution is:

$$
\mathbf{\Delta x} = \mathbf{H}^{-1} \cdot \mathbf{\Delta \rho} \quad \text{(or } \mathbf{\Delta x} = (\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{H})^{-1}\mathbf{H}^T\mathbf{\Delta \rho} \text{ for more than 4 satellites)}
$$

### Step 8: Iterate

Update the position estimate:

$$
x_u^{k+1} = x_u^k + \Delta x_u, \quad y_u^{k+1} = y_u^k + \Delta y_u, \quad z_u^{k+1} = z_u^k + \Delta z_u
$$

Repeat steps 5–7 until the corrections $\Delta x$ are below a convergence threshold (typically **0.001 m**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Convergence:** A well-conditioned system usually converges in **3–5 iterations** for automotive GPS receivers operating in open-sky conditions.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Vehicle Control Architectures Using GNSS

The following analytical matrix maps GNSS techniques to specific vehicle control functions and engineering trade-offs. This is a high-yield KTU analysis framework.

| GNSS Technique | Vehicle Control Function | Update Rate | Typical Accuracy | Limitation / Pitfall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard SPS GPS** | Fleet tracking, route logging | 1 Hz | **2.5–5 m** | Too coarse for lane keeping |
| **DGPS (Differential)** | Geofencing, urban navigation | 1–10 Hz | **1–3 m** | Requires nearby base station |
| **SBAS (WAAS/EGNOS)** | Highway ADAS, predictive ACC | 1–5 Hz | **1–2 m** | Regional coverage only |
| **RTK GNSS** | Autonomous lane-keeping, precision agriculture | 10–20 Hz | **1–2 cm** | Needs base station or NTRIP; integer ambiguity resolution needed |
| **PPP / PPP-RTK** | Nationwide lane-level | 1–10 Hz | **5–10 cm** | Long convergence time (~10 min) |
| **INS/GNSS Fusion (Kalman)** | Continuous navigation in tunnels | 100–200 Hz (IMU) | Drift-corrected | Filter tuning complexity |
| **GNSS + Visual SLAM** | Urban autonomous driving | 10–30 Hz | Lane-level | High computational cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RTK is critical for autonomous vehicles:** Lane widths are typically **3.5 m** in many jurisdictions. Standard GPS (2.5 m) cannot reliably distinguish lanes. RTK with **cm-level** accuracy is therefore the de-facto positioning technology for Level 3+ autonomous vehicles.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Geometric Dilution of Precision (GDOP)

DOP quantifies how satellite geometry amplifies ranging errors into position errors.

### Step 1: Measurement covariance

The covariance of the position estimate is:

$$
\text{cov}(\mathbf{\Delta x}) = \sigma_{URE}^2 \cdot (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}
$$

Where $\sigma_{URE}$ is the User Range Error (URE) of the pseudoranges.

### Step 2: Diagonal elements give position variances

The variance of the $x$-coordinate estimate is:

$$
\sigma_x^2 = \sigma_{URE}^2 \cdot [(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}]_{11}
$$

Similarly for $y$, $z$, and the clock bias.

### Step 3: GDOP definition

$$
\text{GDOP} = \frac{\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2 + \sigma_{ct}^2}}{\sigma_{URE}}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}[(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1}]}
$$

**Reasoning:** A good satellite geometry (satellites well-spread across the sky) gives a **low GDOP** (close to 1), while a clustered geometry (all satellites in one part of the sky) gives a **high GDOP** (greater than 5), degrading accuracy.

| GDOP Value | Quality | Practical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1–3** | Excellent | Ideal for high-precision applications |
| **3–6** | Good | Acceptable for automotive |
| **6–8** | Moderate | Position usable but degraded |
| **> 8** | Poor | Avoid using for safety-critical control |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level GNSS Vehicle Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of a GNSS-based vehicle control system, from satellite signal reception to steering and throttle actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Satellite Constellation L1 L2 L5 Signals] --> node2[GNSS Antenna on Vehicle Roof]
    node2 --> node3[GNSS Receiver Front End LNA Filter]
    node3 --> node4[Baseband Signal Processing Acquisition Tracking]
    node4 --> node5[Pseudorange and Carrier Phase Extraction]
    node5 --> node6[Position Velocity Time PVT Solver]
    node6 --> node7[Kalman Filter Sensor Fusion]
    node8[IMU Accelerometer Gyroscope] --> node7
    node9[Wheel Speed Sensors Odometer] --> node7
    node7 --> node10[Vehicle State Estimator Position Velocity Heading]
    node10 --> node11[Path Planning and Waypoint Manager]
    node12[HD Map Database] --> node11
    node11 --> node13[Trajectory Tracking Controller Stanley MPC Pure Pursuit]
    node13 --> node14[Vehicle Actuators Steering Throttle Brake]
    node14 --> node15[Vehicle Motion]
    node15 --> node16[Feedback to State Estimator]
    node6 -.DGPS or RTK Corrections.-> node17[Differential Correction Source NTRIP SBAS]
    node17 --> node6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The forward chain (left → right) shows data flow from satellites to actuators. The IMU, wheel sensors, and differential corrections feed back to improve robustness — this is the essence of an **INS/GNSS tightly-coupled architecture**.

---

## 4.2 Pseudorange Computation Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Receive Signal at Time tr] --> nodeB[Decode Navigation Message Timestamp ts]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Signal Travel Time dT = tr minus ts]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Pseudorange rho = c times dT]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Atmospheric Corrections I and T]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Apply Satellite Clock Correction]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Corrected Pseudorange R available for Trilateration]
    nodeG --> nodeH{At least 4 Satellites Visible?}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Solve 4x4 Position Equation]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ[Hold Last Fix and Propagate with Dead Reckoning]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Output ECEF Position fix]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Convert to Latitude Longitude Altitude]
```

---

## 4.3 DOP Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Bad Geometry High GDOP]
        sat1[Sat 1] --- sat2[Sat 2] --- sat3[Sat 3] --- sat4[Sat 4]
    end
    subgraph S2[Good Geometry Low GDOP]
        sa1[Sat A North] 
        sa2[Sat B South]
        sa3[Sat C East]
        sa4[Sat D West]
    end
    S1 --> op1[Range errors amplified 5x]
    S2 --> op2[Range errors amplified 1.2x]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Tip for KTU:** A 1-unit increase in GDOP directly multiplies the position error. If URE is 1 m and GDOP is 4, position error is 4 m. Choose satellite geometries with GDOP < 4 for safety-critical control.

---

## 4.4 Vehicle Control Modes Enabled by GNSS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX1[GNSS Position Fix] --> nodeX2{Application Tier}
    nodeX2 -- Tier 1 -- nodeX3[Tracking and Telematics Fleet Management]
    nodeX2 -- Tier 2 -- nodeX4[Geofencing Speed Limiting Zone Control]
    nodeX2 -- Tier 3 -- nodeX5[Highway Pilot ACC LKA L2]
    nodeX2 -- Tier 4 -- nodeX6[Urban Autonomous L4 L5 with HD Map]
    nodeX2 -- Tier 5 -- nodeX7[Precision Applications Auto Valet Parking Agriculture]
    nodeX6 --> nodeX8[Requires RTK or PPP-RTK]
    nodeX7 --> nodeX8
    nodeX5 --> nodeX9[Suitable with SBAS or DGPS]
```
