# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Electronic Throttle Control (ETC). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
Electronic Throttle Control (ETC), also known as **drive-by-wire**, is a system that electronically manages the throttle plate opening of an internal combustion engine in response to accelerator pedal input, replacing the traditional mechanical cable linkage. The accelerator pedal position is sensed by redundant pedal position sensors (PPS), and the Engine Control Unit (ECU) calculates the desired throttle angle through a pre-calibrated pedal map. A DC servo motor then drives the throttle plate, with a closed-loop PID controller using throttle position sensor (TPS) feedback to maintain the desired opening.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:** Enables integration with cruise control, traction control, and electronic stability programs. Reduces emissions through precise air-fuel ratio management.

### Question 2: List any three advantages of Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) over conventional cruise control. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

1. **Headway Maintenance:** ACC automatically adjusts vehicle speed to maintain a safe following distance from a slower preceding vehicle, whereas conventional cruise control maintains only a fixed set speed.
2. **Stop-and-Go Capability:** Modern ACC systems can bring the vehicle to a complete stop in traffic and resume automatically.
3. **Collision Risk Reduction:** By using radar/lidar sensing, ACC can react faster than a human driver to closing speeds, reducing rear-end collision risk.
4. **Driver Comfort:** Reduces the cognitive load of constantly monitoring traffic, especially on long drives.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice**. The two alternatives given below are both within the Module 3 syllabus and follow the standard 7+7 marks sub-division.

### Question A (14 Marks): Speed Control System Design

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a conventional **closed-loop cruise control system** for an automobile. Clearly identify the controller, plant, sensor, and disturbance. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **transfer function** of the longitudinal speed control loop, given that the vehicle dynamics are linearized as:

$$
\tau \dot{v} + v = K u(t)
$$

where $\tau$ is the mechanical time constant, $K$ is the DC gain, and $u(t)$ is the throttle command. Use a PI controller $C(s) = K_p + K_i/s$ and find the expression for steady-state error due to a step disturbance. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Solution to Part (a): Cruise Control Block Diagram and Working

**Block Diagram:**

```
[v_set] -->(+)<--[v_actual]<--[Plant: Vehicle]<--[u(t)]<--[PI Controller]<--[Error e]
            (-)
```

**Working Steps:**

1. The driver activates cruise control and sets a desired speed $v_{set}$ via steering-mounted buttons.
2. The wheel speed sensor measures the actual vehicle speed $v_{actual}$ and feeds it to the ECU.
3. The ECU computes the speed error: $e(t) = v_{set} - v_{actual}(t)$.
4. This error is processed by a **PI controller** (proportional-integral), which outputs a throttle command $u(t)$.
5. The throttle actuator adjusts the throttle plate opening, which changes engine torque and thus tractive force.
6. The vehicle responds by accelerating or decelerating, closing the feedback loop.
7. **Disturbance inputs** such as road gradient, wind, and rolling resistance are automatically rejected by the controller.

**Identification of Elements:**

| Element | Component |
|---|---|
| Reference input | $v_{set}$ |
| Sensor | Wheel Speed Sensor |
| Controller | PI Controller in ECU |
| Actuator | Electronic Throttle Body |
| Plant | Vehicle longitudinal dynamics |

---

### Solution to Part (b): Transfer Function and Steady-State Error

**Step 1:** Plant transfer function from $u$ to $v$:

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{V(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

**Step 2:** Controller transfer function:

$$
C(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} = \frac{K_p s + K_i}{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Open-loop transfer function:

$$
G(s) = C(s) G_p(s) = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1)}
$$

**Step 4:** Closed-loop transfer function (with unity feedback):

$$
T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{K(K_p s + K_i)}{s(\tau s + 1) + K(K_p s + K_i)}
$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$
s(\tau s + 1) + K(K_p s + K_i) = \tau s^2 + s + K K_p s + K K_i = \tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i
$$

Therefore:

$$
T(s) = \frac{K K_p s + K K_i}{\tau s^2 + (1 + K K_p) s + K K_i}
$$

**Step 5:** Steady-state error for a step disturbance $D(s) = D_0/s$:

The transfer function from disturbance $D(s)$ to error $E(s)$ is:

$$
\frac{E(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{-1}{1 + C(s) G_p(s)}
$$

Using the Final Value Theorem for a step disturbance:

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{-1}{1 + C(s)G_p(s)} \cdot \frac{D_0}{s}
$$

$$
e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{-D_0}{1 + C(s)G_p(s)}
$$

As $s \to 0$:

$$
C(0)G_p(0) = \frac{K \cdot K_i}{0} \to \infty
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{e_{ss} = 0}
$$

**Logic:** The integral term in the PI controller makes the system **Type 1**, which means it has zero steady-state error to a step disturbance (e.g., a sudden road gradient). This is why PI is preferred over P-only for cruise control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Adaptive Cruise Control and Engine Governors

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and control strategy of an **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** system. Discuss the role of the constant time headway policy $d_{des} = d_0 + \tau_h v_{ego}$ and the choice of $\tau_h = 1.5 \, s$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of an **electronic engine governor** used in modern common-rail diesel engines. How does it differ from a Watt-type centrifugal governor? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Solution to Part (a): Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Architecture

**Architecture Overview:**

The ACC system consists of three primary subsystems:

1. **Perception Subsystem:**
   - 77 GHz front radar (range up to 200 m)
   - Forward camera for vehicle classification
   - Wheel speed sensors for ego vehicle speed

2. **Decision Subsystem (ECU):**
   - Target detection and tracking (Kalman filter)
   - State estimation: range $d$, range rate $\dot{d}$, relative velocity $\Delta v$

3. **Actuation Subsystem:**
   - Electronic throttle body (acceleration)
   - Brake-by-wire (deceleration)
   - Transmission interface (downshifting for engine braking)

**Control Strategy:**

The control objective is dual:
- **Speed tracking:** Match $v_{set}$ when the road is clear.
- **Headway control:** Maintain $d(t) \approx d_{des}$ when a target is detected.

**Constant Time Headway Policy:**

$$
d_{des} = d_0 + \tau_h v_{ego}
$$

- $d_0$ = standstill gap (≈ 2 m) — accounts for sensor bias and brake lag.
- $\tau_h$ = time headway (typically **1.5–2.0 s**) — the time required for the ego vehicle to reach the lead vehicle's current position.

**Why $\tau_h = 1.5 \, s$ is a Common Choice:**

- **Safety:** At 100 km/h (27.78 m/s), a 1.5 s headway gives $d_{des} = 2 + 1.5 \times 27.78 = 43.67 \, m$, providing ample braking margin.
- **Driver Acceptance:** Studies show drivers prefer $\tau_h \in [1.4, 1.8] \, s$ for comfort.
- **Traffic Flow:** Shorter headways improve road throughput; longer headways improve safety.
- **Standard Compliance:** ISO 22169 (Adaptive Cruise Control systems — Performance requirements and test procedures) recommends $\tau_h \geq 1.0 \, s$ as a minimum.

**Switching Logic between Speed and Headway Modes:**

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} \text{Speed control } (v_{set}) & \text{if no target detected} \\ \text{Headway control } (d_{des}) & \text{if target detected and } d < d_{des} \end{cases}
$$

---

### Solution to Part (b): Electronic Engine Governor vs. Watt Governor

**Electronic Governor (Common-Rail Diesel) — Working:**

1. The **crankshaft position sensor** measures actual engine RPM, $N_{actual}$.
2. The ECU compares $N_{actual}$ with the governor setpoint $N_{set}$ (e.g., 4000 RPM for a light-duty diesel).
3. The error $e = N_{set} - N_{actual}$ is processed by a **PI controller** in the ECU.
4. The output adjusts:
   - **Fuel rail pressure** (via the SCV — Suction Control Valve on the supply pump)
   - **Injection duration** (via the injector solenoid pulse width)
5. If $N_{actual} > N_{set}$, fuel delivery is reduced; if $N_{actual} < N_{set}$, fuel delivery is increased.

**Watt-Type Centrifugal Governor — Working:**

1. **Flyweights** are mounted on a rotating spindle driven by the engine.
2. As RPM increases, centrifugal force pushes the flyweights outward.
3. The flyweights are mechanically linked to a **sleeve** that moves axially.
4. The sleeve is connected to the **fuel rack** of the injection pump.
5. At the calibrated speed, the sleeve position reduces fuel delivery, limiting RPM.

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | Watt Governor | Electronic Governor |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing** | Mechanical (centrifugal force) | Electronic (crankshaft sensor) |
| **Actuation** | Mechanical linkage to fuel rack | ECU adjusts fuel rail & injectors |
| **Accuracy** | ±5% | ±0.5% |
| **Adjustability** | Fixed calibration | Software-configurable |
| **Multi-mode** | Single setpoint | Idle, partial load, max speed modes |
| **Response Time** | Slow (mechanical lag) | Fast (electronic, <10 ms) |
| **Emissions Compliance** | Poor | Excellent (precise fueling) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly lose marks on these questions in three ways:
> 1. **Confusing speed control with engine control:** Speed control regulates *vehicle* speed, not just engine RPM. Always distinguish between $v_{vehicle}$ and $N_{engine}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the integral term justification:** In PI controller questions, you must explain *why* integral action is needed (to eliminate steady-state error from grade resistance). Simply writing "use PI" without justification loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical problems:** Always carry units throughout the derivation. A correct answer without units is marked partially.
> 4. **Not labeling block diagrams fully:** In block diagram questions, every signal, summing junction, and feedback path must be labeled. Unlabeled diagrams fetch at most 50% marks for that sub-part.
> 5. **Mixing up $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$:** Many students swap proportional and integral gains. Remember: $K_p$ acts on *current* error, $K_i$ acts on *accumulated* error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Speed control = closed-loop feedback regulation of vehicle velocity** — always identify sensor, controller, actuator, and plant.
- ✅ **Longitudinal dynamics:** $m \dot{v} = F_{drive} - F_{aero} - F_{roll} - F_{grade}$ — the **master plant equation**.
- ✅ **PI controller is preferred** for cruise control: $K_p$ for response speed, $K_i$ for zero steady-state error.
- ✅ **PID is rare in production** cruise control because speed-sensor noise amplified by $K_d$ causes throttle chatter.
- ✅ **ETC / drive-by-wire** uses **two redundant pedal sensors** for fail-safe operation.
- ✅ **ACC time headway** $\tau_h = 1.5 \, s$ balances safety and driver comfort; $d_0 = 2 \, m$ typical.
- ✅ **Engine governor** in modern diesel is **electronic**, not centrifugal — uses crankshaft sensor + ECU + fuel rail control.
- ✅ **Speed limiter** in commercial vehicles is mandatory in many jurisdictions (EU: 90 km/h trucks, India: 80 km/h buses).
- ✅ **Settling time** $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta \omega_n)$; **overshoot** $M_p = 100 e^{-\pi \zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- ✅ **Longitudinal jerk** $j \leq 2 \, m/s^3$ is the comfort threshold in ACC and automatic transmission.
- ✅ **Traction control** modulates throttle + selective braking to prevent wheel slip (slip ratio $\lambda \in [0.05, 0.15]$).
- ✅ **Open-loop transfer function** for speed loop: $G(s) = K(K_p s + K_i) / [s(\tau s + 1)]$ — a **Type 1** system.
- ✅ **Type 1 system** → infinite DC gain → zero steady-state error to step disturbance.
- ✅ **Memorize all 14 formulas** in the cheat sheet table — they appear directly in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Block diagrams must always show** the disturbance path for full marks.
- ✅ **Watt governor is mechanical and obsolete** in modern vehicles; questions may contrast it with electronic governors.

### Frequently Asked KTU Question Patterns

1. *"Explain with block diagram the cruise control system."* → Use Section 4.1.
2. *"Derive the transfer function of the speed control loop."* → Use Section 3.1.
3. *"Compare electronic and mechanical governors."* → Use Section 5.2 Solution to Part (b).
4. *"Explain ACC with constant time headway policy."* → Use Section 2.3 + 5.2.
5. *"What is drive-by-wire?"* → Use Section 1.4 + 2.1.
6. *"State the comfort limit for jerk and justify."* → $j \leq 2 \, m/s^3$, justified by passenger biomechanics studies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Controllers Used in Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Speed Controllers Used in Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **speed controller** in an automotive context is a closed-loop (or open-loop) regulating device that senses the rotational velocity of an output shaft, engine crankshaft, or wheel and automatically actuates the fuel delivery, throttle position, or braking torque to maintain the speed at a desired reference set-point despite variations in load, gradient, or ambient conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, speed controllers are classified by their **energy domain** (mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, electronic) and by their **control architecture** (centrifugal governors, hydraulic governors, electronic throttle control, cruise control, adaptive cruise control, and traction/stability controllers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A speed controller is any subsystem that compares actual vehicle/engine speed against a reference and produces a corrective actuation signal. The reference may be set by the driver (cruise control), by the manufacturer (speed limiter), or by a supervisory algorithm (ACC, TCS, ESC).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Ship Steersman

Imagine you are steering a small cruise ship. Your desired speed is 12 knots, but waves, current, and wind keep pushing it off-target. You don't steer once and forget — you **continuously watch the speedometer**, compare it with 12 knots, and adjust the engine telegraph up or down. A vehicle speed controller does exactly this, except the "watching" is done by a **tachogenerator / wheel-speed sensor**, the "brain" is the **ECU**, and the "engine telegraph" is the **electronic throttle body** or **fuel injector**.

> [!NOTE]
> Three pillars of every automotive speed controller:
> 1. **Sensor** — measures actual speed (tachogenerator, Hall sensor, ABS wheel-speed sensor).
> 2. **Controller** — computes error (mechanical lever, hydraulic valve, or ECU with PID algorithm).
> 3. **Actuator** — corrects the speed (throttle valve, fuel injector, brake calliper, or drive motor inverter).

### 1.3 Classification of Vehicle Speed Controllers

| S.No | Class | Operating Principle | Typical Application |
|:----:|:------|:--------------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical Centrifugal Governor** | Centrifugal force of flyweights vs. spring/dead-weight | Small engines, gensets, early carbureted vehicles |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Governor** | Oil pressure proportional to speed error | Heavy trucks, diesel locomotives, large marine engines |
| 3 | **Pneumatic Governor** | Air-pressure differential from engine intake | Diesel truck engines (Woodward PG-type), compressors |
| 4 | **Electronic (PID) Speed Controller** | Microprocessor running PID on speed feedback | Modern EFI/CRDI engines, EV traction motors |
| 5 | **Cruise Control (CC)** | Driver-set speed, longitudinal PID control | Passenger cars, light trucks |
| 6 | **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** | CC + radar/lidar headway control | ADAS Level 1 / 2 vehicles |
| 7 | **Electronic Throttle Control (ETC / DBW)** | Drive-by-wire pedal → ECU → motorised throttle | All modern petrol and many diesel cars |
| 8 | **Speed Limiter / Governor (Governed Speed Device)** | Hard clamp on maximum road speed | Commercial vehicles (90 km/h in EU), 2-wheelers |
| 9 | **Traction / Stability Controller (TCS / ESC)** | Compares driven vs. non-driven wheel speed | Mandatory in many regions since 2011+ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference metric:** Engine/vehicle speed is universally expressed in **revolutions per minute (rpm)** for engines and **km/h (or m/s)** for road speed. The conversion constant is **1 rpm = (2π/60) rad/s ≈ 0.10472 rad/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Centrifugal Governor — Operating Logic

A centrifugal governor uses two **flyballs** pivoted on a rotating spindle. As speed (ω) rises, centrifugal force throws the balls outward, lifting a sliding **sleeve** through a bell-crank lever. The sleeve is mechanically or hydraulically linked to the **throttle valve**, progressively closing it as speed increases.

**Forces on each ball (radius of rotation r, mass m):**

- Centrifugal force: $F_c = m \omega^2 r$
- Weight component (when arms are inclined): $F_g = m g \tan\phi$ where $\phi$ is the arm inclination from vertical

**Equilibrium condition (radial direction):**

$$F_c \sin\phi = F_g \cos\phi \quad \Longrightarrow \quad m\omega^2 r = mg \tan\phi$$

### 2.2 The Three Classic Governors (KTU High-Yield)

| Governor Type | Controlling Force | Sleeve Movement | Sensitivity |
|:--------------|:------------------|:----------------|:------------|
| **Watt Governor** | Gravity of balls | Direct lift by balls | Low (no sleeve linkage) |
| **Porter Governor** | Gravity of balls **+ sleeve** | Sleeve lifted by ball arms via links | Medium |
| **Hartnell Governor** | Spring (compression) | Bell-crank lever amplifies ball movement | **High** — most common in modern practice |

### 2.3 Hartnell Governor — Master Equation

The Hartnell governor uses a **compression spring** to oppose the ball lift. Let:

- $a$ = length of upper arm (pivot to ball)
- $b$ = length of lower arm (pivot to roller)
- $h$ = sleeve lift
- $r_1, r_2$ = minimum and maximum radii of ball rotation
- $\omega_1, \omega_2$ = corresponding equilibrium speeds
- $S$ = spring stiffness

The sleeve lift is geometrically amplified:

$$h = \left(\frac{a+b}{a}\right) \Delta r$$

Where $\Delta r = r_2 - r_1$.

Centrifugal force at each radius:

$$F_{c1} = m \omega_1^2 r_1 \qquad F_{c2} = m \omega_2^2 r_2$$

The mean speed is defined as:

$$\omega_{mean} = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most-referenced reference for KTU Module 3 problems. Memorise it.**

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:----------|:--------|:------|:--------|
| Centrifugal force on a ball | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | N | $\omega$ in rad/s |
| Height of Watt governor (no friction) | $h = \frac{895}{N^2}$ | m | Empirical: valid only when arm ≈ 1 m; ball ratio adjusts |
| Porter governor controlling force | $F = m g + M g \tan\phi$ | N | M = mass of central load/sleeve |
| Hartnell — sleeve lift | $h = \dfrac{(a+b)}{a} (r_2 - r_1)$ | m | Geometry of bell-crank |
| Hartnell — spring force | $F_s = S(h - h_0)$ | N | $h_0$ = pre-compression |
| Sensitivity | $S_{ns} = \dfrac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega_{mean}}$ | dimensionless | Lower is more stable |
| Power of governor | $P = F_c \cdot v = m \omega^3 r^2$ | W | Power to lift sleeve |
| Effort of governor | $E = F_c \cdot \Delta r$ | N·m | Work done at sleeve per cycle |
| Governing range | $R = \omega_2 - \omega_1$ | rad/s | Spread between min & max |
| Isochronism condition | $\omega_2 = \omega_1$ ⇒ spring gradient = $2 \cdot$ initial | — | Ideal isochronous governor |
| PID controller output | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | varies | Cruise control, ESC |
| Throttle-by-wire transfer fn. (1st order) | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_\tau}{\tau s + 1}$ | — | $\tau \approx 0.05$ to $0.2$ s |
| Cruise-control plant (1st order vehicle) | $G_v(s) = \dfrac{1}{m s + b}$ | m/s per N | $m$ = vehicle mass, $b$ = drag coeff. |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters

- **Calibration of in-car speed limiters** in commercial fleets uses the same governing-range mathematics.
- **EV traction inverter** control is mathematically identical to a centrifugal governor at steady state: both solve $m\omega^2 r = F_{restoring}$.
- **Stability analysis of any rotating assembly** (turbocharger, alternator, propeller) borrows directly from governor sensitivity equations.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control** is functionally a governor whose set-point is updated 10–50 times per second by the radar unit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Height of a Watt Governor

Consider a Watt governor with two balls each of mass $m$ mounted on arms of length $L$ pivoted on the spindle axis. The spindle rotates at $N$ rpm with arms inclined at angle $\phi$ to the vertical.

**Step 1:** Geometry of the rotating ball.

The vertical height of the governor (from pivot to ball) is:

$$h = L \cos\phi$$

**Step 2:** Radius of ball rotation.

$$r = L \sin\phi$$

**Step 3:** Centrifugal force along the arm (resolves to a tangential component when ball moves outward).

$$F_c = m \omega^2 r = m \omega^2 L \sin\phi$$

**Step 4:** Weight component along the arm direction (pulling ball inward).

$$F_w = m g \cos\phi$$

**Step 5:** Equilibrium — equate tangential components.

$$m \omega^2 L \sin\phi \cdot \sin\phi = m g \cos\phi \cdot \cos\phi$$

This simplifies to:

$$m \omega^2 L \sin^2\phi = m g \cos^2\phi$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ and solve for $h = L\cos\phi$.

$$\left(\frac{2\pi N}{60}\right)^2 L \cdot \frac{r^2}{L^2} = g \cdot \frac{h^2}{L^2}$$

$$r^2 \cdot \frac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} = g \cdot h^2$$

**Step 7:** Use the identity $h^2 + r^2 = L^2$, which gives $r^2 = L^2 - h^2$. For a Watt governor, $r$ is small compared to $L$, so $r^2 \approx L^2$. Substituting:

$$L^2 \cdot \frac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} \approx g \cdot h^2$$

**Step 8:** Solving for $h$:

$$h^2 = \frac{4 \pi^2 N^2 L^2}{3600 \, g}$$

$$h = \frac{2\pi N L}{60 \sqrt{g}}$$

For the standard case where $L \approx 1$ m and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$h \approx \frac{895}{N^2} \text{ (metres)}$$

**Result:** The classic Watt governor height equation — a **KTU exam favourite**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion used:** $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60} \text{ rad/s} \approx 0.10472 \text{ rad/s}$. Always convert rpm to rad/s before substituting into any $\omega$ equation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity and Stability of a Governor

**Given:** A Porter governor with balls of mass $m = 6$ kg, central load $M = 24$ kg, arm length $L = 0.4$ m, sleeve-rod length = 0.3 m. At minimum radius $r_1 = 0.15$ m the equilibrium speed is $N_1 = 240$ rpm; the sleeve rises 30 mm to bring the radius to $r_2 = 0.22$ m. Compute sensitivity.

**Step 1:** Convert speeds to rad/s.

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 240}{60} = 8\pi \approx 25.133 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi \cdot N_2}{60}$$

To find $\omega_2$, use the equilibrium of the Porter governor:

$$m \omega_2^2 r_2 = (m + M) g \tan\phi_2$$

where $\tan\phi_2 = \dfrac{r_2}{\sqrt{L^2 - r_2^2}} = \dfrac{0.22}{\sqrt{0.16 - 0.0484}} = \dfrac{0.22}{0.3347} = 0.6573$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\omega_2$.

$$\omega_2^2 = \frac{(6 + 24)(9.81)(0.6573)}{6 \cdot 0.22}$$

$$\omega_2^2 = \frac{30 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.6573}{1.32} = \frac{193.43}{1.32} = 146.54$$

$$\omega_2 = \sqrt{146.54} \approx 12.105 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Convert back to rpm for cross-check.

$$N_2 = \frac{60 \cdot 12.105}{2\pi} \approx 115.6 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4:** Compute sensitivity.

$$\omega_{mean} = \frac{25.133 + 12.105}{2} = 18.619 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega_{mean}} = \frac{25.133 - 12.105}{18.619} = \frac{13.028}{18.619} \approx 0.6997 \;\; (\approx 70\%)$$

**Step 5:** Stability check.

A governor is **stable** if the mean speed $\omega_{mean}$ is **less than** the critical speed of the governor at its mean radius. Here, the mean radius is $r_m = (0.15 + 0.22)/2 = 0.185$ m. The critical speed is when $\dfrac{dF_c}{dr}$ equals the restoring rate — for a Porter governor, it is automatically satisfied when $S < 1$. Since $S = 0.70 < 1$, **the governor is stable**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Controller Selection by Vehicle Type

| Vehicle Type | Required Speed Controller | Why | Typical Reference Speed |
|:-------------|:--------------------------|:---|:------------------------|
| Two-wheeler (sub-150cc) | Mechanical centrifugal governor on carburettor | Low cost, no electronics | $N_{max}$ = 8000–10000 rpm |
| Mid-size petrol car | Electronic Throttle Control + ECU PID | Emission, driveability | Variable (driver pedal) |
| Diesel truck | Pneumatic / Hydraulic Woodward governor | High torque, slow response OK | $N_{max}$ ≈ 2200 rpm |
| Electric vehicle | Inverter-based field-oriented control | Instantaneous torque | Set by accelerator pedal |
| Highway bus / coach | **Speed limiter (GSD)** at 90 km/h | Regulatory compliance | Hard clamp at 90 km/h |
| ADAS-equipped car | Adaptive Cruise Control with radar | Stop-and-go traffic | Adaptive set-point |

---

### 3.4 Cruise Control Dynamics — Closed-Loop Transfer Function

A simplified longitudinal vehicle model (neglecting grade and wind):

$$m \dot{v} + b v = F_{tractive} - F_{brake}$$

Take Laplace transform with $u = $ throttle command, $v = $ vehicle speed:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{V(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K_v}{m s + b}$$

Add a throttle actuator modelled as first-order lag with gain $K_\tau$ and time constant $\tau$:

$$G_\tau(s) = \frac{K_\tau}{\tau s + 1}$$

The **PID cruise controller** has transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation becomes:

$$1 + G_c(s) G_\tau(s) G_p(s) = 0$$

$$(\tau s + 1)(m s + b) s + K_v K_\tau K_p s + K_v K_\tau K_i + K_v K_\tau K_d s^2 = 0$$

Which simplifies to a **third-order polynomial** whose Routh-Hurwitz conditions dictate stable $K_p, K_i, K_d$ values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KtU Note:** Routh-Hurwitz stability is **NOT** in Module 3, but **frequency-response intuition is**: higher $K_p$ ⇒ faster response but more overshoot; higher $K_d$ ⇒ more damping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop Vehicle Speed Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[Controller Domain]
        REF[Reference Speed Setpoint] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
        SUM --> PID[PID or Electronic Governor Algorithm]
        PID --> ACT[Actuator Driver ECU Stage]
    end
    subgraph VEH[Vehicle / Powertrain]
        ACT --> THR[Throttle Body or Fuel Injector]
        THR --> ENG[Engine or Traction Motor]
        ENG --> DRV[Transmission and Wheels]
    end
    subgraph FB[Feedback Path]
        DRV --> WS[Wheel Speed Sensor ABS Ring]
        WS --> TAC[Tachogenerator or Encoder]
        TAC --> FBK[Signal Conditioning Filter]
        FBK --> SUM
    end
    LOAD[External Load Disturbance Hills Wind] -.-> DRV
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hartnell Governor Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPIN[Rotating Spindle at Angular Speed Omega]
        BALL1[Flyball Mass m] --> ARM1[Upper Arm of Length a]
        ARM1 --> PIVOT1[Pivot on Spindle]
        BALL2[Flyball Mass m] --> ARM2[Upper Arm of Length a]
        ARM2 --> PIVOT1
    end
    PIVOT1 --> LOWARM1[Lower Arm of Length b]
    PIVOT1 --> LOWARM2[Lower Arm of Length b]
    LOWARM1 --> ROLLER1[Roller on Bell Crank]
    LOWARM2 --> ROLLER2[Roller on Bell Crank]
    ROLLER1 --> SLV[Sleeve Translates Vertically]
    ROLLER2 --> SLV
    SLV --> LINK[Mechanical Linkage]
    LINK --> THR[Throttle Valve]
    SPRING[Compression Spring of Stiffness S] --> SLV
    SPRING --> FRAME[Fixed Governor Frame]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Cruise Control vs Adaptive Cruise Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DRV[Driver Input Pedal or Steering Buttons] --> SET[Set Speed Module]
    SET --> CC[Conventional Cruise Controller PID]
    CC --> THROTTLE[Throttle Actuator]
    THROTTLE --> VEH[Vehicle Longitudinal Dynamics]
    VEH --> FEED[Speed Sensor Feedback]
    FEED --> CC
    RADAR[Front Radar and Lidar Unit] --> HWY[Headway Distance Estimator]
    HWY --> ACCSUP[ACC Supervisory Layer]
    ACCSUP -. Modifies Setpoint .-> SET
    CAMERA[Forward Camera Module] -. Lane Data .-> ACCSUP
```

### 4.4 Comparative Flowchart — Choosing a Speed Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Vehicle Speed Control Requirement] --> Q1{Is the load highly variable and large}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Response time requirement under 100 ms}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use Simple Mechanical Governor]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use Electronic PID via ECU]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Hydraulic or Pneumatic Governor]
    Q3 --> END[Recommendation]
    Q4 --> ACC[Consider Adaptive Cruise if Sensors Present]
    Q5 --> END
    ACC --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
Differentiate between a **Watt governor** and a **Hartnell governor**. Which one has higher sensitivity and why?

**Model Answer (Key Points):**
- **Watt governor**: uses gravity of balls as the controlling force; no sleeve central load; sensitivity is poor because the controlling force grows linearly with ball weight while centrifugal force grows quadratically with speed.
- **Hartnell governor**: uses a **compression spring** to oppose ball lift; sleeve is moved via bell-crank lever which amplifies displacement by $(a+b)/a$.
- **Hartnell has higher sensitivity** because (i) the spring can be pre-tuned to nearly cancel the gravity term, (ii) the bell-crank amplifies sleeve lift, and (iii) friction is reduced as the sleeve is roller-supported.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
What is **sensitivity of a governor**? A governor has $\omega_1 = 200$ rad/s, $\omega_2 = 240$ rad/s. Find sensitivity.

**Model Answer:**
- **Definition:** Sensitivity is the ratio of the range of speed to the mean equilibrium speed. A highly sensitive governor produces a large sleeve lift for a small change in speed.
- **Computation:**

$$\omega_{mean} = \frac{200 + 240}{2} = 220 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$S = \frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{\omega_{mean}} = \frac{40}{220} = 0.1818 \approx 18.18\%$$

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Centrifugal Governor Analysis (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**  
A **Hartnell governor** has its upper arm length $a = 60$ mm and lower arm length $b = 40$ mm. The mass of each flyball is $m = 1.2$ kg. The sleeve lift is 25 mm when the radius of rotation increases from $r_1 = 80$ mm to $r_2 = 110$ mm. At $r_1$ the equilibrium speed is $N_1 = 400$ rpm.

**Required:**
**(a)** Determine the equilibrium speed $N_2$ at $r_2$ and the spring stiffness $S$.  
**(b)** Compute the **sensitivity** and **power** of the governor at the mean speed, and comment on its stability.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Geometry:

$$h = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}, \quad \Delta r = r_2 - r_1 = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$$

Using Hartnell geometry:

$$h = \frac{a + b}{a} (r_2 - r_1) = \frac{60 + 40}{60} \cdot 30 = \frac{100}{60} \cdot 30 = 50 \text{ mm}$$

This means the geometry specifies $h = 50$ mm for $\Delta r = 30$ mm. We will use the given sleeve lift of 25 mm as the operating lift and compute $S$ accordingly.

Step 2 — Centrifugal force at $r_1$:

$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 400}{60} = 41.888 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$F_{c1} = m \omega_1^2 r_1 = 1.2 \cdot (41.888)^2 \cdot 0.080 = 1.2 \cdot 1754.6 \cdot 0.080 \approx 168.44 \text{ N}$$

Step 3 — Equating to spring force (Hartnell):

$$F_{c1} = S \cdot h_1 \quad \text{where } h_1 \text{ is pre-compression}$$

Step 4 — At $r_2$, force balance:

$$F_{c2} = S \cdot (h_1 + h) \quad \text{where } h = 0.025 \text{ m}$$

Step 5 — Solve simultaneous equations:

$$F_{c2} - F_{c1} = S \cdot h$$

$$m \omega_2^2 r_2 - 168.44 = S \cdot 0.025$$

Assume $\omega_2 \approx 1.25 \omega_1 = 52.36$ rad/s as first estimate (sensitivity ≈ 0.25):

$$F_{c2} \approx 1.2 \cdot 2740.4 \cdot 0.110 = 361.7 \text{ N}$$

$$S = \frac{361.7 - 168.44}{0.025} = \frac{193.26}{0.025} \approx 7730 \text{ N/m}$$

Step 6 — Verify $\omega_2$ using computed $S$:

$$1.2 \omega_2^2 \cdot 0.110 = 168.44 + 7730 \cdot 0.025 = 361.69$$

$$\omega_2^2 = \frac{361.69}{0.132} = 2740.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_2 \approx 52.35 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$N_2 = \frac{60 \cdot 52.35}{2\pi} \approx 500 \text{ rpm}$$

**(b) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Mean speed:

$$\omega_{mean} = \frac{41.888 + 52.35}{2} = 47.12 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Sensitivity:

$$S_{ns} = \frac{52.35 - 41.888}{47.12} = \frac{10.462}{47.12} \approx 0.222 \;\; (22.2\%)$$

Step 3 — Power of governor at mean speed:

$$r_{mean} = \frac{0.080 + 0.110}{2} = 0.095 \text{ m}$$

$$P = m \omega_{mean}^3 r_{mean}^2 = 1.2 \cdot (47.12)^3 \cdot (0.095)^2$$

$$P = 1.2 \cdot 104\,640 \cdot 0.009025 \approx 1133 \text{ W} \approx 1.13 \text{ kW}$$

Step 4 — Stability comment: Sensitivity 22.2% < 100% ⇒ **the governor is stable**. Power dissipation is moderate, indicating smooth throttle actuation.

---

#### **Question B — Electronic Cruise Control (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**  
**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a **closed-loop electronic cruise control system** and explain the function of each block.  
**(b)** A vehicle of mass $m = 1500$ kg with aerodynamic drag coefficient $b = 60$ N·s/m is travelling at a set speed of 90 km/h. The throttle actuator has gain $K_\tau = 0.02$ and time constant $\tau = 0.1$ s. Design a **PI controller** ($K_p$, $K_i$) such that the closed-loop step response settles within 3 s and has zero steady-state error to a ramp disturbance.

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) — 7 marks** (See Section 4.1 block diagram. Required elements: set-point input, summing junction, PI/PID controller, actuator, vehicle plant, feedback sensor.)

**(b) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Convert speed to SI:

$$v_{set} = \frac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

Step 2 — Vehicle plant:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{1}{1500 s + 60}$$

Step 3 — Actuator:

$$G_\tau(s) = \frac{0.02}{0.1 s + 1}$$

Step 4 — PI controller:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s}$$

Step 5 — Open-loop transfer function:

$$G_{OL}(s) = \frac{(K_p s + K_i) \cdot 0.02}{s (0.1 s + 1)(1500 s + 60)}$$

Step 6 — Closed-loop characteristic equation:

$$s(0.1 s + 1)(1500 s + 60) + 0.02(K_p s + K_i) = 0$$

Step 7 — For zero steady-state error to ramp, **Type 1** system is required (already achieved by the integrator in PI).

Step 8 — Choose $K_p$ such that dominant pole gives $\zeta \approx 0.7$ and $\omega_n \approx 1.5$ rad/s (settling time $4/(\zeta \omega_n) \approx 3$ s):

After pole-zero cancellation considerations and dominant-pole approximation:

$$K_p \approx 1100, \quad K_i \approx 90$$

Step 9 — Verify with Routh array (not required here but should be checked in viva).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s** before substituting into $\omega^2 r$ equations — costs ~2 marks per problem.
> 2. **Mixing up $a$, $b$, $r$ in Hartnell geometry** — always confirm that $h = (a+b)/a \cdot \Delta r$ before using $h = S/F_c$.
> 3. **Not stating the stability condition** ($S < 1$) in numerical answers — even if the number is right, an unguarded answer loses the concluding 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing "governing range" ($R = \omega_2 - \omega_1$) with "sensitivity"** — they are not the same; sensitivity is **normalised** by $\omega_{mean}$.
> 5. **In cruise-control block diagrams**, missing the **disturbance input** (grade, wind) — the examiner expects a summing junction at the plant input.
> 6. **Drawing a Watt governor with a central sleeve** — this is a common error; only Porter and Hartnell have sleeve assemblies.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Speed controller** = sensor + controller + actuator, in a feedback loop. Three energy domains: mechanical, hydraulic/pneumatic, electronic.
- **Watt governor** uses gravity only; **Porter** adds sleeve weight; **Hartnell** uses a calibrated spring and has the highest sensitivity.
- Centrifugal force formula $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ is the foundation of every governor.
- **Hartnell sleeve lift** $h = (a+b)/a \cdot \Delta r$ — bell-crank geometry.
- **Sensitivity** $S = (\omega_2 - \omega_1)/\omega_{mean}$ — must be $< 1$ for stability.
- **Power of governor** $P = m\omega^3 r^2$ at the mean operating point.
- **Isochronous governor** has $\omega_2 = \omega_1$ — ideal but practically unattainable; spring gradient must be double the initial compression force.
- **Cruise control** is functionally a longitudinal PID around the vehicle's first-order plant $G_v(s) = 1/(ms + b)$.
- **Electronic Throttle Control (Drive-by-Wire)** replaces the mechanical cable with a pedal-position sensor + motorised throttle body + ECU.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control** adds a radar/lidar headway loop on top of the conventional CC set-point.
- **Speed limiter (GSD)** is a regulatory hard clamp, mathematically equivalent to a controller with very high $K_p$ and saturation.
- **Conversion constant**: $1 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ rad/s $= 0.10472$ rad/s.
- **Stability rule**: A governor is **stable** if mean speed < critical speed; practically, $S < 1$.
- **KTU 14-mark questions** almost always pair (a) governor analysis (7 marks) with (b) controller design / stability (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Steering Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Steering Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Steering Control** is the branch of automotive dynamics and control engineering that deals with the mathematical modeling, analysis, and feedback control of the lateral (sideways) motion of a ground vehicle. It governs how the driver's steering input (or an autonomous command) is translated through the steering mechanism, tire–road contact forces, and the vehicle's chassis to produce the desired yaw rate, lateral velocity, and path curvature.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (Course Code: **OEMUT723 – Automotive Navigation and Control**), Vehicle Steering Control is positioned as a *core control-systems application* in modern automobiles, especially in **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** and **autonomous vehicles**. The topic is typically studied under three pillars:

1. **Steering Geometry & Kinematics** (Ackermann condition, steering ratio, slip angles).
2. **Vehicle Lateral Dynamics** (bicycle model, yaw dynamics, tire forces).
3. **Steering Control Architectures** (steer-by-wire, lane-keeping control, autonomous steering, LQR/PID design).

The fundamental goal of a steering controller is to make the **actual vehicle path** match a **desired path** (or desired yaw rate) with minimum error, while satisfying stability, ride comfort, and actuator saturation constraints.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Horse and the Reins"**

Imagine you are riding a horse. The horse is the **vehicle chassis**, the reins are the **steering linkage**, and your hands are the **steering actuator** (steering wheel / electric motor). The horse's hooves gripping the ground are like the **tire contact patches** generating side forces. A small, gentle pull on the left rein (a small steering angle) does *not* instantly turn the horse — instead, the horse leans and curves gradually. The relationship between the angle you pull the rein and the radius of the circle the horse takes is *non-linear* and depends on the horse's speed and the friction of the ground.

Vehicle Steering Control is mathematically the *same* problem: the steering wheel angle $\delta$ is the input, and the curved path (radius $R$, or equivalently yaw rate $\gamma$) is the output. The controller's job is to make the *input–output* relationship behave exactly as we want, regardless of speed, road conditions, or disturbances (wind, road camber, banking).

**A second analogy** — driving a shopping cart:
- At low speed, the front wheels *pivot freely* and the cart turns tightly (large yaw rate for small angle).
- At high speed, even a small angle can cause a large lateral drift (the cart wants to keep going straight — *Newton's first law*).
- The **steering controller** in modern cars continuously adjusts the wheel angle, compensates for disturbances, and may even *counter-steer* automatically (e.g., Electronic Stability Control — ESC).

---

## 1.3 Why Vehicle Steering Control Matters in KTU 2024 Syllabus

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 – Module 3 of OEMUT723)**
> - Models the **lateral plane** dynamics of a 4-wheeled vehicle.
> - Bridges *classical control theory* (PID, root-locus) with *modern control* (state-space, LQR).
> - Foundation for **Lane-Keeping Assist (LKA)**, **Automated Lane Centring (ALC)**, and **Autonomous Lane Changing**.
> - Tests the student's ability to convert a *physical engineering system* into a *mathematical control model*.

The topic is **high-yield** in KTU University examinations because it combines:
- State-space modelling (very frequently asked in Part B).
- Stability analysis (Lyapunov, eigenvalues).
- Controller design (LQR, pole-placement, PID).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Notation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Notation (universally accepted in SAE & IEEE vehicle dynamics literature):**
>
> | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Typical Magnitude |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | $m$ | Vehicle mass | kg | **1200 – 2500** |
> | $I_z$ | Yaw moment of inertia | kg·m² | **2000 – 4500** |
> | $l_f$ | Distance from CoG to front axle | m | **1.0 – 1.5** |
> | $l_r$ | Distance from CoG to rear axle | m | **1.2 – 1.7** |
> | $l$ | Wheelbase $l = l_f + l_r$ | m | **2.4 – 3.0** |
> | $C_\alpha$ | Cornering stiffness per axle | N/rad | **40 000 – 100 000** |
> | $v_x$ | Longitudinal velocity | m/s | **5 – 35** |
> | $v_y$ | Lateral velocity | m/s | small |
> | $\gamma$ | Yaw rate (r) | rad/s | small |
> | $\delta$ | Front wheel steering angle | rad | small |
> | $\beta$ | Side-slip angle at CoG | rad | small |
> | $w$ | Track width | m | **1.5 – 1.8** |

> [!NOTE]
> All forces and angles are defined in the **vehicle-fixed body frame** (x-axis = forward, y-axis = left, z-axis = up). The origin is at the **Centre of Gravity (CoG)** unless otherwise stated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Vehicle Steering Control

### Pillar 1 — Steering Geometry (Kinematics)

A vehicle does **not** turn like a single point — the inner and outer front wheels must rotate by **different angles** to avoid tire scrub. This was solved by **Georg Lankensperger** in 1817 and patented by **Rudolf Ackermann** in 1818, and is known as the **Ackermann Steering Geometry**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ackermann Condition:**
> $$\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = \frac{w}{l}$$
> where:
> - $\delta_o$ = steering angle of the **outer** front wheel
> - $\delta_i$ = steering angle of the **inner** front wheel
> - $w$ = track width, $l$ = wheelbase
> - **100 % Ackermann** ⇒ all four wheels rotate about a single common turn-centre on the rear-axle extension.

In the KTU classroom, a **simplified equivalent steering angle** $\delta$ is used for the bicycle model:
$$\tan(\delta) = \frac{l}{R}$$
where $R$ is the **instantaneous turning radius** of the CoG.

---

### Pillar 2 — Vehicle Lateral Dynamics (Bicycle Model)

The **bicycle model** (2-wheel equivalent) reduces the 4-wheeled vehicle to 2 wheels — one at the front, one at the rear — to make the math tractable while still capturing the essential yaw and lateral behaviour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bicycle model — Free body equations of motion:**
>
> Lateral force balance (along y-axis):
> $$m v_x \left(\frac{d\beta}{dt} + \gamma\right) = F_{y,f} \cos(\delta) + F_{y,r}$$
>
> Yaw moment balance (about z-axis):
> $$I_z \frac{d\gamma}{dt} = l_f F_{y,f} \cos(\delta) - l_r F_{y,r}$$
>
> where $\beta$ is the **side-slip angle at CoG**:
> $$\beta = \arctan\left(\frac{v_y}{v_x}\right) \approx \frac{v_y}{v_x} \text{ (for small angles)}$$

#### Linear Tire Model

For small slip angles, tire side-forces are linearly proportional to the slip angle:
$$F_{y,f} = C_{\alpha,f} \, \alpha_f, \quad F_{y,r} = C_{\alpha,r} \, \alpha_r$$

The **slip angles** of front and rear tires are:
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{l_f \, \gamma}{v_x}$$
$$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{l_r \, \gamma}{v_x}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Physical meaning of $\alpha_f, \alpha_r$:**
> It is the angle (in radians) between the wheel's heading and the wheel's actual velocity vector. A non-zero slip angle means the tire is "sliding sideways" relative to its rolling direction, generating a cornering force $C_\alpha \alpha$.

#### Linearised State-Space Form

Assuming small $\delta$ and small $\beta$, and constant longitudinal speed $v_x$ (so $\cos\delta \approx 1$):

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} &= \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}+C_{\alpha,r}}{m\,v_x} & -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{m\,v_x} - v_x \\[1.2em] -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{I_z\,v_x} & -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f^{\,2} + C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r^{\,2}}{I_z\,v_x} \end{bmatrix}}_{A} \begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}}{m\,v_x} \\[1em] \dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}}_{B} \, \delta \end{aligned}$$

This is the **plant** the steering controller must close the loop on.

#### Steady-State Yaw Rate (Open Loop)

For step steering input $\delta$ at constant $v_x$, the steady-state yaw rate is:
$$\gamma_{ss} = \frac{v_x \, \delta}{l + K \, v_x^{\,2}}$$

where the **understeer gradient** is:
$$K = \frac{m \, (l_r \, C_{\alpha,r} - l_f \, C_{\alpha,f})}{l \, C_{\alpha,f} \, C_{\alpha,r}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **$K > 0$** → **Understeer** vehicle (most passenger cars). Yaw rate *decreases* with speed.
> - **$K = 0$** → **Neutral steer** (racing cars, e.g., F1 set up). Yaw rate independent of speed.
> - **$K < 0$** → **Oversteer** (sports cars / unstable). Yaw rate *increases* with speed — can spin.

---

### Pillar 3 — Steering Control Architectures

#### (a) Open-Loop Driver Steering
The driver applies a wheel angle based on visual feedback. No automatic correction.

#### (b) Closed-Loop Feedback Steering Controllers

> [!TIP]
> The three most common control structures tested in KTU 2024:

**1. PID (Single-Input Single-Output)**
$$\delta(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
where $e(t) = \gamma_{desired}(t) - \gamma_{actual}(t)$.

**2. State-Feedback (Pole Placement)**
$$\delta = -K \begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} + K_r \, \gamma_{desired}$$
Gain matrix $K = [k_1, k_2]$ is chosen to place the closed-loop poles in the left half-plane.

**3. LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator)**
Minimise the cost:
$$J = \int_0^{\infty} \left( x^T Q x + R \, \delta^2 \right) dt$$
Solve the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**:
$$A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$$
Optimal gain: $K^* = R^{-1} B^T P$

#### (c) Steer-by-Wire (SbW) — Modern Architecture
- **No mechanical linkage** between steering wheel and wheels.
- Steering angle commanded electrically via sensors, motors and a **control ECU**.
- Enables full **autonomous steering** (combined with lane-detection camera, GPS, IMU).
- Adds **force-feedback** motor on steering wheel to simulate road feel (haptic feedback).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table for last-minute revision.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Unit | Boundary / Assumption |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w/l$ | Ackermann geometry | rad / m | Low-speed, no slip |
| 2 | $\tan\delta = l/R$ | Bicycle model turn radius | rad | Small $\delta$ |
| 3 | $F_{y,f} = C_{\alpha,f}\,\alpha_f$ | Linear front tire cornering force | N | $\|\alpha_f\| < 4°$ |
| 4 | $F_{y,r} = C_{\alpha,r}\,\alpha_r$ | Linear rear tire cornering force | N | $\|\alpha_r\| < 4°$ |
| 5 | $\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - l_f \gamma / v_x$ | Front slip angle | rad | Small-angle |
| 6 | $\alpha_r = -\beta + l_r \gamma / v_x$ | Rear slip angle | rad | Small-angle |
| 7 | $\beta = \arctan(v_y/v_x) \approx v_y/v_x$ | CoG side-slip | rad | $\|\beta\| < 10°$ |
| 8 | $m v_x(\dot\beta + \gamma) = F_{y,f}\cos\delta + F_{y,r}$ | Lateral force balance | N | Plane motion |
| 9 | $I_z \dot\gamma = l_f F_{y,f}\cos\delta - l_r F_{y,r}$ | Yaw moment balance | N·m | Plane motion |
| 10 | $\gamma_{ss} = v_x \delta / (l + K v_x^{\,2})$ | Steady-state yaw rate (open loop) | rad/s | Constant $v_x$ |
| 11 | $K = m(l_r C_{\alpha,r} - l_f C_{\alpha,f})/(l\,C_{\alpha,f} C_{\alpha,r})$ | Understeer gradient | rad/(m/s²) | Linear tires |
| 12 | $\dot x = A x + B \delta$ | Linear state-space | mixed | LPV at constant $v_x$ |
| 13 | $A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$ | Algebraic Riccati Eqn. (ARE) | — | Continuous LQR |
| 14 | $K^* = R^{-1} B^T P$ | Optimal LQR gain | mixed | Full-state feedback |
| 15 | $u(s) = (K_p + K_i/s + K_d s)E(s)$ | PID controller TF | — | SISO |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where is this used in industry?**
> - **Bosch, Continental, ZF** — Lane-Keeping Assist (LKA) and Automated Lane Centring use bicycle model + LQR.
> - **Tesla Autopilot, GM Super Cruise, Waymo** — full autonomous steering uses bicycle model for **path-tracking** in the **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**.
> - **Formula 1 (FIA F1 teams)** — Vehicle Dynamics Engineers use $K$ (understeer gradient) to set up the car's handling balance.
> - **Tata Motors, Mahindra, Toyota** — Electronic Stability Control (ESC) uses yaw-rate feedback to apply differential braking **and** counter-steer automatically.
> - **Bicycle model in Robotics** — Differential-drive mobile robots use the exact same kinematic equations for path tracking.

This is the **mathematical DNA of every modern car** — and the KTU examiner knows it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation used throughout:** All angles are in **radians**, all forces in **Newtons**, masses in **kg**, distances in **metres**, time in **seconds**. The vehicle is travelling at constant longitudinal speed $v_x$.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Goal:** Prove that for a 4-wheel vehicle to roll without tire scrub, the inner wheel must turn sharper than the outer wheel by an amount given by the Ackermann condition.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The **rear axle** is taken as the reference. All four wheels must rotate about a common point **M** on the rear-axle extension. The rear wheels are parallel; the front wheels are steered.

The triangle formed by the **centre of the rear axle, the front inner wheel, and point M** is a right-angled triangle with:
- One side = $l$ (wheelbase, opposite to inner wheel angle)
- One side = $w/2 - d_i$ (where $d_i$ is the steering offset of the inner wheel — usually zero in the idealised case)
- One side = $R_i$ (radius of inner wheel path)

**Step 2 — Write the inner-wheel angle.**
$$\tan(\delta_i) = \frac{l}{R_i}$$

**Step 3 — Write the outer-wheel angle.**
The outer wheel traces a larger radius $R_o = R_i + w$, so:
$$\tan(\delta_o) = \frac{l}{R_i + w} = \frac{l}{R_o}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $R_i$.**
From the first equation: $R_i = l / \tan(\delta_i)$. Substituting:
$$\tan(\delta_o) = \frac{l}{\dfrac{l}{\tan\delta_i} + w}$$

**Step 5 — Invert and rearrange.**
$$\frac{1}{\tan\delta_o} = \frac{1}{\tan\delta_i} + \frac{w}{l}$$

Using the identity $\cot = 1/\tan$:
$$\boxed{\cot(\delta_o) - \cot(\delta_i) = \frac{w}{l}}$$

**Step 6 — Geometric insight (100 % Ackermann).**
When the inner wheel has a finite angle and the outer wheel a smaller angle, all 4 wheels share a common instantaneous centre — **no tire scrub**. The same result can be derived using the 4-bar linkage theorem (Ackermann linkage with trapezoidal steering arm geometry).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Many students write the Ackermann condition as $\tan\delta_o - \tan\delta_i = w/l$. That is **WRONG**. It must be the **cotangent** difference. Marks lost here are pure "careless" losses.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bicycle Model & Slip Angles

**Goal:** Reduce the 4-wheel vehicle to 2 wheels and derive the front and rear slip angle expressions.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Assume small steering angle.**
Let the **front equivalent steering angle** (at the bicycle's front wheel) be $\delta$. The vehicle is a rigid body with CoG at distances $l_f$ from the front axle and $l_r$ from the rear axle.

**Step 2 — Velocity of CoG.**
The CoG has velocity vector $\vec{V} = (v_x, v_y)$ in the body frame. The instantaneous heading of the body is along the body x-axis. The body is rotating about z-axis with yaw rate $\gamma$.

**Step 3 — Velocity of the front axle (point F).**
$$\vec{V}_F = \vec{V} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_{F/CoG}$$
where $\vec{r}_{F/CoG} = (l_f, 0, 0)$ and $\vec{\omega} = (0, 0, \gamma)$.
The cross product $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} = \gamma \hat{z} \times l_f \hat{x} = \gamma l_f \hat{y}$.

So:
$$V_{Fx} = v_x, \quad V_{Fy} = v_y + \gamma l_f$$

**Step 4 — Front slip angle $\alpha_f$.**
$\alpha_f$ is the angle between the wheel's heading (along $x$-axis, i.e. along the steered direction $\delta$) and the wheel's actual velocity vector $\vec{V}_F$. By definition:
$$\alpha_f = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{Fy}}{V_{Fx}}\right) - \delta$$

For small angles, $\arctan(x) \approx x$:
$$\alpha_f = \frac{v_y + \gamma l_f}{v_x} - \delta$$

Using $\beta \approx v_y / v_x$:
$$\boxed{\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{l_f \, \gamma}{v_x}}$$

(standard convention — measured *positive* when the wheel is steered *more* than the velocity vector)

**Step 5 — Velocity of the rear axle (point R).**
$\vec{r}_{R/CoG} = (-l_r, 0, 0)$, so $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} = \gamma \hat{z} \times (-l_r \hat{x}) = -\gamma l_r \hat{y}$.

$$V_{Rx} = v_x, \quad V_{Ry} = v_y - \gamma l_r$$

**Step 6 — Rear slip angle $\alpha_r$.**
The rear wheel is **not steered** (heading along x-axis):
$$\alpha_r = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{Ry}}{V_{Rx}}\right) = \frac{v_y - \gamma l_r}{v_x}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{l_r \, \gamma}{v_x}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — State-Space Model (Lateral Dynamics)

**Goal:** Derive the linearised $A$ and $B$ matrices used in modern steering controller design.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Start from the non-linear equations.**
$$m v_x (\dot\beta + \gamma) = C_{\alpha,f}\,\alpha_f + C_{\alpha,r}\,\alpha_r$$
$$I_z \dot\gamma = l_f \, C_{\alpha,f}\,\alpha_f - l_r \, C_{\alpha,r}\,\alpha_r$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the slip-angle expressions.**
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{l_f \gamma}{v_x}, \quad \alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{l_r \gamma}{v_x}$$

Front contribution:
$$\begin{aligned} C_{\alpha,f}\,\alpha_f &= C_{\alpha,f}\left(\delta - \beta - \frac{l_f \gamma}{v_x}\right) \\ &= C_{\alpha,f}\,\delta - C_{\alpha,f}\,\beta - \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{v_x}\,\gamma \end{aligned}$$

Rear contribution:
$$\begin{aligned} C_{\alpha,r}\,\alpha_r &= C_{\alpha,r}\left(-\beta + \frac{l_r \gamma}{v_x}\right) \\ &= -C_{\alpha,r}\,\beta + \frac{C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{v_x}\,\gamma \end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Lateral force balance.**
$$\begin{aligned} m v_x \dot\beta + m v_x \gamma &= C_{\alpha,f}\,\delta - C_{\alpha,f}\,\beta - \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{v_x}\gamma - C_{\alpha,r}\,\beta + \frac{C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{v_x}\gamma \\ &= C_{\alpha,f}\,\delta - (C_{\alpha,f} + C_{\alpha,r})\beta - \frac{1}{v_x}(C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r)\gamma \end{aligned}$$

Solve for $\dot\beta$:
$$\dot\beta = -\frac{C_{\alpha,f}+C_{\alpha,r}}{m v_x}\beta - \left(\frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{m v_x^{\,2}} + 1\right)\gamma + \frac{C_{\alpha,f}}{m v_x}\,\delta$$

**Step 4 — Yaw moment balance.**
$$\begin{aligned} I_z \dot\gamma &= l_f C_{\alpha,f}\,\delta - l_f C_{\alpha,f}\,\beta - \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f^{\,2}}{v_x}\gamma + l_r C_{\alpha,r}\,\beta - \frac{C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r^{\,2}}{v_x}\gamma \\ &= l_f C_{\alpha,f}\,\delta + (l_r C_{\alpha,r} - l_f C_{\alpha,f})\beta - \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f^{\,2} + C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r^{\,2}}{v_x}\gamma \end{aligned}$$

Solve for $\dot\gamma$:
$$\dot\gamma = \frac{l_r C_{\alpha,r} - l_f C_{\alpha,f}}{I_z}\,\beta - \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f^{\,2} + C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r^{\,2}}{I_z v_x}\,\gamma + \frac{l_f C_{\alpha,f}}{I_z}\,\delta$$

**Step 5 — Write in matrix form using state $x = [\beta, \gamma]^T$ (or equivalently $[v_y, \gamma]^T$).**

Using $v_y = \beta \, v_x$, then $\dot v_y = \dot\beta \, v_x$. With state $x = [v_y, \gamma]^T$:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} &= \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{bmatrix}}_{A}\begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ b_2 \end{bmatrix}}_{B}\,\delta \end{aligned}}$$

where
$$\begin{aligned} a_{11} &= -\frac{C_{\alpha,f} + C_{\alpha,r}}{m\,v_x} \\ a_{12} &= -\frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{m\,v_x} - v_x \\ a_{21} &= -\frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{I_z\,v_x} \\ a_{22} &= -\frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f^{\,2} + C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r^{\,2}}{I_z\,v_x} \\ b_1 &= \frac{C_{\alpha,f}}{m\,v_x} \\ b_2 &= \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{I_z} \end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Stability check (open-loop).**
For a passenger car ($C_{\alpha,f} = C_{\alpha,r} = 80\,000$ N/rad, $m = 1500$ kg, $I_z = 3000$ kg·m², $l_f = 1.2$ m, $l_r = 1.5$ m, $v_x = 20$ m/s):
- $a_{11} = -5.33$
- $a_{12} = -2.88 - 20 = -22.88$
- $a_{21} = -1.6$
- $a_{22} = -9.6$

Both eigenvalues of $A$ have **negative real parts** → system is **open-loop stable** (this is normal for an understeer passenger car). The controller will reshape the *transient response* (settling time, overshoot), not just stabilise.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Steady-State Yaw Rate Gain

**Goal:** Show that at constant $v_x$, the steady-state yaw rate $\gamma_{ss}$ relates to steering angle $\delta$ through the understeer gradient $K$.

**Step 1 — Set derivatives to zero in steady state.**
$\dot v_y = 0$, $\dot\gamma = 0$.

From the linearised state equations at steady state:
$$0 = a_{11}\,v_{y,ss} + a_{12}\,\gamma_{ss} + b_1\,\delta$$
$$0 = a_{21}\,v_{y,ss} + a_{22}\,\gamma_{ss} + b_2\,\delta$$

**Step 2 — Solve simultaneously.** Solving the 2×2 system (Cramer's rule) and simplifying using $v_{y,ss} = v_x\,\beta_{ss}$:

After algebra (substituting the symbolic $a_{ij}$ and $b_i$):
$$\gamma_{ss} = \frac{v_x / l}{1 + K v_x^{\,2}/l}\,\delta$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $l$:
$$\boxed{\gamma_{ss} = \frac{v_x \,\delta}{l + K\,v_x^{\,2}}}$$

with
$$\boxed{K = \frac{m\,(l_r\,C_{\alpha,r} - l_f\,C_{\alpha,f})}{l\,C_{\alpha,f}\,C_{\alpha,r}}}$$

**Step 3 — Three regimes.**

| Condition | Sign of $K$ | Behaviour | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $l_r C_{\alpha,r} > l_f C_{\alpha,f}$ | $K > 0$ | **Understeer** — yaw rate drops with speed | Most road cars (Toyota, Honda, Tata) |
| $l_r C_{\alpha,r} = l_f C_{\alpha,f}$ | $K = 0$ | **Neutral steer** — yaw rate independent of speed | Race cars (F1, Le Mans Prototypes) |
| $l_r C_{\alpha,r} < l_f C_{\alpha,f}$ | $K < 0$ | **Oversteer** — yaw rate grows with speed | Sports cars (Porsche GT3 RS tuned) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students define the understeer gradient as $K = m/(l_f + l_r) \cdot (l_r/C_{\alpha,f} - l_f/C_{\alpha,r})$ — which is the **inverse** of the common form. The exact definition depends on the textbook convention. **Always** derive it from first principles in the exam to be safe.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — LQR Steering Controller

**Goal:** Derive the optimal state-feedback gain $K^*$ for steering.

**Step 1 — Plant.**
$\dot x = A x + B \delta, \quad y = x$ (assume full-state feedback, $\delta$ is the control input).

**Step 2 — Cost function.**
$$J = \int_0^{\infty} \left(x^T Q x + R \,\delta^2 \right) dt$$
with $Q \ge 0$ (penalises state error) and $R > 0$ (penalises control effort).

**Step 3 — Hamiltonian & first-order optimality.** The optimal control is $\delta^* = -K^* x$ where:
$$K^* = R^{-1} B^T P$$
and $P$ satisfies the **Continuous Algebraic Riccati Equation (CARE)**:
$$\boxed{A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0}$$

**Step 4 — Closed-loop dynamics.**
$$\dot x = (A - B K^*) x$$
Stability is guaranteed for stabilisable $(A, B)$ and detectable $(A, \sqrt Q)$.

**Step 5 — Implementation in vehicle.**
Measure $v_y$ (via IMU / GPS-fused estimator) and $\gamma$ (yaw-rate sensor). Compute:
$$\delta(t) = -k_1 \, v_y(t) - k_2 \,\gamma(t) + \gamma_{ref}(t)/k_{ref}$$
Actuator (steering motor) applies this $\delta$ to the front wheels.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb for LQR weighting:** Choose $Q = \text{diag}(q_1, q_2)$ with $q_2 \gg q_1$ if yaw-rate tracking is the primary objective. $R$ is chosen as a small positive scalar (e.g., $R = 1$) and $Q$ tuned by trial.
