# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Part-A pattern:** 2-mark short answer, 3-mark short derivation. Below are 3-mark style questions.

---

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the Ackermann steering condition. Why is it important in a 4-wheeled vehicle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ackermann steering geometry** is the condition that ensures all four wheels of a turning vehicle rotate about a **common instantaneous centre** on the rear-axle extension, so that none of the tires experience lateral scrub during cornering.

It is mathematically stated as:
$$\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = \frac{w}{l}$$

where $\delta_o, \delta_i$ are the **outer** and **inner** front wheel steer angles, $w$ is the track width and $l$ is the wheelbase.

**Importance:**
- Eliminates tire scrub → reduces tire wear (1 mark).
- Improves cornering precision and roll stability (1 mark).
- Forms the kinematic basis of the steering linkage design (trapezoidal arm). (1 mark)

*(Full marks = 3)*

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is the understeer gradient $K$? How is a vehicle classified as understeer, neutral steer, or oversteer based on $K$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **understeer gradient** $K$ quantifies how the steady-state yaw rate of a vehicle changes with longitudinal speed for a given steering angle. It is defined as:
$$K = \frac{m\,(l_r\,C_{\alpha,r} - l_f\,C_{\alpha,f})}{l\,C_{\alpha,f}\,C_{\alpha,r}}$$

**Classification** (based on the steady-state yaw-rate gain $\gamma_{ss}/\delta = v_x / (l + K v_x^{\,2})$):
- $K > 0$ → **Understeer** (yaw rate falls with speed) — typical road cars.
- $K = 0$ → **Neutral steer** (yaw rate constant) — race cars.
- $K < 0$ → **Oversteer** (yaw rate rises with speed) — prone to spin. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Part-B pattern:** Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Two choices provided. Below are 2 fully-solved 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam – Module 3, OEMUT723]**

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the linearised state-space model of a vehicle's lateral dynamics using the bicycle model. List the $A$ and $B$ matrices with all elements.

**Solution:**

We start with the bicycle model: a 2-wheel equivalent vehicle with front steering angle $\delta$, lateral velocity $v_y$ at the centre of gravity, and yaw rate $\gamma$.

**Step 1 — Slip angles** (1 mark):
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \frac{v_y + l_f \gamma}{v_x}, \quad \alpha_r = -\frac{v_y - l_r \gamma}{v_x}$$

**Step 2 — Linear tire model** (1 mark):
$$F_{y,f} = C_{\alpha,f}\,\alpha_f, \quad F_{y,r} = C_{\alpha,r}\,\alpha_r$$

**Step 3 — Equations of motion** (2 marks):
$$m \dot v_y - m v_x \gamma = F_{y,f} + F_{y,r}, \quad I_z \dot\gamma = l_f F_{y,f} - l_r F_{y,r}$$

**Step 4 — Linearisation for small angles** (1 mark) and substitution of slip angles into the equations of motion, then solving for $\dot v_y$ and $\dot\gamma$:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot v_y &= -\frac{C_{\alpha,f}+C_{\alpha,r}}{m\,v_x}\,v_y + \left(\frac{C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r - C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{m\,v_x} - v_x\right)\gamma + \frac{C_{\alpha,f}}{m\,v_x}\,\delta \\ \dot\gamma &= \frac{C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r - C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{I_z\,v_x}\,v_y - \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f^{\,2} + C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r^{\,2}}{I_z\,v_x}\,\gamma + \frac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{I_z}\,\delta \end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Matrix form** (2 marks):
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot v_y \\ \dot\gamma \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}+C_{\alpha,r}}{m\,v_x} & -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{m\,v_x} - v_x \\[1.2em] -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f - C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r}{I_z\,v_x} & -\dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f^{\,2} + C_{\alpha,r}\,l_r^{\,2}}{I_z\,v_x} \end{bmatrix}}_{A}\begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ \gamma \end{bmatrix} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}}{m\,v_x} \\[1em] \dfrac{C_{\alpha,f}\,l_f}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}}_{B}\,\delta$$

State vector: $x = [v_y,\; \gamma]^T$, control input: $u = \delta$.

---

#### **(b) [7 marks]** For the state-space model in part (a), design an LQR state-feedback steering controller. State the cost function and the Riccati equation, and explain how the gain $K^*$ is implemented in the vehicle.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cost function** (2 marks):
The LQR controller minimises the infinite-horizon quadratic cost:
$$J = \int_0^{\infty} \left( x^T Q x + R\,\delta^2 \right) dt$$
where $Q \ge 0$ is a symmetric positive-semi-definite matrix penalising state error, and $R > 0$ penalises control effort. A common choice is $Q = \text{diag}(q_1, q_2)$ with $q_2 \gg q_1$ (prioritise yaw-rate tracking) and $R = 1$.

**Step 2 — Algebraic Riccati equation** (2 marks):
The optimal feedback law is $\delta = -K^* x$ where:
$$K^* = R^{-1} B^T P$$
and $P$ is the unique positive-definite solution of:
$$A^T P + P A - P B R^{-1} B^T P + Q = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solution procedure** (1 mark):
1. Solve the ARE for $P$ (using `lqr` command in MATLAB / Python `scipy.linalg.solve_continuous_are`).
2. Compute $K^* = R^{-1} B^T P$.
3. Verify closed-loop stability: all eigenvalues of $(A - B K^*)$ must have negative real parts.

**Step 4 — Hardware implementation** (2 marks):
- **Sensors:** IMU measures $\gamma$; GPS-fused estimator computes $v_y$ (or use a Kalman Filter).
- **ECU:** Computes $\delta_{cmd} = -K^* \hat x + \delta_{ref}$ at 100 Hz.
- **Actuator:** A brushless DC motor in the Steer-by-Wire column applies the commanded $\delta$ to the front-wheel pinion.
- **Reference input:** $\delta_{ref}$ is obtained from the driver's steering wheel OR the autonomous planner's desired yaw rate.

---

### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam – Module 3, OEMUT723 — Alternative Choice]**

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Define the slip angles of the front and rear tires. Derive the steady-state yaw rate gain of a vehicle as a function of the steering angle $\delta$, longitudinal speed $v_x$, and understeer gradient $K$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Slip angle definition** (1 mark):
The **tire slip angle** $\alpha$ is the angle (in radians) between the wheel's heading direction and the actual velocity vector of the wheel's contact patch.

**Step 2 — Bicycle model slip angles** (1 mark):
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \frac{v_y + l_f \gamma}{v_x}, \quad \alpha_r = -\frac{v_y - l_r \gamma}{v_x}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state assumption** (1 mark):
At steady state, $\dot v_y = 0$ and $\dot\gamma = 0$. Substituting the linearised state equations and using $v_{y,ss} = v_x\,\beta_{ss}$:

$$0 = a_{11}\,\beta_{ss} + a_{12}\,\gamma_{ss} + b_1\,\delta$$
$$0 = a_{21}\,\beta_{ss} + a_{22}\,\gamma_{ss} + b_2\,\delta$$

**Step 4 — Solve the 2×2 linear system** (2 marks) for $\gamma_{ss}$. After algebraic elimination (Cramer's rule) of $\beta_{ss}$:

$$\gamma_{ss} = \frac{b_2 a_{11} - b_1 a_{21}}{a_{22} a_{11} - a_{12} a_{21}}\,\delta$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the symbolic $a_{ij}, b_i$** and simplify using the identity $l_f + l_r = l$:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{ss} = \frac{v_x}{l + K\,v_x^{\,2}}\,\delta}$$

where the **understeer gradient** is:
$$\boxed{K = \frac{m\,(l_r\,C_{\alpha,r} - l_f\,C_{\alpha,f})}{l\,C_{\alpha,f}\,C_{\alpha,r}}}$$

(2 marks for the boxed expressions.)

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation** (1 mark — extra credit by KTU examiners):
- Low speed ($v_x \to 0$): $\gamma_{ss} \to v_x \delta / l$ — the **Ackermann kinematic** limit.
- High speed: if $K > 0$, $\gamma_{ss}$ falls below the kinematic value (understeer). If $K < 0$, $\gamma_{ss}$ grows (oversteer, unstable).

---

#### **(b) [7 marks]** A passenger car has the following data: $m = 1500$ kg, $I_z = 3000$ kg·m², $l_f = 1.2$ m, $l_r = 1.5$ m, $C_{\alpha,f} = 80\,000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha,r} = 90\,000$ N/rad, $v_x = 25$ m/s. Calculate (i) the understeer gradient $K$, (ii) the steady-state yaw rate for $\delta = 2°$, and (iii) the turning radius $R$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
$\delta = 2° = 0.0349$ rad, $l = l_f + l_r = 1.2 + 1.5 = 2.7$ m.

**Step 2 — Compute $K$** (3 marks):
$$\begin{aligned} K &= \frac{m\,(l_r\,C_{\alpha,r} - l_f\,C_{\alpha,f})}{l\,C_{\alpha,f}\,C_{\alpha,r}} \\ &= \frac{1500 \times (1.5 \times 90\,000 - 1.2 \times 80\,000)}{2.7 \times 80\,000 \times 90\,000} \\ &= \frac{1500 \times (135\,000 - 96\,000)}{2.7 \times 7.2 \times 10^9} \\ &= \frac{1500 \times 39\,000}{1.944 \times 10^{13}} \\ &= \frac{5.85 \times 10^7}{1.944 \times 10^{13}} \\ &= 3.009 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{rad/(m/s)}^2 \end{aligned}$$

So $K \approx 3.01 \times 10^{-6}$ rad·s²/m². This is **positive** → the vehicle is **understeer**.

**Step 3 — Compute $\gamma_{ss}$** (2 marks):
$$\begin{aligned} \gamma_{ss} &= \frac{v_x \,\delta}{l + K v_x^{\,2}} \\ &= \frac{25 \times 0.0349}{2.7 + 3.009 \times 10^{-6} \times 625} \\ &= \frac{0.8725}{2.7 + 0.00188} \\ &= \frac{0.8725}{2.70188} \\ &= 0.3229\;\text{rad/s} \end{aligned}$$

So $\gamma_{ss} \approx 0.323$ rad/s $\approx 18.5°/$s.

**Step 4 — Compute turning radius $R$** (2 marks):
$$\begin{aligned} R &= \frac{v_x}{\gamma_{ss}} \\ &= \frac{25}{0.3229} \\ &= 77.42\;\text{m} \end{aligned}$$

So the vehicle traces a circle of radius approximately **77.4 m** at 25 m/s with 2° steering input.

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** The kinematic (low-speed) radius would be $l/\tan\delta \approx 2.7/0.0349 = 77.36$ m. The understeer term $K v_x^2$ is tiny (0.00188) at 25 m/s — confirming that at moderate highway speeds, the understeer effect is small but non-negligible. At $v_x = 60$ m/s, $K v_x^2 \approx 0.011$, and the radius grows to ~79 m, demonstrating understeer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign of slip angles:** Many students write $\alpha_r = +\beta - l_r \gamma / v_x$ — the sign convention depends on the chosen body frame. KTU examiners expect the **standard ISO sign convention** (positive yaw rate = turning left, positive $\alpha$ = tire slip in the direction of positive y). Get it right the **first** time.
> 2. **Ackermann vs. cotangent:** The Ackermann condition uses **cotangent**, **not tangent**. Marks are routinely lost here.
> 3. **Units of $K$:** $K$ has units of **rad·s²/m²** (or equivalent). In SI, $K$ is often quoted in **°/g** where $g$ is gravitational acceleration. Confusing these is a common error.
> 4. **State vector choice:** KTU examiners accept both $x = [v_y, \gamma]^T$ and $x = [\beta, \gamma]^T$. However, the $A$ and $B$ matrices **change** accordingly. State your choice explicitly.
> 5. **B vs. D in matrix form:** Some students use $u(t)$ for control input, others use $\delta(t)$. Use $\delta$ since it is the standard in the syllabus.
> 6. **LQR — never skip the ARE:** Writing $K = R^{-1}B^TP$ without stating the ARE loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section the night before the exam.**

### A. Steering Geometry
- ☐ Ackermann: $\cot\delta_o - \cot\delta_i = w/l$ — inner wheel turns *more* than outer.
- ☐ 100% Ackermann = zero tire scrub; in practice, vehicles use **60–80% Ackermann** for high-speed stability.
- ☐ Bicycle-model equivalent angle: $\tan\delta = l/R$.

### B. Bicycle Model
- ☐ States: $v_y$ (lateral velocity) and $\gamma$ (yaw rate); input: $\delta$ (steering angle).
- ☐ Slip angles: $\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - l_f\gamma/v_x$, $\alpha_r = -\beta + l_r\gamma/v_x$.
- ☐ Side-slip at CoG: $\beta = \arctan(v_y/v_x) \approx v_y/v_x$ (small-angle).
- ☐ Linear tire model: $F_y = C_\alpha \alpha$ (valid for $|\alpha| < 4°$).

### C. Equations of Motion
- ☐ Lateral: $m(\dot v_y + v_x \gamma) = F_{y,f}\cos\delta + F_{y,r}$.
- ☐ Yaw: $I_z \dot\gamma = l_f F_{y,f}\cos\delta - l_r F_{y,r}$.

### D. Steady-State Yaw Rate
- ☐ $\gamma_{ss} = v_x \delta / (l + K v_x^{\,2})$.
- ☐ Understeer gradient: $K = m(l_r C_{\alpha,r} - l_f C_{\alpha,f}) / (l\,C_{\alpha,f} C_{\alpha,r})$.
- ☐ $K > 0$ understeer, $K = 0$ neutral, $K < 0$ oversteer.

### E. State-Space Form
- ☐ $A$ has both eigenvalues in the open-left-half-plane (passenger car is open-loop stable).
- ☐ $B = [C_{\alpha,f}/(m v_x), \; C_{\alpha,f} l_f / I_z]^T$.

### F. Controllers
- ☐ **PID**: simplest; for SISO yaw-rate tracking.
- ☐ **State-Feedback Pole Placement**: full design freedom over closed-loop dynamics.
- ☐ **LQR**: optimal; minimises quadratic cost; solution of **ARE** $A^TP + PA - PBR^{-1}B^TP + Q = 0$.
- ☐ **MPC** (advanced): uses a *predictive* model; not in KTU 2024 syllabus core but in elective modules.

### G. Steer-by-Wire (SbW)
- ☐ No mechanical linkage — uses torque sensor, ECU, road-feel motor, and front-wheel motor.
- ☐ Foundation of **autonomous steering** and **lane-keeping assist**.

### H. Examiner's Pet Topics
- ☐ Ackermann derivation (very frequently asked).
- ☐ Steady-state yaw rate formula + understeer classification.
- ☐ State-space $A, B$ matrix derivation.
- ☐ LQR design with Riccati equation.

### I. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ☐ Don't write Ackermann with **tan** instead of **cot**.
- ☐ Don't forget $\cos\delta \approx 1$ assumption in linearisation.
- ☐ Don't use $v_y$ and $\beta$ interchangeably in matrices — declare the state vector.
- ☐ Don't apply LQR to an unstable plant without checking stabilisability.

### J. Numerical Values to Remember
- ☐ $C_\alpha \approx 80\,000$ N/rad for a typical 205/55 R16 tire.
- ☐ $I_z \approx 2500-3500$ kg·m² for a sedan.
- ☐ $m \approx 1500$ kg for a mid-size sedan.
- ☐ $l \approx 2.5-2.8$ m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Students:**
> The examiner allocates approximately **40% of marks** for **derivation steps**, **30%** for **correct final expressions**, **20%** for **diagrams and physical interpretation**, and **10%** for **neatness, units, and sign conventions**. Make sure to:
> 1. **Show every algebraic step** (don't skip lines "as it is well known").
> 2. **Box your final answers**.
> 3. **Mention assumptions** (small angles, constant $v_x$, linear tires).
> 4. **Draw free-body diagrams** and label all forces and distances.
> 5. **Include units** in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Yaw Rate Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Yaw Rate Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yaw Rate Controller (YRC)** is a closed-loop vehicle dynamics control subsystem that regulates the **rotational angular velocity** of a vehicle about its vertical (z) axis, denoted as **$\dot{\psi}$** (psi-dot), in order to track a **desired reference yaw rate** $\dot{\psi}_{des}$ derived from the driver's steering input and the instantaneous vehicle velocity.

The yaw rate controller forms the **inner-loop core** of modern **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)**, **Vehicle Stability Assist (VSA)**, and **Direct Yaw Control (DYC)** systems mandated under **ECE R13 / R140** and **ISO 26262 (ASIL-D)** functional safety standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Yaw Rate Value (Industry Benchmark):**
> A typical passenger car at **$V = 100 \text{ km/h}$** on a curve of radius **$R = 100 \text{ m}$** exhibits a steady-state yaw rate of approximately:
> $$\dot{\psi}_{ss} = \frac{V}{R} \approx 0.278 \text{ rad/s} \approx 16^\circ/\text{s}$$

The mathematical reference yaw rate for a low-speed, low-acceleration maneuver (Ackermann kinematic condition) is given by:
$$\dot{\psi}_{des} = \frac{V_x \cdot \delta_f}{L \cdot \left(1 + K \cdot V_x^2\right)}$$

where the **stability factor** $K$ is defined as:
$$K = \frac{m \cdot \left(b \cdot C_r - a \cdot C_f\right)}{L^2 \cdot C_f \cdot C_r}$$

with $C_f$ and $C_r$ being the **cornering stiffness coefficients** of the front and rear tires respectively.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bowling Ball & The Fingers"

Imagine you are rolling a bowling ball down a wooden lane. The ball naturally wants to travel in a **straight line** (low yaw rate). Now, you want the ball to gently curve into the 1-3 pocket. You do not push the ball sideways (that would be a **sideslip / lateral velocity** $V_y$ problem). Instead, you **spin it** with your fingertips — you control its **angular spin rate** (yaw rate) without changing its center-of-mass path.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Analogy Mapped to Engineering:**
> - The **bowling ball** $\rightarrow$ the vehicle mass $m$
> - The **fingertip spin** $\rightarrow$ the controlled yaw moment $M_z$ applied by differential braking
> - The **lane curve** $\rightarrow$ the driver's intended reference trajectory $\dot{\psi}_{des}$
> - Your **eyes measuring the spin** $\rightarrow$ the **yaw rate sensor (gyroscope)** providing feedback
> - Your **brain's correction** $\rightarrow$ the **ECU controller algorithm** (PID / LQR)

The Yaw Rate Controller is essentially the **electronic "fingertip"** of the vehicle, ensuring the car **spins (yaws) at exactly the rate** the driver intends through the steering wheel — no more, no less. If the car yaws too little, it **understeers** (plows wide); if it yaws too much, it **oversteers** (spins out). The controller continuously minimizes the error $e_\psi = \dot{\psi}_{des} - \dot{\psi}_{measured}$.

---

## 1.3 Why a Yaw Rate Controller is Necessary (Problem Statement)

| Driving Scenario | Without YRC | With YRC |
|---|---|---|
| Sharp lane change at 80 km/h | Vehicle spins out (oversteer) | Yaw moment counteracts spin |
| Slippery curve entry | Rear breaks traction, fishtail | Differential brake applied to outer front wheel |
| Braking on split-µ surface | Vehicle pulls violently to grip side | Selective braking restores yaw tracking |
| Double lane change (Elk test) | Rollover / loss of control | Active moment maintains path within 1.5 m corridor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Yaw Rate Controller is **NOT** a comfort feature. It is a **mandatory active safety system** in the European Union (ESC mandate since 2014, ECE R140) and reduces single-vehicle crash risk by approximately **38%** (NHTSA 2018 study).

---

## 1.4 Block-Level Role in Vehicle Architecture

The YRC sits in the **lateral dynamics control hierarchy**, downstream of the **path planner** and upstream of the **actuator allocation layer**:

1. **Driver Input Layer** — Steering wheel angle $\delta_{sw}$ and wheel speeds
2. **Reference Generator** — Computes $\dot{\psi}_{des}$ and $\beta_{des}$
3. **Yaw Rate Controller (This Topic)** — Computes corrective yaw moment $M_{z,corr}$
4. **Actuator Allocation** — Distributes $M_{z,corr}$ to differential braking $\Delta T_b$, steering override $\Delta\delta$, or active differential torque vectoring
5. **Vehicle Plant** — 2-DOF bicycle model
6. **Sensors (Feedback)** — Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) with yaw gyroscope, wheel speed encoders, lateral accelerometer

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 2-DOF Bicycle Model — Foundation of Yaw Control

The entire yaw rate controller design rests upon the **linearized 2-DOF bicycle model**, which captures the two essential lateral dynamic states: **sideslip angle** $\beta$ and **yaw rate** $r = \dot{\psi}$.

### 2.1.1 Coordinate System & State Definitions

- **$x$-axis:** Longitudinal, pointing forward from vehicle CG
- **$y$-axis:** Lateral, pointing left from vehicle CG
- **$z$-axis:** Vertical, pointing upward (yaw axis)
- **$a$:** Distance from CG to front axle
- **$b$:** Distance from CG to rear axle
- **$L = a + b$:** Wheelbase
- **$m$:** Sprung mass (kg)
- **$I_z$:** Yaw moment of inertia about z-axis (kg·m²)
- **$V_x$:** Longitudinal velocity (assumed constant for short maneuvers)
- **$\beta$:** Sideslip angle at CG, $\beta \approx V_y / V_x$
- **$r = \dot{\psi}$:** Yaw rate
- **$\delta_f$:** Front steering angle at the wheel

### 2.1.2 Equations of Motion (Newton-Euler)

**Lateral force balance** along $y$-axis:
$$m \cdot V_x \cdot \left(\dot{\beta} + r\right) = F_{yf} \cos\delta_f + F_{yr}$$

**Yaw moment balance** about z-axis:
$$I_z \cdot \dot{r} = a \cdot F_{yf} \cos\delta_f - b \cdot F_{yr} + M_z$$

For small steering angles ($\cos\delta_f \approx 1$):

$$m \cdot V_x \cdot \left(\dot{\beta} + r\right) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

$$I_z \cdot \dot{r} = a \cdot F_{yf} - b \cdot F_{yr} + M_z$$

### 2.1.3 Linear Tire Force Model (Small Slip Assumption)

For small slip angles $\alpha_f, \alpha_r$ (within the linear region of the tire curve):
$$F_{yf} = C_f \cdot \alpha_f \qquad F_{yr} = C_r \cdot \alpha_r$$

where the slip angles are:
$$\alpha_f = \delta_f - \beta - \frac{a \cdot r}{V_x} \qquad \alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{b \cdot r}{V_x}$$

---

## 2.2 The Yaw Rate Controller — Control Law Architectures

### 2.2.1 PID Yaw Rate Controller (Classical Approach)

The simplest, most industrially robust implementation is the **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) feedback controller** acting on the yaw rate error:

$$M_{z,corr}(t) = K_p \cdot e_\psi(t) + K_i \cdot \int_0^t e_\psi(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \cdot \frac{d e_\psi(t)}{dt}$$

where the **yaw rate error** is:
$$e_\psi(t) = \dot{\psi}_{des}(t) - \dot{\psi}_{measured}(t)$$

**Tuning Rationale:**
- $K_p$ — Provides **stiffness** of yaw tracking. Higher $K_p$ = faster response but risks oscillation.
- $K_i$ — Eliminates **steady-state tracking error** in steady cornering. Risk: integrator windup during transient oversteer.
- $K_d$ — Provides **damping** to suppress yaw oscillations. Critical for high-speed stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Windup (MANDATORY):** All production ESC ECUs implement **back-calculation** or **clamping** anti-windup on the integrator, because the corrective moment is bounded by the friction circle: $\vert M_{z,corr} \vert \leq \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot d$, where $d$ is the track width.

### 2.2.2 State-Feedback Yaw Controller (Modern / Pole-Placement)

Using the state-space form of the bicycle model, a linear state-feedback law is:

$$\mathbf{M}_{z,corr} = -\mathbf{K} \cdot \left(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{des}\right)$$

with the state vector $\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \beta \\ r \end{bmatrix}^T$ and gain matrix $\mathbf{K} = \begin{bmatrix} K_\beta & K_r \end{bmatrix}$.

Thus:
$$M_{z,corr} = -K_\beta \cdot (\beta - \beta_{des}) - K_r \cdot (r - r_{des})$$

**Note:** The negative sign inverts the measured state error, so the controller applies a counteracting moment.

### 2.2.3 LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator) Yaw Controller — Optimal Approach

LQR minimizes the cost function:
$$J = \int_0^{\infty} \left( \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + u^T R u \right) dt$$

The optimal gain is found by solving the **Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE)**:
$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{P} \mathbf{B} R^{-1} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{Q} = 0$$

yielding:
$$\mathbf{K}_{LQR} = R^{-1} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{P}$$

The weighting matrices are typically chosen as:
$$\mathbf{Q} = \text{diag}\left( q_\beta, q_r \right) \qquad R = \rho$$
where $q_r \gg q_\beta$ to prioritize yaw tracking over sideslip suppression.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical**. Memorize the boxed ones first.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\psi}_{des} = \dfrac{V_x \cdot \delta_f}{L \cdot (1 + K V_x^2)}$ | Reference yaw rate (Ackermann + understeer) | rad/s |
| 2 | $K = \dfrac{m(b C_r - a C_f)}{L^2 C_f C_r}$ | Understeer gradient (stability factor) | rad/(m/s²) |
| 3 | $\dot{\beta} = \dfrac{F_{yf} + F_{yr}}{m V_x} - r$ | Sideslip rate equation | rad/s |
| 4 | $\dot{r} = \dfrac{a F_{yf} - b F_{yr} + M_z}{I_z}$ | Yaw acceleration equation | rad/s² |
| 5 | $\alpha_f = \delta_f - \beta - \dfrac{a r}{V_x}$ | Front tire slip angle | rad |
| 6 | $\alpha_r = -\beta + \dfrac{b r}{V_x}$ | Rear tire slip angle | rad |
| 7 | $M_{z,corr} = K_p e_\psi + K_i \int e_\psi \, dt + K_d \dot{e}_\psi$ | PID yaw controller | N·m |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$ | State-space bicycle model | — |
| 9 | $\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ | System matrix (see Section 3) | — |
| 10 | $\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1/I_z \end{bmatrix}$ | Input matrix for yaw moment | — |
| 11 | $\vert M_{z,corr} \vert \leq \mu m g \cdot d/2$ | Friction circle bound | N·m |
| 12 | $e_\psi = \dot{\psi}_{des} - \dot{\psi}_{measured}$ | Yaw rate tracking error | rad/s |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The yaw rate controller is the cornerstone of:

- **Autonomous Emergency Steering (AES)** — evasive lane changes
- **Self-driving cars (SAE Level 3+)** — trajectory tracking in Tesla FSD, Waymo Driver
- **Racing / Motorsport** — Torque Vectoring Control (TVC) in Formula 1, WRC, drift control
- **Heavy commercial vehicles** — Roll Stability Control (RSC) for trucks and tankers to prevent rollover
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** — Left/right motor torque vectoring replaces mechanical differentials
- **Defense / Military** — Skid-steer and articulated vehicle yaw regulation in unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transition from **hydraulic brake-based yaw control** (Bosch ESP 8.0, Continental MK 100) to **electromechanical brake (EMB) and steer-by-wire (SbW) based yaw control** represents the next generation, enabling **sub-10 ms control loops** versus the legacy 50–100 ms hydraulic response time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: State-Space Form of the 2-DOF Bicycle Model

We begin with the lateral force and yaw moment equations from Section 2.1.2, substitute the linear tire model, and rearrange into a standard state-space form.

### Step 1: Substitute Linear Tire Forces into Lateral Force Equation

$$m V_x \left(\dot{\beta} + r\right) = C_f \left(\delta_f - \beta - \frac{a r}{V_x}\right) + C_r \left(-\beta + \frac{b r}{V_x}\right)$$

Expanding the right-hand side:
$$m V_x \left(\dot{\beta} + r\right) = C_f \delta_f - C_f \beta - \frac{a C_f r}{V_x} - C_r \beta + \frac{b C_r r}{V_x}$$

### Step 2: Isolate $\dot{\beta}$

Solving for $\dot{\beta}$:
$$\dot{\beta} = -\frac{(C_f + C_r)}{m V_x}\beta + \left(\frac{b C_r - a C_f}{m V_x^2} - 1\right)r + \frac{C_f}{m V_x}\delta_f$$

This is the first row of the system matrix. We now define the system constants:

$$a_{11} = -\frac{C_f + C_r}{m V_x} \quad \left[\text{s}^{-1}\right]$$

$$a_{12} = \frac{b C_r - a C_f}{m V_x^2} - 1 \quad \left[\text{dimensionless}\right]$$

$$b_1 = \frac{C_f}{m V_x} \quad \left[\text{rad}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}\right]$$

### Step 3: Substitute Linear Tire Forces into Yaw Moment Equation

$$I_z \dot{r} = a C_f \left(\delta_f - \beta - \frac{a r}{V_x}\right) - b C_r \left(-\beta + \frac{b r}{V_x}\right) + M_z$$

Expanding:
$$I_z \dot{r} = a C_f \delta_f - a C_f \beta - \frac{a^2 C_f r}{V_x} + b C_r \beta - \frac{b^2 C_r r}{V_x} + M_z$$

### Step 4: Isolate $\dot{r}$

$$\dot{r} = \frac{(b C_r - a C_f)}{I_z}\beta - \frac{(a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r)}{I_z V_x} r + \frac{a C_f}{I_z}\delta_f + \frac{1}{I_z}M_z$$

This is the second row of the system matrix:

$$a_{21} = \frac{b C_r - a C_f}{I_z} \quad \left[\text{m}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}\right]$$

$$a_{22} = -\frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{I_z V_x} \quad \left[\text{s}^{-1}\right]$$

$$b_2 = \frac{1}{I_z} \quad \left[\text{kg}^{-1} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}\right]$$

### Step 5: Assemble the State-Space Matrices

The standard form is:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\beta} \\ \dot{r} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \beta \\ r \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 & 0 \\ 0 & b_2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \delta_f \\ M_z \end{bmatrix}$$

Expanded explicitly:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\dfrac{C_f+C_r}{m V_x} & \dfrac{b C_r - a C_f}{m V_x^2} - 1 \\[8pt] \dfrac{b C_r - a C_f}{I_z} & -\dfrac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{I_z V_x} \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{A}} \mathbf{x} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{C_f}{m V_x} & 0 \\[4pt] \dfrac{a C_f}{I_z} & \dfrac{1}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{B}} \mathbf{u}$$

The measured output (yaw rate) is given by:
$$\mathbf{y} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{C}} \begin{bmatrix} \beta \\ r \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{A}$ determine the **open-loop stability** of the vehicle. If $\text{Re}(\lambda_i) < 0$ for both eigenvalues, the vehicle is **stable** (understeering). If any eigenvalue has $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$, the vehicle is **unstable** (oversteering) and the yaw controller MUST stabilize it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PID Yaw Rate Controller Transfer Function

Starting from the closed-loop yaw dynamics with the corrective moment $M_{z,corr} = K_p e_\psi + K_i \int e_\psi \, dt + K_d \dot{e}_\psi$:

### Step 1: Express the PID in Laplace Domain

$$M_{z,corr}(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E_\psi(s)$$

Define the **PID transfer function**:
$$C_{PID}(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

### Step 2: Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation

For the linearized yaw dynamics, the open-loop plant from $M_z$ to $r$ (neglecting steering) is approximately:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{1}{I_z s + \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x}}$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation is:
$$1 + C_{PID}(s) \cdot G_p(s) = 0$$

Substituting:
$$1 + \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{I_z s + \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x}} = 0$$

Multiplying both sides by $s \cdot \left(I_z s + \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x}\right)$:

$$s \left(I_z s + \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x}\right) + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i = 0$$

### Step 3: Expand and Collect Powers of $s$

$$I_z s^2 + \frac{(a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r) s}{V_x} + K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i = 0$$

$$(I_z + K_d) s^2 + \left(\frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x} + K_p\right) s + K_i = 0$$

### Step 4: Apply Routh-Hurwitz / Pole-Placement

For a desired natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$, we set:
$$I_z + K_d = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_d = 1 - I_z$$

$$\frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x} + K_p = 2 \zeta \omega_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = 2 \zeta \omega_n - \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x}$$

$$K_i = \omega_n^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The $K_i = \omega_n^2$ relation assumes normalized coefficients. In a real design, engineers use **loop-shaping** or **Ziegler-Nichols** tuning. The above is illustrative for KTU exam purposes.

### Step 5: Final Tuned Controller (Generic Form)

$$\boxed{K_p = 2 \zeta \omega_n - D, \qquad K_i = \omega_n^2, \qquad K_d = 1 - I_z}$$

where $D = \dfrac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x}$ is the **yaw damping coefficient** of the passive vehicle.

This completes the analytical derivation. The student should now be able to:
1. Compute $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}$ matrices for a given vehicle.
2. Check open-loop stability via eigenvalues.
3. Design a PID yaw controller for a desired closed-loop response ($\omega_n, \zeta$).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU Style Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Given:** A passenger car with $m = 1500$ kg, $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², $a = 1.2$ m, $b = 1.5$ m, $L = 2.7$ m, $C_f = 80{,}000$ N/rad, $C_r = 80{,}000$ N/rad, $V_x = 20$ m/s (≈72 km/h). The driver commands a step steering input $\delta_f = 0.05$ rad (≈2.87°).

**Task:** (a) Compute the steady-state reference yaw rate. (b) Determine if the vehicle is understeering or oversteering. (c) Design a PID yaw rate controller for $\omega_n = 2$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.7$.

**Solution (a) — Reference Yaw Rate:**

$$K = \frac{m(b C_r - a C_f)}{L^2 C_f C_r} = \frac{1500 \cdot (1.5 \cdot 80000 - 1.2 \cdot 80000)}{(2.7)^2 \cdot 80000 \cdot 80000}$$

$$K = \frac{1500 \cdot (120000 - 96000)}{7.29 \cdot 6.4 \times 10^9} = \frac{1500 \cdot 24000}{4.6656 \times 10^{10}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^7}{4.6656 \times 10^{10}}$$

$$K = 7.717 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad/(m/s)²}$$

$$\dot{\psi}_{des} = \frac{20 \cdot 0.05}{2.7 \cdot (1 + 7.717 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 400)} = \frac{1.0}{2.7 \cdot 1.3087} = \frac{1.0}{3.534} = 0.283 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Solution (b) — Stability Classification:**

Since $b C_r = 1.5 \cdot 80000 = 1.2 \times 10^5$ and $a C_f = 1.2 \cdot 80000 = 9.6 \times 10^4$, we have $b C_r > a C_f$, thus $K > 0$. **The vehicle is UNDERSTEERING (stable)**.

**Solution (c) — PID Gains:**

$$D = \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x} = \frac{1.44 \cdot 80000 + 2.25 \cdot 80000}{20} = \frac{1.152 \times 10^5 + 1.8 \times 10^5}{20} = \frac{2.952 \times 10^5}{20} = 14760 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

$$K_p = 2 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 2 - 14760 = 2.8 - 14760 \approx -14757 \text{ (Note: negative means use direct form, sign is convention-dependent)}$$

$$K_i = \omega_n^2 = 4 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$K_d = 1 - I_z = 1 - 2500 = -2499$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The negative signs** arise because the $K_p, K_d$ formulas in Step 4 assumed normalized plant gain of 1. In practice, the engineer uses **MATLAB/Simulink Control System Toolbox** or **CarSim / CarMaker** to tune PID via **pilot-induced oscillation (PIO) avoidance** and **phase margin** constraints (target $\geq 45°$).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Yaw Rate Controller — Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DRIVER STEERING ANGLE delta_sw] --> B[STEERING RATIO GEAR]
    B --> C[FRONT WHEEL ANGLE delta_f]
    C --> D[REFERENCE YAW RATE GENERATOR]
    V[VEHICLE SPEED V_x] --> D
    D --> E[DESIRED YAW r_des]
    E --> F[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    S[YAW RATE SENSOR IMU] --> G[MEASURED YAW r_meas]
    G --> F
    F --> H[PID CONTROLLER Kp Ki Kd]
    H --> I[CORRECTIVE YAW MOMENT Mz_corr]
    I --> J[ACTUATOR ALLOCATION]
    J --> K1[DIFFERENTIAL BRAKING dTb]
    J --> K2[STEER BY WIRE OVERRIDE dd]
    J --> K3[TORQUE VECTORING dTm]
    K1 --> L[VEHICLE PLANT BICYCLE MODEL]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> M[YAW RATE r_meas OUTPUT]
    M --> S
    L --> N[LATERAL VELOCITY Vy OUTPUT]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style S fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define yaw rate and explain its significance in vehicle stability control. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Yaw rate** $r = \dot{\psi}$ is the **angular velocity of a vehicle about its vertical (z) axis**, measured in **rad/s** (or deg/s). It describes how fast the vehicle body is rotating in the horizontal plane.

**Significance in vehicle stability control:**
1. **Measures directional response** — A yaw rate sensor quantifies how sharply the car is turning.
2. **Feedback signal for ESC** — The yaw rate error $e_\psi = r_{des} - r_{measured}$ is the primary feedback in Electronic Stability Control.
3. **Detects instability** — Excessive yaw rate indicates oversteer; insufficient yaw rate indicates understeer.
4. **Sensor location** — Typically integrated into the Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), often a MEMS gyroscope (e.g., Bosch SMI 240, Murata SCC2000 series).
5. **Standard range** — Production yaw sensors measure $\pm 100^\circ/\text{s}$ to $\pm 250^\circ/\text{s}$ depending on vehicle class.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **yaw rate** with **sideslip angle** $\beta$. They are different: yaw rate is **rotational**, sideslip is the **angle between velocity vector and vehicle longitudinal axis**. The yaw rate controller regulates $\dot{\psi}$, but advanced systems also regulate $\beta$ to maintain the vehicle within the **friction ellipse**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the function of the reference yaw rate generator. Write its mathematical expression. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Function:** The reference yaw rate generator computes the **desired yaw rate** $r_{des}$ (or $\dot{\psi}_{des}$) that the vehicle should exhibit to follow the driver's intended path, based on:
- Front wheel steering angle $\delta_f$
- Vehicle longitudinal velocity $V_x$
- Vehicle geometric parameters ($a, b, L$) and tire data ($C_f, C_r$)

**Mathematical expression (Ackermann + understeer):**
$$\dot{\psi}_{des} = \frac{V_x \cdot \delta_f}{L \cdot \left(1 + K \cdot V_x^2\right)}$$

where the **stability/understeer factor** is:
$$K = \frac{m \cdot (b \cdot C_r - a \cdot C_f)}{L^2 \cdot C_f \cdot C_r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $K V_x^2$ term accounts for the **load transfer** and **tire saturation** at high lateral acceleration. Without it, the formula would predict infinite yaw rate on tight curves.

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE)

> **ESE Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the following (Q.10A **OR** Q.10B). Each is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — THEORY-DESIGN FOCUS

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the **2-DOF linear bicycle model** for lateral vehicle dynamics, clearly defining all symbols. Write the resulting state-space form. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a **PID yaw rate controller** for the bicycle model, showing the closed-loop transfer function and deriving the expressions for the PID gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ in terms of the desired natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — State variables:** $\beta$ (sideslip angle at CG) and $r = \dot{\psi}$ (yaw rate).

**Step 2 — Force balance in lateral direction:**
$$m V_x (\dot{\beta} + r) = F_{yf} + F_{yr}$$

**Step 3 — Moment balance about yaw axis:**
$$I_z \dot{r} = a F_{yf} - b F_{yr} + M_z$$

**Step 4 — Linear tire model:** $F_{yf} = C_f \alpha_f$, $F_{yr} = C_r \alpha_r$ with:
$$\alpha_f = \delta_f - \beta - \frac{a r}{V_x}, \quad \alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{b r}{V_x}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and isolate $\dot{\beta}, \dot{r}$:**

$$\dot{\beta} = -\frac{C_f + C_r}{m V_x}\beta + \left(\frac{b C_r - a C_f}{m V_x^2} - 1\right)r + \frac{C_f}{m V_x}\delta_f$$

$$\dot{r} = \frac{b C_r - a C_f}{I_z}\beta - \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{I_z V_x}r + \frac{a C_f}{I_z}\delta_f + \frac{1}{I_z}M_z$$

**Step 6 — State-space form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix}\dot{\beta}\\\dot{r}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12}\\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\beta\\r\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} b_1 & 0\\ 0 & b_2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\delta_f\\M_z\end{bmatrix}$$

where $a_{11}, a_{12}, a_{21}, a_{22}$ are as defined in Section 3.1.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Define the yaw rate error:** $e_\psi(t) = r_{des}(t) - r_{measured}(t)$.

**Step 2 — PID control law:**
$$M_{z,corr}(t) = K_p e_\psi(t) + K_i \int_0^t e_\psi(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de_\psi(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Laplace form:**
$$C_{PID}(s) = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Closed-loop TF (simplified plant, $\delta_f = 0$):**

The yaw rate to yaw moment transfer function (with $\beta$ quasi-steady assumption) simplifies to:
$$G_r(s) = \frac{1}{I_z s + D}, \quad \text{where } D = \frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{V_x}$$

**Step 5 — Closed-loop characteristic equation:**
$$1 + \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s(I_z s + D)} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Expand:**
$$(I_z + K_d)s^2 + (D + K_p)s + K_i = 0$$

**Step 7 — Compare with standard 2nd-order form** $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$\frac{K_i}{I_z + K_d} = \omega_n^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_i = \omega_n^2(I_z + K_d)$$

$$\frac{D + K_p}{I_z + K_d} = 2\zeta\omega_n \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p = 2\zeta\omega_n(I_z + K_d) - D$$

$$\boxed{K_d \text{ is a free design parameter (often chosen } \approx 0.1 I_z \text{ to } 0.3 I_z)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (b):**
> - **(–2 marks)** Failing to mention **anti-windup** on the integrator $K_i$ term. ABSOLUTELY MANDATORY for production ESC.
> - **(–1 mark)** Not specifying the **friction circle constraint** $\vert M_{z,corr}\vert \leq \mu m g d/2$ on the controller output.
> - **(–1 mark)** Using unnormalized plant gain in the comparison with standard 2nd-order form. Always divide by $(I_z + K_d)$ first.
> - **(–1 mark)** Confusing $K_p$ (PID) with the understeer gradient $K$ (stability factor). Different symbols, different meanings.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — APPLIED/ANALYTICAL FOCUS

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** A passenger car has the following data: $m = 1800$ kg, $I_z = 3000$ kg·m², $a = 1.3$ m, $b = 1.4$ m, $L = 2.7$ m, $C_f = 90{,}000$ N/rad, $C_r = 90{,}000$ N/rad, $V_x = 25$ m/s. Compute the **system matrix $\mathbf{A}$** and determine the **open-loop eigenvalues**. Comment on stability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the same vehicle, the driver performs a lane change with $\delta_f = 0.04$ rad. Compute the **reference yaw rate** and design an **LQR yaw controller** with $\mathbf{Q} = \text{diag}(1, 50)$ and $R = 1$, given the ARE solution $\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} p_1 & p_2\\ p_2 & p_3\end{bmatrix}$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute $a_{11}$:**
$$a_{11} = -\frac{C_f + C_r}{m V_x} = -\frac{180000}{1800 \cdot 25} = -\frac{180000}{45000} = -4.0 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a_{12}$:**
$$a_{12} = \frac{b C_r - a C_f}{m V_x^2} - 1 = \frac{1.4 \cdot 90000 - 1.3 \cdot 90000}{1800 \cdot 625} - 1 = \frac{9000}{1125000} - 1 = 0.008 - 1 = -0.992$$

**Step 3 — Compute $a_{21}$:**
$$a_{21} = \frac{b C_r - a C_f}{I_z} = \frac{9000}{3000} = 3.0 \text{ s}^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $a_{22}$:**
$$a_{22} = -\frac{a^2 C_f + b^2 C_r}{I_z V_x} = -\frac{1.69 \cdot 90000 + 1.96 \cdot 90000}{3000 \cdot 25} = -\frac{(1.69 + 1.96) \cdot 90000}{75000}$$

$$a_{22} = -\frac{3.65 \cdot 90000}{75000} = -\frac{328500}{75000} = -4.38 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — System matrix:**
$$\mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} -4.0 & -0.992\\ 3.0 & -4.38 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Eigenvalues (characteristic equation $|\lambda \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A}| = 0$):**
$$\lambda^2 - \text{tr}(\mathbf{A})\lambda + \det(\mathbf{A}) = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 + 8.38\lambda + (4.0 \cdot 4.38 - 3.0 \cdot (-0.992)) = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 + 8.38\lambda + (17.52 + 2.976) = 0$$
$$\lambda^2 + 8.38\lambda + 20.496 = 0$$
$$\lambda = \frac{-8.38 \pm \sqrt{70.22 - 81.98}}{2} = \frac{-8.38 \pm \sqrt{-11.76}}{2}$$
$$\lambda = -4.19 \pm j 1.715$$

**Step 7 — Comment:** Both eigenvalues have **negative real parts** ($\text{Re}(\lambda) = -4.19 < 0$). The vehicle is **stable** in open-loop, exhibiting an **understeering** damped oscillatory response with natural frequency $\omega_d = 1.715$ rad/s and damping $\zeta \approx 0.925$.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Reference yaw rate:**
$$K = \frac{1800 \cdot (1.4 \cdot 90000 - 1.3 \cdot 90000)}{(2.7)^2 \cdot 90000 \cdot 90000} = \frac{1800 \cdot 9000}{7.29 \cdot 8.1 \times 10^9} = 2.74 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\dot{\psi}_{des} = \frac{25 \cdot 0.04}{2.7 \cdot (1 + 2.74 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 625)} = \frac{1.0}{2.7 \cdot 1.171} = 0.316 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Input matrix (yaw moment channel only):**
$$\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0\\ 1/I_z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0\\ 1/3000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the Algebraic Riccati Equation:**
$$\mathbf{A}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P} \mathbf{A} - \mathbf{P} \mathbf{B} R^{-1} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{P} + \mathbf{Q} = 0$$

Substituting and solving the three nonlinear equations (yielded by symmetry of $\mathbf{P}$):

For brevity (exam-appropriate), assume numerical solution yields:
$$\mathbf{P} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 12.4 & 5.1\\ 5.1 & 78.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute LQR gain:**
$$\mathbf{K}_{LQR} = R^{-1} \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{P} = 1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \frac{1}{3000} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 12.4 & 5.1\\ 5.1 & 78.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{K}_{LQR} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{5.1}{3000} & \frac{78.6}{3000} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.0017 & 0.0262 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Control law:**
$$M_{z,corr} = -\mathbf{K}_{LQR}(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_{des}) = -0.0017(\beta - \beta_{des}) - 0.0262(r - r_{des})$$

For pure yaw tracking ($\beta_{des} \approx 0$):
$$\boxed{M_{z,corr} = -0.0017\beta - 0.0262(r - 0.316)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (b):**
> - **(–2 marks)** Skipping the **trace and determinant** calculation in the eigenvalue step.
> - **(–2 marks)** Forgetting that for the **ARE**, the ARE is a coupled set of three nonlinear equations; you cannot just "guess" $\mathbf{P}$. Show that you are solving (or state the numerical solver used).
> - **(–1 mark)** Using $r$ and $\dot{\psi}$ interchangeably is fine, but you must use **consistent units** (rad/s vs deg/s is a common trap — $1$ deg/s $= 0.01745$ rad/s).
> - **(–1 mark)** Not mentioning that **LQR guarantees stability only if $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B})$ is stabilizable and $(\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{Q}^{1/2})$ is detectable**.

---

## 🔖 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Yaw rate** $r = \dot{\psi}$ is the **rotational angular velocity** of the vehicle about its vertical axis, measured in **rad/s**.
- ✅ The **reference yaw rate** is computed using the **Ackermann-understeer formula**: $\dot{\psi}_{des} = V_x \delta_f / [L(1 + K V_x^2)]$.
- ✅ The **stability factor** $K$ has units of **rad/(m/s)²**. Positive $K$ → understeer; negative $K$ → oversteer; $K = 0$ → neutral steer.
- ✅ The **2-DOF bicycle model** has two states: $\beta$ and $r$, and two inputs: $\delta_f$ and $M_z$.
- ✅ The system matrix $\mathbf{A}$ has the form: top-left entry $a_{11} = -(C_f + C_r)/(m V_x)$ — **always negative**.
- ✅ **Open-loop stability check** = eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$ must have **negative real parts**.
- ✅ **PID yaw controller** acts on the error $e_\psi = r_{des} - r_{meas}$ with gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$.
- ✅ **Anti-windup** is mandatory on the integrator $K_i$ to prevent **integrator windup** during saturation.
- ✅ **Friction circle bound** on corrective moment: $\vert M_{z,corr}\vert \leq \mu m g \cdot d/2$.
- ✅ **LQR** minimizes $J = \int (\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + R u^2) dt$; solved via the **Algebraic Riccati Equation**.
- ✅ Weighting $q_r \gg q_\beta$ prioritizes **yaw rate tracking** over sideslip minimization.
- ✅ Real-world ESC systems use **differential braking** (Bosch ESP), **steer-by-wire override** (Infiniti Q50), and **torque vectoring** (Tesla, Audi e-tron, Ford Focus RS).
- ✅ Yaw controller sits at **Layer 3** of the vehicle control hierarchy (after path planning and trajectory tracking; before actuator allocation).
- ✅ **Sensors required:** IMU (yaw gyro), wheel speed sensors (WSS), steering angle sensor (SAS), lateral accelerometer, longitudinal accelerometer.
- ✅ **ECE R140** mandates ESC for all new passenger cars in the EU since 2014; **FMVSS 126** equivalent in the USA.
- ✅ Yaw rate control reduces single-vehicle crash fatality risk by **~38%** (NHTSA 2018 data).
- ✅ In MATLAB, use `ss()` for state-space, `lqr()` for LQR design, `pidtune()` for PID tuning, and **CarSim/CarMaker** for co-simulation with nonlinear vehicle models.
- ✅ **Time constant** of production hydraulic ESC: 50–100 ms. **Electromechanical ESC** (future): 10–20 ms.
- ✅ The **β-method** (sideslip-based) and **β̇-method** (sideslip-rate-based) are two alternative yaw control philosophies; β̇-method avoids needing a direct β sensor (which is expensive).
- ✅ Remember the **sign convention**: positive yaw rate = **counter-clockwise** (left turn) when viewed from above (SAE J670e).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lateral Movement Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lateral Movement Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Lateral Movement Controller (Lateral Controller)** is a closed-loop feedback control subsystem within an autonomous or semi-autonomous vehicle's longitudinal-lateral control architecture that is responsible for regulating the vehicle's **sideways (lateral) motion** along a desired path. It computes a corrective steering command such that the vehicle's actual trajectory converges to a pre-defined reference trajectory (waypoints, lane centerline, or a generated path).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> The Lateral Controller operates on the **lateral error** (cross-track error, $e_y$) and **heading error** ($e_\psi$) between the vehicle's actual pose $(x, y, \psi)$ and the reference path. Its primary output is the **front-wheel steering angle**, $\delta_f$, applied to actuate the steering mechanism and bring the vehicle back onto the desired trajectory.

The controller is mathematically formulated using either:
- A **Kinematic Bicycle Model** (low-speed, ignores tire forces), or
- A **Dynamic Bicycle Model** (high-speed, includes tire slip, cornering stiffness $C_{\alpha}$, and vehicle mass $m$).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Driving a Car with a Blindfold Removed Slowly"**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car and trying to stay exactly in the middle of your lane. Your **eyes** measure two things continuously: **how far you are from the center of the lane** (lateral error) and **how much your car is rotated relative to the lane direction** (heading error). Your **brain** acts as the controller: if you drift right, you turn the wheel left; if you are pointing sideways, you turn the wheel more aggressively to align. The **steering wheel** is the actuator.
>
> The **Lateral Movement Controller** is exactly this "brain + steering muscle" — but encoded as mathematical equations. It takes the errors as input and outputs the steering angle $\delta_f$.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bolded Constants & Metrics)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| $e_y$ | Lateral (cross-track) error | **m (meters)** |
| $e_\psi$ | Heading (yaw) error | **rad (radians)** |
| $\delta_f$ | Front steering angle | **rad (radians)** |
| $L$ | Wheelbase | **m** (e.g., **2.5 m – 3.0 m**) |
| $v$ | Vehicle longitudinal speed | **m/s** |
| $l_d$ | Look-ahead distance | **m** |
| $k$ | Controller gain | **dimensionless / 1/m** |
| $C_\alpha$ | Cornering stiffness | **N/rad** (≈ **80000 N/rad** typical) |

---

## 1.4 Why Lateral Control Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)**: Highway ADAS feature.
> - **Lane Centering**: Tesla Autopilot, GM Super Cruise.
> - **Autonomous Lane Change**: Pre-requisite for Level 3+ autonomy.
> - **Path Tracking in GPS-Denied Zones**: Industrial AGVs, warehouse robots.
> - **Platooning**: Convoys of trucks maintaining lateral alignment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Track Problem: Longitudinal vs. Lateral Control

Modern autonomous driving stacks split vehicle control into two independent loops:

1. **Longitudinal Controller** → Manages **throttle & brake** to control speed ($v$) and longitudinal position.
2. **Lateral Controller** → Manages **steering angle** ($\delta_f$) to control lateral position ($y$) and heading ($\psi$).

> This decoupling is valid because at moderate speeds, the lateral and longitudinal dynamics are weakly coupled (small slip angles assumption).

---

## 2.2 The Kinematic Bicycle Model — Foundation of All Lateral Controllers

The kinematic bicycle model lumps the two front wheels into a single wheel at the center of the front axle, and similarly for the rear axle. It is the **most-tested model in KTU exams** for this module.

The governing equations are:

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos(\psi)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v \sin(\psi)
$$

$$
\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L} \tan(\delta_f)
$$

> **Why these equations make sense:**
> - $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ are pure velocity projections on the world frame.
> - $\dot{\psi}$ (yaw rate) is derived from the **Ackermann steering geometry**: a car turning at radius $R$ has $\tan(\delta_f) = L / R$, and $\dot{\psi} = v / R$, hence $\dot{\psi} = (v/L) \tan(\delta_f)$.

---

## 2.3 The Three Pillars of Lateral Control (KTU High-Yield)

### Pillar 1: **Pure Pursuit Controller** (Geometric)
- **How it works**: Pretends a "ghost" target point exists on the path at a look-ahead distance $l_d$ ahead. Steers the vehicle so its front axle follows an arc to that point.
- **Why it works**: Simple geometry, no PID tuning, robust to noisy paths.
- **Disadvantage**: Cuts corners; oscillatory at high speeds.

### Pillar 2: **Stanley Controller** (Used by Stanford's DARPA Grand Challenge winner)
- **How it works**: Combines lateral error AND heading error in a nonlinear feedback law.
- **Why it works**: Cross-track error is corrected by a steering term proportional to $e_y$, plus a heading alignment term.
- **Disadvantage**: Can be aggressive at low speeds (singularity at $v \to 0$).

### Pillar 3: **Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) / PID**
- **How it works**: Linearize the dynamic bicycle model around the reference trajectory; design state-feedback using Riccati equation.
- **Why it works**: Optimal control, handles constraints via MPC extension.
- **Disadvantage**: Requires accurate vehicle parameters ($m$, $I_z$, $C_\alpha$).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the table below. Every line is a potential 2 or 7 mark question.**

| # | Controller / Concept | Key Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bicycle Model Yaw Rate | $\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L} \tan(\delta_f)$ | $\dot{\psi}$ rad/s, $v$ m/s, $L$ m, $\delta_f$ rad | Kinematic, no slip |
| 2 | Pure Pursuit Steering | $\delta_f = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 L \sin(\alpha)}{l_d}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = angle to goal point, $l_d$ = look-ahead | $\alpha$ in **rad** |
| 3 | Stanley Steering Law | $\delta_f(t) = \theta_e(t) + \arctan\!\left(\frac{k \cdot e_y(t)}{v(t)}\right)$ | $\theta_e$ = heading error, $k$ = gain, $v$ m/s | $\arctan$ in **rad** |
| 4 | Look-ahead Distance | $l_d = k_{ld} \cdot v$ | $k_{ld}$ in **seconds** (time headway) | Speed-adaptive |
| 5 | Dynamic Bicycle Yaw Rate | $I_z \ddot{\psi} = l_f F_{y,f} \cos(\delta_f) - l_r F_{y,r}$ | $I_z$ kg·m², $l_f, l_r$ m, $F_y$ N | Uses tire forces |
| 6 | Lateral Tire Force (Linear) | $F_{y,i} = C_{\alpha,i} \cdot \alpha_i$ | $C_\alpha$ N/rad, $\alpha$ rad | Small-angle regime |
| 7 | Slip Angle Front | $\alpha_f = \delta_f - \arctan\!\left(\frac{v_y + l_f \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right)$ | rad | From geometry |
| 8 | Slip Angle Rear | $\alpha_r = -\arctan\!\left(\frac{v_y - l_r \dot{\psi}}{v_x}\right)$ | rad | From geometry |
| 9 | Lateral Error Update | $\dot{e_y} = v \sin(e_\psi) + v_x \sin(\psi - \psi_{ref})$ | m/s | Error dynamics |
| 10 | Heading Error Update | $\dot{e_\psi} = \dot{\psi} - \dot{\psi}_{ref}$ | rad/s | Yaw tracking |
| 11 | Radius of Curvature | $R = \frac{L}{\tan(\delta_f)}$ | m | For path generation |
| 12 | Curvature $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{\tan(\delta_f)}{L}$ | 1/m | Inverse of radius |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE**: The above table uses parentheses and `arctan` to avoid the literal pipe `|` symbol, which would break markdown table rendering. For exam answers, write $\arctan$ or $\tan^{-1}$ as the inverse tangent.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADAS Production**: Bosch, Continental, and Mobileye use Stanley-like + LQR hybrid controllers in production LKA systems.
- **Autonomous Trucks (TuSimple, Waymo Via)**: LQR-MPC controllers with 2-second prediction horizon.
- **Agricultural Robotics**: Pure Pursuit is the workhorse in John Deere and AGCO tractors.
- **Racing & Drifting**: Model Predictive Control (MPC) with nonlinear bicycle model.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Kinematic Bicycle Model from First Principles

### Step 1: Ackermann Steering Geometry Setup
Consider a 4-wheel vehicle. The two front wheels turn by an angle $\delta_f$ (assuming small slip, we use the average front wheel angle). The wheelbase is $L = l_f + l_r$, where $l_f$ is the distance from the CoG to the front axle and $l_r$ is the distance from the CoG to the rear axle.

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies on the line perpendicular to the rear axle, at a distance $R$ from the rear axle, where:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\tan(\delta_f)}
$$

### Step 2: Velocity Constraints
Since the rear wheels are non-steering, they roll in their longitudinal direction. Therefore, the velocity of the rear axle is purely longitudinal:

$$
v_r = v \quad \text{(speed at CoG projected to rear axle)}
$$

The yaw rate of the vehicle, $\dot{\psi}$, is the angular velocity around the ICR. By rigid-body rotation:

$$
\dot{\psi} = \frac{v_r}{R} = \frac{v}{R}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute R
Substituting the expression for $R$ from Step 1:

$$
\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{\frac{L}{\tan(\delta_f)}} = \frac{v \tan(\delta_f)}{L}
$$

This is the **fundamental yaw rate equation** of the bicycle model.

### Step 4: World-Frame Position Derivatives
The vehicle's heading is $\psi$ measured counterclockwise from the x-axis. The longitudinal velocity $v$ projects onto the world frame as:

$$
\dot{x} = v \cos(\psi)
$$

$$
\dot{y} = v \sin(\psi)
$$

> **Logic**: The body's x-axis is rotated by $\psi$ relative to the world x-axis, so we rotate the body-frame velocity vector $(v, 0)$ by $\psi$ using the standard 2D rotation matrix:
> $$
> \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\psi) & -\sin(\psi) \\ \sin(\psi) & \cos(\psi) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} v\cos\psi \\ v\sin\psi \end{bmatrix}
> $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stanley Controller Steering Law

### Step 1: Define Errors in the Path-Frame
Let the reference path be a curve with tangent angle $\psi_{ref}(s)$, parameterized by arc length $s$. Define:

- **Heading error**: $e_\psi = \psi - \psi_{ref}$
- **Lateral error**: $e_y$ = signed perpendicular distance from vehicle CoG to the path.

### Step 2: Decompose the Steering Command
The Stanley controller proposes that the steering angle has **two additive components**:

$$
\delta_f = \delta_{heading} + \delta_{lateral}
$$

The first aligns the vehicle's heading to the path; the second corrects lateral offset.

### Step 3: Heading Alignment Term
For the vehicle to align with the path direction, we need a steering correction proportional to the heading error. A simple proportional law:

$$
\delta_{heading} = e_\psi
$$

### Step 4: Lateral Error Term — Geometric Argument
The cross-track error $e_y$ at the front axle, when projected to the steering geometry, requires a steering angle:

$$
\tan(\delta_{lateral}) = \frac{e_y}{d}
$$

where $d$ is some "effective" distance. Stanley's insight was to set $d = v / k$ where $k$ is a tunable gain. The intuition: at higher speeds, the controller should react more gently to the same lateral error (since the same $\delta$ produces a larger lateral acceleration). Thus:

$$
\tan(\delta_{lateral}) = \frac{k \cdot e_y}{v}
$$

$$
\delta_{lateral} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{k \cdot e_y}{v}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Combine

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_f(t) = e_\psi(t) + \arctan\!\left(\frac{k \cdot e_y(t)}{v(t)}\right)\;}
$$

### Step 6: Stability Intuition
- As $e_y \to 0$, the $\arctan$ term $\to 0$, leaving only heading correction.
- As $v \to 0$, the denominator causes the lateral term to saturate at $\pm \pi/2$ — a known **singularity** of Stanley at very low speeds, often mitigated by adding a small $\epsilon$ in the denominator: $\arctan(ke_y / (v + \epsilon))$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Pure Pursuit Controller

### Step 1: Setup the Pursuit Geometry
Place the look-ahead point $G$ on the path at distance $l_d$ ahead of the front axle. The line from the rear axle to $G$ makes an angle $\alpha$ with the vehicle's longitudinal axis.

### Step 2: Use the Inscribed Angle Theorem
The vehicle traces a circular arc from its current position to $G$. The chord of this arc has length $l_d$, and the radius is $R$. By the inscribed angle / chord-radius geometry:

$$
\frac{l_d}{\sin(2\alpha)} = 2R \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{l_d}{2 \sin(\alpha)}
$$

### Step 3: Equate to Bicycle Model
From the bicycle model, $R = L / \tan(\delta_f)$. Equating:

$$
\frac{L}{\tan(\delta_f)} = \frac{l_d}{2 \sin(\alpha)}
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Steering Angle

$$
\tan(\delta_f) = \frac{2 L \sin(\alpha)}{l_d}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\delta_f = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2 L \sin(\alpha)}{l_d}\right)\;}
$$

### Step 5: Look-Ahead Distance Selection
A common choice is speed-proportional look-ahead: $l_d = k_{ld} \cdot v$ where $k_{ld}$ is in **seconds** (typically **0.5 s to 1.5 s**).

> **Logic**: A larger $l_d$ produces smoother but less accurate tracking; a smaller $l_d$ produces tight tracking but oscillates.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Dynamic Bicycle Model — Lateral Force & Moment Balance

### Step 1: Newton's Second Law in the Lateral Direction
The lateral acceleration of the CoG is $a_y = m (\dot{v}_y + v_x \dot{\psi})$, where $v_y$ is the lateral velocity in the body frame.

$$
m a_y = F_{y,f} \cos(\delta_f) + F_{y,r}
$$

For small steering angles, $\cos(\delta_f) \approx 1$:

$$
m(\dot{v}_y + v_x \dot{\psi}) = F_{y,f} + F_{y,r}
$$

### Step 2: Yaw Moment Balance
The yaw moment about the CoG is balanced by the lateral forces multiplied by their lever arms:

$$
I_z \ddot{\psi} = l_f F_{y,f} - l_r F_{y,r}
$$

### Step 3: Linear Tire Model
For small slip angles, lateral tire force is linear in slip:

$$
F_{y,f} = C_{\alpha f} \cdot \alpha_f, \quad F_{y,r} = C_{\alpha r} \cdot \alpha_r
$$

### Step 4: Slip Angles
From velocity geometry:

$$
\alpha_f = \delta_f - \frac{v_y + l_f \dot{\psi}}{v_x}
$$

$$
\alpha_r = -\frac{v_y - l_r \dot{\psi}}{v_x}
$$

### Step 5: Final State-Space Form
Define state $\mathbf{x} = [v_y, \dot{\psi}]^T$ and input $u = \delta_f$. The system becomes:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -\frac{2 C_{\alpha f} + 2 C_{\alpha r}}{m v_x} & -v_x - \frac{2 C_{\alpha f} l_f - 2 C_{\alpha r} l_r}{m v_x} \\ -\frac{2 C_{\alpha f} l_f - 2 C_{\alpha r} l_r}{I_z v_x} & -\frac{2 C_{\alpha f} l_f^2 + 2 C_{\alpha r} l_r^2}{I_z v_x} \end{bmatrix}}_{A} \mathbf{x} + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \frac{2 C_{\alpha f}}{m} \\ \frac{2 C_{\alpha f} l_f}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}}_{B} u
$$

This is the linearized **dynamic bicycle model**, used directly in LQR and MPC lateral controller design.

> **Why this matters for KTU**: An examiner may ask you to "derive the A and B matrices" or to "comment on stability via eigenvalues of A" — both are 14-mark favorites.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

**Problem**: A vehicle with $L = 2.5$ m, $v = 15$ m/s, has a lateral error of $0.4$ m and heading error of $0.05$ rad. Using Stanley controller with $k = 1.0$, find $\delta_f$.

**Solution**:

Heading term: $\delta_{heading} = 0.05$ rad.

Lateral term: $\delta_{lateral} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{1.0 \cdot 0.4}{15}\right) = \arctan(0.0267) = 0.0267$ rad.

Total: $\delta_f = 0.05 + 0.0267 = 0.0767$ rad $\approx 4.39°$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Lateral Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refPath[Reference Path] --> errComp[Error Computation]
    vehicleState[Vehicle State xy psi] --> errComp
    errComp --> lErr[Lateral Error ey]
    errComp --> hErr[Heading Error epsi]
    lErr --> ctrlLogic[Controller Stanley or PP or LQR]
    hErr --> ctrlLogic
    ctrlLogic --> steerCmd[Steering Angle deltaf]
    steerCmd --> act[Steering Actuator]
    act --> dyn[Bicycle Model Dynamics]
    dyn --> vehicleState
    dyn --> poseOut[Vehicle Pose Output]
```

> **Reading the diagram**: The reference path and the actual vehicle state feed an error computation block. The errors ($e_y$, $e_\psi$) drive the controller, which outputs a steering command $\delta_f$. The actuator applies this to the vehicle dynamics (bicycle model), and the new state is fed back.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pure Pursuit Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pathPts[Path Waypoints] --> goalSelect[Goal Point Selection at look-ahead ld]
    vehiclePose[Current Vehicle Pose] --> goalSelect
    goalSelect --> alphaCalc[Compute Angle alpha to Goal]
    alphaCalc --> purePursuit[Pure Pursuit Formula]
    bicycleParams[Wheelbase L] --> purePursuit
    purePursuit --> steerAngle[Steering Angle deltaf]
    steerAngle --> applySteer[Apply to Front Wheels]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Dynamic Bicycle Model for LQR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    deltaf[Steering Angle deltaf] --> slipF[Slip Angle Front alpha_f]
    deltaf --> yawDyn[Yaw Dynamics]
    vy[Lateral Velocity vy] --> slipF
    yawRate[Yaw Rate psidot] --> slipF
    vy --> slipR[Slip Angle Rear alpha_r]
    yawRate --> slipR
    slipF --> tireF[Lateral Tire Force Front]
    slipR --> tireR[Lateral Tire Force Rear]
    tireF --> latEq[Newton Lateral F = ma]
    tireR --> latEq
    tireF --> yawEq[Yaw Moment Balance]
    tireR --> yawEq
    latEq --> vy
    yawEq --> yawRate
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart — Choosing the Right Lateral Controller

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Choose Lateral Controller] --> q1{Operating Speed?}
    q1 -- Low to Moderate, 0-15 m/s --> q2{Precision Required?}
    q1 -- High Speed, above 15 m/s --> q3{Vehicle Parameters Known?}
    q2 -- Smooth Path, Less Precision --> pp[Pure Pursuit]
    q2 -- Tight Tracking --> stan[Stanley Controller]
    q3 -- Yes --> lqr[LQR with Dynamic Model]
    q3 -- No --> mpc[MPC with Robust Tuning]
    pp --> out[Implement]
    stan --> out
    lqr --> out
    mpc --> out
```

---

## 4.5 State Machine — Lateral Controller Mode Switching

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Standby: Vehicle Started
    Standby --> Tracking: Path Available and Authorized
    Tracking --> LaneChange: Maneuver Requested
    LaneChange --> Tracking: Maneuver Complete
    Tracking --> EmergencyOverride: Obstacle Detected
    EmergencyOverride --> Tracking: Path Cleared
    Tracking --> Standby: Path Lost
    Standby --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the kinematic bicycle model. Write its three governing equations and explain the role of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The kinematic bicycle model is a simplified 2D representation of a 4-wheeled vehicle, where the two front wheels are combined into a single wheel at the center of the front axle, and similarly for the rear axle.

The three governing equations are:
- $\dot{x} = v \cos(\psi)$ — projection of longitudinal velocity on world x-axis.
- $\dot{y} = v \sin(\psi)$ — projection of longitudinal velocity on world y-axis.
- $\dot{\psi} = \frac{v}{L} \tan(\delta_f)$ — yaw rate from Ackermann steering geometry.

**Role**: (1 mark each for the three equations and their role)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**List any three differences between the Pure Pursuit and Stanley lateral controllers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each)**:

| # | Pure Pursuit | Stanley |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses a look-ahead point at distance $l_d$ ahead | Uses cross-track error $e_y$ and heading error $e_\psi$ |
| 2 | Geometric formulation based on arc to goal point | Nonlinear feedback with $\arctan$ term |
| 3 | No singularity at low speed; can fail at high speed | Singular at $v \to 0$; smooth at high speed |

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Note (KTU ESE 2024 Pattern)**: Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Stanley Controller Deep Dive

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper 2]`**

**(a)** With suitable derivations, explain the working of the **Stanley controller** for lateral vehicle control. Derive its steering law and discuss the role of the gain $k$ and the velocity $v$ in the lateral error term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle is traveling at $v = 20$ m/s on a highway. The Stanley controller has a gain $k = 0.8$. The vehicle's heading error is $e_\psi = 0.03$ rad and lateral error is $e_y = 0.5$ m. Compute the steering angle $\delta_f$ in degrees. What happens to the steering command if the vehicle suddenly decelerates to $v = 2$ m/s while keeping the same lateral error? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 Marks Detailed Solution**

1. **Introduction (1 mark)**: The Stanley controller is a nonlinear feedback lateral controller developed at Stanford University. It won the DARPA Grand Challenge (2005). It minimizes both heading error and cross-track error simultaneously.

2. **Error Definitions (1 mark)**: Define heading error $e_\psi = \psi - \psi_{ref}$ and lateral error $e_y$ as the perpendicular distance from the vehicle's CoG to the reference path.

3. **Derivation of Steering Law (3 marks)**:
   - Decompose $\delta_f = \delta_{heading} + \delta_{lateral}$.
   - Heading term: $\delta_{heading} = e_\psi$.
   - Lateral term: Using Ackermann geometry, $\tan(\delta_{lateral}) = \frac{e_y}{R_{eff}}$ with $R_{eff} = v/k$, so $\delta_{lateral} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{k e_y}{v}\right)$.
   - Final law: $\delta_f = e_\psi + \arctan\!\left(\frac{k e_y}{v}\right)$.

4. **Role of $k$ and $v$ (2 marks)**:
   - $k$ is the cross-track gain: higher $k$ → more aggressive correction → faster convergence but more oscillation.
   - $v$ in the denominator makes the controller **speed-adaptive**: at high speeds, the same $e_y$ produces a smaller correction (smoother); at low speeds, the correction grows large, ensuring precise tracking in parking scenarios.

**(b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution**

**At $v = 20$ m/s:**

Heading term: $\delta_{heading} = 0.03$ rad.

Lateral term: $\delta_{lateral} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.8 \times 0.5}{20}\right) = \arctan(0.02) = 0.0200$ rad.

Total: $\delta_f = 0.03 + 0.0200 = 0.0500$ rad.

Convert to degrees: $\delta_f = 0.0500 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 2.86°$.

**At $v = 2$ m/s (decelerated):**

Lateral term: $\delta_{lateral} = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.8 \times 0.5}{2}\right) = \arctan(0.2) = 0.1974$ rad.

Total: $\delta_f = 0.03 + 0.1974 = 0.2274$ rad $= 13.03°$.

**Observation (1 mark for this insight)**: The steering command jumped from $2.86°$ to $13.03°$ — a **4.5× increase** — when speed dropped to one-tenth. This is the speed-adaptive behavior, but it also reveals the **low-speed singularity** of the Stanley controller, which is why a regularization term $\epsilon$ is added in the denominator in production systems: $\arctan\!\left(\frac{k e_y}{v + \epsilon}\right)$.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Pure Pursuit & Dynamic Model

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 - Model Paper 1]`**

**(a)** With a neat geometric diagram, derive the steering law of the **Pure Pursuit** lateral controller. State its advantages and disadvantages compared to a Stanley controller. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a vehicle with $m = 1500$ kg, $I_z = 2500$ kg·m², $l_f = 1.2$ m, $l_r = 1.3$ m, $C_{\alpha f} = 80000$ N/rad, $C_{\alpha r} = 80000$ N/rad, traveling at $v_x = 25$ m/s with steering angle $\delta_f = 0.05$ rad, write down the dynamic bicycle model in state-space form $[\dot{v}_y, \ddot{\psi}]^T = A [v_y, \dot{\psi}]^T + B \delta_f$. Identify the entries of the A matrix. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 Marks Detailed Solution**

1. **Geometric Setup (2 marks)**: Draw a diagram with rear axle at origin, front axle at distance $L$, look-ahead point $G$ at distance $l_d$ ahead of front axle. The line from rear axle to $G$ makes angle $\alpha$ with vehicle heading.

2. **Derivation (3 marks)**:
   - Chord-radius relation: $l_d = 2R \sin(\alpha) \Rightarrow R = \frac{l_d}{2\sin\alpha}$.
   - Bicycle model: $R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta_f}$.
   - Equate: $\frac{L}{\tan\delta_f} = \frac{l_d}{2\sin\alpha}$.
   - Solve: $\delta_f = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2L\sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$.

3. **Comparison (2 marks)**:
   - **Advantages of Pure Pursuit**: Simpler, no PID tuning, robust to path noise, no singularity at low speed.
   - **Disadvantages**: Cuts corners (systematic offset), can oscillate at high speed if $l_d$ is too small, less accurate than Stanley.
   - **Advantages of Stanley**: Better tracking accuracy, combines heading + lateral errors, smoother at high speed.
   - **Disadvantages of Stanley**: Singular at $v \to 0$, requires careful gain $k$ tuning.

**(b) — 7 Marks State-Space Solution**

Using the formulas derived in Section 3.4:

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} -\frac{2C_{\alpha f} + 2C_{\alpha r}}{m v_x} & -v_x - \frac{2C_{\alpha f} l_f - 2C_{\alpha r} l_r}{m v_x} \\ -\frac{2C_{\alpha f} l_f - 2C_{\alpha r} l_r}{I_z v_x} & -\frac{2C_{\alpha f} l_f^2 + 2C_{\alpha r} l_r^2}{I_z v_x} \end{bmatrix}, \quad B = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{2C_{\alpha f}}{m} \\ \frac{2C_{\alpha f} l_f}{I_z} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute common coefficients (1 mark)**:

- $2C_{\alpha f} + 2C_{\alpha r} = 320000$ N/rad
- $2C_{\alpha f} l_f - 2C_{\alpha r} l_r = 80000 \times 1.2 - 80000 \times 1.3 = 96000 - 104000 = -8000$ N
- $2C_{\alpha f} l_f^2 + 2C_{\alpha r} l_r^2 = 2 \times 80000 \times (1.44 + 1.69) = 160000 \times 3.13 = 500800$

**Step 2 — Compute A entries (3 marks)**:

$$
A_{11} = -\frac{320000}{1500 \times 25} = -\frac{320000}{37500} = -8.533
$$

$$
A_{12} = -25 - \frac{-8000}{1500 \times 25} = -25 + 0.00213 = -24.998
$$

$$
A_{21} = -\frac{-8000}{2500 \times 25} = \frac{8000}{62500} = 0.128
$$

$$
A_{22} = -\frac{500800}{2500 \times 25} = -\frac{500800}{62500} = -8.013
$$

**Step 3 — Compute B entries (1 mark)**:

$$
B_1 = \frac{2 \times 80000}{1500} = 106.67
$$

$$
B_2 = \frac{2 \times 80000 \times 1.2}{2500} = 76.8
$$

**Step 4 — Final State-Space (1 mark)**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{v}_y \\ \ddot{\psi} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8.533 & -24.998 \\ 0.128 & -8.013 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v_y \\ \dot{\psi} \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 106.67 \\ 76.8 \end{bmatrix} \delta_f
$$

**Step 5 — Stability Check (1 mark)**: Both eigenvalues of $A$ have negative real parts (trace negative, determinant positive), so the open-loop system is **stable**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are lost:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units in $\arctan$ arguments**: Stanley's lateral term $\arctan\!\left(\frac{k e_y}{v}\right)$ — the argument is **dimensionless** only if $k$ has units of $1/\text{s}$. If $k$ is unitless, you must include a calibration constant. Many students write $k$ as a pure number and lose 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing arc length $s$ with look-ahead distance $l_d$**: $l_d$ is the straight-line chord from front axle to goal point, **not** the arc length along the path.
>
> 3. **Forgetting $\cos(\delta_f) \approx 1$ in dynamic model**: The full model has $\cos(\delta_f)$ on the front tire force. Examiners expect you to state this small-angle assumption explicitly.
>
> 4. **Mixing up slip angles**: $\alpha_f$ and $\alpha_r$ have **opposite sign conventions** depending on the textbook. Always define your convention first.
>
> 5. **In numerical problems, forgetting to convert rad → degrees** when the question asks for degrees — loses 1 full mark.
>
> 6. **Stanley singularity at $v = 0$**: If a question mentions parking or very low speed, you **must** mention the $\epsilon$ regularization or switch to Pure Pursuit. Examiners test this explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Lateral Movement Controller**

- ☐ The **kinematic bicycle model** has 3 equations: $\dot{x} = v\cos\psi$, $\dot{y} = v\sin\psi$, $\dot{\psi} = (v/L)\tan\delta_f$.
- ☐ The **dynamic bicycle model** includes $m(\dot{v}_y + v_x\dot\psi) = F_{y,f} + F_{y,r}$ and $I_z\ddot\psi = l_f F_{y,f} - l_r F_{y,r}$.
- ☐ **Lateral tire force** (linear regime): $F_{y,i} = C_{\alpha i} \alpha_i$, with $C_\alpha \approx 80000$ N/rad for passenger cars.
- ☐ **Pure Pursuit**: $\delta_f = \arctan\!\left(\frac{2L\sin\alpha}{l_d}\right)$; look-ahead $l_d = k_{ld} v$ with $k_{ld} \in [0.5, 1.5]$ s.
- ☐ **Stanley**: $\delta_f = e_\psi + \arctan\!\left(\frac{k e_y}{v + \epsilon}\right)$; speed-adaptive, has low-speed singularity.
- ☐ **Slip angles**: $\alpha_f = \delta_f - (v_y + l_f \dot\psi)/v_x$; $\alpha_r = -(v_y - l_r \dot\psi)/v_x$.
- ☐ **Curvature**: $\kappa = \tan(\delta_f)/L = 1/R$.
- ☐ **Controller choice**: Pure Pursuit for low-speed, simple paths; Stanley for highway lane keeping; LQR/MPC for high-precision, high-speed, model-rich applications.
- ☐ **Stability check**: Eigenvalues of $A$ (in state-space form) must have negative real parts.
- ☐ **Singularity awareness**: Always add $\epsilon$ to denominators containing $v$ when $v$ can approach zero.
- ☐ **Units matter**: $\delta$ in **rad**, $\psi$ in **rad**, $v$ in **m/s**, $L$ in **m**, $C_\alpha$ in **N/rad**.
- ☐ **Real-world gains**: $k$ (Stanley) typically $0.5$ to $2.0$; $k_{ld}$ (Pure Pursuit) typically $0.5$ to $1.5$ s.

---

**End of Notes — Lateral Movement Controller (Module 3, OEMUT723)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lateral Position Controller
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lateral Position Controller

# Lateral Position Controller — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lateral Position Controller (LPC):** A closed-loop feedback control subsystem in an automotive navigation stack that computes the corrective steering command required to keep the vehicle's actual lateral (cross-track) position and heading aligned with a desired reference trajectory or lane centerline.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Automotive Navigation and Control* (OEMUT723), Module 3 covers vehicle motion control, where the Lateral Position Controller is the principal output stage that interfaces the higher-level **path planner** with the **steering actuator**. It takes as inputs the **cross-track error ($e_y$)**, the **heading error ($e_\psi$)**, and optionally the **lateral velocity ($v_y$)** and **yaw rate ($r$)**, and produces the steering wheel angle command ($\delta_{cmd}$) or the front-wheel steering angle ($\delta_f$).

### 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are **cycling on a narrow one-meter-wide painted line on a road**. You keep your eyes fixed on a point a few meters ahead on the line (preview). If the line starts curving right, you gently rotate your handlebars right; if the line drifts left, you counter-steer left. You never yank the wheel — your corrections are smooth and proportional to how far you have drifted. The **Lateral Position Controller is the automated version of that rider's brain and hands**, working in milliseconds using math instead of human reflex.

The controller must balance **two conflicting objectives**:
1. **Accuracy** — drive the cross-track error to zero.
2. **Comfort** — keep steering rate and lateral acceleration within acceptable bounds (typical comfort envelope: lateral acceleration $\leq 0.3g$ for passenger comfort, lateral jerk $\leq 0.9 \, \text{m/s}^3$).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Look-ahead distance | $L_d$ | 5 – 20 | m |
