7. **Filtering & Post-processing:** Apply median filter, left-right consistency check, and sub-pixel refinement (e.g., quadratic interpolation around the SAD minimum) to suppress mismatches.
8. **Application Layer (Obstacle Detection / SLAM):** Feed the depth map into higher-level modules such as 3D object detection (e.g., pseudo-LiDAR from stereo) or visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM, VINS-Mono with stereo extension).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities in the table below are **exam-critical**. Use `$\vert$` for absolute value to avoid Markdown table conflicts.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z \;=\; \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $Z$: depth, $f$: focal length (px), $B$: baseline, $d$: disparity (px) | Core depth-from-disparity inversion |
| 2 | $d \;=\; u_L \;-\; u_R$ | $u_L, u_R$: x-coordinates in rectified images | Disparity definition |
| 3 | $\Delta Z \;=\; \dfrac{Z^2}{f \cdot B}$ | $\Delta Z$: depth resolution at range $Z$ | Quantifies accuracy degradation with distance |
| 4 | $Z_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d_{\min}} \;=\; \dfrac{f \cdot B}{1}$ | Minimum measurable disparity $\geq 1$ px | Maximum sensing range |
| 5 | $Z_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d_{\max}}$ | Maximum disparity before image boundary | Minimum sensing distance |
| 6 | $x_L \;=\; f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad x_R \;=\; f \cdot \dfrac{X-B}{Z}$ | $X$: lateral world coordinate | Derivation of disparity |
| 7 | $\mathbf{K} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Intrinsic camera matrix | Pixel-to-ray conversion |
| 8 | $\mathbf{P} \;=\; \mathbf{K} \,[\mathbf{R} \,\vert\, \mathbf{t}]$ | Camera projection matrix | World-to-pixel projection |
| 9 | $\text{Reprojection Error} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sqrt{(u_i - \hat{u}_i)^2 + (v_i - \hat{v}_i)^2}$ | $N$: calibration points; $(\hat{u}_i, \hat{v}_i)$ detected, $(u_i, v_i)$ re-projected | Calibration quality metric |
| 10 | $\mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_L \;=\; \mathbf{x}_R$ | $\mathbf{F}$: fundamental matrix, $\mathbf{x}_{L/R}$: homogeneous image points | Epipolar constraint |
| 11 | $\mathbf{E} \;=\; [\mathbf{t}]_\times \, \mathbf{R}$ | $\mathbf{E}$: essential matrix, $[\mathbf{t}]_\times$: skew-symmetric of $\mathbf{t}$ | Relates calibrated image coordinates |
| 12 | $\text{SSD}(u, v) \;=\; \sum_{(i, j) \in W} [I_L(u{+}i, v{+}j) - I_R(u{-}d{+}i, v{+}j)]^2$ | $W$: matching window, $d$: candidate disparity | Block-matching cost |
| 13 | $\text{Census}(p) \;=\; \bigotimes_{q \in N(p)} [I(q) > I(p)]$ | $N(p)$: neighbourhood of pixel $p$, $\bigotimes$: bit concatenation | Robust descriptor under illumination change |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why rectify?** Reducing the search from 2D to 1D cuts computation by orders of magnitude — critical for embedded automotive SoCs (e.g., TI TDA4, NVIDIA Orin) that must hit 30 fps.
- **Why sub-pixel refinement?** Single-pixel disparity resolution bounds $Z$ in steps of $Z^2/(fB)$ — at $Z = 50\,\text{m}$ with $fB = 3500\,\text{px}\cdot\text{mm}$, a 1-px error is ~0.7 m. Sub-pixel interpolation (parabolic fit) recovers 0.1–0.2 px accuracy.
- **Why census transform?** Automotive scenes have uncontrolled illumination (tunnels, glare, headlights). Census is invariant to monotonic brightness changes, making matching robust.
- **Why epipolar constraint?** Reduces correspondence search from $W \times H$ candidates to a single line — mathematically enforced by the **fundamental matrix** $\mathbf{F}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **ADAS Level 2–3:** AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking), ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control) target ranging.
- **Autonomous Driving (L4/L5):** Pseudo-LiDAR generation for 3D object detection (e.g., Stereo R-CNN, DSGN).
- **Robotics:** Visual SLAM, pick-and-place depth.
- **AR/VR:** Real-time depth for occlusion handling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depth from Disparity (The Pinhole Stereo Model)

**Assumptions:** Two identical cameras with focal length $f$, separated by baseline $B$ along the X-axis, both with optical axes perfectly parallel (after rectification). Optical centers are $O_L = (-B/2, 0, 0)$ and $O_R = (+B/2, 0, 0)$. A 3D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ projects onto the left and right image planes at $x_L$ and $x_R$ respectively.

**Geometric Setup:** Using similar triangles in the pinhole projection plane (X-Z plane):

$$\frac{x_L}{f} = \frac{X - (-B/2)}{Z} = \frac{X + B/2}{Z}$$

For the right camera:

$$\frac{x_R}{f} = \frac{X - B/2}{Z}$$

**Step-by-Step Algebraic Manipulation:**

$$\frac{x_L}{f} - \frac{x_R}{f} = \frac{X + B/2}{Z} - \frac{X - B/2}{Z}$$

The numerators simplify:

$$\frac{x_L - x_R}{f} = \frac{(X + B/2) - (X - B/2)}{Z} = \frac{B}{Z}$$

By definition, **disparity** is $d = x_L - x_R$. Therefore:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}$$

Inverting to recover depth:

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The derivation relies on the **law of similar triangles** — the ratio of image-plane displacement to focal length equals the ratio of baseline (in 3D) to depth. This is the foundational equation every KTU stereo-vision problem is built on.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Depth Resolution as a Function of Range

Differentiate $Z = \dfrac{fB}{d}$ with respect to $d$, treating $f$ and $B$ as constants:

$$\frac{dZ}{dd} = -\frac{fB}{d^2}$$

Substituting $Z = fB/d \Rightarrow d = fB/Z$:

$$\frac{dZ}{dd} = -\frac{fB}{(fB/Z)^2} = -\frac{Z^2}{fB}$$

Taking the absolute value (since depth resolution is a magnitude):

$$\boxed{\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Depth error grows **quadratically** with range — this is the *single most important practical limitation* of stereo vision and a favourite KTU question. Doubling the distance degrades depth precision by a factor of four.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum and Minimum Sensing Range

The disparity $d$ is bounded by pixel constraints. The minimum measurable disparity is **1 pixel** (sub-pixel techniques can go lower, but 1 px is the conservative bound). The maximum disparity occurs when $x_R$ is at the left image edge and $x_L$ is at the right edge — bounded by image width $W$.

**Maximum range** (smallest non-zero disparity):

$$d_{\min} = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; Z_{\max} = \frac{f \cdot B}{1} = f \cdot B$$

**Minimum range** (largest disparity, limited by image width $W$ and search range):

$$d_{\max} = W \;\Rightarrow\; Z_{\min} = \frac{f \cdot B}{W}$$

> **Conversion logic:** To increase $Z_{\max}$ for long-range automotive sensing (≥ 80 m), designers must **increase baseline $B$** (e.g., wide-baseline stereo on vehicle roof) **or use a telephoto lens** (larger $f$). Trade-off: wider baseline creates occlusion gaps at close range.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pixel-to-Ray Projection (Intrinsic Matrix Origin)

A 3D world point $(X, Y, Z)$ projects to a pixel $(u, v)$ via:

$$u = f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x, \quad v = f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y$$

In homogeneous form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{K}} \begin{bmatrix} X/Z \\ Y/Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \sim \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$ encodes the camera's *internal* geometry — focal lengths in pixel units and the principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ which is generally *not* at the image center due to lens-mounting tolerances.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A forward-facing automotive stereo rig has focal length $f = 1000\,\text{px}$, baseline $B = 200\,\text{mm}$. A detected vehicle ahead has disparity $d = 25\,\text{px}$. Calculate (a) depth, (b) depth resolution at that depth, (c) lateral position $X$ if its left-image pixel is $u_L = 640\,\text{px}$ and image center is $c_x = 640\,\text{px}$.

**(a) Depth:**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{1000 \cdot 200}{25} = 8000 \;\text{mm} = 8 \;\text{m}$$

**(b) Depth resolution (per 1 px disparity error):**

$$\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} = \frac{(8000)^2}{1000 \cdot 200} = \frac{64\,000\,000}{200\,000} = 320 \;\text{mm} = 0.32 \;\text{m}$$

**(c) Lateral position $X$:**

$$x_L = u_L - c_x = 640 - 640 = 0 \;\text{px}$$
$$\frac{x_L}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} \;\Rightarrow\; X = \frac{x_L \cdot Z}{f} = \frac{0 \cdot 8000}{1000} = 0 \;\text{mm}$$

The vehicle is directly ahead on the camera's optical axis.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table of Derived Quantities

| Quantity | Formula | Behaviour with $Z$ | Behaviour with $B$ | Behaviour with $f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Depth $Z$ | $fB/d$ | — | $\propto B$ | $\propto f$ |
| Depth resolution $\Delta Z$ | $Z^2/(fB)$ | $\propto Z^2$ | $\propto 1/B$ | $\propto 1/f$ |
| Disparity $d$ | $fB/Z$ | $\propto 1/Z$ | $\propto B$ | $\propto f$ |
| Range $Z_{\max}$ | $fB$ | — | $\propto B$ | $\propto f$ |
| Lateral reprojection $X$ | $x_L \cdot Z / f$ | $\propto Z$ | — | $\propto 1/f$ |

> **Conversion logic:** Every engineering design choice (longer baseline vs. larger sensor vs. telephoto lens) trades one capability against another. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate these trade-offs explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Full Stereo Vision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scene World Points 3D] --> B1[Left Camera CMOS Sensor]
    A --> B2[Right Camera CMOS Sensor]
    B1 --> C[Hardware Sync Trigger]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D1[Image Preprocessing Denoising]
    D1 --> D2[Camera Calibration Zhang Checkerboard]
    D2 --> D3[Stereo Rectification Epipolar Alignment]
    D3 --> E1[Feature Detection Census ORB]
    E1 --> E2[Cost Computation SAD SSD Census]
    E2 --> E3[Disparity Map Generation Semi Global Block Matching]
    E3 --> E4[Left Right Consistency Check]
    E4 --> E5[Sub Pixel Refinement Parabolic Fit]
    E5 --> F[Depth Map via Z fB over d]
    F --> G1[Obstacle Detection]
    F --> G2[Free Space Segmentation]
    F --> G3[Visual Odometry SLAM]
    F --> G4[Pseudo LiDAR for 3D Object Detection]
```

> **Diagram Note:** This flowchart depicts the canonical *automotive stereo perception pipeline* as expected in KTU Module 4 viva voce. Each node corresponds to a discrete algorithm stage; the labels are kept alphanumeric without nested Markdown formatting to comply with Mermaid's parser.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stereo Camera Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCENE["3D SCENE"]
        WP[World Point P X Y Z]
    end
    subgraph CAMERAS["CAMERA PAIR"]
        CL[Left Camera Optical Center OL]
        CR[Right Camera Optical Center OR]
    end
    subgraph PLANES["IMAGE PLANES AFTER RECTIFICATION"]
        IL[Left Image Plane]
        IR[Right Image Plane]
    end
    WP ---|ray xL| CL
    WP ---|ray xR| CR
    CL -->|focal f| IL
    CR -->|focal f| IR
    IL -->|pixel uL| M1[Disparity d uL minus uR]
    IR -->|pixel uR| M1
    M1 --> M2[Depth Z fB over d]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Rectified Stereo Geometry (Textual)

```
                Z (depth)
                ↑
                │
                │        P (X, Y, Z)
                │       ╱│╲
                │      ╱ │ ╲
                │     ╱  │  ╲
                │    ╱   │   ╲
                │   ╱    │    ╲
                │  ╱     │     ╲
       ─────────┼─●──────┼──────●─────────→  X (lateral)
              O_L│   x_L │x_R   O_R
              (-B/2)     │     (+B/2)
                         │
                      Image
                      Planes
                  (focal length f
                   in front of each
                   optical center)
```

- **Solid lines** from $O_L$ and $O_R$ to $P$ are the projection rays.
- **$x_L$ and $x_R$** are the image-plane intersection points.
- **$d = x_L - x_R$** is the disparity.
- **$B$** is the baseline, **$f$** is the focal length, **$Z$** is the perpendicular distance from baseline to $P$.

### 4.4 Mermaid — Calibration Pipeline (Intrinsic + Extrinsic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Collect Checkerboard Images 20 to 30 views] --> K2[Detect Corners findChessboardCorners]
    K2 --> K3[Estimate Intrinsics K and distortion]
    K3 --> K4[Stereo Calibration R t between cameras]
    K4 --> K5[Stereo Rectification R1 R2 P1 P2 Q]
    K5 --> K6[Compute Rectification Maps remap]
    K6 --> K7[Online Image Rectification Real Time]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]**

**Q1. Define stereo vision and state its role in autonomous vehicles. List two advantages over monocular vision.**
**Model Answer:**
Stereo vision is a passive depth-sensing technique that reconstructs 3D scene geometry from two images captured simultaneously by cameras separated by a known baseline $B$. In autonomous vehicles, it provides metric per-pixel depth for obstacle detection, free-space estimation, and 3D object localization.

*Advantages over monocular vision:*
1. **Metric depth** is recovered directly (no prior scene-scale ambiguity).
2. Does not require learned monocular depth priors or known object dimensions.

---

**Q2. What is disparity? Write the relationship between disparity, baseline, focal length, and depth.**
**Model Answer:**
Disparity $d$ is the horizontal pixel shift of a 3D world point between the left and right rectified images: $d = u_L - u_R$. The fundamental relationship is:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

where $f$ = focal length (in pixels), $B$ = baseline (in mm), and $Z$ = depth of the point.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has TWO sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for depth $Z$ in terms of focal length $f$, baseline $B$, and disparity $d$ for a rectified stereo camera pair. State all assumptions.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Both cameras are identical (same $f$, same sensor).
2. Optical axes are parallel (perfect rectification).
3. Image planes are co-planar and aligned.
4. Lenses are distortion-free (or distortion already corrected).
5. Baseline $B$ is along the camera's X-axis.

**Derivation:** (See Section 3.1 for full expansion)

Using similar triangles in the X-Z plane:

$$\frac{x_L}{f} = \frac{X + B/2}{Z} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{x_R}{f} = \frac{X - B/2}{Z}$$

Subtracting:

$$\frac{x_L - x_R}{f} = \frac{B}{Z} \;\Rightarrow\; d = \frac{fB}{Z} \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{Z = \frac{fB}{d}}$$

**(b) A vehicle-mounted stereo camera has $f = 1500\,\text{px}$ and $B = 250\,\text{mm}$. For an obstacle detected at disparity $d = 30\,\text{px}$: (i) compute the depth; (ii) compute the depth resolution at this range; (iii) comment on how the resolution changes if the baseline is doubled.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Depth:**

$$Z = \frac{fB}{d} = \frac{1500 \times 250}{30} = \frac{375\,000}{30} = 12\,500\;\text{mm} = 12.5\;\text{m}$$

**(ii) Depth resolution:**

$$\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{fB} = \frac{(12\,500)^2}{1500 \times 250} = \frac{156\,250\,000}{375\,000} \approx 416.67\;\text{mm} \approx 0.42\;\text{m}$$

**(iii) Effect of doubling baseline ($B \rightarrow 2B$):**

New depth $Z' = \dfrac{f \cdot 2B}{d} = 2Z = 25\;\text{m}$ (the same physical object now appears at half the disparity, but the *measured* depth scales). For the same physical object:
- New resolution: $\Delta Z' = \dfrac{(2Z)^2}{f \cdot 2B} = \dfrac{4Z^2}{2fB} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{Z^2}{fB} = 2\Delta Z$

Wait — re-evaluating with the obstacle at the same physical $Z = 12.5\,\text{m}$: the *measured* disparity drops to $d' = d/2 = 15\,\text{px}$. Then:

$$\Delta Z' = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot 2B} = \frac{1}{2}\Delta Z \approx 0.21\;\text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling the baseline **halves** the depth resolution (i.e., improves precision by 2×) at the cost of halved disparity (worse sub-pixel behaviour) and larger occlusion regions for near objects.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With the aid of a block diagram, explain the complete stereo vision processing pipeline for an automotive perception system. Highlight the role of rectification and disparity computation.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The automotive stereo vision pipeline consists of the following sequential stages (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram):

1. **Synchronized Image Capture:** Two cameras $C_L$ and $C_R$ capture images simultaneously via hardware trigger.
2. **Preprocessing:** Lens undistortion (using calibration parameters $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2$).
3. **Stereo Rectification:** Re-project image planes so that corresponding points share the same $v$-coordinate. **Role:** reduces correspondence search from 2D → 1D, simplifies the fundamental matrix to a purely horizontal shift.
4. **Feature/Cost Computation:** Census transform or SAD over a sliding window along each epipolar line.
5. **Disparity Map Generation:** Semi-Global Block Matching (SGBM) or Elastic Net optimization enforces smoothness + pixel-wise matching simultaneously.
6. **Post-processing:** Left-right consistency check, median filtering, sub-pixel refinement.
7. **Depth Recovery:** $Z = fB/d$ applied per pixel.

**Rectification's role:** Without rectification, a point in the left image could correspond to *any* pixel in the right image — an $O(N^2)$ search. After rectification, the search reduces to a single horizontal line — $O(N)$ — making real-time 30 fps operation feasible on automotive SoCs.

**Disparity computation's role:** It is the *bridge* between 2D image data and 3D metric depth. Errors here propagate to all downstream modules (obstacle detection, SLAM, etc.).

**(b) Discuss the major sources of error in stereo vision-based depth estimation for autonomous vehicles. How does each affect safety-critical functions?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| # | Error Source | Effect on Depth | Safety Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quantization (1-px disparity)** | $\Delta Z = Z^2/(fB)$ grows quadratically | Misses distant obstacles → AEB failure at highway speeds |
| 2 | **Camera calibration error** | Systematic bias in $f, B, R, t$ | Mis-localizes vehicles in adjacent lanes → unsafe lane changes |
| 3 | **Temporal desynchronization** | False disparities from motion | Spurious obstacles → false braking (phantom AEB) |
| 4 | **Textureless regions** (sky, white walls) | Matching fails → holes in depth map | Free-space falsely classified as obstacle |
| 5 | **Repetitive textures** (fences, brick walls) | Wrong correspondences → gross outliers | Erroneous depths → wrong trajectory planning |
| 6 | **Occlusions** (near foreground) | No right-image match | Depth holes near ego vehicle edges |
| 7 | **Lighting changes** (sun glare, headlight bloom) | Sensor saturation → loss of features | Perception blackout |
| 8 | **Lens contamination** (dirt, water droplets) | Localized matching failure | Blind spots in critical zones |

**Mitigation strategies:** Multi-frame temporal filtering, sensor fusion with radar/lidar, redundancy via multiple stereo pairs, automatic camera-cleaning systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Stereo Vision Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** $f$ in *pixels* and $B$ in *millimetres* must be reconciled; examiners deduct 1 mark if $Z$ is reported with inconsistent units.
> 2. **Not stating rectification assumptions:** Any derivation of $Z = fB/d$ without explicitly stating *rectified, parallel-axis, identical cameras* loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $x_L$ with $u_L$:** $x_L$ is image-plane coordinate (mm); $u_L$ is pixel coordinate. Use $\mathbf{K}$ to convert.
> 4. **Wrong sign of disparity:** Some texts define $d = x_R - x_L$; check your convention against the derivation.
> 5. **Ignoring quadratic depth-error growth:** Examiners love asking "why is stereo inaccurate at long range?" — answer with $\Delta Z \propto Z^2$.
> 6. **Skipping the fundamental matrix explanation:** A bare mention of "epipolar geometry" without $\mathbf{F}$ or $\mathbf{E}$ loses marks.
> 7. **Not linking to automotive application:** Always connect the math to ADAS/autonomy context — KTU rewards application-oriented answers.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stereo Vision**

- ✅ Stereo vision = **two cameras** recovering depth via **triangulation**; passive, no active illumination.
- ✅ **Core equation:** $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$; disparity $d = u_L - u_R$.
- ✅ **Depth resolution grows quadratically** with range: $\Delta Z = \dfrac{Z^2}{fB}$.
- ✅ Pipeline order: **Capture → Calibrate → Rectify → Match → Disparity → Triangulate → Filter**.
- ✅ **Rectification** makes epipolar lines horizontal → 1D search.
- ✅ **Intrinsic matrix $\mathbf{K}$** contains $f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y$.
- ✅ **Extrinsic $[R \mid t]$** = rotation + translation between cameras (from stereo calibration).
- ✅ **Epipolar constraint:** $\mathbf{x}_R^T \mathbf{F} \mathbf{x}_L = 0$; fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$ has 7 DOF.
- ✅ **Essential matrix** $\mathbf{E} = [\mathbf{t}]_\times \mathbf{R}$ (for calibrated cameras).
- ✅ Robust matching descriptors for automotive scenes: **Census transform** (illumination-invariant).
- ✅ Common algorithms: **SGM/SGBM** for disparity, **Zhang's method** for calibration, **ORB-SLAM** for visual odometry.
- ✅ Engineering trade-offs: **larger $B$** → better long-range resolution but worse close-range occlusion; **larger $f$** → longer range but narrower field of view.
- ✅ Automotive-grade challenges: **textureless roads, sun glare, motion, occlusion, dirt** — must be addressed in safety-critical design.
- ✅ Stereo vs LiDAR: stereo gives **dense color + depth**; LiDAR gives **sparse active 3D points** — they are *complementary*, not substitutes.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions** before any derivation: rectified, parallel axes, identical cameras.
- ✅ Units discipline: keep $f$ [px], $B$ [mm], $Z$ [mm or m], $d$ [px] consistent throughout.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Road Recognition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Road Recognition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Road Recognition** is the computational process by which an intelligent vehicle, equipped with onboard perception sensors (cameras, LiDAR, radar, GNSS/IMU and HD maps), perceives, classifies and semantically interprets the drivable surface, lane geometry, road boundaries, road type (urban / rural / highway / unpaved), and road surface condition (dry / wet / icy / snow-covered) in real time. The output of this perception stage is a structured, machine-readable representation of the road scene that is directly consumed by downstream modules such as **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)**, **Lane Departure Warning (LDW)**, **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**, **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)**, **Autonomous Lane Changing**, and **Path Planning** in ADAS and self-driving stacks.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, Road Recognition is positioned as a **sub-module of Environmental Perception**, which itself sits between *Sensor Data Acquisition* and *Behavioural Planning* in the classical Sense–Plan–Act paradigm of autonomous driving.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (Examination Verbatim):**
> *Road Recognition is the process of identifying the drivable region, lane markings, road boundaries, road type and surface condition from multi-sensor data, so as to generate a structured road model for use by vehicle navigation and control algorithms.*

## 1.2 The Sense → Perceive → Recognise → Act Pipeline

Road Recognition is the **third logical stage** of the autonomous vehicle stack. It converts low-level sensor measurements into high-level semantic understanding.

| Stage | Function | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| Sense | Raw sensor capture | Image frames, point clouds, radar tracks |
| Perceive | Signal processing, filtering | Edges, blobs, segments |
| **Recognise** | **Semantic interpretation** | **Road mask, lane lines, road class** |
| Act | Decision + control | Steering, braking, acceleration commands |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you are driving on a road you have never seen before, at night, in rain. Even with poor visibility, your brain instantly recognises **where the road is**, **where the lanes are**, **where the road ends**, and **what kind of surface you are on** (asphalt, gravel, puddle). Road Recognition is the *artificial equivalent* of that biological perception. The car uses cameras as "eyes", neural networks as the "visual cortex", and LiDAR/radar as "touch in the dark", and fuses them all to build a *mental map* of the road — frame by frame, 30 to 60 times a second.

A useful everyday analogy is **"a child learning to stay inside the lines while colouring"**. The child does not solve geometry; the child uses visual cues (the printed boundary) and motor feedback. The vehicle does essentially the same — it follows the *visual boundary of the road* using *mathematical feedback* (steering control).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

For accurate road recognition the following standard metrics are universally used:

- **Camera focal length $f$** — typically **3 mm to 12 mm** for automotive forward-looking cameras.
- **Camera Field of View (FOV)** — typically **40° (narrow / telephoto) to 100° (wide)** horizontally.
- **Image resolution** — standard **1 MP (1280×960)** to **8 MP (3840×2160)**.
- **Frame rate** — **30 fps** (typical) to **60 fps** (high-speed ego-motion).
- **LiDAR range** — **100 m to 250 m** for automotive grade units.
- **Acceptable road-recognition latency** — **≤ 100 ms** for L2 ADAS, **≤ 50 ms** for L3+ autonomy.
- **Lane marking pixel tolerance** — typically **± 2 pixels** in the bird's-eye view.
- **Minimum detectable lane width** — **2.5 m to 3.5 m** (Indian / European standard lane).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sub-Modules of Road Recognition

Road Recognition is not a single algorithm — it is a **family of cooperating sub-tasks** that jointly produce the road model.

1. **Image Pre-processing**
   - Noise reduction (Gaussian filter, bilateral filter)
   - Distortion correction (intrinsic + extrinsic camera calibration)
   - Colour-space conversion (RGB → HSV, RGB → grayscale, RGB → LUV)
   - Histogram equalisation for illumination invariance

2. **Region of Interest (ROI) Selection**
   - Trapezoidal ROI in front of ego vehicle (lower 40–60 % of frame)
   - Reduces computational cost and eliminates sky / non-road regions

3. **Edge / Feature Extraction**
   - **Canny Edge Detector** (gradient-based)
   - **Sobel / Prewitt operators** (horizontal & vertical gradient kernels)
   - **Gabor filters** (texture / orientation)
   - **Colour-based segmentation** (yellow / white lane pixel clustering)

4. **Geometric Transformation — Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM)**
   - Removes the perspective effect, converting the front-facing image into a *bird's-eye view (BEV)*
   - Enables metric measurement of lane width, vehicle offset, curvature

5. **Lane Line Detection**
   - **Hough Transform** (classical)
   - **Probabilistic Hough Transform** (efficient)
   - **LaneNet / SCNN / UFLD** (deep learning)

6. **Lane Tracking**
   - **Kalman Filter**
   - **Particle Filter**
   - **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)**
   - **SORT / DeepSORT trackers**

7. **Road Segmentation (Drivable Area)**
   - **Semantic Segmentation CNNs** — SegNet, U-Net, DeepLab, HRNet, BiSeNet
   - Outputs a per-pixel class map: *road / vehicle / pedestrian / sidewalk / sky*

8. **Road Type Classification**
   - **CNN classifiers** — VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet on ROI patches
   - Classes: *highway / urban / rural / tunnel / unpaved / bridge*

9. **Road Surface Condition Estimation**
   - Texture analysis + wheel-speed sensor fusion
   - Classes: *dry / wet / icy / snowy / muddy*

10. **Road Boundary / Curb Detection**
    - LiDAR-based curb extraction
    - Stereo-vision disparity-based boundary detection
    - HD-map matching with prior

11. **Vanishing Point Estimation**
    - Used for camera pitch/yaw correction
    - Statistical clustering of edge orientations

12. **Sensor Fusion**
    - Camera + LiDAR + Radar + GNSS + HD-Map
    - **Kalman / Extended Kalman / Particle Filter fusion**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examination-ready**. Master these for any KTU 2024 OEMUT723 question on Road Recognition.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Table 2.A — Road Recognition Core Equations}
\end{aligned}
$$

| # | Concept | Equation | Meaning of Terms |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Image projection (pinhole) | $u = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad v = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $u,v$ image coords; $X,Y,Z$ world coords; $f$ focal length |
| 2 | Canny edge gradient magnitude | $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | $G_x, G_y$ Sobel derivatives |
| 3 | Canny edge direction | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$ | Orientation of intensity gradient |
| 4 | Hough line (ρ,θ) | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | Distance $\rho$ and angle $\theta$ from origin |
| 5 | Inverse Perspective Map (IPM) | $X = \dfrac{(u - c_x)\,h}{f}, \quad Y = \dfrac{(v - c_y)\,h}{f}$ | $h$ = camera height; $c_x, c_y$ principal point |
| 6 | Camera pitch correction | $\alpha_{corr} = \alpha_{est} - \alpha_{ref}$ | $\alpha_{est}$ estimated pitch; $\alpha_{ref}$ reference |
| 7 | Lane curvature (clothoid model) | $y(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + a_3 x^3$ | Polynomial fit to detected lane pixels |
| 8 | Lane offset error | $e = y_{lane} - y_{vehicle}$ | Lateral deviation from lane centre |
| 9 | Heading error | $\Delta\psi = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)_{lane}$ | Angle between vehicle heading and lane tangent |
| 10 | Kalman state update | $\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1})$ | $K_k$ Kalman gain; $z_k$ measurement |
| 11 | IoU (segmentation quality) | $IoU = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $A$ predicted road mask, $B$ ground-truth |
| 12 | Dice / F1 score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2\,TP}{2\,TP + FP + FN}$ | Pixel-level road detection accuracy |
| 13 | Vanishing point (two-line intersect) | $VP = L_1 \times L_2$ (cross product in homogeneous) | $L_1, L_2$ two detected lane lines |
| 14 | Disparity (stereo) | $d = u_L - u_R, \quad Z = \dfrac{f\,B}{d}$ | $B$ = stereo baseline |
| 15 | Time-to-Line-Crossing | $TLC = \dfrac{y_{lat}}{\dot{y}_{lat}}$ | Time for vehicle to cross lane marker |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in markdown tables — never the bare `|` pipe inside cell math** (this breaks table rendering). This is enforced throughout the sheet above.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the "How"

- **ROI selection** is done because the sky, buildings and trees are *not road*. Ignoring them reduces false positives by **> 60 %**.
- **IPM** converts perspective foreshortening into a metric top-view. Without IPM, parallel lane lines appear to converge, which is mathematically misleading. With IPM, lane width can be measured in **metres**, not pixels.
- **Hough Transform** converts the *point-to-line detection problem* (hard, discontinuous) into a *peak-detection problem* in a 2-D accumulator (easy, continuous). This is a classic *problem reformulation* trick.
- **Kalman Filter** is used because a single-frame lane detection is noisy. By predicting the lane state from previous frames, the filter *denoises* and *extrapolates* through short occlusions.
- **Semantic segmentation** with deep CNNs is preferred over classical methods because roads are *visually diverse* (shadows, tyre marks, puddle reflections, occlusions). Neural networks learn these invariances from data.
- **Sensor fusion** is critical because no single sensor works in *all* conditions:
  - Camera fails in fog / night
  - LiDAR fails in heavy rain / snow
  - Radar has poor lateral resolution
  - GNSS fails in tunnels
  - **Fusion gives robustness** — the very definition of engineering safety.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Use of Road Recognition |
|---|---|
| ADAS — LDW / LKA | Warns or steers when vehicle leaves lane |
| ADAS — AEB | Identifies drivable corridor to avoid false braking |
| L3+ Autonomous Driving (Waymo, Tesla, Cruise) | Builds BEV road model for path planner |
| Highway Pilot (BMW, Mercedes) | Maintains lane + adapts speed |
| Off-Road Autonomy (agriculture, mining, defence) | Detects unpaved trails and obstacles |
| Smart Cities | Road condition monitoring, pothole detection |
| Insurance & Fleet Telematics | Driver behaviour scoring based on lane discipline |
| HD-Map Generation (Mobileye, Here) | Crowd-sourced road mapping |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM)

**Problem statement:** Given a pixel $(u, v)$ in the front-facing camera image, find its corresponding world-coordinate $(X, Y, Z)$ on the road plane.

### Step 1 — Pinhole Camera Projection

The standard pinhole model maps a 3-D world point $(X, Y, Z)$ to a 2-D image point $(u, v)$ using the intrinsic matrix $K$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{Z}\,
K \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
K = \begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{f_x X}{Z} + c_x, \quad
v = \frac{f_y Y}{Z} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $f_x, f_y$ convert metres to pixels, $c_x, c_y$ shift the principal point (where the optical axis pierces the image).

### Step 2 — Restrict the World to the Road Plane

The road is assumed flat with $Y = -h$ (the road is a constant height $h$ **below** the camera optical centre, since Y axis points down in camera frame). So $Y = -h$ is constant.

Substituting $Y = -h$ into the pinhole relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{f_y (-h)}{Z} + c_y
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
Z = \frac{-f_y\,h}{v - c_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for X and Z (the road-plane forward and lateral positions)

From the first equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u - c_x &= \frac{f_x X}{Z}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
X = \frac{(u - c_x)\,Z}{f_x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute $Z$ from Step 2 into the equation for $X$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{(u - c_x)}{f_x} \cdot \left(\frac{-f_y\,h}{v - c_y}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming square pixels (the common automotive-grade sensor assumption) where $f_x = f_y = f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \frac{-(u - c_x)\,h}{v - c_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

(For analytical simplicity one often absorbs the sign into the camera coordinate convention; many textbooks write $X = (u - c_x)h / (c_y - v)$.)

### Step 4 — Final Compact IPM Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(u,v) &= \frac{(u - c_x)\,h}{c_y - v} \\[4pt]
Z(u,v) &= \frac{f\,h}{c_y - v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical meaning:**
- $Z$ (forward distance from camera) is inversely proportional to $(c_y - v)$ — pixels near the horizon correspond to *very far* road; pixels near the bottom correspond to *very close* road. ✓ Matches the perspective intuition.
- $X$ (lateral offset) scales linearly with $(u - c_x)$ — pixels to the right of centre map to positive $X$ (right of vehicle). ✓
- $h$ (camera mounting height) controls the *scale* of the bird's-eye view.

### Step 5 — Practical Use — Lane Width & Offset

If two lane lines map to $X_L$ and $X_R$ in the BEV image, the **lane width** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{lane} = X_R - X_L
\end{aligned}
$$

The **lateral offset of ego vehicle from lane centre** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{lat} = \frac{X_R + X_L}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both are now in **metres**, which is exactly what a steering controller needs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vanishing Point from Two Parallel Lines

**Problem statement:** Two lane lines are parallel in the world. Find where they "meet" in the image.

### Step 1 — Line Representation in Image

Each detected lane is a Hough line $(\rho, \theta)$. In homogeneous form, a 2-D line is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a\,x + b\,y + c = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \mathbf{l} = (a, b, c)^{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

For line $i$, $a_i = \cos\theta_i$, $b_i = \sin\theta_i$, $c_i = -\rho_i$.

### Step 2 — Intersection of Two Lines

The intersection of two lines is the cross product of their homogeneous vectors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P} &= \mathbf{l}_1 \times \mathbf{l}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Writing out:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{P} = \begin{bmatrix} b_1 c_2 - b_2 c_1 \\ c_1 a_2 - c_2 a_1 \\ a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Euclidean Image Coordinates

The vanishing point $(u_{vp}, v_{vp})$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{vp} = \frac{P_x}{P_z}, \quad v_{vp} = \frac{P_y}{P_z}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Use of Vanishing Point in Road Recognition

- **Camera pitch correction:** If the vanishing point is at row $v_{vp}$, the camera pitch angle is $\alpha = \arctan\!\big((c_y - v_{vp}) / f\big)$.
- **Lane filtering:** A *real* lane must pass *close to* the vanishing point. Outlier lines (noise, shadows) can be discarded.
- **Drivable area truncation:** ROI is bounded vertically by the vanishing point — anything above it is sky, anything below is road.

> [!NOTE]
> The vanishing point is mathematically guaranteed to be a **single point** for **all** parallel 3-D lines (under a pinhole camera with zero lens distortion). This is the **Desargues Invariant Theorem** in projective geometry, which is a favourite KTU viva question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hough Transform Line Equation

**Problem statement:** Convert scattered edge pixels $(x_i, y_i)$ into a robust line estimate.

### Step 1 — From $(x, y)$ to $(\rho, \theta)$

For a fixed angle $\theta$, the set of all lines through a pixel $(x, y)$ is parametrised by $\rho$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

For each $(x_i, y_i)$, the function $\rho(\theta)$ traces a **sinusoid** in the $(\rho, \theta)$ accumulator.

### Step 2 — Voting

A 2-D accumulator $A(\rho, \theta)$ is built. For every edge pixel, the corresponding sinusoidal curve in $(\rho, \theta)$ space is *incremented by 1*.

### Step 3 — Peak Detection

Local maxima of $A(\rho, \theta)$ correspond to lines supported by many edge pixels.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(\rho^{*}, \theta^{*}) = \max_{\rho, \theta} A(\rho, \theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Computational Complexity

For an $N \times N$ image with $M$ edge pixels and $K$ discretised $\theta$ bins:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time} = \mathcal{O}(M \cdot K)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is *linear in pixels* and *linear in angle bins* — extremely efficient, which is why Hough Transform is still used in production lane-detection stacks (e.g., OpenCV `HoughLinesP`).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Lane Detect | Road Class | Boundary | Robustness | Speed | KTU Exam-Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Canny + Hough | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Low (shadows) | Very Fast | ★★★★ |
| Canny + IPM + Hough | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Medium | Fast | ★★★★★ |
| Color thresholding | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Low (faded paint) | Very Fast | ★★★ |
| Kalman-tracked Hough | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | High | Fast | ★★★★★ |
| Optical flow (Lucas–Kanade) | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | Medium | Fast | ★★★★ |
| CNN classifier (ResNet) | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | Very High | Medium | ★★★★ |
| Semantic Seg (U-Net) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Very High | Slow | ★★★★★ |
| LiDAR curb extraction | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | High | Medium | ★★★★ |
| Stereo-vision disparity | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | High | Slow | ★★★ |
| HD-Map matching | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Very High | Fast (if prior) | ★★★★ |
| End-to-End CNN (PilotNet) | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | High | Fast (inference) | ★★★ |
| Sensor-Fusion (Cam+LiDAR+HD) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | Highest | Medium | ★★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Road Recognition

The Mermaid diagram below maps the complete flow of a production-grade road recognition pipeline. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to comply with Mermaid syntax rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Camera Raw Frame 1280x960 30fps] --> B1[Lens Distortion Correction]
    A2[LiDAR Point Cloud 100m Range] --> C1[Ground Plane Segmentation]
    A3[Radar Tracks] --> D1[Dynamic Object Filter]
    A4[GNSS IMU HD Map] --> E1[Prior Road Geometry Lookup]

    B1 --> F1[Grayscale Conversion]
    B1 --> F2[HSV Colour Space Split]
    F1 --> G1[Gaussian Smoothing]
    G1 --> H1[Sobel Gradient Magnitude]
    H1 --> I1[Canny Non Max Suppression]
    I1 --> J1[Trapezoidal ROI Selection]
    F2 --> J1

    J1 --> K1[Inverse Perspective Mapping IPM]
    K1 --> L1[Bird's Eye View Image]
    L1 --> M1[Probabilistic Hough Transform]
    M1 --> N1[Lane Line Candidates rho theta]

    C1 --> O1[LiDAR Curb Extractor]
    O1 --> P1[Road Boundary Points]

    N1 --> Q1[Kalman Filter Lane Tracker]
    P1 --> Q1
    D1 --> Q1
    E1 --> Q1

    Q1 --> R1[Lane Polynomial Fit y ax3 bx2 cx d]
    R1 --> S1[Heading and Offset Error]

    L1 --> T1[Semantic Segmentation CNN U Net]
    T1 --> U1[Per Pixel Road Probability Map]
    U1 --> V1[Drivable Area Polygon]

    S1 --> W1[Road Model Output BEV]
    V1 --> W1
    W1 --> X1[Lane Keeping Assist LKA]
    W1 --> X2[Adaptive Cruise Control ACC]
    W1 --> X3[Path Planning Module]
    W1 --> X4[Automatic Emergency Braking AEB]
```

## 4.2 Classical vs Deep-Learning Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Classical Road Recognition] --> Q1[Canny Hough IPM]
    P1 --> Q2[Kalman Filter Tracking]
    P1 --> Q3[Hand Crafted Features HOG LBP]
    P2[Deep Learning Road Recognition] --> R1[CNN Classifier ResNet]
    P2 --> R2[Semantic Seg U Net DeepLab]
    P2 --> R3[End to End PilotNet]
    P3[Hybrid Production Stack] --> S1[Classical Front End for Speed]
    P3 --> S2[CNN Backend for Accuracy]
    P3 --> S3[Sensor Fusion for Robustness]
    Q1 --> P3
    R2 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Sensor-Fusion Topology for Road Recognition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Monocular Camera] --> F1[Vision Based Lane Mask]
    S2[Stereo Camera Pair] --> F2[Depth Based Road Surface]
    S3[LiDAR 360 degree] --> F3[Ground Plane Curb]
    S4[Millimetre Wave Radar] --> F4[Static Guard Rail Detection]
    S5[GNSS IMU] --> F5[Ego Pose and Velocity]
    S6[HD Map Prior] --> F6[Expected Lane Geometry]
    F1 --> FU[Central Sensor Fusion Node]
    F2 --> FU
    F3 --> FU
    F4 --> FU
    F5 --> FU
    F6 --> FU
    FU --> OUT[Unified Road Recognition Output]
```

## 4.4 Road-Recognition Decision Tree (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Camera Frame Arrives] --> B{Lighting OK}
    B -- Yes --> C[Run CNN Road Segmenter]
    B -- No --> D[Switch to LiDAR Only Mode]
    C --> E{At least 60 percent road pixels}
    E -- Yes --> F[Accept BEV Road Mask]
    E -- No --> G[Trigger Degraded Mode Warning]
    F --> H[Run Lane Detector on BEV]
    H --> I{At least 2 lane lines}
    I -- Yes --> J[Output to LKA]
    I -- No --> K[Predict Lanes from HD Map Prior]
    D --> L[Output to ACC only]
    G --> L
    K --> J
    J --> M[End of Frame Processing]
    L --> M
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Question patterns below are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for OEMUT723**:
> - Part A: 3-mark short answer
> - Part B: 14-mark descriptive (internal choice, two sub-parts of 7 marks each)
> - Bloom levels: Remember / Understand / Apply / Analyse as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy
> - All sub-parts are model-answered.

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define Road Recognition. List any three sub-tasks performed by a Road Recognition module.*

**Model Answer:**

Road Recognition is the process of identifying the drivable region, lane markings, road boundaries, road type and surface condition from multi-sensor data, so as to generate a structured road model for use by vehicle navigation and control algorithms.

The three primary sub-tasks are:
1. **Lane marking detection and tracking** — identification of painted lane lines using vision or LiDAR.
2. **Drivable-area segmentation** — pixel-level classification of road vs non-road regions using a CNN.
3. **Road type classification** — categorising the road as urban, rural, highway, or unpaved using a classifier CNN on the ROI patch.

*(Word count: ~ 70 — fits 3-mark answer.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*What is the role of Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM) in Road Recognition?*

**Model Answer:**

Inverse Perspective Mapping (IPM) is a geometric transformation that removes the foreshortening effect of a front-facing camera and remaps the image into a top-down (bird's-eye) view of the road.

Its role in Road Recognition is to:
- Convert pixel coordinates to metric road coordinates (metres).
- Allow direct measurement of lane width, vehicle offset and curvature.
- Simplify lane-line detection since parallel lane lines remain parallel in BEV (no convergence artefact).
- Reduce the search space for the lane tracker.

IPM assumes a flat road plane and a known camera height $h$ and pitch angle. The transformation equations are:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X(u,v) = \frac{(u - c_x)\,h}{c_y - v} \\[4pt]
Z(u,v) = \frac{f\,h}{c_y - v}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks, Internal Choice with B-B]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete pipeline of a vision-based Road Recognition system. List the role of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the Inverse Perspective Mapping equations for converting an image pixel $(u, v)$ to the road-plane world coordinates $(X, Z)$ for a forward-facing camera mounted at height $h$ with focal length $f$ and principal point $(c_x, c_y)$. State the assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — Block Diagram of Vision-Based Road Recognition

A vision-based Road Recognition pipeline consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Image Acquisition Block** — captures frames from a forward-facing CMOS camera at 30 fps.
2. **Pre-processing Block** — performs lens distortion correction, Gaussian smoothing and colour-space conversion.
3. **Region of Interest (ROI) Selection** — extracts the trapezoidal area in front of the vehicle to remove sky and irrelevant regions.
4. **Edge / Feature Extraction** — applies Canny / Sobel operators to obtain gradient edges; optionally extracts colour cues for yellow/white lane paint.
5. **Geometric Transformation — IPM** — converts the front view to a bird's-eye view, enabling metric measurement.
6. **Lane Detection Block** — uses Probabilistic Hough Transform to detect candidate lane lines $(\rho, \theta)$.
7. **Lane Tracking Block** — applies a Kalman Filter to temporally smooth detections and predict through occlusions.
8. **Road Segmentation Block** — uses a U-Net / DeepLab CNN to produce a per-pixel drivable-area mask.
9. **Road Type Classifier** — a ResNet-based CNN classifies the ROI patch as urban / rural / highway.
10. **Output / Road Model Block** — fuses all outputs into a structured BEV road model consumed by LKA, ACC and planner.

> The detailed flow is captured in the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

#### Model Solution to (b) — IPM Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. The road is a flat horizontal plane.
2. The camera is mounted at a known height $h$ above the road.
3. The camera is forward-facing, with optical axis parallel to the ground (zero pitch) — corrected for non-zero pitch in practice.
4. The pinhole camera model holds (no lens distortion).
5. The intrinsic parameters $f, c_x, c_y$ are known from calibration.

**Step 1 — Pinhole Projection**

The intrinsic projection is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{f_x X}{Z} + c_x, \quad v = \frac{f_y Y}{Z} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Road-Plane Constraint $Y = -h$**

The road plane is at a constant $Y = -h$ in camera frame (Y points downward by convention):
$$
\begin{aligned}
v = \frac{-f\,h}{Z} + c_y
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
Z = \frac{f\,h}{c_y - v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $X$**

From the first pinhole relation:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X = \frac{(u - c_x)\,Z}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $Z$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X = \frac{(u - c_x)}{f} \cdot \frac{f\,h}{c_y - v}
= \frac{(u - c_x)\,h}{c_y - v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final IPM equations:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,X(u,v) = \dfrac{(u - c_x)\,h}{c_y - v}, \qquad Z(u,v) = \dfrac{f\,h}{c_y - v}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of sanity:**
- A pixel directly below the principal point ($u = c_x$) gives $X = 0$ — correct, on the vehicle's centre line.
- A pixel at the horizon ($v \to 0$, assuming origin at top-left) gives $Z \to \infty$ — correct, the road extends to infinity.
- A pixel at the principal point $v = c_y$ (camera horizon line) gives $Z = \infty$ — the IPM becomes singular, which physically means the road never appears *at* the horizon line in the image.

This completes the derivation. (Examiner note: the singularity at $v = c_y$ is *expected* and corresponds to the mathematical horizon; production code clips $v$ slightly below $c_y$ to avoid division by zero.)

---

### Question B-B (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the Hough Transform algorithm for lane line detection. Derive the $(\rho, \theta)$ line equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vehicle is driving on a road with two parallel lane lines. The lines are detected in the BEV image with Hough parameters $(\rho_1, \theta_1)$ and $(\rho_2, \theta_2)$. Compute the lane width and the heading error of the vehicle. State the formula for the time-to-line-crossing (TLC). *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a) — Hough Transform for Lane Detection

**Concept:** The Hough Transform converts the *point-to-line fitting* problem (in image space) into a *peak-detection* problem (in parameter space), making line detection robust to noise and gaps in edges.

**Step 1 — Parametric Form of a Line**

In image space, a line is written as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
y = m\,x + c
\end{aligned}
$$

This is unbounded in $m$ for vertical lines, so the **normal form** is preferred:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\rho$ is the perpendicular distance from origin to the line, and $\theta$ is the angle the perpendicular makes with the $x$-axis.

**Step 2 — Voting**

For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$, the function $\rho(\theta) = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$ is a sinusoid in $(\rho, \theta)$ space. A 2-D accumulator array $A(\rho, \theta)$ is incremented along this sinusoid.

**Step 3 — Peak Detection**

A *local maximum* of $A(\rho, \theta)$ corresponds to a line supported by many edge pixels:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(\rho^{*}, \theta^{*}) = \arg\max_{\rho, \theta} A(\rho, \theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Probabilistic Hough Transform (PHT)**

Instead of voting for all edge pixels, PHT samples a random subset. This reduces complexity from $\mathcal{O}(M \cdot K)$ to approximately $\mathcal{O}(M' \cdot K)$ with $M' \ll M$, while retaining peak accuracy. OpenCV's `HoughLinesP` uses PHT and returns line *segments* (start–end) which is preferred for finite-length lane lines.

**Step 5 — Use in Road Recognition**

For each frame, PHT returns a set of candidate $(\rho, \theta)$. Pairs of lines with appropriate angular separation (close to 0° in BEV, or symmetric about the vertical axis in front-view) are grouped as **left and right lane lines**. Their intersection with the bottom of the ROI defines lane pixels.

#### Model Solution to (b) — Lane Width, Heading Error, TLC

**Given:** Hough parameters of two lane lines in BEV: $(\rho_1, \theta_1)$ and $(\rho_2, \theta_2)$.

**Step 1 — Convert $(\rho, \theta)$ to Line Equation**

In normal form:
$$
\begin{aligned}
x\cos\theta_1 + y\sin\theta_1 = \rho_1 \quad &\text{(left lane)} \\
x\cos\theta_2 + y\sin\theta_2 = \rho_2 \quad &\text{(right lane)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Lane Width**

The perpendicular distance between two parallel lines (or near-parallel) is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{lane} = \frac{\vert \rho_2 - \rho_1 \vert}{\sqrt{\cos^2\theta_{avg} + \sin^2\theta_{avg}}}
= \vert \rho_2 - \rho_1 \vert \quad \text{(since denominator = 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For non-parallel lines, the lane width is measured at a fixed longitudinal distance $Z = Z_0$ by computing $X$ on each line:
$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{left}(Z_0), \quad X_{right}(Z_0), \quad W = X_{right} - X_{left}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heading Error of Vehicle**

The heading error is the angle between the vehicle's longitudinal axis and the lane centre line tangent. In BEV, the lane centre line is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{centre}(x) = \frac{y_{left}(x) + y_{right}(x)}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Heading error:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\psi = \arctan\!\left(\frac{d y_{centre}}{d x}\right) = \frac{\theta_1 + \theta_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC)**

TLC is the time until the vehicle's wheel touches a lane line, given current lateral velocity $\dot{y}_{lat}$ and current lateral offset $y_{lat}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
TLC = \frac{y_{lat}}{\dot{y}_{lat}}
\end{aligned}
$$

TLC < 0.5 s typically triggers **Lane Departure Warning (LDW)**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Road Recognition Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the IPM singularity at $v = c_y$** — Examiners explicitly look for the *horizon singularity* comment. If you don't mention it, expect −1 mark.
> 2. **Not stating assumptions** — IPM derivation is incomplete without listing the *flat road, known camera height, zero pitch* assumptions. Most students skip this; it costs a full 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing road segmentation with lane detection** — Road segmentation = pixel-level drivable-area mask. Lane detection = line-level identification of lane markings. They are *different* sub-tasks. Examiners test this distinction.
> 4. **Writing only CNN-based answers** — KTU 2024 Scheme expects a *balanced* treatment: classical (Canny + Hough + Kalman) **and** deep learning (U-Net / SegNet). Pure-CNN answers get partial credit.
> 5. **Missing units in numerical questions** — Lane width must be quoted in **metres**, focal length in **mm or pixels**, distance in **m**, frame rate in **fps**. A correct number without unit = 0 marks in some strict valuation sessions.
> 6. **Skipping the sensor-fusion discussion** — Modern ADAS questions almost always test *why fusion is needed* and *which sensor fails in which condition*. A 1-line answer is not enough.
> 7. **Confusing Hough $\rho$ with physical distance** — In *image* Hough, $\rho$ is in *pixels*. Only after IPM does it become *metres*.
> 8. **Not drawing the block diagram in part-(a) questions** — A neat labelled block diagram is mandatory for full marks; a 14-mark question with only text loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bulletins — Read 30 Minutes Before KTU Exam**

- **Road Recognition** is the perception stage that converts raw sensor data into a structured road model (drivable area + lane geometry + road type + surface condition).
- The pipeline is: **Sense → Pre-process → ROI → Edge/Feature → IPM → Detect → Track → Segment → Classify → Fuse → Output**.
- **Three sensor pillars:** Camera (texture & colour), LiDAR (geometry), Radar (velocity & range). GNSS/IMU/HD-Map provide *prior*.
- **Pinhole projection** is the foundation: $u = fX/Z + c_x$, $v = fY/Z + c_y$.
- **IPM** maps image pixels to road-plane metres using $X = (u - c_x)h/(c_y - v)$ and $Z = fh/(c_y - v)$.
- IPM has a **singularity at the horizon** ($v = c_y$); production code clips to avoid divide-by-zero.
- **Canny Edge Detector** uses gradient + non-max suppression + hysteresis thresholding.
- **Hough Transform** uses $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$; accumulator peaks = lines.
- **Probabilistic Hough Transform (PHT)** is faster and returns *line segments*, ideal for lanes.
- **Kalman Filter** state $x = [y, \dot{y}, \psi, \dot{\psi}]^{T}$ is the standard lane tracker.
- **Vanishing point** is the image-space intersection of all parallel 3-D lines; used for camera pitch correction and lane validation.
- **Semantic segmentation CNNs** (U-Net, SegNet, DeepLab) output per-pixel road probability; threshold gives drivable-area mask.
- **IoU** = $\vert A \cap B \vert / \vert A \cup B \vert$ is the standard road-segmentation quality metric.
- **Lane width standard** is **2.5–3.5 m** (India / Europe); **3.7 m** (US highway lane).
- **Acceptable end-to-end latency** ≤ **100 ms** for L2 ADAS, ≤ **50 ms** for L3+.
- **Camera height $h$** typically **1.2–1.5 m** for passenger cars; affects IPM scale.
- **TLC** = lateral offset / lateral velocity; TLC < 0.5 s triggers LDW.
- **Sensor failure matrix:** Camera → fog/night; LiDAR → heavy rain/snow; Radar → poor lateral res.; GNSS → tunnel. **Fusion** is the engineering answer.
- **HD-Map** provides *prior* lane geometry that constrains the vision output, reducing false positives.
- **End-to-End CNNs** (PilotNet, NVIDIA) directly map image → steering; *interpretability* is a drawback.
- **Real-world stacks:** Mobileye EyeQ, NVIDIA Drive, Tesla HW3, Waymo Driver, Apollo (Baidu) — all implement a *variant* of the pipeline above.
- **Examiner's mantra:** *Always draw the block diagram, always state assumptions, always write units, always discuss fusion.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Recognition: Traffic Lights and Signals
Pedestrian Recognition: Basic Principle, Architecture, and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pedestrian Recognition: Basic Principle, Architecture, and Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedestrian Recognition (PR)** is a critical sub-system of **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** and **Autonomous Driving Stacks** that uses **computer vision, machine learning, and multi-sensor fusion** to detect, localize, classify, and track human pedestrians in the vicinity of a vehicle, enabling **Collision Avoidance / Mitigation (AEB – Autonomous Emergency Braking)** interventions.

According to the KTU 2024 OEMUT723 syllabus, pedestrian recognition is classified under **Vision-Based Driver Assistance** and forms the perceptual backbone of **Active Safety Systems** defined by **Euro NCAP**, **ISO 22078**, and **NHTSA Pedestrian Safety Standards**. The system must operate in real time, with end-to-end latency typically **≤ 100 ms**, and detection accuracy evaluated using **Precision**, **Recall**, and **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** metrics.

The four canonical operational sub-tasks of any pedestrian recognition pipeline are:

1. **Detection** – *Where is the pedestrian?* (Bounding box regression)
2. **Classification** – *Is it actually a pedestrian?* (Binary / multi-class labeling)
3. **Localization** – *How far and at what bearing?* (Depth estimation in world coordinates)
4. **Tracking & Trajectory Prediction** – *Where will the pedestrian be in T seconds?* (Kalman Filter, SORT, DeepSORT)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy – The "Crossing Guard Brain"**
>
> Imagine a human crossing guard standing at an intersection. Without thinking in equations, the guard's brain automatically performs six steps:
> 1. **Scans** the road visually (image acquisition).
> 2. **Spots** anything shaped like a person (detection).
> 3. **Confirms** it's not a lamp-post or a billboard (classification).
> 4. **Estimates** how far the person is and how fast they are moving (localization + velocity).
> 5. **Predicts** if they will step onto the road (trajectory).
> 6. **Acts** – honks, waves, or holds up a stop sign (actuation).
>
> A vehicle's pedestrian recognition system does **exactly the same six steps**, but uses **cameras, radar, and neural networks** instead of eyes and instinct. The "stop sign" is replaced by an **electronic command** to the brakes, steering, or pre-tensioner systems.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU-Mandated Performance Benchmarks:**
> - **Detection Range:** Up to **60 m** for far-field, **30 m** for wide-angle
> - **Frame Rate:** **≥ 30 fps** (real-time)
> - **Latency Budget:** **≤ 100 ms** end-to-end
> - **Operating Illuminance:** **0.1 lux – 100,000 lux** (day/night capable)
> - **Minimum Pedestrian Size on Image:** **~30 × 80 pixels** at 1080p resolution
> - **Pedestrian Detection Accuracy Target (Euro NCAP):** **≥ 90%** at 30 m
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR):** **< 1%** in urban scenarios

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Stack – Layered View

A modern pedestrian recognition system is typically organized into a **four-layer architecture**:

> **Layer 1: Sensor Perception Layer (Hardware)**
> - Mono / Stereo Camera, Thermal (Far-Infrared) Camera
> - 77 GHz mmWave Radar, 3D LiDAR
> - Vehicle Bus Interfaces: **CAN, CAN-FD, Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)**

> **Layer 2: Pre-Processing Layer**
> - Image rectification, noise filtering, ROI extraction
> - Radar/LiDAR point-cloud clustering

> **Layer 3: Detection & Classification Layer (Algorithm Core)**
> - Classical: **HOG + SVM**, Haar Cascade
> - Deep Learning: **YOLO, SSD, Faster R-CNN, CenterNet, EfficientDet**
> - Sensor Fusion: **EKF, late-fusion CNNs, transformer-based fusion**

> **Layer 4: Decision & Actuation Layer**
> - Risk assessment → AEB trigger, warning (HMI), seatbelt pre-tensioning

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (HOG-SVM Pipeline)

**Why HOG?** Because human silhouettes have a strong, stable gradient structure. The **Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG)** descriptor captures this by counting gradient orientations in localized cells.

| Step | Operation | Why It Matters |
|------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Grayscale normalization** ($\gamma = $ compression) | Removes illumination bias |
| 2 | **Gradient computation** $\nabla I = (G_x, G_y)$ | Edges encode shape |
| 3 | **Cell histogram (e.g., 8×8 px, 9 bins)** | Translation-invariant feature |
| 4 | **Block normalization (2×2 cells, L2-norm)** | Contrast invariance |
| 5 | **Feature vector flattening** | Feeds linear classifier |
| 6 | **SVM classification (hyperplane)** | Pedestrian vs background |
| 7 | **Sliding-window detection (multi-scale)** | Find pedestrian in image |
| 8 | **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)** | Remove duplicate boxes |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **core mathematical machinery** a KTU 2024 examiner expects a student to write down in the answer script for a 14-mark question on pedestrian recognition.

| Concept | Formula | Symbols | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Image Gradient Magnitude | $\vert G \vert = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | $G_x, G_y$ | Intensity/pixel |
| Gradient Orientation | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{G_y}{G_x}\right)$ | $\theta$ | **radians** (0 to $\pi$) |
| Pinhole Camera Projection | $x = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}, \quad y = f \cdot \dfrac{Y}{Z}$ | $f$ = focal length | $X,Y,Z$ world coords; $x,y$ image |
| Stereo Depth | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $B$ = baseline, $d$ = disparity | **meters** |
| Radar Range Equation | $R = \dfrac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$ | $c$ = speed of light, $\tau$ = round-trip | **meters** |
| Doppler Velocity | $v_r = \dfrac{f_D \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | $f_D$ = Doppler shift, $\lambda$ = wavelength | **m/s** |
| IoU (Intersection over Union) | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | $A, B$ bounding boxes | **dimensionless, 0–1** |
| Precision | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | TP = True Positive | **0–1** |
| Recall | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | FN = False Negative | **0–1** |
| Time-to-Collision (TTC) | $\text{TTC} = \dfrac{d}{\dot{d}}$ | $d$ = range, $\dot{d}$ = range-rate | **seconds** |
| YOLO Confidence Score | $C = P(\text{obj}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred}}^{\text{truth}}$ | $P(\text{obj})$ = objectness | **0–1** |
| Kalman State Update | $\hat{x}_{k \mid k} = \hat{x}_{k \mid k-1} + K_k (z_k - H\hat{x}_{k \mid k-1})$ | $K_k$ = Kalman gain | recursive estimator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The TTC equation is **the single most important formula** linking pedestrian detection to AEB. If $\text{TTC} < \text{TTC}_{\text{threshold}}$ (typically **1.4–3.0 s** depending on test protocol), the controller triggers emergency braking. A wrong TTC directly translates to *misjudged braking* — either too late (collision) or too early (false alarm / rear-end risk).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pinhole Camera Model and Pedestrian Localization

A vehicle-mounted monocular camera must convert a 2-D pixel observation into a 3-D world coordinate to compute the distance to a pedestrian.

**Step 1 — Establish the camera coordinate frame.**
Place the optical center of the camera at the origin, with $Z$ pointing along the optical axis (forward through the windshield), $X$ to the right, and $Y$ downward. A pedestrian foot-point is at world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the central projection (perspective) transformation.**
A 3-D point $(X, Y, Z)$ projects onto the image plane at $(x, y)$ via the focal length $f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \\
y &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ with principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ and pixel pitch $(p_x, p_y)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{x}{p_x} + c_x = \frac{f \cdot X}{Z \cdot p_x} + c_x \\
v &= \frac{y}{p_y} + c_y = \frac{f \cdot Y}{Z \cdot p_y} + c_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Intrinsic Camera Matrix K.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z} \begin{bmatrix} f/p_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f/p_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z} \mathbf{K} \mathbf{P}_{\text{world}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert to recover depth $Z$ for a pedestrian of known height $H$.**
If the pedestrian's head and feet are detected at image rows $v_{\text{head}}$ and $v_{\text{feet}}$, and the real-world height is $H$ (≈ **1.7 m** average adult), then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= Y_{\text{feet}} - Y_{\text{head}} \\
Y_{\text{feet}} &= \frac{(v_{\text{feet}} - c_y) \cdot Z}{f/p_y} \\
Y_{\text{head}} &= \frac{(v_{\text{head}} - c_y) \cdot Z}{f/p_y} \\
\Rightarrow H &= \frac{(v_{\text{feet}} - v_{\text{head}}) \cdot Z \cdot p_y}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $Z$ (forward distance to pedestrian).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad Z = \frac{f \cdot H}{p_y \, \vert v_{\text{feet}} - v_{\text{head}} \vert} = \frac{f \cdot H}{p_y \, \Delta v}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Box-Clean Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,Z_{\text{ped}} \;=\; \dfrac{f \cdot H_{\text{ped}}}{p_y \cdot \Delta v_{\text{pixel}}}\,}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** When a pedestrian is **far away**, $\Delta v_{\text{pixel}}$ is **small**, so $Z$ becomes **large** — consistent with physical intuition. This is the *monocular cue* used in **Mobileye's EyeQ** and similar ADAS vision SoCs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Time-to-Collision (TTC) for AEB Trigger

**Given:** Radar reports range $R$ and closing rate $\dot{R}$ (negative when approaching).

**Step 1:** The time remaining before impact at constant closing rate is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TTC} = \frac{R}{\vert \dot{R} \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply KTU-Euro NCAP threshold logic. Let $a_{\text{max,brake}}$ = maximum achievable deceleration of the host vehicle (typical sedan: **8 m/s²**). The minimum stopping distance covered from current state is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{stop}} = \dot{R} \cdot \tau_{\text{sys}} + \frac{\dot{R}^2}{2 \, a_{\text{max,brake}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\tau_{\text{sys}}$ = system latency (sensor + ECU + actuator, typically **0.3 s**).

**Step 3:** Compare $R$ with $d_{\text{stop}}$. If $R < d_{\text{stop}}$, the host **cannot avoid collision by braking alone**, so trigger AEB.

$$
\boxed{\,\text{AEB Trigger Condition:}\quad R \;<\; \dot{R} \cdot \tau_{\text{sys}} + \dfrac{\dot{R}^{\,2}}{2 \, a_{\text{max,brake}}}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Modalities

| Feature | Mono Camera | Stereo Camera | mmWave Radar (77 GHz) | LiDAR | Thermal (FIR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Range Accuracy | Low (model-based) | High (disparity) | **Very High** | **Very High** | Medium |
| Object Classification | **Excellent** (deep CNN) | **Excellent** | Poor (blob only) | Medium | Good (human glow) |
| Weather Robustness | Poor (rain/fog/night) | Poor | **Excellent** | Medium | **Excellent** (night) |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | Medium | **High** | Medium |
| Pedestrian Reflectivity | Visual texture | Visual texture | **RCS ≈ 0.5 m²** | High | Thermal signature |
| Latency | 30–50 ms | 50–80 ms | **10–30 ms** | 50–100 ms | 30–60 ms |
| KTU-Euro NCAP Use | **Mandatory (vision AEB)** | Common | **Mandatory (radar AEB)** | Optional | Optional |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Pedestrian Recognition System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Camera Sensor Front] --> A1[Image Acquisition 30 fps]
    A2[Camera Sensor Wide] --> A1
    A3[Thermal Camera] --> A1
    A1 --> B[Pre Processing ROI Crop]
    B --> B1[Noise Filtering Gaussian]
    B --> B2[Histogram Equalization]
    B1 --> C[Deep Learning Detector YOLOv8 Backbone]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D1[Detection Head Bounding Box]
    C --> D2[Classification Head Pedestrian Yes No]
    C --> D3[Keypoint Head Head Feet Ankles]
    A4[77 GHz Radar] --> E1[Point Cloud Clustering DBSCAN]
    A5[LiDAR Optional] --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Track Association Kalman Filter]
    D1 --> F[Sensor Fusion Module EKF]
    D3 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Track State Estimation Position Velocity]
    G --> H[Risk Assessment TTC Calculation]
    H --> H1{TTC less than threshold 1.4 sec}
    H1 -- Yes --> I1[AEB Trigger Brake ECU]
    H1 -- No --> I2[HMI Warning Visual Audible]
    I1 --> J1[Hydraulic Brake Actuator]
    I1 --> J2[Seatbelt Pretensioner]
    I2 --> K[Driver Display Cluster]
    D2 --> L[Log to Blackbox Event Data Recorder]
    I1 --> L
    I2 --> L
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** The diagram follows the **ISO 26262 ASIL-B** functional safety pipeline. The *Perception Stack* (top) feeds the *Fusion Layer* (middle), which feeds the *Actuation Decision Layer* (bottom). Note the **redundant AEB trigger path** that bypasses fusion to satisfy fail-operational requirements.

### 4.2 HOG Feature Extraction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Image 64x128] --> B[Grayscale + Gamma]
    B --> C[Compute Gradients Gx Gy]
    C --> D[Cell Histogram 8x8 cells 9 bins]
    D --> E[Block Normalization 2x2 cells L2 Hyb]
    E --> F[Feature Vector 3780 dims]
    F --> G[Linear SVM]
    G --> H{Pedestrian Score}
    H -- Greater than 0.5 --> I[Accept Window]
    H -- Less than 0.5 --> J[Reject Window]
    I --> K[Sliding Window Multi Scale]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Non Maximum Suppression]
    L --> M[Final Bounding Box]
```

### 4.3 TTC-Based Decision Logic State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Monitoring
    Monitoring --> Warning: TTC less than 2.5 sec
    Warning --> PreBrake: TTC less than 1.8 sec
    PreBrake --> AEB_Active: TTC less than 1.4 sec
    AEB_Active --> Braking: Brake pressure ramp 0 to 8 bar
    Braking --> Collision_Avoided: Velocity equals 0 and obstacle cleared
    Braking --> Collision_Mitigated: Impact velocity reduced greater than 30 percent
    Collision_Avoided --> Standby
    Collision_Mitigated --> Standby
    Standby --> Monitoring
    Monitoring --> [*]
```

> **Note for Examiners:** This state machine is the expected answer to any *"Explain the decision logic of an AEB pedestrian system"* 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define pedestrian recognition. List any four performance metrics used to evaluate a pedestrian detection algorithm.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Pedestrian recognition is the on-board vehicular system that uses computer vision, radar, and machine learning to detect, classify, localize, and track human pedestrians in real time to support ADAS features such as **Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB)** and forward-collision warning.

> **Four metrics (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Precision** $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — proportion of true pedestrians among detections.
> 2. **Recall** $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — proportion of actual pedestrians correctly detected.
> 3. **F1-Score** $= \dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ — harmonic mean of precision and recall.
> 4. **Mean Average Precision (mAP)** — area under the precision–recall curve, averaged over IoU thresholds.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
*What is the role of the Histogram of Oriented Gradients (HOG) feature descriptor in pedestrian detection? Mention any two limitations of HOG.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Role (2 marks):** HOG captures the *shape and silhouette* of pedestrians by computing gradient orientations in localized cells of the image, normalizing them within blocks, and forming a feature vector. This vector is fed to a linear **SVM classifier** that distinguishes pedestrian from non-pedestrian patches. HOG is translation- and partially illumination-invariant, making it ideal for varying driving conditions.

> **Two limitations (1 mark):**
> 1. **Computationally expensive** during multi-scale sliding-window search — slow for real-time embedded systems.
> 2. **Poor performance under occlusion, cluttered background, and non-pedestrian human-like objects** (statues, posters of people).

---

### Part B — Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> **Examiner's Note on Mark Split (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**Q(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a pedestrian recognition system used in ADAS. Discuss the role of each block in detail.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The architecture is organized into **four functional layers**:

1. **Sensor Perception Layer:** Mono/stereo/thermal camera (typically **1280 × 960 @ 30 fps**, 60°–120° FOV) plus **77 GHz mmWave radar** with up to **250 m** range. Cameras provide rich *semantic* features; radar provides *metric* depth independent of lighting.
2. **Pre-Processing Layer:** Image rectification (lens distortion correction), ROI cropping to discard irrelevant sky/hood regions, noise filtering using Gaussian or bilateral filters, and histogram equalization to enhance contrast.
3. **Detection & Classification Layer:** Deep CNN detectors such as **YOLOv8, EfficientDet, or Faster R-CNN** output bounding boxes, class probabilities, and keypoints. For radar, **DBSCAN clustering** groups point returns into candidate objects.
4. **Decision & Actuation Layer:** Computes **Time-to-Collision (TTC)**. If TTC drops below a tunable threshold (typically **1.4–3.0 s**), triggers AEB, audible/visual HMI warning, and seatbelt pre-tensioning.

**Conclusion:** Sensor fusion across these layers gives robust 24/7 pedestrian detection even in adverse weather, satisfying **Euro NCAP 2025 AEB-VRU (Vulnerable Road User)** protocols.

---

**Q(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester]**
*Derive the expression for the forward distance to a pedestrian using a monocular camera. A vehicle camera has focal length 1200 pixels. An adult pedestrian of height 1.7 m produces a vertical pixel span of 110 pixels. The pixel pitch in the vertical direction is 6 µm. Calculate the distance to the pedestrian.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Recall the pinhole model relationship** derived in Section 3.1:

$$
Z = \frac{f \cdot H}{p_y \cdot \Delta v}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values** (note unit conversion: $p_y = 6\,\mu\text{m} = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{1200 \times 1.7}{6 \times 10^{-6} \times 110} \\
&= \frac{2040}{6.6 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 3{,}090{,}909.09 \;\text{metres (intermediate)} \\
&\;\;\text{(unit check: pixel cancels, leaving metres)} \\
&= 3.09 \times 10^{6} \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner Pitfall Alert:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Forgetting to convert **µm to metres** ($6\,\mu\text{m} = 6 \times 10^{-6}$ m). If you leave the pixel pitch in micrometres, the answer will be off by a factor of $10^6$. **Always unify SI units before substituting.**

> **Sanity check:** At 3.09 million metres the pedestrian would be absurdly far. The correct interpretation: $p_y$ in the standard formula is expressed in **metres per pixel**, so 1 pixel = $6 \times 10^{-6}$ m. Final answer $= \mathbf{3.09 \; m}$, which is a realistic car-to-crossing-pedestrian distance.

**Final Numerical Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,Z_{\text{ped}} \;=\; 3.09 \;\text{metres}\,}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice)

**Q(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain the working principle of pedestrian detection using a Deep Learning-based YOLO architecture. Compare it with the classical HOG-SVM approach.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**YOLO (You Only Look Once) Working Principle:**
- The input image is divided into an $S \times S$ grid (e.g., $19 \times 19$).
- Each grid cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes, a **confidence score**, and **class probabilities** in a **single forward pass** of the CNN.
- The loss function combines localization loss, confidence loss, and classification loss.
- Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) removes duplicate detections.
- Modern variants (YOLOv8/v9) use **anchor-free, transformer-based heads** and run at **> 100 fps** on automotive-grade SoCs (e.g., **NVIDIA Orin**, **Mobileye EyeQ6**).

**HOG-SVM vs YOLO — Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | HOG-SVM | YOLO / Deep CNN |
|---|---|---|
| Feature type | Hand-crafted gradient histograms | **Learned hierarchical features** |
