**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ and its image projection $p = (x, y)$ through a pinhole at distance $f$ (focal length).

**Step 2 — Apply similar triangles along X-axis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(\theta) &= \frac{x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} \\
\Rightarrow x &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Similarly for Y-axis:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add principal point offset $(c_x, c_y)$ and convert $f$ to pixel units using pixel pitch $(s_x, s_y)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{pixel} &= f_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \quad \text{where} \quad f_x = \frac{f}{s_x} \\
y_{pixel} &= f_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y \quad \text{where} \quad f_y = \frac{f}{s_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in homogeneous matrix form using intrinsic matrix $K$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \frac{1}{Z} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} f_x & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f_y & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{K \text{ (intrinsic matrix)}} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Significance:** The 5 intrinsic parameters $(f_x, f_y, c_x, c_y)$ and the skew (zero here for modern CMOS) are determined via **camera calibration** (Zhang's chessboard method). This intrinsic matrix $K$ is essential for all subsequent metric vision tasks — stereo depth, SLAM, structure from motion, and AR overlays in ITS.

---

**b) A stereo camera rig has focal length $f = 1000$ pixels and baseline $B = 0.20$ m. A vehicle ahead is observed with disparity $d = 5$ pixels. Calculate the distance to the vehicle. If the disparity drops to 4 pixels for the same vehicle, by how much has the vehicle moved closer? Also comment on the role of Cooperative Perception in this scenario.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the stereo depth formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d_1} = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{5} = \frac{200}{5} = 40 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recompute for disparity drop to 4 pixels:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2 &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d_2} = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{4} = \frac{200}{4} = 50 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wait, an important interpretation check:** A *drop* in disparity means the object is *farther* (since $Z \propto 1/d$). The vehicle has therefore moved **away** by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Z &= Z_2 - Z_1 = 50 - 40 = 10 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

The vehicle has moved **10 m farther** away (not closer). This counter-intuitive result is a classic *trap* question — students often wrongly subtract $Z_1 - Z_2$.

**Step 4 — Role of Cooperative Perception:**
In this scenario, the ego vehicle can only see the target vehicle up to its **monocular/stereo range (~150 m)**. Beyond this, line-of-sight is lost (e.g., behind a truck, around a curve). Through **V2V communication**, the ego vehicle can receive the *same* disparity-derived distance measurement from a **second vehicle ahead of the truck**, fusing it into an Extended World Model. This effectively extends the perception horizon to **500–1000 m** and prevents rear-end collisions in non-line-of-sight (NLOS) scenarios.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]` (Alternative Choice)

**a) Explain the end-to-end pipeline of a vision-based ITS communication system. With a neat block diagram, describe the function of each block.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The vision-based ITS communication pipeline has **five sequential blocks**:

**Block 1 — Image Acquisition:**
On-board cameras (mono, stereo, or surround-view) capture the road scene at 30–60 fps. Roadside ITS cameras (at intersections, toll booths) supplement vehicle-side perception. Lens characteristics: focal length $f$, field-of-view 50°–120°, aperture $f/1.6$–$f/2.8$.

**Block 2 — Image Pre-processing:**
- **Lens distortion correction:** Removes radial (barrel/pincushion) and tangential distortion using calibrated coefficients $k_1, k_2, p_1, p_2$.
- **Noise filtering:** Gaussian or bilateral filters suppress sensor noise.
- **Histogram equalisation:** Normalises contrast under varying illumination.
- **Image rectification:** Aligns stereo images to epipolar lines (y-constant).

**Block 3 — Feature Extraction and Detection:**
Classical methods (Canny edges, Harris corners, ORB keypoints) or learned methods (CNN backbones such as ResNet-50, EfficientNet, YOLOv8) extract **bounding boxes, lane polynomials, traffic sign classes**.

**Block 4 — Object Tracking and Local World Model:**
Detected entities are temporally linked using **Kalman filter, SORT, or DeepSORT** to maintain stable identity across frames. Output: a local 3D world model with each object's position $(X, Y, Z)$, velocity $(V_x, V_y, V_z)$, and class label.

**Block 5 — V2X Communication and Cooperative Fusion:**
- **Encoding:** Local world model is encoded into **CAM** (Cooperative Awareness Messages — periodic, 1–10 Hz) or **DENM** (Decentralised Environmental Notification Messages — event-triggered).
- **Transmission:** Packets broadcast via **IEEE 802.11p** (ETSI ITS-G5) or **C-V2X PC5 mode 4** (3GPP Rel. 14+) at 5.9 GHz.
- **Receiver fusion:** Each receiving vehicle decodes packets and fuses them with its own perception using **Kalman or particle filters**, building the **Extended World Model (EWM)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(a):**
> Students frequently *omit* Block 5 and stop at object detection. Marks are reserved for explaining the **communication and fusion** layer. Always close the loop with V2X message encoding, broadcasting, and EWM construction.

---

**b) Discuss the role of Visible Light Communication (VLC) in ITS. Compare VLC with RF-based V2X communication in terms of range, bandwidth, security, and susceptibility to interference.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Role of VLC in ITS:**
Visible Light Communication uses **modulated LED light sources** (headlamps, tail-lamps, traffic signals, street lighting) as transmitters and **photodiodes or CMOS image sensors** as receivers. The IEEE 802.15.7 standard governs short-range optical wireless communication.

**VLC Operation Principle:**
Data is encoded onto the LED drive current using **OOK (On-Off Keying), VPPM (Variable Pulse Position Modulation), or OFDM**. The intensity modulation is imperceptible to the human eye (frequencies > 200 Hz) but detectable by a fast photodiode. Using a **rolling-shutter CMOS camera**, the modulation appears as **alternating bright/dark stripes** across the rows of the captured frame, which are decoded into bits.

**Comparison Table — VLC vs. RF V2X:**

| Parameter | VLC (Optical) | RF V2X (802.11p / C-V2X) |
|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Short (~50 m LOS) | Medium-Long (~300–1000 m) |
| **Bandwidth** | Very High (THz-class optical) | Limited (10–20 MHz channel at 5.9 GHz) |
| **Security** | **Excellent** — confined to line-of-sight, no wall penetration | Susceptible to eavesdropping/sniffing |
| **Interference Susceptibility** | Blocked by fog, heavy rain, snow, dust | Susceptible to multipath fading, jamming |
| **Regulatory Licensing** | **Unlicensed** spectrum (visible band) | Licensed 5.9 GHz band |
| **Dual-Use Hardware** | **Yes** — headlamp + camera | No |
| **Mobility Support** | Limited (requires pointing) | Excellent (omni-directional) |
| **Data Rate** | Up to ~1 Gbps (lab) | ~3–27 Mbps |

**Engineering Conclusion:**
VLC is *complementary*, not *replacement*, to RF V2X. It is best suited for **short-range, high-bandwidth, secure** applications such as **intersection movement assist, platooning initiation, and infrastructure-to-vehicle signage data** (e.g., traffic light phase and timing — **SPaT/MAP** messages). RF V2X remains the workhorse for long-range safety messaging.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> A common student mistake is to claim VLC is *better* than RF V2X. Examiners deduct marks for missing the **complementary relationship**. Always conclude with a clear statement: *"VLC augments RF V2X in specific short-range, high-security scenarios; it does not replace it."*

---

## 5.3 High-Density Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this 2 hours before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Vision for ITS Communication = cameras + CV algorithms + V2X messaging for cooperative perception.
- **Pinhole Model:** $x = fX/Z$, $y = fY/Z$; extended via intrinsic matrix $K$ (5 parameters).
- **Stereo Depth:** $Z = fB/d$ — *inversely proportional* to disparity.
- **Five-Stage Pipeline:** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Feature Extraction → Tracking → V2X Communication.
- **CAM** (periodic, 1–10 Hz) vs. **DENM** (event-triggered) — know the difference.
- **Communication Standards:** IEEE 802.11p (DSRC), ETSI ITS-G5, C-V2X (3GPP Rel. 14+).
- **Latency Budget:** Total ≤ **100 ms** for safety-critical V2X (ETSI EN 302 637-2).
- **Extended World Model (EWM):** Range extended from 150 m (own) to 500–1000 m (cooperative).
- **VLC:** LED-based optical communication; IEEE 802.15.7; uses rolling-shutter camera as receiver; **complementary to RF V2X**, not a replacement.
- **Sensor Fusion:** Camera + Radar + LiDAR + Ultrasonic — combines semantic richness with weather robustness.
- **Key Formulae to Memorise:** Pinhole projection, stereo depth, friis path loss, SNR (dB), SSIM, latency decomposition.
- **Standard Frame Rates:** 30 fps (standard) and 60 fps (high-speed ADAS).
- **V2X Range:** ~300 m (802.11p), ~1000 m (C-V2X PC5 mode 4).
- **CAM Packet Size:** 100–800 bytes; transmitted at 6 Mbps.
- **Object Detection Models:** YOLO, ResNet, EfficientNet, Vision Transformers.
- **Tracking Algorithms:** Kalman filter, SORT, DeepSORT.
- **Privacy Concern:** Vision captures **PII** (faces, license plates); GDPR/anonymisation pipelines mandatory in EU deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner's Advice:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, always (i) draw a labelled block diagram, (ii) state the governing equation, (iii) show the numerical substitution, and (iv) give a concluding engineering interpretation. This 4-step structure scores 90%+ marks in any Module 4 question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ITS Communication in Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ITS Communication in Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ITS Communication in Automobiles** refers to the standardized exchange of data, information, and control signals between vehicles, infrastructure, pedestrians, and networks using wireless and wired communication protocols. It forms the digital nervous system of modern Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS), enabling safety, efficiency, and autonomous driving.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme for **OEMUT723**, **Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) Communication** is defined as:

> The integrated framework of electronic, communication, and information technologies applied to surface transportation systems, encompassing in-vehicle networks, short-range wireless protocols (DSRC), cellular-based vehicular communications (C-V2X), and cooperative driving mechanisms.

The communication taxonomy in automobiles is broadly classified into:

| Layer | Communication Type | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **In-Vehicle** | CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Automotive Ethernet | Inside a single vehicle |
| **Short-Range V2X** | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p), C-V2X PC5 | < 1 km |
| **Long-Range V2X** | 4G/5G cellular (C-V2X Uu) | Cellular coverage |
| **Infrastructure** | V2I via RSU (Roadside Units) | Roadside-to-vehicle |
| **Network** | V2N (Vehicle-to-Network) | Cloud-based |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Talking Highway"**
> Imagine a highway where every car is a person walking with a **walkie-talkie**. As you drive, your car constantly broadcasts: "I am here, going 80 km/h, braking now!" Other cars receive this and react instantly. Traffic lights also listen and broadcast their timing. Even the cloud listens to plan traffic flow. **That is ITS Communication — cars and infrastructure constantly talking to prevent accidents, reduce congestion, and enable self-driving.**

### 1.3 Key Stakeholders in ITS Communication

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary ITS Communication Stakeholders:**
> 1. **OBU (On-Board Unit)** — Communication device inside the vehicle.
> 2. **RSU (Roadside Unit)** — Fixed communication unit along roads (e.g., traffic signals, toll booths).
> 3. **Traffic Management Center (TMC)** — Central monitoring authority.
> 4. **Pedestrian Mobile Devices** — Smartphones with V2P capability.
> 5. **Cloud Servers** — Big-data analytics, HD map updates.

### 1.4 Standard Frequency Bands and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical ITS Communication Frequencies (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **DSRC Band: 5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (IEEE 802.11p, 75 MHz allocated by FCC/ETSI)
> - **C-V2X Band: 5.9 GHz (PC5 sidelink)** + 4G/5G cellular bands
> - **Maximum DSRC Range: ~1000 m** (line-of-sight)
> - **Latency Target: < 100 ms** (safety-critical), ideally **< 20 ms** for V2V collision avoidance
> - **Data Rate: 3 – 27 Mbps** (DSRC), up to **1 Gbps** (5G-V2X)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication Architecture

V2X is the umbrella term that covers all forms of vehicular communication:

| Sub-Mode | Full Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Cars exchanging kinematics and intent |
| **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Cars ↔ RSUs (traffic lights, signs) |
| **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Car ↔ smartphone/VRU (Vulnerable Road User) |
| **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Car ↔ cellular/cloud |
| **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | EV ↔ power grid (bidirectional charging) |
| **V2D** | Vehicle-to-Device | Car ↔ wearables / driver's phone |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — How ITS Communication Works

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

1. **Data Acquisition:** Sensors (LiDAR, radar, camera, GPS, IMU) collect vehicle state — speed, position, heading, brake status, steering angle.
2. **Message Packaging:** The data is packaged into standardized messages (e.g., **CAM** — Cooperative Awareness Message, **DENM** — Decentralized Environmental Notification Message in ETSI EN 302 637).
3. **Transmission:** OBU broadcasts the message via DSRC or C-V2X radio.
4. **Reception:** Nearby OBUs and RSUs receive the message.
5. **Processing:** Receiver applies safety algorithms (e.g., Time-To-Collision, Forward Collision Warning).
6. **Actuation:** If a hazard is detected, the system alerts the driver or triggers ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems).

### 2.3 In-Vehicle Communication Protocols (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern vehicles contain **70–100+ ECUs (Electronic Control Units)** networked together. The choice of bus depends on bandwidth, latency, and cost.

**CAN (Controller Area Network — ISO 11898):**
- Originally developed by Bosch in **1986**.
- Two-wire differential bus, speeds up to **1 Mbps** (CAN 2.0) and **5 Mbps** (CAN-FD).
- Used for engine, braking, transmission — the backbone of vehicle networking.

**LIN (Local Interconnect Network — ISO 17987):**
- Single-wire, low-cost sub-bus, max **19.2 kbps**.
- Used for body electronics — windows, mirrors, seat controls.

**FlexRay (ISO 17458):**
- High-speed deterministic bus, **up to 10 Mbps**.
- Used in safety-critical systems like **drive-by-wire** and active suspension.

**Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1):**
- Single-pair twisted cable, **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps**.
- Backbone for ADAS, infotainment, OTA (Over-The-Air) updates.

### 2.4 DSRC vs. C-V2X — Comparative Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **DSRC** is based on IEEE 802.11p (Wi-Fi variant). **C-V2X** is based on cellular 4G/5G, defined by 3GPP Release 14+.

| Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (3GPP) |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | IEEE 802.11p / ETSI EN 302 636 | 3GPP Rel. 14/15/16 |
| Frequency | **5.9 GHz** | 5.9 GHz (PC5) + cellular |
| Latency | ~4 ms | ~5–20 ms (5G: < 5 ms) |
| Range | ~300–1000 m | ~500–2000 m |
| Architecture | Ad-hoc (no cellular needed) | PC5 direct + Uu via network |
| Maturity | Deployed (US/EU) | Rapidly growing (China, EU) |
| Future-proofing | Limited | 5G evolution path |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield ITS Communication Formulas:**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{4\pi f^2} \cdot \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{k T_s N_f \cdot \text{SNR}_{\min}}$ | Max communication range via radar/FSPL | m |
| 2 | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-Space Path Loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| 3 | $\text{TTC} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | Time-To-Collision (used in V2V safety) | s |
| 4 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{P_{\text{payload}}}{T_{\text{frame}}}$ | Effective data throughput of bus | bps |
| 5 | $\text{Bus Utilization} = \dfrac{\text{Bus load (bits)}}{\text{Bus capacity (bps)} \cdot T}$ | CAN/Ethernet load fraction | unitless |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{L_{packet}}{R_{bit}} + t_{propagation} + t_{processing}$ | End-to-end communication latency | s |
| 7 | $P_{\text{collision}} = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Probability of message collision (n nodes, p per-node) | unitless |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Note:** Vertical bars `|` inside equations (e.g., absolute value `|x|`) have been replaced with `\vert` where required to preserve markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications of ITS Communication:**
> 1. **Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control (C-ACC):** V2V lets cars platoon with cm-level spacing, reducing fuel consumption by **10–20%**.
> 2. **Intersection Collision Avoidance:** V2I warns of red-light runners.
> 3. **Emergency Vehicle Preemption (EVP):** V2I gives green corridors to ambulances.
> 4. **Tolling & Tolling ETC:** DSRC-based FASTag / GNSS tolling.
> 5. **Autonomous Driving (L4/L5):** V2X complements on-board sensors — Tesla, Waymo, Mercedes Drive Pilot.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) for ITS Link Budget

The **Friis Transmission Equation** is the foundation of any wireless link budget. We derive the FSPL below — a must-know for KTU problems.

**Step 1: Starting from Friis Transmission Equation**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_r &= P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $P_r$ = received power (W)
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (W)
- $G_t$, $G_r$ = transmit/receive antenna gains
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $d$ = distance between antennas (m)

**Step 2: Express in dB form**

Take $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_r\text{(dBm)} &= P_t\text{(dBm)} + G_t\text{(dBi)} + G_r\text{(dBi)} \\
&\quad + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Isolate the path-loss term**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL (dB)} &= -\,20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d) - 20 \log_{10}(\lambda)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Convert $\lambda = c/f$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL (dB)} &= 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{c}{f}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{f}{c}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Substitute speed of light $c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$** and simplify constants

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL (dB)} &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right) \\
&\text{(with } d \text{ in metres, } f \text{ in Hz)} \\
\text{FSPL (dB)} &= 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \\
&\text{(with } d \text{ in km, } f \text{ in MHz)} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in ITS:** At $f = 5.9$ GHz and $d = 500$ m: $\text{FSPL} \approx 20\log_{10}(0.5) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 \approx -6.02 + 75.42 + 32.44 = \mathbf{101.84 \text{ dB}}$. This determines the required transmitter power for V2V links.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Latency Decomposition for V2X

End-to-end latency $\tau_{\text{total}}$ from sensor trigger to action:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\text{total}} &= \tau_{\text{sense}} + \tau_{\text{proc}} + \tau_{\text{queue}} + \tau_{\text{tx}} + \tau_{\text{prop}} + \tau_{\text{rx}} + \tau_{\text{act}}
\end{aligned}$$

| Stage | Symbol | Typical Value (5G-V2X) |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing | $\tau_{\text{sense}}$ | 10–30 ms |
| On-board processing | $\tau_{\text{proc}}$ | 5–15 ms |
| MAC queueing | $\tau_{\text{queue}}$ | 1–5 ms |
| Transmission | $\tau_{\text{tx}}$ | 1–4 ms |
| Propagation | $\tau_{\text{prop}}$ | < 0.003 ms (sub-km) |
| Reception & decode | $\tau_{\text{rx}}$ | 2–6 ms |
| Actuation | $\tau_{\text{act}}$ | 10–20 ms |
| **Total** | $\tau_{\text{total}}$ | **~30 – 80 ms** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a vehicle moving at 100 km/h, a 30 ms delay corresponds to ~0.83 m travel. For collision avoidance, latency must be **< 100 ms**.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — V2V Range Calculation

**Problem:** A V2V link uses $f = 5.9$ GHz, $P_t = 20$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 3$ dBi, receiver sensitivity $-90$ dBm. Find the maximum range.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_r\text{(min)} &= P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} \\
-90 &= 20 + 3 + 3 - \text{FSPL} \\
\text{FSPL}_{\max} &= 116 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

Apply FSPL equation (d in km, f in MHz):

$$\begin{aligned}
116 &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 \\
20\log_{10}(d) &= 116 - 32.44 - 75.42 = 8.14 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= 0.407 \\
d &\approx 2.55 \text{ km} \quad \text{(theoretical, LOS)}
\end{aligned}$$

In practice (fading, obstacles, NLOS), realistic range ≈ **300–500 m**.

### 3.4 Probability of Successful Reception in V2V

For $n$ vehicles transmitting cooperatively, the probability that at least one message is received successfully is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{success}} &= 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - p_i)
\end{aligned}$$

If all $n$ nodes have equal single-link success probability $p$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{success}} = 1 - (1-p)^{n}
\end{aligned}$$

For $p = 0.7$, $n = 5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{success}} &= 1 - (0.3)^{5} \\
&= 1 - 0.00243 = 0.99757 \quad (99.76\%)
\end{aligned}$$

This justifies the **cooperative awareness** principle in ITS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 V2X Communication Ecosystem (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VehCluster[Vehicle Cluster]
        node1[OBU Car A]
        node2[OBU Car B]
        node3[OBU Truck C]
    end

    subgraph InfraCluster[Roadside Infrastructure]
        node4[RSU Traffic Light]
        node5[RSU Toll Booth]
        node6[Road Sensors]
    end

    subgraph NetCluster[Network and Cloud]
        node7[Cellular Tower eNB gNB]
        node8[TMC Traffic Management]
        node9[Cloud HD Maps]
    end

    subgraph VRUCluster[Vulnerable Road Users]
        node10[Pedestrian Smartphone]
        node11[Cyclist Wearable]
    end

    node1 ---|V2V DSRC or C-V2X PC5| node2
    node2 ---|V2V| node3
    node1 ---|V2I| node4
    node3 ---|V2I| node5
    node4 ---|Fiber Backhaul| node7
    node5 ---|Fiber Backhaul| node8
    node6 ---|Sensor Data| node8
    node7 ---|5G Uu| node9
    node1 ---|V2P| node10
    node3 ---|V2P| node11
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the V2X ecosystem. Each block represents a communication endpoint; arrows represent the wireless/wired links defined in the SAE J3061 / ETSI EN 302 636 standards.

### 4.2 ITS Communication Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["Application Layer - CAM DENM BSM Signal Phase Timing"]
    L6["Facilities Layer - Message formatting Local dynamic map LDM"]
    L5["Networking and Transport Layer - GeoNetworking IPv6 UDP TCP"]
    L4["Access Layer - 80211p or C V2X PC5 3GPP Radio"]
    L3["Physical Layer - 5.9 GHz OFDM 10 MHz channel"]

    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ITS protocol stack is **NOT identical to the OSI 7-layer model**. ETSI defines a simplified 5-layer model optimised for low-latency vehicular safety messages.

### 4.3 In-Vehicle Network Topology (Modern Car)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Powertrain["Powertrain Domain - CAN FD"]
        e1[ECU Engine]
        e2[ECU Transmission]
        e3[ECU Braking ABS ESP]
    end

    subgraph Chassis["Chassis Domain - FlexRay"]
        e4[ECU Steering]
        e5[ECU Suspension]
    end

    subgraph Body["Body Domain - LIN"]
        e6[ECU Windows]
        e7[ECU Lights]
        e8[ECU HVAC]
    end

    subgraph ADAS["ADAS Domain - Automotive Ethernet"]
        e9[ECU Radar]
        e10[ECU Camera]
        e11[ECU LiDAR]
    end

    subgraph IVI["Infotainment Domain - Ethernet MOST"]
        e12[Head Unit]
        e13[Telematics TCU]
    end

    GW[Central Gateway ECU]

    e1 --- GW
    e2 --- GW
    e3 --- GW
    e4 --- GW
    e5 --- GW
    e6 --- GW
    e7 --- GW
    e8 --- GW
    e9 --- GW
    e10 --- GW
    e11 --- GW
    e12 --- GW
    e13 --- GW
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Central Gateway ECU** is the modern vehicle's communication hub — it translates between CAN, FlexRay, LIN, and Ethernet domains, much like a router in a computer network.

### 4.4 Message Flow Sequence — Cooperative Collision Avoidance

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C1 as Car A OBU
    participant C2 as Car B OBU
    participant R1 as RSU at Junction
    participant D1 as Driver Display

    C1->>C2: CAM Beacon Speed 80 km/h Position GPS
    C2->>C1: CAM Beacon Speed 60 km/h Braking
    C1->>R1: Probe data Vehicle density
    R1-->>C1: DENM Ice patch 50 m ahead
    C1->>C1: Compute TTC Time To Collision
    C1->>D1: Haptic Warning + Audio Alert
    C1->>C2: DENM Hazard ahead slow down
```

> [!NOTE]
> **CAM** (Cooperative Awareness Message) is sent periodically (typically 1–10 Hz). **DENM** (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message) is event-driven (e.g., accident, ice, brake).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **V2X communication**. List its four primary sub-modes.

**Model Answer:**
V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) is a vehicular communication paradigm enabling data exchange between a vehicle and its surrounding entities. The four primary sub-modes are:
1. **V2V** – Vehicle-to-Vehicle
2. **V2I** – Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (RSU)
3. **V2P** – Vehicle-to-Pedestrian
4. **V2N** – Vehicle-to-Network (cellular/cloud)

It enhances road safety, traffic efficiency, and supports autonomous driving by enabling cooperative awareness beyond line-of-sight and sensor range.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Compare **DSRC and C-V2X** in terms of frequency band, latency, and standard.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DSRC | C-V2X |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | IEEE 802.11p | 3GPP Release 14+ |
| Frequency | **5.9 GHz** | 5.9 GHz (PC5) + 4G/5G bands |
| Latency | ~4 ms | 5–20 ms (4G); < 5 ms (5G) |

DSRC is a Wi-Fi-based ad-hoc protocol; C-V2X is cellular-based with a clear evolution path to 5G.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE norms, Module 4 offers internal choice. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full (14 marks = 7 + 7).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **V2X communication architecture** in Intelligent Transportation Systems. Differentiate between **OBU and RSU** with their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The V2X architecture consists of three major tiers:
- **Vehicle Tier:** OBU (On-Board Unit) — installed in the vehicle; collects sensor data (GPS, CAN bus, IMU), packages it into CAM/DENM messages, and broadcasts via DSRC or C-V2X radio. Also receives messages from other vehicles and RSUs.
- **Infrastructure Tier:** RSU (Roadside Unit) — fixed units along roads at traffic signals, toll booths, intersections. They aggregate V2I data, push traffic signal phase/timing (SPaT) data, and forward cloud info to OBUs.
- **Network Tier:** Cellular base stations (eNB/gNB), TMC (Traffic Management Centre), and cloud servers — provide HD map updates, software OTA, and long-range coordination.

**OBU vs. RSU:**

| Aspect | OBU | RSU |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Mobile (in vehicle) | Fixed (roadside) |
| Power | Vehicle battery | Mains/solar |
| Primary Role | Broadcast own kinematics | Relay infrastructure data |
| Antenna | Omnidirectional | Directional + omnidirectional |

**(b)** Derive the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) equation** for an ITS link and compute the maximum range for a V2V system with $P_t = 23$ dBm, $G_t = G_r = 2$ dBi, $f = 5.9$ GHz, and receiver sensitivity $-92$ dBm. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Starting from Friis equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_r &= P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

In dB form (Friis):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL (dB)} &= P_t\text{(dBm)} + G_t\text{(dBi)} + G_r\text{(dBi)} - P_r\text{(dBm)} \\
&= 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{FSPL}_{\max} &= 23 + 2 + 2 - (-92) \\
&= 119 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}$$

Solve for $d$ (km):

$$\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(d) &= 119 - 32.44 - 20\log_{10}(5900) \\
&= 119 - 32.44 - 75.42 \\
&= 11.14 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= 0.557 \\
d &\approx 3.61 \text{ km} \quad \text{(theoretical LOS)}
\end{aligned}$$

In real NLOS/fading conditions, practical range ≈ **500–800 m** at this frequency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **in-vehicle communication protocols** — CAN, LIN, FlexRay, and Automotive Ethernet. Compare them on the basis of speed, application, and cost. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **CAN (Controller Area Network):** Developed by Bosch (1986), ISO 11898. Multi-master, message-prioritised bus. Speed up to **1 Mbps** (CAN 2.0) and **5 Mbps** (CAN-FD). Used for engine, ABS, airbag. Low-cost.
2. **LIN (Local Interconnect Network):** Single-wire UART-based, max **19.2 kbps**. Cheap sub-bus for non-critical body electronics (windows, mirrors).
3. **FlexRay:** Time-triggered + event-triggered, **up to 10 Mbps**, deterministic. Used for **drive-by-wire**, active suspension, ADAS.
4. **Automotive Ethernet:** Single-pair **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps** (100/1000BASE-T1). Used for ADAS sensor fusion, infotainment, OTA updates.

**Comparison:**

| Protocol | Speed | Application | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| CAN | 1–5 Mbps | Powertrain, chassis | Low |
| LIN | 19.2 kbps | Body electronics | Very Low |
| FlexRay | 10 Mbps | Safety-critical, x-by-wire | High |
| Auto Ethernet | 100 Mbps–1 Gbps | ADAS, IVI, backbone | Medium-High |

**(b)** Describe the **ETSI ITS protocol stack**. Explain the function of **CAM and DENM** messages with example use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The ETSI ITS stack has 5 layers (from top to bottom):
1. **Application Layer** — Safety/efficiency applications (forward collision warning, emergency vehicle approaching).
2. **Facilities Layer** — Message formatting, Local Dynamic Map (LDM), station management.
3. **Networking & Transport Layer** — GeoNetworking (GeoAdhoc routing), IPv6, UDP/TCP.
4. **Access Layer** — IEEE 802.11p (DSRC) or C-V2X (3GPP).
5. **Physical Layer** — 5.9 GHz OFDM, 10 MHz channels.

**CAM (Cooperative Awareness Message):**
- Sent periodically (1–10 Hz).
- Contains vehicle ID, position, speed, heading, acceleration.
- Example use case: Surrounding vehicles build a real-time map of nearby traffic.

**DENM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Message):**
- Event-triggered (e.g., accident, road works, icy road).
- Contains event type, position, duration.
- Example use case: A vehicle detects sudden braking and broadcasts DENM to warn trailing cars.

Both messages follow **ETSI EN 302 637-2 (CAM)** and **EN 302 637-3 (DENM)** standards.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing V2X with V2V only.** V2X is the umbrella; V2V is just one of five sub-modes. Examiners often expect all five listed.
> 2. **Wrong frequency band.** The DSRC band is **5.85–5.925 GHz**, NOT 2.4 GHz (that's Wi-Fi). Mixing up loses 1–2 marks instantly.
> 3. **Skipping the standard names.** Mentioning IEEE 802.11p, 3GPP Rel. 14, ETSI EN 302 637, SAE J2945 gives the examiner proof of syllabus-aligned knowledge.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units in FSPL.** Mixing km with Hz, or m with MHz, yields a wrong answer.
> 5. **Omitting block diagrams.** Even a hand-drawn box diagram of the V2X architecture fetches 2 bonus marks.
> 6. **In Part B, students often write CAM and DENM without their full forms.** Always expand the acronym once on first use.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ITS Communication:**
> - **V2X = V2V + V2I + V2P + V2N + V2G + V2D**
> - **DSRC frequency = 5.9 GHz; Standard = IEEE 802.11p; Latency ≈ 4 ms**
> - **C-V2X = 3GPP Rel. 14+; supports PC5 direct + Uu cellular**
> - **CAM = periodic cooperative awareness (ETSI EN 302 637-2)**
> - **DENM = event-driven hazard notification (ETSI EN 302 637-3)**
> - **In-vehicle buses: CAN (1–5 Mbps), LIN (19.2 kbps), FlexRay (10 Mbps), Auto Ethernet (100 Mbps–1 Gbps)**
> - **FSPL(dB) = 20log₁₀(d) + 20log₁₀(f) + 32.44** (d in km, f in MHz)
> - **Safety-critical latency target < 100 ms; ideal < 20 ms**
> - **OBU = in-vehicle; RSU = roadside; TMC = central control**
> - **5G-V2X enables Cooperative ACC, intersection safety, platooning**
> - **Standardization bodies: ETSI (EU), IEEE/SAE (US), 3GPP (cellular), ISO (in-vehicle buses)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle-to-Vehicle and Road-Vehicle Communication Systems: Basic Principle and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle-to-Vehicle and Road-Vehicle Communication Systems: Basic Principle and Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication** is a short-range, wireless, ad-hoc networking paradigm in which automobiles exchange real-time kinematic and dynamic state data (such as position, velocity, heading, acceleration, brake status, and turn-signal state) with neighbouring vehicles within a broadcast radius of typically **300 m** to **1000 m**, using the **5.9 GHz** Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC) band or the Cellular-V2X (C-V2X) PC5 sidelink interface.

**Vehicle-to-Roadside (V2R) / Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I) Communication** is a complementary wireless communication mode in which the in-vehicle On-Board Unit (OBU) communicates with fixed Road-Side Units (RSUs) — such as traffic-light controllers, toll-gate transceivers, curve-warning signs, and weather stations — to receive road-condition, signal-phase, and infrastructure-state information that cannot be sensed by the host vehicle's onboard sensors alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** V2V and V2R together constitute the two pillars of **Cooperative Intelligent Transport Systems (C-ITS)**. They form the perception-extension layer that enables a vehicle to "see" beyond the line-of-sight, beyond occlusion, and beyond the host-vehicle sensor range.

The combined ecosystem is often termed **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)**, where:

$$\text{V2X} = \text{V2V} + \text{V2I (V2R)} + \text{V2P (Pedestrian)} + \text{V2N (Network)}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Polite Walkers":**
> Imagine you are walking blindfolded on a busy footpath. To avoid collisions, you could either (a) feel with a cane (this is your **onboard sensor** — radar, lidar, camera) which only detects what is right in front of you, or (b) shout out your position to nearby walkers and listen to theirs. Option (b) is **V2V communication**. Now imagine a traffic policeman standing at the corner shouting, "The light is red in 4 seconds!" That policeman is the **Roadside Unit (RSU)** — that is **V2R communication**.
>
> Together, the walkers can now avoid collisions they could not see, and the policeman helps them plan — exactly what V2V + V2R does for automobiles.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Frequency | **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** | DSRC band, allocated globally for ITS |
| Bandwidth per Channel | **10 MHz** (up to 20 MHz in C-V2X) | Seven 10-MHz channels reserved |
| Transmit Power | **Up to 33 dBm (≈ 2 W) EIRP** | Class A DSRC OBU |
| Data Rate | **3 – 27 Mbps** | Varies with modulation (BPSK → 64-QAM) |
| Broadcast Range | **300 m – 1000 m** | Highly dependent on environment |
| Message Frequency | **10 Hz** | Basic Safety Message (BSM) update rate |
| Message Latency Target | **< 100 ms** | Critical for active safety |
| Standard Protocol | **IEEE 802.11p / SAE J2735** | DSRC stack |
| Cellular Alternative | **3GPP Release 14+ (C-V2X PC5)** | LTE/5G sidelink |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

### Step 1: Information Acquisition (Sensor Layer)
The vehicle's **Controller Area Network (CAN) bus** continuously streams kinematic state from the Electronic Control Units (ECUs):
- Wheel-speed sensors → velocity $v$
- GPS / GNSS receiver → position $(x, y, z)$
- Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) → heading $\psi$, yaw rate $\dot{\psi}$, acceleration $a$
- Brake / throttle ECUs → pedal status, brake-line pressure
- Wiper / light ECUs → weather / hazard flags

### Step 2: Message Formulation (Application Layer)
Every 100 ms, the **V2X application stack** packages this data into a **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** as per SAE J2735. The BSM contains:
- **Part 1 (Core Data):** Position, speed, heading, acceleration — transmitted **every 100 ms**, mandatory.
- **Part 2 (Optional):** Path history, path prediction, brake-system status — transmitted **as needed** when event-driven.

### Step 3: Wireless Transmission (Network & PHY Layer)
The **802.11p** (or C-V2X) MAC/PHY layer broadcasts the BSM on a dedicated Control Channel (**CCH, CH 178**) at **5.9 GHz**. There is **no handshake or pairing** — it is a pure broadcast over a shared medium using CSMA/CA contention.

### Step 4: Reception and Decoding (Receiver-Side)
All vehicles within radio range:
1. Detect the preamble via energy-detect threshold (typically **−85 dBm** receiver sensitivity).
2. Decode the BSM frame.
3. Validate the cryptographic signature (IEEE 1609.2 security stack) to reject spoofed/invalid packets.

### Step 5: Threat Assessment and Actuation (Application Layer)
The **V2X application processor** runs a *remote-sensor fusion* algorithm:

$$\text{Risk} = f(d_{\text{rel}}, \Delta v, \text{TTC}, \text{BogieState})$$

If the computed Time-To-Collision $\text{TTC} < 4\ \text{s}$, a warning (visual, haptic, or audible) is issued to the driver; if $\text{TTC} < 1.5\ \text{s}$, automatic emergency braking (AEB) may be commanded.

### Step 6: V2R Downlink (Roadside → Vehicle)
For V2R, the RSU broadcasts **Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT)**, **Map Data (MAP)**, **Roadside Alerts**, and **Traveller Information Messages (TIM)**. The vehicle's OBU decodes these and:
- Adjusts **Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory (GLOSA)**.
- Warns of upcoming road works, school zones, or icy curves.
- Enables **e-tolling** without stopping.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulae below are examinable for derivations, numericals, or short-answer derivations.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle L_{fs} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | Free-space path loss (Friis, dB form) | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 2 | $\displaystyle L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | Free-space path loss (linear ratio) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\displaystyle \text{TTC} = \frac{d_{\text{rel}}}{\Delta v}$ | Time-to-Collision (longitudinal, closing) | seconds (s) |
| 4 | $\displaystyle R_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} P_{r,\min}}}$ | Maximum reliable communication range (Friis) | metres (m) |
| 5 | $\displaystyle \text{SNR} = \frac{P_r}{N_0 B}$ | Receiver signal-to-noise ratio | dB or linear |
| 6 | $\displaystyle N_0 = k_B T$ | Thermal noise spectral density | W/Hz |
| 7 | $\displaystyle d_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$ | 2-D Euclidean inter-vehicle distance | metres (m) |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \Delta v = v_1 - v_2$ | Relative velocity (closing if positive) | m/s |
| 9 | $\displaystyle C = B\log_{2}\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon channel capacity | bits/s |
| 10 | $\displaystyle T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{c}$ | One-way propagation delay | seconds (s) |

**Boundary Conditions Used in Examinations:**
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- Wavelength at 5.9 GHz: $\lambda = c/f \approx 0.0508$ **m ≈ 5.08 cm**
- Standard noise temperature: $T = 290$ **K** (IEEE reference)

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The Friis equation determines the maximum **broadcast radius** of an OBU, which directly decides how far ahead a vehicle can "sense" danger — critical for highway free-flow speeds of 120 km/h where a 1-second earlier warning equals ~33 m of saved stopping distance.

## 2.3 Communication Topologies — Why and How

- **Broadcast (One-to-All):** Used for BSMs — every nearby vehicle receives. **Why:** Safety requires low-latency, no pairing overhead.
- **Unicast (One-to-One):** Used for V2R toll / firmware updates. **Why:** Requires acknowledgements and confidentiality.
- **Geo-Casting (One-to-Area):** Used for emergency warnings — broadcast only within a defined geographic polygon. **Why:** Conserves spectrum; targets only relevant vehicles.
- **Ad-Hoc Mesh:** No fixed infrastructure; multi-hop packet forwarding. **Why:** Resilience when no RSU is present.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Reliable Communication Range from Friis Equation

**Given:**
- Transmit power $P_t = 20$ dBm $= 0.1$ W
- Transmit antenna gain $G_t = 1$ (omnidirectional)
- Receive antenna gain $G_r = 1$ (omnidirectional)
- Operating frequency $f = 5.9$ GHz
- Minimum receiver sensitivity $P_{r,\min} = -85$ dBm

**Find:** Maximum reliable range $R_{\max}$.

**Step 1:** Convert wavelength from frequency.

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{5.9 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$\lambda = 0.0508\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Convert all parameters to linear scale.

$$P_t = 10^{20/10} \times 10^{-3} = 0.1\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{r,\min} = 10^{-85/10} \times 10^{-3} = 3.16 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Friis free-space transmission equation solved for distance.

$$R_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_t\, G_t\, G_r\, \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2}\, P_{r,\min}}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values.

$$R_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1 \times 1 \times 1 \times (0.0508)^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \times 3.16 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate numerator.

$$0.1 \times (0.0508)^{2} = 0.1 \times 2.5806 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5806 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate denominator.

$$(4\pi)^{2} = (12.566)^{2} = 157.91$$

$$157.91 \times 3.16 \times 10^{-12} = 4.990 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 7:** Take the ratio.

$$\frac{2.5806 \times 10^{-4}}{4.990 \times 10^{-10}} = 5.171 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 8:** Take the square root.

$$R_{\max} = \sqrt{5.171 \times 10^{5}} = 719.1\ \text{m}$$

**Conclusion:** Under ideal free-space conditions, the OBU can reliably communicate up to approximately **719 m** — well above the 300 m minimum required for highway cooperative awareness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-To-Collision (TTC) for a Following Vehicle

**Given:**
- Leading vehicle position: $(x_1, y_1) = (0, 0)$ with velocity $v_1 = 25$ m/s
- Following vehicle position: $(x_2, y_2) = (60, 0)$ with velocity $v_2 = 30$ m/s
- Both on same lane, same heading.

**Step 1:** Compute inter-vehicle distance (longitudinal, same y).

$$d_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^{2} + (y_1 - y_2)^{2}}$$

$$d_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{(0 - 60)^{2} + 0^{2}} = 60\ \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute relative velocity (positive = closing).

$$\Delta v = v_1 - v_2 = 25 - 30 = -5\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Interpret sign convention. Since $\Delta v < 0$, vehicles are actually **separating** (the following vehicle is faster than the leader, but at a distance of 60 m the leader is pulling *away* only if the follower is faster *and* ahead — here leader is ahead, so if follower is faster it is closing).

**Corrected interpretation for closing scenario:** A closing relative velocity requires $v_2 > v_1$ with leader *ahead*. Closing rate $= v_2 - v_1 = 5$ m/s.

**Step 4:** Compute TTC.

$$\text{TTC} = \frac{d_{\text{rel}}}{\Delta v_{\text{closing}}} = \frac{60}{5} = 12\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5:** Compare to safety thresholds.

- $\text{TTC} = 12\ \text{s}$: **Safe** — no warning.
- $\text{TTC} < 4\ \text{s}$: **Caution warning** to driver.
- $\text{TTC} < 1.5\ \text{s}$: **Imminent collision warning** + AEB activation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Path Loss at 5.9 GHz for d = 500 m

**Step 1:** Convert distance to km: $d = 0.5$ km. Convert frequency to MHz: $f = 5900$ MHz.

**Step 2:** Apply free-space path loss formula in dB form.

$$L_{fs} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$$

$$L_{fs} = 20\log_{10}(0.5) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.45$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate logarithms.

$$20\log_{10}(0.5) = 20 \times (-0.3010) = -6.02\ \text{dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.7709 = 75.42\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4:** Sum the terms.

$$L_{fs} = -6.02 + 75.42 + 32.45 = 101.85\ \text{dB}$$

**Conclusion:** A 500 m link at 5.9 GHz incurs approximately **101.85 dB** of free-space path loss. This is why V2V uses a high EIRP and a sensitive receiver (−85 dBm typical).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | V2V | V2R (V2I) |
|---|---|---|
| Counterpart | Other moving vehicles | Fixed roadside infrastructure |
| Network Mode | Pure ad-hoc / broadcast | Infrastructure-supported |
| Update Latency | < 100 ms (BSM) | 100 ms – 10 s (SPaT/MAP) |
| Typical Range | 300 – 1000 m | 100 – 300 m (gantry/RSU) |
| Data Volume | Small (~400 B/BSM) | Moderate (MAP ~ few kB) |
| Critical Dependency | Penetration rate (% equipped) | RSU coverage density |
| Main Use | Collision avoidance | Signal info, tolling, work zones |
| Frequency Reuse | All OBUs share CCH | Spatial reuse by RSUs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — V2V + V2R Integrated Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VehA["Vehicle A - Host"]
        A1["CAN Bus\nWheel Speed, GPS, IMU"]
        A2["V2X Application Processor\nBSM Generator"]
        A3["802.11p / C-V2X\nRadio Modem"]
        A4["Antenna 5.9 GHz\nOmnidirectional 0 dBi"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4
    end

    subgraph VehB["Vehicle B - Neighbour"]
        B1["V2X Receiver\nBSM Decoder"]
        B2["Risk Assessment\nTTC Engine"]
        B3["HMI / AEB\nDriver Warning"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph RSU["Roadside Unit - V2R"]
        R1["Traffic Signal Controller\nSPaT Source"]
        R2["RSU Processor\nMAP plus SPaT"]
        R3["RSU Radio 5.9 GHz\nFixed Antenna"]
        R1 --> R2 --> R3
    end

    A4 -- BSM Broadcast\n5.9 GHz --> B1
    R3 -- SPaT plus MAP plus TIM\nDownlink --> A3
    A4 -- Acknowledgement\nUplink --> R3
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — BSM Exchange and Collision Warning

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V1 as Vehicle A
    participant V2 as Vehicle B
    participant RSU as Roadside Unit

    Note over V1,V2: Both vehicles broadcast BSM every 100 ms
    V1->>V2: BSM Part 1 - position, speed, heading
    V2->>V1: BSM Part 1 - position, speed, heading

    V1->>V1: Compute TTC vs Vehicle B
    Note over V1: TTC = 3.2 s - below 4 s threshold

    V1->>V1: Issue Forward Collision Warning to Driver

    RSU->>V1: SPaT Message - Signal 5 red in 12 s
    RSU->>V1: MAP Message - Intersection geometry
    V1->>V1: GLOSA advisory - reduce to 45 km/h
```

## 4.3 Functional Layer Architecture — V2X Protocol Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["Layer 7 - Applications\nForward Collision Warning, GLOSA, EEBL"]
    L6["Layer 6 - Facilities\nSAE J2735: BSM, SPaT, MAP, TIM"]
    L5["Layer 5 - Transport plus Network\nIEEE 1609.3 WSMP / IPv6"]
    L4["Layer 4 - Security\nIEEE 1609.2 - Digital Signatures"]
    L3["Layer 3 - MAC\nIEEE 802.11p CSMA-CA / C-V2X PC5"]
    L2["Layer 2 - PHY\nOFDM at 5.9 GHz, 10 MHz channel"]
    L1["Layer 1 - RF Front-End\nAntenna, LNA, PA, Filter"]

    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication. List any four typical safety applications enabled by V2V.**

**Model Answer:**
V2V communication is a short-range, wireless broadcast mode in which nearby vehicles exchange kinematic data (position, speed, heading, acceleration, brake status) at 10 Hz on the 5.9 GHz band, enabling each vehicle to extend its perception horizon beyond line-of-sight.

Four typical safety applications:
1. **Forward Collision Warning (FCW)** — warns when closing rate is too high.
2. **Emergency Electronic Brake Light (EEBL)** — alerts following vehicles of hard braking ahead.
3. **Blind Spot Warning (BSW)** — detects vehicles in adjacent lanes via BSM data.
4. **Intersection Movement Assist (IMA)** — prevents left-turn-acpath collisions at junctions.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Distinguish between DSRC and C-V2X in terms of radio access technology and deployment.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (3GPP) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Wi-Fi-derived | Cellular-derived |
| Radio Access | CSMA/CA contention | Sidelink PC5 / Uu uplink |
| Spectrum | 5.9 GHz ITS band | 5.9 GHz (PC5) or licensed cellular (Uu) |
| Latency | ~ 4 ms typical | ~ 20 ms typical |
| Network Dependency | None (ad-hoc) | Optional; can work ad-hoc via PC5 |
| Synchronization | Asynchronous | Synchronous (cellular timing) |
| Migration Path | Stable, mature | Evolving to 5G NR-V2X |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture and working of a Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) communication system** using the DSRC protocol stack. Clearly describe the contents of a Basic Safety Message and the role of each layer of the IEEE 802.11p / SAE J2735 stack. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Two vehicles are approaching an intersection. Vehicle A is at (0, 0) moving with velocity $v_A = 20$ m/s along the +x axis. Vehicle B is at (0, 200) moving with velocity $v_B = 18$ m/s along the −y axis. Compute:
  (i) The relative distance $d_{\text{rel}}$
  (ii) The closing speed $v_{\text{rel}}$
  (iii) The Time-To-Collision TTC
  (iv) Comment on whether an Intersection Movement Assist warning should be issued if the warning threshold is TTC < 4 s. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Architecture and Working of V2V Communication System:**

A V2V system has two main building blocks at each vehicle — the **OBU (On-Board Unit)** and the **Antenna** — plus the shared radio channel.

**Block Diagram:**

```
+-----------------------------------+
|   V2X Application Processor       |  <-- SAE J2735 BSM/SPaT/MAP
+-----------------------------------+
                |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Facilities + Network (1609.3)   |  <-- WSMP / IPv6
+-----------------------------------+
                |
+-----------------------------------+
|   Security (1609.2)               |  <-- Sign + Verify
+-----------------------------------+
                |
+-----------------------------------+
|   MAC (802.11p CSMA-CA)           |  <-- Channel access
+-----------------------------------+
                |
+-----------------------------------+
|   PHY (OFDM, 5.9 GHz, 10 MHz)     |  <-- Radio modulation
+-----------------------------------+
                |
+-----------------------------------+
|   RF Front-End (PA, LNA, Ant.)    |  <-- Wireless interface
+-----------------------------------+
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The host vehicle's CAN bus feeds kinematic data (speed, position, heading, brake status) into the V2X application processor every 100 ms.
2. The processor packages this into a **Basic Safety Message (BSM)** as defined in SAE J2735. BSM Part 1 contains core data (position, speed, heading, acceleration); BSM Part 2 contains optional data (path history, brake status, wiper state).
3. The message is signed using **IEEE 1609.2** digital signature, then passed to the MAC layer (**IEEE 802.11p**), which uses **CSMA-CA** contention to access the Control Channel (CH 178).
4. The PHY layer modulates the message using **OFDM** on a 10 MHz channel centered at **5.9 GHz** and transmits via the omnidirectional antenna.
5. Surrounding vehicles receive, verify the signature, decode the BSM, and feed it into their **remote-sensor fusion** algorithm.
6. If a collision risk is detected (TTC below threshold), the HMI issues a warning, and AEB may be commanded.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** Vehicle A at $(0, 0)$, $\vec{v}_A = (20, 0)$ m/s. Vehicle B at $(0, 200)$, $\vec{v}_B = (0, -18)$ m/s.

**(i) Relative distance** between A and B:

$$d_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{(0 - 0)^{2} + (0 - 200)^{2}} = 200\ \text{m}$$

**(ii) Closing speed** — magnitude of relative velocity:

$$\vec{v}_{\text{rel}} = \vec{v}_A - \vec{v}_B = (20 - 0,\ 0 - (-18)) = (20, 18)\ \text{m/s}$$

$$v_{\text{rel}} = \sqrt{20^{2} + 18^{2}} = \sqrt{400 + 324} = \sqrt{724} = 26.91\ \text{m/s}$$

**(iii) Time-To-Collision** — assuming straight-line trajectories maintained:

$$\text{TTC} = \frac{d_{\text{rel}}}{v_{\text{rel}}} = \frac{200}{26.91} = 7.43\ \text{s}$$

**(iv) Decision:** Since $\text{TTC} = 7.43\ \text{s} > 4\ \text{s}$, **no IMA warning is required** at this instant. However, as both vehicles continue, TTC will fall rapidly and the system must re-evaluate every 100 ms.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **principle and working of Vehicle-to-Roadside (V2R) communication**, with a neat diagram of the RSU deployment at a signalised intersection. Describe the **SPaT and MAP messages** and their role in enabling **Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory (GLOSA)**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A DSRC On-Board Unit (OBU) transmits with $P_t = 33$ dBm on a carrier frequency of $f = 5.9$ GHz. The transmit and receive antennas are both omnidirectional with 0 dBi gain. The receiver sensitivity is $P_{r,\min} = -89$ dBm. Compute the **maximum theoretical free-space communication range**. State one engineering reason why the practical range is shorter than this value. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) V2R Communication Principle and Working:**

**Principle:** Vehicle-to-Roadside (V2R), a subset of V2I, is a fixed-to-mobile communication mode where a stationary **Road-Side Unit (RSU)** mounted on traffic-signal poles, gantries, or toll plazas exchanges standardised messages with passing OBUs. It enables the vehicle to receive information it cannot sense alone — current signal state, intersection geometry, road work zones, tolling data, weather alerts.

**RSU Deployment at a Signalised Intersection:**

```
                   North
                    |
   [RSU-1]          |
   (NW corner)----[Intersection]
        \              |
         \         [RSU-2] (NE corner)
          \           |
           \          |
            \         |
             ----[RSU-3]----
                  (South, gantry)
              |          |
           [OBU]      [OBU]
        Vehicle A    Vehicle B
```

**Working Steps:**
1. **Signal Controller → RSU:** The traffic-signal controller exposes its live phase/timing data over an industry bus (e.g., NTCIP 1202) to the RSU processor.
2. **SPaT Generation:** The RSU encodes the current phase, time-to-next-phase, and minimum/maximum green durations into a **Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT)** message every 100 ms (SAE J2735).
3. **MAP Generation:** The RSU broadcasts a **MAP** message containing the geometric description of the intersection — lane topology, stop-bar locations, allowed movements.
4. **OBU Reception:** A passing OBU receives SPaT + MAP, decodes them, and fuses with its own GPS position to determine which lane it is in.
5. **GLOSA Computation:** Knowing (i) distance to stop-bar $d$, (ii) current speed $v$, and (iii) remaining red/green time $t_r$, the OBU computes the optimal speed:

$$v_{\text{opt}} = \frac{d}{t_{\text{green,remaining}}} \quad \text{or recommended deceleration to avoid stopping.}$$

6. The HMI displays "Reduce to 35 km/h — green in 12 s", improving fuel efficiency and reducing hard braking.

**(b) Maximum Theoretical Range Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $P_t = 33$ dBm $\Rightarrow 10^{33/10} \times 10^{-3} = 2.0\ \text{W}$
- $f = 5.9$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = c/f = 0.0508\ \text{m}$
- $G_t = G_r = 1$
- $P_{r,\min} = -89$ dBm $\Rightarrow 10^{-89/10} \times 10^{-3} = 1.259 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$

**Step 1:** Apply Friis equation solved for $R_{\max}$.

$$R_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} P_{r,\min}}}$$

**Step 2:** Numerator.

$$P_t \lambda^{2} = 2.0 \times (0.0508)^{2} = 2.0 \times 2.581 \times 10^{-3} = 5.161 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Denominator.

$$(4\pi)^{2} \times P_{r,\min} = 157.91 \times 1.259 \times 10^{-12} = 1.988 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 4:** Ratio.

$$\frac{5.161 \times 10^{-3}}{1.988 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.596 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 5:** Square root.

$$R_{\max} = \sqrt{2.596 \times 10^{7}} = 5095\ \text{m} \approx 5.1\ \text{km}$$

**Engineering Reason for Shorter Practical Range:**
In real urban/highway environments, the signal does **not** propagate in pure free-space. It undergoes:
- **Multipath fading** due to reflections from buildings, vehicles, and terrain.
- **Shadowing** by trucks, foliage, and overpasses.
- **Atmospheric and rain attenuation** at 5.9 GHz.
- **Receiver noise figure** degradation and **co-channel interference** from neighbouring OBUs.

These losses typically add **15 – 25 dB** beyond the free-space path loss, reducing the practical OBU broadcast range to **300 – 1000 m**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in TTC** — students often forget that *closing* speed requires $v_{\text{follower}} > v_{\text{leader}}$ in the same direction. Always draw the geometry first.
> 2. **Mixing dBm and dB** — receiver sensitivity of −85 dBm is a **power** (referenced to 1 mW), not a voltage. Convert to watts before plugging into Friis.
> 3. **Confusing V2V with V2I** — examiners deduct marks if you describe SPaT/MAP as part of V2V; they belong to V2I/V2R.
> 4. **Units of frequency in Friis** — the constant 32.45 in $L_{fs} = 20\log(d) + 20\log(f) + 32.45$ is valid **only** when $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**. Mixing SI units (m, Hz) gives wrong answers.
> 5. **Forgetting antenna gain** — if the antenna has gain $G > 1$ (e.g., a directional RSU antenna), it must be included; ignoring it yields ~3 – 10 dB underestimation of range.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **V2V** = vehicle ↔ vehicle, **V2R** = vehicle ↔ roadside infrastructure. Together = part of **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)**.
- Operating frequency: **5.9 GHz DSRC band** (5.85 – 5.925 GHz); alternative is **C-V2X PC5 sidelink** on cellular.
- Channel bandwidth: **10 MHz**, control channel **CH 178**.
- BSM broadcast frequency: **10 Hz** (every 100 ms); BSM size ≈ **400 bytes**; latency target **< 100 ms**.
- **802.11p** = PHY/MAC, **SAE J2735** = message dictionary (BSM, SPaT, MAP, TIM), **IEEE 1609.2** = security/signing, **IEEE 1609.3** = networking/WSMP.
- Free-space path loss: $L_{fs} = 20\log(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 32.45$ **(dB)**.
- Friis maximum range: $R_{\max} = \sqrt{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^{2} / [(4\pi)^{2} P_{r,\min}]}$.
- TTC = $d_{\text{rel}} / \Delta v_{\text{closing}}$; thresholds typically **4 s** (caution) and **1.5 s** (imminent).
- Wavelength at 5.9 GHz: $\lambda \approx 5.08$ cm — short, hence highly directional-feeling but uses omnidirectional antennas for broadcast.
- V2V is **ad-hoc** (no infrastructure), **broadcast** (no handshake), **CSMA-CA** contention-based.
- V2R enables **GLOSA**, **e-tolling**, **SPaT-based advisory**, **TIM alerts**.
- Practical range **300 – 1000 m**, far less than free-space theoretical because of multipath, shadowing, and interference.
- Co-operative awareness = each vehicle acts as a **remote sensor** for its neighbours, extending perception beyond line-of-sight.
- Vehicle penetration rate is the **single biggest determinant** of V2V safety benefit — a 50% equipped fleet reduces unimpaired-driver crashes by an estimated ~30%.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relative Merits and Demerits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT723 - AUTOMOTIVE NAVIGATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relative Merits and Demerits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Navigation and Control**, the topic **"Relative Merits and Demerits"** refers to a structured, comparative analytical study of the principal vehicle positioning, navigation, and guidance methodologies used in modern automotive telematics. Each method — Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), Dead Reckoning (DR) / Inertial Navigation System (INS), Map Matching (MM), Cellular / Network-Based Positioning (LBS), and Sensor Fusion (Integrated Navigation) — is evaluated against a common set of engineering criteria: **accuracy, availability, continuity, integrity, cost, complexity, immunity to interference, and suitability for real-time in-vehicle deployment.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner's Phrasing):**
> *Relative merits and demerits denote the systematic evaluation of competing automotive navigation techniques based on measurable performance parameters, qualitative engineering trade-offs, and application-specific suitability, so that an optimal architecture can be selected for a given vehicle platform.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Tourist With Multiple Maps" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are a tourist in a new city trying to reach a hotel. You have several maps (tools) at your disposal:
> - **A GPS device (Satnav)** → Tells you exactly where you are *if the sky is visible* (good outdoors, poor in tunnels).
> - **A Pedometer + Compass (Dead Reckoning)** → Tracks where you came from using your last known position and step count (works in tunnels but accumulates error).
> - **A Local Guide holding a city map (Map Matching)** → Snaps your approximate position onto the nearest road, hiding GPS noise.
> - **A Friend tracking your phone signal (LBS)** → Gives a rough location via cell towers (low accuracy but works indoors).
> - **A Smart Watch combining GPS + Step Counter + Barometer (Sensor Fusion)** → Combines everything for the most reliable answer.
>
> Each tool has **merits** (what it does best) and **demerits** (where it fails). An automotive navigation system engineer must *combine* these tools to make sure the car never loses its way — that is the essence of studying **relative merits and demerits**.

### 1.3 Common Evaluation Criteria (Standard Metrics)

| Criterion | Symbol / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Position Accuracy | CEP, m | Circular Error Probable — radius within which 50% of fixes lie |
| Update Rate | Hz | Number of position fixes computed per second |
| Availability | % | Percentage of time a usable fix is produced |
| Continuity | — | Probability of losing the fix during an operation |
| Integrity | m (alert limit) | System's ability to warn when it should not be used |
| TTFF | s | Time To First Fix (cold/warm/hot start) |
| Cost | $ | Bill of materials for OEM integration |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 exams, any comparative answer should always reference **at least three of the above metrics**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comparative Analysis Framework

To rigorously discuss relative merits and demerits, we classify navigation methods into **five fundamental categories** as per the KTU 2024 OEMUT723 syllabus:

1. **GNSS-based Positioning** (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, IRNSS, BeiDou)
2. **Dead Reckoning (DR) / Inertial Navigation** (Gyroscope + Odometer)
3. **Map Matching** (Algorithmic / Probabilistic)
4. **Cellular / Network-Based Positioning (LBS)** (GSM, LTE, 5G NR)
5. **Integrated / Sensor-Fused Navigation** (Kalman Filter based hybrid)

### 2.2 System-by-System Merits & Demerits

#### 2.2.1 GNSS-Based Navigation (GPS/GNSS)

**Operational Principle:** A receiver on the vehicle measures pseudoranges to ≥ 4 satellites and solves a trilateration equation to obtain (x, y, z, t).

**Merits:**
- High absolute accuracy (2.5 m civilian, sub-meter with SBAS).
- Global coverage with consistent coordinate frame (WGS-84).
- Low cost of receiver ICs (~$2–$5 in 2024).
- Standardized, interoperable across OEMs.

**Demerits:**
- Requires line-of-sight to satellites — fails in **urban canyons, tunnels, parking garages, and dense foliage**.
- Susceptible to **multipath errors** (5–50 m) in dense cities.
- Vulnerable to **jamming and spoofing**.
- Cold start TTFF may be 30–120 s.
- Refresh rate limited to 1–10 Hz (insufficient for high-dynamics control).

#### 2.2.2 Dead Reckoning (DR) / Inertial Navigation

**Operational Principle:** Position is integrated forward from a known starting point using heading (yaw rate gyro) and distance (wheel-tick odometry).

**Merits:**
- **Fully autonomous** — no external signal required.
- Works in tunnels, underground, and GNSS-denied environments.
- High update rate (50–200 Hz).
- Impervious to jamming/spoofing.

**Demerits:**
- **Unbounded error growth** with time/distance (typically 1–2% of distance traveled).
- Requires **periodic GNSS re-initialization**.
- MEMS-grade IMUs suffer bias drift (5–20 deg/hr).
- Sensitive to wheel-slip and tire-pressure variations.
- Cannot provide absolute position without a reference.

#### 2.2.3 Map Matching (MM)

**Operational Principle:** A software algorithm projects a noisy raw GPS/DR trajectory onto the digital road network using geometric, topological, or probabilistic matching.

**Merits:**
- Eliminates positional noise (snaps to correct road segment).
- Computationally inexpensive (suitable for embedded ECUs).
- Enables logical navigation (turn-by-turn instructions).
- Improves perceived accuracy from ±10 m to ±2 m.

**Demerits:**
- **Requires an up-to-date digital map** — costly to maintain.
- Fails on **unnamed roads, informal paths, or newly constructed roads**.
- Cannot function in **off-road** or **parking-lot** scenarios.
- Sensitive to road network density and topology errors.

#### 2.2.4 Cellular / Network-Based Positioning (LBS)

**Operational Principle:** Position is estimated via triangulation/trilateration of the vehicle's signal across cellular base stations (GSM/LTE/5G).

**Merits:**
- Works where GNSS fails (indoor parking, urban low-sky-visibility zones).
- Leverages existing telecom infrastructure — no extra receiver hardware.
- Cost-effective for fleet management applications.

**Demerits:**
- **Very low accuracy** (50–500 m in suburban, 100 m–2 km in rural).
- High latency compared to GNSS.
- Dependent on network operator and coverage.
- Privacy concerns with continuous tracking.

#### 2.2.5 Integrated / Sensor-Fused Navigation (Hybrid Architecture)

**Operational Principle:** Combines GNSS + DR + Map + LBS via an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** or **Particle Filter**, exploiting the complementary strengths of each subsystem.

**Merits:**
- **Best overall accuracy and availability** (sub-meter to meter-level continuous).
- Drift correction — GNSS periodically resets INS error growth.
- Robust to individual sensor failures (graceful degradation).
- Enables ADAS functions (lane-level positioning, predictive cruise).

**Demerits:**
- **Highest system cost and complexity** (multi-sensor fusion, software).
- Filter tuning is non-trivial; divergence possible under poor models.
- Requires expert calibration and ISO 26262 functional safety validation.
- Higher power consumption on embedded ECUs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{CEP} = 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{xy} \sqrt{\ln 4}$ | Circular Error Probable from horizontal scatter | GPS: ~2.5 m |
| 2 | $\sigma_{DR}(t) = \sigma_{0} + k \cdot v \cdot t$ | Dead-reckoning error grows linearly with time & velocity | $k \approx 0.01 - 0.02$ |
| 3 | $\dot{\phi} = \phi + \omega_{ie}\Delta t$ | INS attitude update (Euler) | MEMS grade |
| 4 | $P_{k\mid k-1} = F_k P_{k-1} F_k^{T} + Q_k$ | Kalman Filter covariance prediction | EKF core |
| 5 | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^{T}(H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^{T} + R_k)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain — weights GNSS vs INS | $0 \le K \le 1$ |
| 6 | $d_{\text{map}} = \min_{i}\Vert \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{p}_{i}^{\text{road}} \Vert$ | Map matching cost (nearest road point) | $< 15$ m typical |
| 7 | $\text{HDOP} = \sqrt{d_{11}+d_{22}}$ | Horizontal Dilution of Precision | $1 \le \text{HDOP} \le 20$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 ESE, examiners expect students to **define HDOP, CEP, TTFF, and EKF covariance** when writing comparative answers. Memorize equations 1, 2, 4, and 5 above.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **merits-demerits framework** is the cornerstone of:
- **OEM platform architecture selection** (which sensor stack to mount on a budget vs premium vehicle).
- **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)** decision-making for ASIL-rated navigation.
- **ADAS design** — lane-keeping, predictive ACC, and V2X all require reliable absolute + relative position.
- **Fleet management & insurance telematics** (low-cost LBS vs premium hybrid).
- **Autonomous Vehicle (L3–L5) sensor stack design** — GNSS alone is never sufficient; sensor fusion is mandatory.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Dead Reckoning Error Grows Unbounded

The DR position update is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_k &= x_{k-1} + v_k \cdot \cos(\psi_k) \cdot \Delta t \\
y_k &= y_{k-1} + v_k \cdot \sin(\psi_k) \cdot \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Assume heading error $\delta\psi_k$ and speed error $\delta v_k$ are present at every step. The propagated position error is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta x_k &= \delta x_{k-1} + \delta v_k \cos(\psi_k)\Delta t - v_k \sin(\psi_k)\delta\psi_k \Delta t \\
\delta y_k &= \delta y_{k-1} + \delta v_k \sin(\psi_k)\Delta t + v_k \cos(\psi_k)\delta\psi_k \Delta t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Squaring and summing gives the 2-D position variance. Ignoring cross-terms (orthogonal assumption), the variance of the magnitude of position error is:

$$
\sigma_{DR}^{2}(k) = \sigma_{DR}^{2}(k-1) + (\delta v^2 + v^2 \delta\psi^2)\Delta t^2
$$

**Step 3:** Treating $\delta v$ and $\delta\psi$ as constants, summing a geometric series over $N = t/\Delta t$ steps yields:

$$
\sigma_{DR}^{2}(t) = \sigma_{0}^{2} + N(\delta v^2 + v^2 \delta\psi^2)\Delta t^2
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $N\Delta t = t$:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{DR}(t) = \sqrt{\sigma_{0}^{2} + (\delta v^2 + v^2 \delta\psi^2)\,t^{2}}}
$$

> This shows DR error grows **quadratically with time**, which is precisely why DR must be **periodically re-aligned with GNSS** to bound the error — the foundation for sensor fusion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Kalman Filter for GNSS/INS Fusion (Linear Case)

We model the INS error state as $\mathbf{x}_k = [\delta x, \delta y, \delta v_x, \delta v_y]^{T}$.

**Step 1 — State Prediction (Time Update):**

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k|k-1} = F_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1|k-1}
$$

$$
P_{k|k-1} = F_k P_{k-1|k-1} F_k^{T} + Q_k
$$

where $Q_k$ is the process noise covariance from INS.

**Step 2 — Measurement Update (GNSS provides position $z_k$):**

$$
\mathbf{z}_k = H_k \mathbf{x}_k + \mathbf{w}_k, \quad \mathbf{w}_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Kalman Gain:**

$$
K_k = P_{k|k-1} H_k^{T} (H_k P_{k|k-1} H_k^{T} + R_k)^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — State Correction:**

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k|k} = \mathbf{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (\mathbf{z}_k - H_k \mathbf{x}_{k|k-1})
$$

**Step 5 — Covariance Update:**

$$
P_{k|k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k|k-1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Kalman Gain $K_k$ automatically balances **GNSS noise ($R_k$)** vs **INS drift ($Q_k$)**. When $R_k$ is small (good GNSS), $K_k \to 1$ → trust GNSS. When $R_k$ is large (GNSS denied, e.g., tunnel), $K_k \to 0$ → trust INS/DR. **This is mathematically the precise reason why hybrid systems are superior.**

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | GNSS (GPS) | Dead Reckoning | Map Matching | Cellular (LBS) | Hybrid / Fused |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy (m)** | 2.5–5 | 10–100 (drift) | 2–5 (snap) | 50–500 | 0.5–3 |
| **Availability** | Outdoor only | Always | Wherever map exists | Urban/suburban | Always (best) |
| **Update Rate** | 1–10 Hz | 50–200 Hz | Algorithm-dependent | 0.1–1 Hz | 10–100 Hz |
| **Cost ($)** | 2–10 | 30–150 (IMU) | 5–20 (map + CPU) | 0 (modem) | 100–500 |
| **Tunnel/Indoor** | Fails | Works | Limited | Works | Works |
| **Jamming Robust** | Poor | Excellent | N/A (software) | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | High |
| **Off-road** | Yes | Yes | Fails | Limited | Yes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Hybrid Automotive Navigation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[GNSS Receiver] -->|Pseudorange| F1[Sensor Fusion Engine EKF]
    A2[IMU Gyroscope] -->|Angular rate| F1
    A3[Wheel Odometry] -->|Speed pulses| F1
    A4[Vehicle CAN Bus] -->|Steering yaw speed| F1
    A5[Cellular Modem LBS] -->|Network fix| F1
    F1 -->|Fused position| B1[Map Matching Module]
    B1 -->|Snapped road segment| C1[Route Guidance Engine]
    C1 --> D1[HMI Display HUD Cluster]
    F1 --> D2[ADAS Controller ACC LKA]
    F1 --> D3[Telematics Cloud V2X]
    B1 --> D1

    classDef sensor fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef fusion fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 sensor
    class F1,B1,C1 fusion
    class D1,D2,D3 app
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree: Selecting a Navigation Method (KTU Exam Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start Selection]) --> Q1{Need absolute position globally?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Sky visibility guaranteed?}
    Q1 -- No --> DR1[Use Dead Reckoning only]
    Q2 -- Yes --> G1[Use GNSS GPS]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Budget allows IMU + Map?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> F1[Use Hybrid Sensor Fusion EKF]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need basic location only?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> L1[Use Cellular LBS]
    Q4 -- No --> DR1
    G1 --> M1[Add Map Matching for road snap]
    F1 --> M1
    DR1 --> M2[No map available off road]
    M1 --> END([Optimal Navigation Output])
    L1 --> END
    M2 --> END

    classDef proc fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    class S,END io
    class Q1,Q2,Q3,Q4,M1,M2,F1,G1,DR1,L1 proc
```

### 4.3 Error-Growth Comparison Graph (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Error magnitude] --> B[Time]
    subgraph ErrorGrowth
    L1[DR line quadratic rise] -.-> A
    L2[GNSS line constant band 2 to 5 m] -.-> A
    L3[Hybrid line bounded oscillation 0.5 to 3 m] -.-> A
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Limitation Note:** Mermaid cannot natively render time-series graphs with axes. The above is a *logical mapping* — in the KTU exam, students should hand-draw an **error-vs-time plot** with three curves: **DR (rising parabola)**, **GNSS (flat band)**, **Hybrid (low oscillating band)**. This is a high-scoring visual.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model QP, 2024 Scheme]**
*List any three merits of GNSS-based positioning over Dead Reckoning in automotive navigation.*

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. GNSS provides **absolute position** globally without accumulated drift, whereas DR requires a known starting point and accumulates error unboundedly.
2. GNSS is independent of vehicle-specific parameters (wheel size, tire pressure), unlike DR.
3. GNSS is unaffected by integration drift orgyro bias, while DR is highly sensitive to MEMS sensor errors.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model QP, 2024 Scheme]**
*What is the primary demerit of relying solely on a cellular network-based positioning system (LBS) for vehicle navigation?*

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Low spatial accuracy** — typically 50 m to 2 km, unacceptable for lane-level guidance.
2. **High dependency on operator network coverage and load**, leading to variable latencies and outages.
3. Inherent **privacy and security vulnerabilities** as the vehicle's location is constantly reported to network infrastructure.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Hybrid / Sensor-Fusion Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 4 Choice]**

**(a)** Compare the relative merits and demerits of GNSS-based positioning and Dead Reckoning (DR) navigation systems for an automotive platform. State the four most important evaluation parameters you would use, and justify each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how an **Extended Kalman Filter (EKF)** fuses GNSS and INS data to overcome the individual demerits of both. Show the two-step EKF recursion and explain the role of Kalman Gain. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks)**

| Parameter | GNSS (GPS) | Dead Reckoning |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | High absolute accuracy (2.5 m CEP) | Degrades with time; 1–2% of distance |
| **Availability** | Fails in tunnels/urban canyons | Works in any environment |
| **Update Rate** | 1–10 Hz | 50–200 Hz |
| **Error Growth** | Bounded, not time-dependent | Unbounded, quadratic with time |

**Justification of parameters (must write):**
