1. **Accuracy** — Primary metric for ADAS; GNSS is more accurate when available.
2. **Availability** — Critical for continuous tracking in tunnels, parking.
3. **Update Rate** — Required for high-speed control loops.
4. **Error Growth** — Determines need for re-initialization.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Neither is sufficient alone; both must be fused.

**(b) EKF-based Fusion (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
GNSS Receiver ──► Position Measurement z_k ──┐
                                              ├──► EKF Fusion ──► Fused Position
INS / Gyro + Odometer ──► Predicted State x_k ─┘
```

**Two-step EKF recursion (3 marks):**

**Prediction step:**
$$
\mathbf{x}_{k|k-1} = F_k \mathbf{x}_{k-1|k-1}, \quad P_{k|k-1} = F_k P_{k-1|k-1} F_k^{T} + Q_k
$$

**Update step:**
$$
K_k = P_{k|k-1} H_k^T (H_k P_{k|k-1} H_k^T + R_k)^{-1}
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_{k|k} = \mathbf{x}_{k|k-1} + K_k (\mathbf{z}_k - H_k \mathbf{x}_{k|k-1})
$$

**Role of Kalman Gain (1 mark):** $K_k$ automatically weights the GNSS measurement vs INS prediction. When GNSS is healthy ($R_k$ low), $K_k \to 1$ (trust GNSS); in tunnels ($R_k$ high), $K_k \to 0$ (trust INS).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Map Matching & LBS Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 4 Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Map Matching**. Discuss its merits and demerits when used as the sole navigation aid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the relative merits and demerits of **Cellular (LBS) positioning** vs **GNSS** for a fleet-management application operating in dense urban areas. Justify which you would recommend and why. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Map Matching (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Map Matching is a software technique that aligns a sequence of raw GPS/DR position estimates with the digital road network stored in a navigation database, in order to identify the correct road segment on which the vehicle is travelling.

**Merits (3 marks):**
1. Eliminates GPS noise and multipath — vehicle appears precisely on a road.
2. Low computational cost; suitable for embedded ECUs.
3. Enables logical turn-by-turn routing and lane guidance.

**Demerits (2 marks):**
1. Fails when the road network is incomplete, outdated, or off-road.
2. Cannot resolve ambiguity in dense parallel-road networks (e.g., highway + service road).

**(b) LBS vs GNSS in Dense Urban Areas (7 marks)**

| Parameter | GNSS (GPS) | Cellular (LBS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | 5–30 m (multipath) | 50–300 m |
| **Urban Canyon** | Degraded (multipath) | Robust (no LOS needed) |
| **Latency** | 1–10 Hz update | 1–30 s update |
| **Cost** | Receiver hardware | Telecom subscription |
| **Privacy** | Local computation | Reported to network |

**Recommendation (2 marks):** For dense-urban fleet management, an **LBS-augmented hybrid** (GNSS primary + LBS fallback) is optimal. Pure GNSS suffers multipath in urban canyons; pure LBS lacks accuracy for individual vehicle tracking. The hybrid gives the best cost–performance trade-off.

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE (Module 4 — Relative Merits & Demerits):**
> 1. **Listing without comparison** — Writing "GPS is accurate" without saying "compared to DR which drifts" → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **No quantitative numbers** — Always cite typical values (e.g., 2.5 m CEP for GPS, 1–2% drift for DR). Examiners reward numerical justification.
> 3. **Forgetting application context** — A fleet-management answer is different from an autonomous-vehicle answer. Always state the **target application** in your opening line.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in EKF questions** — A neat EKF block diagram is worth ~3 marks alone.
> 5. **Conflating GNSS with GPS** — GNSS is the *family* (GPS + GLONASS + Galileo + IRNSS + BeiDou). Use the correct term.
> 6. **Ignoring ISO 26262 / safety** — In 2024 Scheme, examiners increasingly reward mention of **functional safety**, **integrity**, and **fail-operational** behavior.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Relative Merits and Demerits:**
>
> - **GNSS** = absolute, global, but LOS-dependent and multipath-vulnerable.
> - **Dead Reckoning** = autonomous, high-rate, but **unbounded error growth** → re-align periodically.
> - **Map Matching** = software-only, snaps to roads, fails off-road and with stale maps.
> - **Cellular (LBS)** = works indoors/urban canyons, but low accuracy (50 m–2 km).
> - **Hybrid / Sensor Fusion (EKF)** = combines all; **best practical automotive solution**.
> - **Key metrics to memorize:** CEP, HDOP, TTFF, EKF covariance, Kalman Gain.
> - **Key equations:** $\sigma_{DR}(t) = \sigma_0 + kvt$ and $K_k = P H^T (H P H^T + R)^{-1}$.
> - **Application-driven selection:** fleet management → LBS + GNSS; ADAS → EKF hybrid; autonomous → GNSS + INS + Lidar + Maps + V2X.
> - **Mention ISO 26262 / functional safety** wherever the question touches ADAS or autonomous driving — this is a 2024-Scheme examiner favorite.
> - **Draw at least one diagram per 14-mark answer** — block diagram of EKF or hybrid architecture fetches 3–4 easy marks.
